> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compartment Fire: Factors Controlling Fire Severity, Ventilation Controlled and Fuel Controlled Fires; Pre-Flashover Compartment Fire - Growth Period and Definition of Flashover, Conditions Necessary for Flashover, Fuel and Ventilation Conditions for Flashover, Factors Affecting Time to Flashover, Factors Affecting Growth; Post-Flashover Compartment Fire - Regimes of Burning, Fully Developed Fire Behaviour, Temperatures Achieved in Fully Developed Fire, Fire Resistance and Fire Severity, Methods of Calculating Fire Resistance, Projection of Flames from Burning Compartments, Spread of Fire from Compartments; Compartment Temperature-Time Response at Pre-Flashover and Post-Flashover Periods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What is a Compartment Fire?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Compartment Fire):**
> A **compartment fire** is a fire that develops within an enclosed space (a compartment) bounded by walls, floor, ceiling, and openings, where the thermal and chemical behaviour of the fire is significantly influenced by the **feedback of heat** from these enclosing boundaries back to the fuel. This feedback mechanism is the defining characteristic that distinguishes a compartment fire from an unconfined (free-burning) fire.

In simple words, a compartment is any room, office, corridor, or enclosed space bounded by fire-resisting construction. Once fire breaks out inside, it is no longer "free-burning" — the walls absorb heat, radiate it back, and progressively raise the temperature of all fuels in the room until catastrophic conditions develop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker Effect":**
> Imagine lighting a small match inside a closed steel box. Initially the match burns normally. But because heat cannot escape, the box's internal temperature begins to rise. The walls heat up and start radiating heat back onto the match and any other fuel. Soon, the fuel ignites not because the match directly touched it, but because the *room itself became an oven*. This auto-acceleration is **compartment fire behaviour**. The walls act like a pressure cooker trapping heat — the more they trap, the hotter the fire gets, and the more the fire heats the walls. This is a self-reinforcing loop called **thermal feedback**.

### 1.3 Factors Controlling Fire Severity

Fire severity is a quantitative measure of the destructive potential of a fully developed fire inside a compartment. The **fire severity** is fundamentally controlled by the following parameters:

| S.No | Controlling Factor | Physical Meaning | Symbol |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | **Fire Load (Q)** | Total calorific energy released by all combustible contents in the compartment (in **MJ**) | Q |
| 2 | **Floor Area (A_t)** | Area of the compartment floor (in **m²**) | A_t |
| 3 | **Ventilation Area (A_v)** | Total area of window/door openings (in **m²**) | A_v |
| 4 | **Ventilation Height (H_v)** | Average height of openings (in **m**) | H_v |
| 5 | **Thermal Properties of Linings** | Thermal inertia (kρc) of walls, floor, ceiling | kρc |
| 6 | **Compartment Geometry** | Shape and height of the room | — |
| 7 | **Fuel Distribution** | Whether fuel is uniformly or concentrated | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The three *primary* factors controlling fire severity are: **Fire Load**, **Ventilation**, and **Compartment Lining Thermal Properties**. Every derivation in Module 1 revolves around these three pillars.

### 1.4 Ventilation Controlled vs Fuel Controlled Fires

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concept — Two Regimes of Compartment Fire:**
> The burning behaviour inside a compartment is governed by whichever factor is the *limiting* one: air (oxygen) supply or fuel supply.

**A. Ventilation-Controlled Fire:**
- Air (oxygen) supply is the **limiting factor**.
- Insufficient oxygen enters the compartment through openings to consume all the pyrolyzed fuel.
- Excess fuel vapors escape unburned through openings and burn outside as **external flaming**.
- Temperature inside is generally lower, but flames extend out of windows dangerously.
- Common in **small rooms with little ventilation** and large fuel loads.

**B. Fuel-Controlled Fire:**
- Fuel supply (pyrolysis rate) is the **limiting factor**.
- Sufficient oxygen is available; burning is limited by the rate at which fuel vapors are released.
- Combustion is **complete inside** the compartment.
- Higher internal temperatures are achieved.
- Common in **well-ventilated rooms** with limited fuel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identification Rule (KTU):**
> A fire is *ventilation-controlled* if the **Opening Factor (O)** is small, and *fuel-controlled* if O is large. The transition occurs when **O ≈ 0.07 m^(1/2)** (approximate threshold for cellulosic fires).

$$\text{Opening Factor: } O = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_t} \quad \left[\text{units: } \text{m}^{1/2}\right]$$

### 1.5 The Universal Fire Development Curve (Big Picture)

A compartment fire progresses through **distinct phases** — the most important framework in fire engineering:

> **Ignition → Growth (Pre-Flashover) → Flashover → Fully Developed (Post-Flashover) → Decay**

This entire arc, when plotted as **Gas Temperature vs Time**, is called the **compartment temperature-time curve** (or **fire development curve**). We will analyze this in great detail in subsequent sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Pre-Flashover Phase: Growth Period

The growth period begins at the moment of **ignition** and ends at **flashover**. During growth:

1. The fire is initially small (e.g., a burning wastebasket, a chair).
2. It spreads by **flame contact**, **radiation**, and **convection** to adjacent fuels.
3. The rate of Heat Release Rate (HRR) follows an approximate **t² (t-squared) growth**, the most widely used model in fire engineering.
4. The compartment is still cool enough that occupants may escape and firefighters may intervene.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — The t² Fire Growth Model:**
> The Heat Release Rate (HRR) during the growth phase is modeled as:
> $$ \dot{Q} = \alpha \, t^{2} $$
> where **α** is the fire growth coefficient (kW/s²) and **t** is time in seconds. The coefficient α categorizes fires into:
> - **Slow**: α = 0.0029 kW/s²
> - **Medium**: α = 0.0117 kW/s²
> - **Fast**: α = 0.0466 kW/s²
> - **Ultra-Fast**: α = 0.1876 kW/s²
>
> This classification is mandated in **ISO 16732** and **NFPA 72** for designing fire detection and suppression systems.

### 2.2 Definition of Flashover

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> **Flashover** is the sudden transition from a localized, fuel-controlled fire to a fully-developed fire in which **all combustible surfaces within the compartment are burning simultaneously**, accompanied by a rapid rise in gas temperature and release of combustible gases.

Multiple equivalent criteria define the moment of flashover:

- **Temperature criterion**: Upper gas layer temperature reaches **600 °C** (most common KTU reference).
- **Radiation criterion**: Heat flux at the floor reaches **20 kW/m²** (radiation from the hot gas layer is sufficient to ignite all exposed fuels).
- **Visible flame criterion**: Flames emerge from the open doorway (visual indicator).

### 2.3 Conditions Necessary for Flashover

Three *necessary and sufficient* conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

> 1. **Sufficient Fuel** — Combustible materials present in the compartment must be capable of producing enough pyrolyzed vapors.
> 2. **Sufficient Oxygen** — Adequate ventilation openings must exist (without which the fire cannot become fully developed).
> 3. **Heat Build-up to Critical Temperature** — The hot gas layer must reach a temperature high enough to radiate back to unburned fuels and ignite them.

### 2.4 Fuel and Ventilation Conditions for Flashover

These are the *quantitative* thresholds used in KTU problems:

- **Critical Heat Release Rate for Flashover (Q_fo)**: 
$$ Q_{fo} = 7.8 \, A_t + 378 \, A_v \sqrt{H_v} \quad \text{[kW]} $$
This is the **Babrauskas correlation** — essential for KTU numericals.

- **Critical Fire Load (q_fo)**: The minimum fire load density (per unit floor area) required to produce flashover.

### 2.5 Factors Affecting Time to Flashover (t_fo)

The **time to flashover** is a critical design parameter (occupant evacuation, structural protection).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Factors (in order of importance):**
> 1. **Fire Growth Rate (α)** — Higher α = faster t_fo.
> 2. **Ventilation Factor (A_v√H_v)** — More ventilation *initially* shortens t_fo but later exhausts fuel.
> 3. **Fire Load Density (q = Q/A_t)** — Higher fire load lengthens t_fo (more fuel to consume) but increases severity.
> 4. **Compartment Thermal Inertia (kρc)** — High thermal inertia walls absorb more heat, *delaying* flashover.
> 5. **Compartment Height** — Taller rooms have larger hot layer volume, delaying flashover.
> 6. **Fuel Distribution and Geometry** — Distributed fuels ignite faster than pile fuels.

### 2.6 Factors Affecting Growth

Growth depends on:
- **Ignition source strength and location**
- **Fuel type and arrangement** (cushion fires grow faster than wardrobe fires)
- **Surface flame spread rate** (depends on orientation, surface treatment)
- **Ceiling jet effects** (radiation from hot ceiling drives spread)
- **External radiation** (if the fuel is near a hot wall)

### 2.7 Post-Flashover Phase: Regimes of Burning

After flashover, the fire enters the **fully developed phase** and operates in one of two regimes:

| Regime | Controlling Parameter | Behaviour | Temperature |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Ventilation-Controlled** | Oxygen through openings | All fuel vapors cannot burn inside; flames extend outside | Up to ~1000 °C |
| **Fuel-Controlled** | Pyrolysis rate | Combustion is internal; oxygen is in excess | Up to ~1200 °C |

**Transition Criterion (Thomas-Law/Layer Zone Model):**
$$ \text{Ventilation Controlled if } \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_t} < 0.07 \, \text{m}^{1/2} $$

### 2.8 Fully Developed Fire Behaviour

In the fully developed phase:
- All combustible contents burn.
- Gas temperatures are at their **maximum**.
- Thermal attack on structural elements is most severe.
- Duration is governed by fuel supply and ventilation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Example:**
> Consider a typical office (A_t = 50 m², H = 3 m) with a single window (A_v = 4 m², H_v = 1.5 m). Wood furniture, paper, and plastics produce Q ≈ 500,000 kJ of fire load. The fully developed phase may last **15–30 minutes** at temperatures exceeding **1000 °C**.

### 2.9 Temperatures Achieved in Fully Developed Fire

- **Cellulosic fuels (wood, paper)**: ~1000–1100 °C
- **Hydrocarbon fuels (plastics, oil)**: ~1100–1200 °C
- **Special cases (chemical warehouses)**: may exceed 1300 °C

The **Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834)** and **Hydrocarbon Curve (HCM)** are reference time-temperature curves used for testing and design.

### 2.10 Fire Resistance vs Fire Severity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):**
> - **Fire Severity** = The actual thermal demand imposed by the fire on the structure, expressed as an **equivalent time of exposure to the standard fire curve** (t_e).
> - **Fire Resistance** = The ability of a structural element to withstand the standard fire for a specified period **without loss of function** (e.g., 2 hours, 4 hours).

**Ingberg's Formula (KTU 2024):**
$$ t_e = \frac{\log_{10}(8Q) + L}{k} $$
where:
- **Q** = fire load (in **pounds** of wood equivalent, or in kg — verify units carefully)
- **L, k** = empirical constants (L ≈ 45 min, k ≈ 8 for typical materials)
- **t_e** = equivalent fire severity (in minutes)

**Alternative (Margaret Law's Method):**
$$ t_e = \frac{W \, q}{A_v \sqrt{H_v}} \times \frac{1}{\text{some factor}} $$

### 2.11 Methods of Calculating Fire Resistance

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Principal Methods (KTU Expected):**
> 1. **Equivalent Fire Severity Method** — Converts real fire to equivalent standard fire duration.
> 2. **Time-Equivalent Formula (Ingberg, Law)** — Empirical algebraic expressions.
> 3. **Heat Balance Method** — Solves the differential equation governing heat transfer in the structural element.
> 4. **Standard Fire Test** — Actual furnace testing (ASTM E119 / IS 3809).

### 2.12 Projection of Flames from Burning Compartments

When a fully developed fire is **ventilation-controlled**, unburned gases escape through openings and burn outside as **flame plumes** or **external flaming**.

**Hasemi's Formula (KTU Board Standard):**
$$ \frac{L_f}{W} = -1.02 + 1.45 \left(\frac{\dot{Q}_w^2}{W^2 \, g \, \rho_a^2 \, c_p^2 \, T_a^2}\right)^{1/3} $$
where:
- **L_f** = length of flame projection
- **W** = width of opening
- **Q̇_w** = HRR per unit width of opening
- **g, ρ_a, c_p, T_a** = gravitational constant, air density, specific heat, ambient temperature

For window frame exposure, flames may project **2–3 m** above and to the side of windows.

### 2.13 Spread of Fire from Compartments

Fire spreads from the burning compartment to **adjacent compartments and floors** via four mechanisms:

1. **Conduction** through walls (slow, but occurs over hours).
2. **Convection** through openings (flames emerging from windows ignite upper-floor façade or adjacent buildings).
3. **Radiation** from external flame plumes and hot façade.
4. **Fire Spread via Cavities and Services** — through ducts, gaps, penetrations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Implication:**
> The **flame projection above windows** is a major cause of fire spread to upper floors. KTU problems often test the calculation of safe separation distances between buildings to prevent **radiation-induced ignition** of adjacent structures.

### 2.14 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ | t-squared fire growth | kW |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A_t}$ | Fire load density | MJ/m² |
| 3 | $O = \dfrac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_t}$ | Opening factor | m^(1/2) |
| 4 | $Q_{fo} = 7.8 A_t + 378 A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ | Critical HRR for flashover (Babrauskas) | kW |
| 5 | $t_e = \dfrac{\log_{10}(8Q) + L}{k}$ | Ingberg's fire severity | min |
| 6 | $T_{gas} \approx 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | ISO 834 standard fire curve | °C, min |
| 7 | $\tau = \sqrt{\dfrac{k \rho c}{t}}$ | Thermal penetration depth | m |
| 8 | $L_f = W \left[-1.02 + 1.45 \left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}_w^2}{W^2 g \rho_a^2 c_p^2 T_a^2}\right)^{1/3}\right]$ | Hasemi flame projection | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:**
> In any fire resistance numerical, the **fire load Q** in Ingberg's formula uses **pounds of wood equivalent** if the constants L and k are imperial. For SI units, use kg or MJ and verify the constant values from the KTU-approved data tables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Fire Severity (Ingberg's Method)

Ingberg's classical approach equates the area under the **real fire curve** to the area under the **standard fire curve** over the same duration. Here is the complete KTU-board derivation:

> **Starting Premise:** Two fires are equivalent if they impart the same total energy to a structural surface.

**Step 1: Write the Area Under the Standard Fire Curve**

The standard fire curve (ISO 834) is given by:
$$ T_{std} = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20 \quad [^\circ C] $$

The total heat energy delivered over time t is:
$$ E_{std} = \int_0^t \left[T_{std}(\tau) - T_{ref}\right] d\tau $$

where T_ref = 20 °C (room temperature).

**Step 2: Evaluate the Integral**

Substituting:
$$ E_{std} = \int_0^t 345 \log_{10}(8\tau + 1) \, d\tau $$

Let u = 8τ + 1, then du = 8 dτ, dτ = du/8:
$$ E_{std} = \frac{345}{8} \int_1^{8t+1} \log_{10}(u) \, du $$

Using $\int \log_{10}(u) \, du = u \log_{10}(u) - \dfrac{u}{\ln 10}$:
$$ E_{std} = \frac{345}{8} \left[ u \log_{10}(u) - \frac{u}{\ln 10} \right]_1^{8t+1} $$

**Step 3: Simplify**

$$ E_{std} = \frac{345}{8} \left[ (8t+1)\log_{10}(8t+1) - \frac{8t+1 - 1}{\ln 10} \right] $$

$$ E_{std} = \frac{345}{8} \left[ (8t+1)\log_{10}(8t+1) - \frac{8t}{2.3026} \right] $$

**Step 4: Equate to Real Fire Energy**

The real fire delivers energy based on fire load:
$$ E_{real} = \frac{Q}{A_t} \times \text{geometry factor} $$

Through empirical calibration, Ingberg found that the equivalent time t_e satisfies:
$$ t_e = \frac{\log_{10}(8Q) + L}{k} $$

This is the result used universally in KTU problems. The constants **L** and **k** depend on the lining material (e.g., L = 30 for concrete, k = 8 typical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Note on Constants:**
> In KTU problems, always check the units of Q. If Q is in **pounds of wood equivalent**:
> - k = 8.0, L = 45 (for ordinary construction)
> If Q is in **kg of wood equivalent**:
> - k = 8.0, L = 30
> The **fire load density** q (in MJ/m²) is the more modern SI parameter.

### 3.2 Derivation of Critical Heat Release Rate for Flashover

The Babrauskas formula relates the energy required to heat the upper gas layer to 600 °C plus the radiation losses:

**Step 1: Energy Balance in the Compartment**

At the moment of flashover, the HRR (Q̇_fo) must supply:
- (a) Energy to raise the upper gas layer to 600 °C
- (b) Energy lost through openings (convective)
- (c) Energy lost through wall conduction

**Step 2: Convective Losses Through Openings**

Mass flow rate of hot gases exiting through openings (per unit area):
$$ \dot{m} = C_d \rho_{\infty} \sqrt{2 g H_v} \quad [\text{kg/s·m}^2] $$

Total heat carried away:
$$ \dot{Q}_{vent} = \dot{m} A_v c_p (T_{fo} - T_{\infty}) $$

**Step 3: Conduction Losses Through Walls**

Using the steady-state heat conduction model (with thermal penetration depth):
$$ \dot{Q}_{wall} = \frac{k A_w (T_{fo} - T_{\infty})}{\sqrt{\alpha t}} $$

**Step 4: Empirical Correlation (Babrauskas, 1980)**

Through experimental fitting:
$$ \dot{Q}_{fo} = 7.8 A_t + 378 A_v \sqrt{H_v} \quad [\text{kW}] $$

This compact form implicitly accounts for all loss mechanisms.

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

> **Problem:**
> A room has floor area A_t = 30 m², one window of area A_v = 3 m², height H_v = 1.2 m. The fire load is Q = 200,000 MJ. Find:
> (a) The fire load density.
> (b) The opening factor.
> (c) The critical HRR for flashover.
> (d) The fire severity in equivalent minutes (Ingberg's formula with k = 8, L = 30, Q in kg of wood).

**Step 1: Fire Load Density**
$$ q = \frac{Q}{A_t} = \frac{200{,}000 \text{ MJ}}{30 \text{ m}^2} = 6{,}666.67 \text{ MJ/m}^2 $$

**Step 2: Opening Factor**
$$ O = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_t} = \frac{3 \times \sqrt{1.2}}{30} = \frac{3 \times 1.0954}{30} = 0.1095 \text{ m}^{1/2} $$

Since O > 0.07, the fire tends to be **fuel-controlled**.

**Step 3: Critical HRR for Flashover**
$$ \dot{Q}_{fo} = 7.8 \times 30 + 378 \times 3 \times \sqrt{1.2} $$
$$ \dot{Q}_{fo} = 234 + 378 \times 3 \times 1.0954 $$
$$ \dot{Q}_{fo} = 234 + 1242.13 = 1476.13 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 4: Fire Severity (t_e)**

Convert Q from MJ to kg of wood equivalent. Wood calorific value ≈ 18 MJ/kg.
$$ Q_{kg} = \frac{200{,}000}{18} = 11{,}111.11 \text{ kg} $$

Apply Ingberg's formula:
$$ t_e = \frac{\log_{10}(8 \times 11111.11) + 30}{8} $$
$$ t_e = \frac{\log_{10}(88888.89) + 30}{8} $$
$$ t_e = \frac{4.9488 + 30}{8} = \frac{34.9488}{8} = 4.37 \text{ min} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> The actual fire has a severity equivalent to a **4.37 minute** standard fire exposure. This is on the lower side; if the structural element is rated for 60 minutes, it has ample margin.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Compartment Fire Phases — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ignition Source] --> B[Incipient Phase]
    B --> C[Growth Phase - t-squared HRR]
    C --> D{Q_fo Reached?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[FLASH-OVER Transition]
    E --> F[Fully Developed Phase]
    F --> G{Fuel Exhausted?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Decay Phase]
    H --> I[Extinction]

    subgraph C1 [Pre-Flashover Zone]
        B
        C
    end
    subgraph C2 [Post-Flashover Zone]
        F
        H
    end
```

### 4.2 Factors Controlling Fire Severity — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Fire Load Q] --> S[Fire Severity t_e]
    V[Ventilation A_v H_v] --> S
    L[Lining Thermal Inertia k rho c] --> S
    G[Compartment Geometry] --> S

    S --> CAL[Ingberg Formula t_e]
    S --> LAW[Law Formula]
    S --> HB[Heat Balance Method]

    CAL --> OUT[Equivalent Standard Fire Duration]
    LAW --> OUT
    HB --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Ventilation vs Fuel Controlled Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Calculate Opening Factor O = A_v sqrt H_v / A_t]
    O --> C{O greater than 0.07 m power 1/2}
    C -- Yes --> FC[Fuel Controlled Regime]
    C -- No --> VC[Ventilation Controlled Regime]
    FC --> R1[Internal Combustion Complete]
    VC --> R2[External Flame Projection]
    R1 --> T1[Higher Internal Temperatures]
    R2 --> T2[External Fire Spread Risk]
```

### 4.4 Heat Release Rate Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Ignition at t=0] --> B[Growth t squared Phase]
    B --> C[Flashover at t_fo]
    C --> D[Steady State Fully Developed]
    D --> E[Decay as Fuel Depletes]
    E --> F[Extinction]

    B -.-> B1[HRR rising as alpha t squared]
    D -.-> D1[Constant HRR Q_max]
    E -.-> E1[HRR decays]
```

### 4.5 Compartment Temperature-Time Curve (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Pre Flashover]
        A1[Room Temperature 20 C] --> A2[Hot Layer Forms 100 200 C]
        A2 --> A3[Rapid Rise 400 600 C]
    end
    P1 --> FLASH[FLASH-OVER 600 C]
    FLASH --> P2
    subgraph P2[Post Flashover]
        B1[Maximum Temp 800 1100 C] --> B2[Sustained Burning Phase]
        B2 --> B3[Decay Phase Cooling]
    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define flashover and list the three conditions necessary for its occurrence.**

**Model Answer:**
> Flashover is the sudden transition of a localized fire to a fully developed fire in which all combustible materials in the compartment burn simultaneously, characterized by a rapid rise in upper gas layer temperature to about **600 °C** and a heat flux at the floor of about **20 kW/m²**.
>
> The three necessary conditions are:
> 1. **Sufficient fuel** — adequate combustible contents inside the compartment.
> 2. **Sufficient oxygen** — adequate ventilation through openings.
> 3. **Critical heat build-up** — hot gas layer temperature reaches the ignition temperature of adjacent fuels (≈ 600 °C).

#### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Distinguish between fire resistance and fire severity.**

**Model Answer:**
> **Fire severity** is a measure of the actual thermal demand imposed by a real fire on a structural element, expressed as an **equivalent time of exposure to a standard fire** (e.g., using Ingberg's formula: t_e = [log₁₀(8Q) + L]/k).
>
> **Fire resistance** is the ability of a structural element to withstand the standard fire for a specified duration without collapse, loss of integrity, or excessive heat transmission. It is determined through standard furnace tests (e.g., ASTM E119 / IS 3809) and is rated in **hours** (e.g., 2 hours, 4 hours).
>
> The relationship: a structural element is *safe* if its **fire resistance ≥ fire severity**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A:** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **pre-flashover growth period** of a compartment fire. Discuss the **factors affecting the time to flashover (t_fo)** with their relative influence.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A compartment has floor area A_t = 25 m², opening area A_v = 4 m², opening height H_v = 1.5 m, and fire load Q = 150,000 MJ. Calculate: (i) opening factor, (ii) fire load density, (iii) critical heat release rate for flashover, and (iv) fire severity in minutes using Ingberg's formula (k = 8, L = 30, with Q in kg of wood equivalent). State the burning regime.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Model Answer:**

> The **pre-flashover growth period** begins at ignition and ends at the moment of flashover. It is characterized by:
> - Localized burning at the seat of fire.
> - Heat Release Rate (HRR) following the **t² (t-squared) model** with growth coefficient α.
> - **Hot smoke layer** accumulating below the ceiling (stratification).
> - Radiation from the hot layer progressively heating lower fuels.
>
> **Factors affecting time to flashover (t_fo):**
> 1. **Fire growth rate (α):** Higher α → faster t_fo. Ultra-fast growth (α = 0.1876) may reach flashover in 1–2 min, while slow (α = 0.0029) may take 15–20 min.
> 2. **Ventilation factor (A_v√H_v):** Greater ventilation reduces t_fo (more air) until fuel-controlled regime.
> 3. **Fire load density (q = Q/A_t):** Higher q → longer t_fo (more fuel to heat to 600 °C), but greater post-flashover severity.
> 4. **Thermal inertia of linings (kρc):** Walls with higher kρc absorb more heat, delaying t_fo.
> 5. **Compartment height:** Taller rooms delay t_fo due to larger hot layer volume.
> 6. **Fuel distribution:** Distributed fuel ignites earlier than concentrated pile.

**Part (b) Model Solution:**

> **Given:** A_t = 25 m², A_v = 4 m², H_v = 1.5 m, Q = 150,000 MJ, k = 8, L = 30.
>
> **(i) Opening factor:**
> $$ O = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_t} = \frac{4 \times \sqrt{1.5}}{25} = \frac{4 \times 1.2247}{25} = 0.196 \text{ m}^{1/2} $$
>
> **(ii) Fire load density:**
> $$ q = \frac{Q}{A_t} = \frac{150{,}000}{25} = 6{,}000 \text{ MJ/m}^2 $$
>
> **(iii) Critical HRR for flashover (Babrauskas):**
> $$ \dot{Q}_{fo} = 7.8 A_t + 378 A_v \sqrt{H_v} = 7.8 \times 25 + 378 \times 4 \times 1.2247 $$
> $$ \dot{Q}_{fo} = 195 + 1852.07 = 2{,}047.07 \text{ kW} $$
>
> **(iv) Fire severity (Ingberg's):**
> Convert Q from MJ to kg of wood equivalent (calorific value ≈ 18 MJ/kg):
> $$ Q_{kg} = \frac{150{,}000}{18} = 8{,}333.33 \text{ kg} $$
> $$ t_e = \frac{\log_{10}(8 \times 8333.33) + 30}{8} = \frac{\log_{10}(66{,}666.67) + 30}{8} $$
> $$ t_e = \frac{4.8239 + 30}{8} = \frac{34.8239}{8} = 4.35 \text{ min} $$
>
> **Burning regime:** Since O = 0.196 > 0.07, the fire is **fuel-controlled**.

---

#### **Question B:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **post-flashover regimes of burning** with a clear distinction between ventilation-controlled and fuel-controlled fires. Use diagrams to support your answer.

**(b)** [7 Marks] What is **fire severity**? Describe **Ingberg's method** of calculating equivalent fire severity, including its assumptions, formula, and limitations. A warehouse has Q = 500,000 MJ of fire load. Compute the fire severity (in minutes) using Ingberg's formula (k = 8, L = 30).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Model Answer:**

> After flashover, the fire enters the **fully developed phase** where all combustible surfaces burn simultaneously. There are two regimes:
>
> **1. Ventilation-Controlled Fire:**
> - Air is the limiting factor.
> - Excess fuel vapors exit through openings unburned.
> - External flames project through windows.
> - Internal temperatures limited to ~1000 °C.
> - Identified when O < 0.07 m^(1/2).
>
> **2. Fuel-Controlled Fire:**
> - Fuel is the limiting factor.
> - Combustion is complete inside the compartment.
> - Internal temperatures may reach 1100–1200 °C.
> - Identified when O > 0.07 m^(1/2).
>
> *(Diagrams showing internal vs. external combustion zones should be drawn.)*

**Part (b) Model Solution:**

> **Definition of Fire Severity:**
> Fire severity is the actual thermal demand on a structural element due to a real fire, expressed as the equivalent duration of exposure to the standard fire (ISO 834) that would cause equivalent damage.
>
> **Ingberg's Method:**
> - **Assumption:** Two fires are equivalent if the areas under their time-temperature curves are equal.
> - **Formula:** 
>   $$ t_e = \frac{\log_{10}(8Q) + L}{k} \quad [\text{minutes}] $$
>   where Q is the fire load in kg of wood equivalent, k = 8 (typical), L = 30.
> - **Limitations:** 
>   1. Assumes uniform fire load distribution.
>   2. Empirical constants may not apply to all materials.
>   3. Does not account for ventilation effects on the actual fire curve.
>   4. Lacks physical basis for modern performance-based design.
>
> **Numerical:** Q = 500,000 MJ; calorific value of wood = 18 MJ/kg
> $$ Q_{kg} = \frac{500{,}000}{18} = 27{,}777.78 \text{ kg} $$
> $$ t_e = \frac{\log_{10}(8 \times 27{,}777.78) + 30}{8} = \frac{\log_{10}(222{,}222.22) + 30}{8} $$
> $$ t_e = \frac{5.3468 + 30}{8} = \frac{35.3468}{8} = 4.42 \text{ min} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Ingberg's formula uses Q in **kg of wood equivalent**; failing to convert from MJ costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong base for logarithm:** Always use log₁₀, not ln. KTU checkers penalize 1 mark for wrong log base.
> 3. **Confusing Q_fo (in kW) with Q (in MJ):** These are entirely different quantities — one is heat release rate, the other is total fire energy. Markers deduct **3 marks** for this confusion.
> 4. **Omitting the regime identification** (ventilation vs fuel) — a 1-mark deduction if not stated.
> 5. **In ISO 834, T_ref is 20 °C, not 0 °C** — for full marks, state the reference temperature.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary — Revise Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Compartment fire** = enclosed-space fire with **heat feedback** from boundaries.
- [ ] **Three factors** control fire severity: **Fire Load (Q), Ventilation (A_v√H_v), Lining Thermal Inertia (kρc)**.
- [ ] **Ventilation-controlled** → O < 0.07 m^(1/2); external flames; lower internal T.
- [ ] **Fuel-controlled** → O > 0.07 m^(1/2); internal combustion; higher T.
- [ ] **t² fire growth** model: Q̇ = αt²; **α values** for slow/medium/fast/ultra-fast: 0.0029 / 0.0117 / 0.0466 / 0.1876 kW/s².
- [ ] **Flashover** at upper layer **T = 600 °C** or floor heat flux **20 kW/m²**.
- [ ] **Babrauskas Q_fo = 7.8 A_t + 378 A_v√H_v** (in kW) — memorize this formula.
- [ ] **Ingberg t_e = [log₁₀(8Q) + L] / k** — Q in kg wood equivalent, k = 8, L = 30.
- [ ] **ISO 834 standard curve:** T = 20 + 345 log₁₀(8t + 1).
- [ ] **Fire severity ≠ fire resistance** — severity = thermal demand; resistance = structural capability.
- [ ] **Hasemi flame projection** governs external flame length above window openings.
- [ ] **Fire spread from compartments:** conduction, convection, radiation, and **flame projection from windows** (most critical).
- [ ] **Decay phase** begins when 70–80% of fuel is consumed; cooling rate ~7–10 °C/min.
- [ ] **Thermal inertia (kρc)** of concrete ≈ 2.0; gypsum ≈ 1.6; steel ≈ 0.1 (relative units).
- [ ] **Calorific value of wood** ≈ 18 MJ/kg (use this constant in all numericals).
- [ ] **Time to flashover** depends on α, q, A_v√H_v, kρc, and compartment height.
- [ ] **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** is the single most important variable in fire dynamics.

> **Best of luck for your KTU University Examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence of Fire Severity of Compartment Fire and Furnace Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equivalence of Fire Severity of Compartment Fire and Furnace Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Fire Resistance vs. Fire Severity — Setting the Stage

In **Structural Fire Safety Engineering**, two distinct but inter-related concepts govern the design philosophy of fire-resistant structures:

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Resistance** is a *property of the structural element* (beam, column, slab, wall). It is measured in the **standard furnace test** (ISO 834 / ASTM E119) as the duration (in minutes) for which the element maintains stability, integrity, and insulation under a prescribed time–temperature curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Severity** is a *property of the actual compartment fire* (real-world room fire). It represents the actual destructive thermal load imposed on the structural element by the burning fuel (fire load) inside the compartment.

The fundamental design equation for fire safety is therefore:

$$\text{Fire Resistance of Element} \;\geq\; \text{Fire Severity of Compartment}$$

Since fire resistance is determined in a **standard furnace** using a uniform ISO 834 time–temperature curve, while the real compartment fire follows a *natural* (parametric) curve that depends on fuel, ventilation, and compartment geometry, we must establish **equivalence** between the two.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Equivalent Fire Severity

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence of Fire Severity (Time-Equivalent Concept):**
> *"The equivalent fire severity of a compartment fire is the time of exposure in the standard furnace test (ISO 834 curve) that would result in the same heating effect (or structural damage) on a structural member as the actual compartment fire under consideration."*

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$t_{e} = \text{Equivalent fire exposure time (minutes, on ISO 834 curve)}$$

The compartment fire and the furnace fire are considered **equivalent** when they cause the **same maximum steel temperature** (or the same thermal/structural response) in a protected steel member.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Thermometer Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
>
> Imagine a structural steel beam sitting in a real burning office room. The room fire has a strange temperature history: it heats up rapidly, peaks high, then cools down (natural curve). Now, imagine the same beam placed in a controlled laboratory furnace where temperature is artificially programmed to rise slowly and steadily forever (ISO 834 curve).
>
> The question is: *"How many minutes of the laboratory furnace fire would produce the **same peak temperature** in the beam as the real room fire?"*
>
> That number of minutes is the **equivalent fire severity (tₑ)**. It is the translator that converts a complex real-world fire into a single number comparable to the standard fire resistance rating.

**Real-world utility:** This equivalence allows structural engineers to:
- Use **laboratory-tested** fire-resistance data for elements in **real buildings**.
- Verify the design equation: *Required Fire Resistance ≤ Actual Element Fire Resistance*.
- Calibrate advanced performance-based design codes (Eurocode, IS codes).

### 1.4 Standard Furnace Fire (ISO 834 Curve) — Reference Baseline

The standard fire curve is the baseline against which all real fires are compared:

$$T - T_0 = 345 \,\log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

Where:
- **T** = Furnace temperature (°C) at time *t* (minutes)
- **T₀** = Initial ambient temperature (**typically 20 °C**)
- **t** = Time of exposure in minutes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: The ISO 834 curve grows **logarithmically without a decay phase** — it represents a sustained, never-ending severe fire. This is the conservative benchmark used globally in **BS 476, ASTM E119, and IS 3809** standard fire tests.

### 1.5 Natural Compartment Fire — The Real-World Phenomenon

A real compartment fire goes through **four distinct phases**, unlike the monotonic ISO 834 curve:

| Phase | Description | Temperature Behavior |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Ignition / Incipient** | Localised burning | Slowly rising |
| **Growth (Pre-flashover)** | Fire spreads over fuels | Rapid rise |
| **Fully Developed (Post-flashover)** | All combustibles involved | Plateau / peak |
| **Decay (Cooling)** | Fuel consumed | Cooling phase |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard furnace fire has **no decay phase** — it keeps heating forever. A real compartment fire **peaks then cools**. This asymmetry is precisely why *equivalence* is a non-trivial engineering concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Heat-Balance Framework for Compartment Fire

The temperature inside a fully-developed compartment fire is governed by the **compartment heat balance**:

$$Q_f = Q_l + Q_w + Q_r + Q_v$$

Where:
- **Q_f** = Total rate of heat release by fire (kW)
- **Q_l** = Heat losses by radiation/convection through openings (ventilation-controlled)
- **Q_w** = Heat absorbed by compartment boundaries (walls, ceiling, floor)
- **Q_r** = Re-radiated heat from boundaries back into the compartment
- **Q_v** = Heat carried away by hot gases venting out

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **fully-developed (post-flashover) regime**, the compartment behaves approximately as a *well-stirred reactor*. The gas temperature T_g can be estimated from the energy balance at the openings.

### 2.2 Ventilation-Controlled vs. Fuel-Controlled Compartment Fires

Two regimes govern fully-developed compartment fires:

- **Ventilation-Controlled Fire:** The fire is starved of oxygen; burning rate is limited by the size of openings. Most realistic compartment fires fall here.
- **Fuel-Controlled Fire:** Sufficient air is available; burning rate is limited by fuel surface area and pyrolysis kinetics.

> [!NOTE]
> For ventilation-controlled fires, the burning rate is given by:
> $$\dot{m} = 0.5 \; A_o \sqrt{H_o}$$
> where A_o is the total area of openings and H_o is the height of opening.

### 2.3 Classical Formulae for Equivalent Fire Severity (Time-Equivalent tₑ)

Multiple researchers have proposed formulations for tₑ. The most important for KTU examinations are:

#### 2.3.1 Ingberg's Formula (1928) — The Classical Approach
**Concept:** Equal fire severity produces equal maximum temperature in the structural element. Time equivalent is proportional to fire load density.

$$\boxed{t_e = k \cdot \frac{W}{A_T}}$$

- **tₑ** = Equivalent fire severity (minutes)
- **W** = Total fire load (kg of wood equivalent or MJ)
- **A_T** = Total internal surface area of compartment (walls + floor + ceiling)
- **k** = Empirical constant ≈ **1.0 minute·m²/kg** (wood equivalent)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** Ignores ventilation and material properties; considers only fuel and compartment surface area.

#### 2.3.2 Law's Formula (1971) — Improved Empirical Approach

$$\boxed{t_e = \frac{0.067 \, L}{\dfrac{A_v}{\sqrt{H_v}}}}$$

- **L** = Fire load density (kg/m² of wood equivalent)
- **A_v** = Area of vertical openings (m²)
- **H_v** = Height of opening (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Explicitly accounts for ventilation through A_v / √H_v factor. More realistic than Ingberg's formula.

#### 2.3.3 Pettersson / Magnusson–Thelandersson Formula (Heat-Balance Based)

This is the most theoretically rigorous approach:

$$\boxed{t_e = \frac{\gamma \cdot L}{\left(\dfrac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_T}\right)^{1/2}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{b}\right)}$$

Where:
- **L** = Fire load density (MJ/m²)
- **A_v** = Total area of vertical openings (m²)
- **H_v** = Height of openings (m)
- **A_T** = Total internal surface area of compartment (m²)
- **γ, b** = Empirical constants (commonly γ ≈ 0.0925, b ≈ √k·ρ·c)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pettersson's approach is based on the assumption that the **compartment fire reaches a steady-state temperature** during fully-developed phase. The equivalent time equals the time on ISO 834 curve that produces the **same steady-state temperature**.

#### 2.3.4 Equilibrium Compartment Temperature

The theoretical steady-state gas temperature in a fully-developed fire:

$$T_g - T_0 = \frac{Q_{tot}}{\dot{m} \cdot c_p}$$

Where Q_tot is the total heat release rate and ṁ·cₚ is the heat carried away by vented gases.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — KTU Board Exam Ready**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Validity |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|----------|
| 1 | ISO 834 Standard Curve | $T - T_0 = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | T in °C, t in min | Standard furnace test |
| 2 | Ingberg's Time-Equivalent | $t_e = k \cdot W / A_T$ | tₑ min, W kg, A_T m² | Empirical, fuel-only |
| 3 | Law's Time-Equivalent | $t_e = 0.067 L / (A_v / \sqrt{H_v})$ | tₑ min, L kg/m², A_v m², H_v m | Empirical w/ ventilation |
| 4 | Pettersson's tₑ | $t_e \propto L \cdot \sqrt{A_T} / \sqrt{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}$ | Heat-balance based | Theoretically rigorous |
| 5 | Ventilation Factor | $\Phi = A_v \sqrt{H_v} / A_T$ | Dimensionless | Common to all modern codes |
| 6 | Burning Rate (Vent. Ctrl.) | $\dot{m} = 0.5 A_o \sqrt{H_o}$ | kg/s (wood equiv.) | Post-flashover |
| 7 | Compartment Heat Balance | $Q_f = Q_w + Q_l + Q_r + Q_v$ | All in kW | Steady-state |
| 8 | Fire Load Density | $L = W / A_f$ | MJ/m² or kg/m² | Where A_f = floor area |
| 9 | Gas Temperature (steady) | $T_g - T_0 = Q_{tot} / (\dot{m} c_p)$ | K | Fully-developed |
| 10 | Design Criterion | $t_{e} \leq R_{element}$ | tₑ = severity, R = resistance | KTU design check |

### 2.5 Why is Fire Severity Equivalence Important?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in Real Practice:**
> 1. **Code Compliance:** Building codes (NBC, IS, Eurocode) specify fire-resistance ratings (30/60/90/120 min). Equivalence allows checking if an actual building's fire severity is within the rated value.
> 2. **Performance-Based Design:** Modern codes permit alternative solutions if engineers prove (via equivalence + heat transfer analysis) that actual fire severity ≤ element fire resistance.
> 3. **Retrofit Decisions:** When an old building changes use, equivalence helps decide whether existing structural members need additional fire protection.
> 4. **Insurance & Risk Assessment:** Quantifies the actual hazard level for insurance underwriting.

### 2.6 Practical Numerical Interpretation — Worked Insight

> [!NOTE]
> For a typical office compartment with:
> - L = 200 kg/m² (wood equivalent)
> - A_v = 6 m², H_v = 1.5 m
> - A_T = 200 m² (walls + ceiling + floor)
>
> **Law's formula:** $t_e = (0.067 \times 200) / (6 / \sqrt{1.5}) = 13.4 / 4.899 ≈ 2.74$ hours → equivalent to about **165 minutes** on ISO 834.
>
> **Interpretation:** The real fire severity is comparable to a 2.7-hour standard furnace test. A 2-hour (120-min) rated element would be *undersized*; a 3-hour rated element is safe.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: ISO 834 Standard Time–Temperature Curve

**Starting Premise:** The standard fire curve was derived to represent a "typical" building fire of fully-developed nature, growing logarithmically.

**Standard form (BS 476 / ISO 834):**

$$T(t) - T_0 = 345 \, \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

**Derivation logic (semi-empirical justification):**
The constant **345** is chosen so that at *t* = 0, T = T₀; at t = 30 min, T ≈ 842 °C; at t = 60 min, T ≈ 945 °C; at t = 120 min, T ≈ 1049 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification at t = 30 min:**
> $$T(30) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 30 + 1)$$
> $$= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(241)$$
> $$= 20 + 345 \times 2.382$$
> $$= 20 + 821.8$$
> $$T(30) \approx 842 \,°C \; \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Ingberg's Equivalent Fire Severity Formula

**Premise:** A structural steel member in both a compartment fire and a furnace fire reaches the same maximum temperature.

**Step 1:** Time to reach a given temperature in the furnace is set by ISO 834:
$$t_f = \frac{1}{8}\left(10^{(T-T_0)/345} - 1\right)$$

**Step 2:** For a compartment, total heat absorption by walls depends on total fire load W (kg of wood equivalent):
$$Q_{abs} = W \cdot H_c$$

where **H_c** is the calorific value of wood (≈ 18,600 kJ/kg).

**Step 3:** Assuming all heat is eventually absorbed by compartment surface A_T with a time-average heat flux q̇_avg:
$$Q_{abs} = q\_{avg} \cdot A_T \cdot t_e$$

**Step 4:** Equating and simplifying:
$$t_e \propto \frac{W}{A_T}$$

**Step 5:** Ingberg's empirical constant calibration yields:

$$\boxed{t_e \;[\text{min}] = \frac{W \;[\text{kg}]}{A_T \;[\text{m}^2]} \times \frac{H_c}{q_{avg}} \approx 1.0 \cdot \frac{W}{A_T}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **fuel load in MJ/m²**, the conversion is: $W \;[\text{kg wood}] = L \;[\text{MJ/m}^2] \times A_f \;[\text{m}^2] / 18.6$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Law's Equivalent Fire Severity (Modified Ingberg)

**Premise:** Ingberg ignored ventilation. Law introduced the ventilation factor.

**Step 1:** Ingberg's result is corrected by dividing by the ventilation factor:

$$t_e = \frac{k_1 \, L}{A_v / \sqrt{H_v}}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting empirical coefficients (Law's calibration with 73 fire tests):

$$t_e = \frac{0.067 \, L}{A_v / \sqrt{H_v}}$$

**Final form:**

$$\boxed{t_e \;[\text{min}] = 0.067 \cdot \frac{L \;[\text{kg/m}^2]}{A_v / \sqrt{H_v} \;[\text{m}^{1.5}]}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Pettersson's Heat-Balance Approach

**Step 1:** In a fully-developed ventilation-controlled fire, the compartment gas temperature reaches a quasi-steady state governed by:

$$T_g - T_0 \approx \frac{L \cdot A_f \cdot H_c}{\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot t_{bd}}$$

where **t_bd** is the duration of the burning phase.

**Step 2:** The temperature of the structural element (e.g., steel beam) is determined by:

$$T_s - T_0 = f(q'', t)$$

where q″ is the net heat flux to the steel.

**Step 3:** For thermal equivalence with the ISO 834 furnace at time tₑ:

$$T_s(T\_g^{\text{compartment}}, t) = T_s(T\_g^{\text{furnace at } t_e}, t_e)$$

**Step 4:** Pettersson proposed the following empirical form based on best-fit to fire test data:

$$\boxed{t_e = \frac{L \cdot \sqrt{A_T}}{\sqrt{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}} \cdot \frac{1}{b}}$$

where **b** is an empirical constant related to thermal properties (≈ √(k·ρ·c) of compartment lining).

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A library compartment has the following data:
- Floor area A_f = 100 m²
- Total internal surface area A_T = 380 m²
- Window opening area A_v = 9 m²
- Height of opening H_v = 1.5 m
- Fire load (books + wood furniture): L = 400 kg/m² (wood equivalent)

**Find:** Equivalent fire severity by Ingberg's and Law's formulae. Comment on adequacy for a **2-hour rated** structural element.

---

**Solution Part (a) — Ingberg's Method:**

$$t_e = k \cdot \frac{W}{A_T}$$

Total fire load: $W = L \cdot A_f = 400 \times 100 = 40{,}000$ kg

$$t_e = 1.0 \times \frac{40{,}000}{380} = 105.26 \; \text{minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Ingberg's tₑ ≈ **105 min** (about 1 hour 45 min)

**Solution Part (b) — Law's Method:**

$$t_e = \frac{0.067 \, L}{A_v / \sqrt{H_v}}$$

First compute ventilation factor:

$$\frac{A_v}{\sqrt{H_v}} = \frac{9}{\sqrt{1.5}} = \frac{9}{1.2247} = 7.348 \; \text{m}^{1.5}$$

$$t_e = \frac{0.067 \times 400}{7.348} = \frac{26.8}{7.348} = 3.65 \; \text{hours}$$

Converting to minutes:

$$t_e = 3.65 \times 60 \approx 219 \; \text{minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Law's tₑ ≈ **219 min** (about 3 hours 39 min)

**Discussion:**
- The same compartment produces vastly different tₑ values depending on the formula used (105 min vs 219 min).
- **Ingberg underestimates** because it ignores the favourable effect of ventilation (smaller openings mean hotter fire for longer).
- **Law's result (219 min) exceeds 120 min** (2-hour rated element).
- **Design verdict:** The 2-hour rated element is **NOT adequate**. A **3-hour (180 min) rated element** would still fall short. **4-hour rated element** is recommended per Law's formula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This huge discrepancy is exactly why KTU exams ask for "comment on adequacy" — engineers must know the limitations of each formula.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Methods

| Aspect | Ingberg | Law | Pettersson |
|--------|---------|-----|------------|
| Accounts for Fire Load (W/L) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Accounts for Ventilation (A_v, H_v) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Accounts for Compartment Geometry (A_T) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Accounts for Lining Thermal Properties | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Theoretical Basis | Empirical | Empirical | Heat-Balance |
| Conservatism | Conservative when A_v small | Moderate | Most rigorous |
| KTU Exam Favourite | ✅ Very High | ✅ High | ✅ Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Equivalence of Fire Severity Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Compartment Fire]:::input
    B[Fire Load W or L]:::factor
    C[Compartment Geometry A_T]:::factor
    D[Ventilation A_v and H_v]:::factor
    E[Compartment Gas Temp T_g t]:::process
    F[Structural Element Heating]:::process
    G[Equivalent Severity t_e]:::output
    H[Standard Furnace ISO 834]:::input
    I[ISO Curve T 345 log10 8t plus 1]:::process
    J[Furnace Element Heating]:::process
    K[Same Maximum Steel Temperature]:::output
    L[Design Check t_e less than Fire Resistance]:::goal

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    B --> E
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    G --> K
    K --> L
    A --> L

    classDef input fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef factor fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#d4ac0d,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Top-left path (Real Fire Branch):** Real compartment fire properties (fire load, geometry, ventilation) determine the gas temperature history, which heats the structural element, yielding the **real fire severity**.
> - **Top-right path (Furnace Branch):** Standard ISO 834 curve in the furnace produces a different temperature history on the same element.
> - **Convergence point:** Both paths converge at "same maximum steel temperature," producing the **equivalent fire severity tₑ**.
> - **Final design check:** Compare tₑ with the element's fire-resistance rating.

### 4.2 Mermaid Time–Temperature Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REAL["Real Compartment Fire Curve"]
        R1[Phase 1 Ignition]:::phase
        R2[Phase 2 Growth]:::phase
        R3[Phase 3 Fully Developed Peak]:::phase
        R4[Phase 4 Decay Cooling]:::phase
        R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    end

    subgraph STD["Standard ISO 834 Furnace Curve"]
        S1[t equals 0]:::start
        S2[Logarithmic Rise]:::growth
        S3[Continuous Rise No Decay]:::growth
        S1 --> S2 --> S3
    end

    EQ[Equivalent Severity t_e]:::equiv

    REAL -- Compute max temp --> EQ
    STD -- Find time for same max temp --> EQ

    classDef phase fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000
    classDef start fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    classDef growth fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef equiv fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

> [!NOTE]
> - The **real compartment fire** has 4 phases including decay.
> - The **standard ISO 834 curve** rises logarithmically with **no decay**.
> - The **equivalent severity tₑ** is the time coordinate on the ISO 834 curve that produces the same thermal response in the structural element as the real fire does.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Equivalence Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 Define Compartment Geometry]:::step
    P2[Step 2 Compute Fire Load Density L]:::step
    P3[Step 3 Compute Ventilation Factor A_v over sqrt H_v]:::step
    P4[Step 4 Compute Surface Area A_T]:::step
    P5[Step 5 Select Equivalence Formula Ingberg or Law or Pettersson]:::step
    P6[Step 6 Compute t_e in minutes]:::step
    P7[Step 7 Compare t_e with Element Fire Resistance R]:::step
    P8[Step 8 Verdict Adequate or Upgrade Protection]:::step

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8

    classDef step fill:#e8f8f5,stroke:#117864,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This flowchart represents the **standard 8-step methodology** that students should follow in any KTU numerical problem on fire severity equivalence.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **'equivalent fire severity'** of a compartment fire. Why is it needed in structural fire safety design?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent fire severity** of a compartment fire is the *time of exposure in the standard furnace test (ISO 834 fire curve)* that would result in the *same maximum temperature (or thermal response)* in a structural element as the actual compartment fire under consideration. It is denoted by *tₑ* (in minutes).
>
> **Need in design:**
> 1. It bridges the gap between laboratory-tested fire resistance and real-world fire exposure.
> 2. It allows verification of the design check: *tₑ ≤ Fire resistance of structural element*.
> 3. It enables use of standardised fire-resistance ratings (30/60/90/120 min) for real buildings.
> 4. It forms the foundation of performance-based fire safety design codes.

*(Expected answer length: 3–4 sentences + formula; 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **'fire resistance'** and **'fire severity'** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Property | Fire Resistance | Fire Severity |
> |----------|-----------------|----------------|
> | Definition | Ability of a structural element to withstand fire | Destructive thermal load imposed by actual fire |
> | Nature | Property of the element | Property of the fire/compartment |
> | Measured by | Standard furnace test (ISO 834) | Real compartment fire exposure |
> | Units | Minutes (e.g., 60-min rating) | Equivalent time *tₑ* (minutes) or heat flux (kW/m²) |
> | Example | A 2-hour fire-rated RCC slab | A library with 400 kg/m² fire load |
> | Design Check | Capacity | Demand |
>
> **Design criterion:** *Fire Resistance ≥ Fire Severity*, i.e., *R ≥ tₑ*.

*(Expected: tabular comparison with examples; 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **four phases of a natural compartment fire** and compare its time–temperature curve with the **standard ISO 834 furnace curve**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential apartment has the following data:
- Floor area = 80 m²
- Total internal surface area (walls + ceiling + floor) A_T = 320 m²
- Ventilation opening area A_v = 6 m²
- Height of opening H_v = 1.2 m
- Fire load density L = 250 kg/m² (wood equivalent)

Compute the **equivalent fire severity** using:
   (i) Ingberg's formula
   (ii) Law's formula

Comment on the adequacy of a **1-hour rated structural element**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Phases of Natural Compartment Fire:**

| Phase | Description | Duration (typical) |
|-------|-------------|--------------------|
| **1. Ignition / Incipient** | Small flame at ignition source; incipient burning | Few seconds to minutes |
| **2. Growth (Pre-flashover)** | Fire spreads over combustible linings; smoke layers form; temperature rises non-linearly | Minutes to tens of minutes |
| **3. Fully Developed (Post-flashover)** | All combustibles involved; flashover occurs at ≈ 600 °C; temperature plateaus near peak (800–1100 °C) | Tens of minutes to hours |
| **4. Decay** | Fuel consumption; temperature falls steadily; smouldering may continue | Slow (hours) |

**Comparison with ISO 834:**

| Feature | Real Compartment Fire | ISO 834 Furnace Curve |
|---------|-----------------------|------------------------|
| Phases | 4 phases (ignition → decay) | Monotonic logarithmic rise only |
| Peak | Finite; followed by cooling | Unlimited (theoretically) |
| Driving variable | Fuel, ventilation, geometry | Time only (formula-driven) |
| Mathematical form | Parametric (Pettersson curves) | $T - T_0 = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ |
| Use | Real-world design check | Standard laboratory test |

> [!NOTE]
> A **sketch** should be drawn showing both curves on the same time-temperature axes. The compartment curve rises faster, peaks, and decays; the ISO 834 curve rises logarithmically forever. The equivalence time tₑ is the time on the ISO 834 axis where it intersects horizontally with the compartment curve's peak temperature line.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** A_f = 80 m², A_T = 320 m², A_v = 6 m², H_v = 1.2 m, L = 250 kg/m²

**(i) Ingberg's Formula:**

$$t_e = k \cdot \frac{W}{A_T} = \frac{L \cdot A_f}{A_T} = \frac{250 \times 80}{320}$$

$$t_e = \frac{20{,}000}{320} = 62.5 \; \text{minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Ingberg's tₑ ≈ **62.5 min** (≈ 1 hour 3 min)

**(ii) Law's Formula:**

$$\text{Ventilation factor} = \frac{A_v}{\sqrt{H_v}} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{1.2}} = \frac{6}{1.0954} = 5.477 \; \text{m}^{1.5}$$

$$t_e = \frac{0.067 \times L}{A_v / \sqrt{H_v}} = \frac{0.067 \times 250}{5.477} = \frac{16.75}{5.477}$$

$$t_e = 3.058 \; \text{hours} = 183.5 \; \text{minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Law's tₑ ≈ **183.5 min** (≈ 3 hours 3 min)

**Comment on Adequacy of 1-hour (60 min) Element:**

- Per **Ingberg**: tₑ = 62.5 min > 60 min → element is **just inadequate (marginally undersized)**.
- Per **Law**: tₑ = 183.5 min ≫ 60 min → element is **grossly inadequate**.
- **Recommendation:** Use a **3-hour (180 min) rated element** (matches Law) or **2-hour** element with conservative interpretation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often forget to **convert units consistently** (Law's formula expects L in kg/m² and tₑ comes out in *hours* — students must multiply by 60).
> 2. Many students mix up **A_T** (total internal area) with **A_f** (floor area).
> 3. Students ignore that Law's formula accounts for ventilation — it gives more realistic (and often more severe) results.
> 4. Always state the **limitation of the formula used** in the comment.
> 5. Marks are awarded for the **comment**, not just the arithmetic.

---

#### **Question B — (14 Marks) [Alternative to Question A]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain **Ingberg's, Law's, and Pettersson's methods** of evaluating equivalent fire severity. Discuss their relative merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial office has:
- Fire load L = 300 MJ/m²
- Ventilation factor A_v √H_v = 8 m^(5/2)
- Total internal surface area A_T = 250 m²
- Empirical constant b = 1200 (Pettersson)

Calculate equivalent fire severity by **Pettersson's formula** and compare with the value obtained using **Law's formula** (assume 1 kg wood ≈ 18 MJ). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Methods of Equivalent Fire Severity:**

**1. Ingberg's Method (1928):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:** $t_e = k \cdot W / A_T$
>
> Based on the assumption that *fire severity is proportional to fire load per unit compartment surface area*. The constant *k* is calibrated empirically.
>
> - ✅ **Merits:** Simple; uses only fire load and compartment surface.
> - ❌ **Demerits:** Ignores ventilation completely; ignores thermal properties of lining.

**2. Law's Method (1971):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:** $t_e = 0.067 \, L / (A_v / \sqrt{H_v})$
>
> Improves Ingberg by including the **ventilation factor** *A_v / √H_v*.
>
> - ✅ **Merits:** Accounts for ventilation; empirical fit from 73 fire tests.
> - ❌ **Demerits:** Still ignores thermal inertia of lining; purely empirical (no theoretical basis).

**3. Pettersson's Method (Heat-Balance Based):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:** $t_e = (L \cdot \sqrt{A_T}) / (b \cdot \sqrt{A_v \sqrt{H_v}})$
>
> Theoretically rigorous; based on the **energy balance** of the compartment. Accounts for fuel, ventilation, geometry, *and* thermal properties of boundaries through the constant *b* (= √(k·ρ·c)).
>
> - ✅ **Merits:** Most realistic; theoretical foundation.
> - ❌ **Demerits:** Complex; requires knowledge of boundary thermal properties; constant *b* is empirical.

**Summary Table:**

| Method | Accounts Ventilation | Accounts Lining | Theoretical Basis | Simplicity |
|--------|---------------------|-----------------|--------------------|------------|
| Ingberg | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (empirical) | ★★★★★ |
| Law | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ (empirical) | ★★★★ |
| Pettersson | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (heat balance) | ★★★ |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** L = 300 MJ/m², A_v √H_v = 8 m^(5/2), A_T = 250 m², b = 1200

**Step 1: Pettersson's Method:**

$$t_e = \frac{L \cdot \sqrt{A_T}}{b \cdot \sqrt{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}}$$

Compute each term:
- $L = 300$ MJ/m²
- $\sqrt{A_T} = \sqrt{250} = 15.81 \; \text{m}$
- $\sqrt{A_v \sqrt{H_v}} = \sqrt{8} = 2.828 \; \text{m}^{5/4}$

$$t_e = \frac{300 \times 15.81}{1200 \times 2.828} = \frac{4743}{3394}$$

$$\boxed{t_e \approx 1.40 \; \text{hours} \approx 84 \; \text{minutes}}$$

**Step 2: Law's Method (with unit conversion):**

Convert L from MJ/m² to kg/m² of wood:
$$L_{wood} = \frac{300 \; \text{MJ/m}^2}{18 \; \text{MJ/kg}} = 16.67 \; \text{kg/m}^2$$

Compute ventilation factor:
$$\frac{A_v}{\sqrt{H_v}} = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{H_v} = \frac{8}{H_v}$$

To avoid unknown H_v, use **Law's formula** in rearranged form:
$$t_e \;[\text{min}] = 0.067 \cdot L_{wood} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{H_v}}{A_v}$$

Since $A_v \sqrt{H_v} = 8$, we can parameterise, but for the simplified comparison we use:

$$t_e = \frac{0.067 \times 16.67 \times H_v}{A_v}$$

Alternatively, taking $H_v = 1.0$ m and $A_v = 8$ m² as a reasonable assumption:
$$\frac{A_v}{\sqrt{H_v}} = \frac{8}{1} = 8 \; \text{m}^{1.5}$$

$$t_e = \frac{0.067 \times 16.67}{8} = \frac{1.117}{8} = 0.140 \; \text{hours} = 8.4 \; \text{minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Law's result is much lower because the office has **low fire load density (16.67 kg/m²)** when converted from MJ/m². Pettersson's formula retains the higher value because it includes the **compartment surface area factor**.

**Comparison:**
- Pettersson's tₑ ≈ **84 min** (more rigorous, accounts for lining)
- Law's tₑ ≈ **8.4 min** (purely empirical)
- The difference arises because Pettersson integrates **heat absorption by compartment boundaries**, which extends severity duration significantly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** When L is given in MJ/m², students forget to convert to kg/m² (dividing by 18 MJ/kg for wood equivalent).
> 2. **Formula mis-application:** Pettersson's formula has square roots in both numerator and denominator — students frequently confuse the placement.
> 3. **Missing comparison comment:** Marks are awarded for *commenting on the difference*, not just computing values.
> 4. **Constant *b*:** Always mention that *b* depends on thermal properties (k, ρ, c) of compartment lining.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

✅ **Core Definitions:**
- Fire Resistance = *Element property* (standard furnace test, in minutes)
- Fire Severity = *Compartment fire property* (real-world thermal load)
- Equivalent Fire Severity *tₑ* = ISO 834 exposure time giving same thermal response as real fire

✅ **Standard Curve (must memorise):**
$$T - T_0 = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

✅ **Three Classical Methods (formula + creator + merit + limitation):**
1. **Ingberg (1928):** $t_e = kW/A_T$ — fuel-only, ignores ventilation
2. **Law (1971):** $t_e = 0.067 L / (A_v / \sqrt{H_v})$ — adds ventilation
3. **Pettersson:** $t_e = L\sqrt{A_T} / (b \sqrt{A_v \sqrt{H_v}})$ — heat-balance based

✅ **Four Phases of Compartment Fire:** Ignition → Growth → Fully Developed → Decay

✅ **Design Equation:** *tₑ ≤ Fire Resistance Rating of Element* (otherwise inadequate)

✅ **Common Ventilation Factor:** $\Phi = A_v \sqrt{H_v} / A_T$ (dimensionless)

✅ **Fire Load Conversion:** 1 kg wood equivalent ≈ **18 MJ** (some codes use 17–18.6)

✅ **Burning Rate (ventilation-controlled):** $\dot{m} = 0.5 A_o \sqrt{H_o}$

✅ **Critical Differences — KTU Examiner's Favourite:**
- Ingberg **underestimates** severity when openings are small.
- Law **overestimates** severity for highly ventilated compartments.
- Pettersson is **most accurate** but **most complex**.

✅ **Always end numerical answers with:** "Comment on adequacy / recommendation for element rating."

✅ **Real-World Application:** Used in performance-based design, retrofit decisions, insurance rating, code compliance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Temperature on the Properties of Structural Materials - Thermal Data for Concrete, Steel, Aluminium, Masonry and Wood
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Temperature on the Properties of Structural Materials - Thermal Data for Concrete, Steel, Aluminium, Masonry and Wood

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Thermal degradation of structural materials* refers to the irreversible and reversible changes in the **physical, thermal, and mechanical properties** of construction materials (concrete, steel, aluminium, masonry, and timber) when subjected to elevated temperatures, typically ranging from **20 °C** (ambient) up to **1200 °C** (hydrocarbon/compartment fire regimes). These changes are quantified by **reduction factors** applied to the ambient-temperature characteristic strength ($f_k$) and the modulus of elasticity ($E$), and by **temperature-dependent** thermal conductivity ($\lambda$), specific heat ($c_p$), and thermal expansion ($\Delta L/L$).

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the design of structural members in fire (termed the **fire design situation**) is governed by the *semi-probabilistic limit state approach* prescribed in **Eurocode 1 (EN 1991-1-2)** for thermal actions and **Eurocode 2 / 3 / 4 / 5 / 6 / 9** for material-specific fire behaviour. For Indian context, **IS 3809 : 1979 (reaffirmed 2014)** – *Fire Resistance Test of Structures* – and **IS 1642 : 1989** – *Code of Practice for Fire Safety of Buildings* – provide the experimental baseline.

### The Five Pillars of Thermal Material Response

| Material | Governing Eurocode | Critical Transformation Temperature |
|----------|-------------------|------------------------------------|
| Concrete | **EN 1992-1-2** | ~**300 °C** (siliceous), ~**600 °C** (calcareous) |
| Steel | **EN 1993-1-2** | ~**400 °C** (yield), ~**600 °C** (collapse) |
| Aluminium | **EN 1999-1-2** | ~**150 °C** (50 % strength) |
| Masonry | **EN 1996-1-2** | ~**600 °C** (mortar binder loss) |
| Wood | **EN 1995-1-2** | ~**300 °C** (charring onset) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Marathon Runner vs. The Sprinter":**
> Imagine each material as an athlete running a fire-resistance "race."
> - **Steel** is a *sprinter* — explosive strength at room temperature, but it *collapses* suddenly above 400 °C because its crystalline grains slide past each other (loss of atomic bonding).
> - **Concrete** is a *marathon runner* — slow and steady; it loses strength gradually, but the outer crust shields the inner core (thermal lag effect of mass).
> - **Aluminium** is the *fragile runner* — at 150 °C (roughly the temperature of a hot oven), it loses half its strength because it has a *low melting point (660 °C)* and very high thermal expansion (~24 × 10⁻⁶ /°C).
> - **Wood** is a *self-sacrificing shield* — the outer layer chars at a near-constant rate (~0.6–0.8 mm/min), forming an insulating charcoal layer that protects the sound inner wood.
> - **Masonry** sits between concrete and wood — the clay units remain stable but the *mortar joints* (the weak link) dehydrate and shrink above 600 °C.

This analogy gives an immediate mental model: **temperature is not merely heat, but a chemical and crystalline destabiliser** that selectively dismantles the bonds that give each material its strength.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Thermal conductivity (λ): W/(m·K)** — rate of heat conduction through unit thickness per unit temperature gradient.
- **Specific heat (c_p): J/(kg·K)** — energy required to raise 1 kg of material by 1 K.
- **Density (ρ): kg/m³** — mass per unit volume at ambient (concrete ≈ **2300 kg/m³**, steel ≈ **7850 kg/m³**, aluminium ≈ **2700 kg/m³**, masonry ≈ **1800–2000 kg/m³**, wood ≈ **450–700 kg/m³**).
- **Thermal expansion coefficient (α_T): 1/K** or **10⁻⁶ /°C** — fractional length change per degree.
- **Charring rate (β_n): mm/min** — for timber.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why and How Materials Degrade — The Underlying Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Concrete (EN 1992-1-2)
Concrete is a composite of *cement paste + aggregates + voids*. At elevated temperature:
- **Free and chemically bound water** evaporates progressively (100 °C – 300 °C), creating internal pore pressure → spalling risk.
- **Calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)₂)** in the cement paste decomposes above ~450 °C → loss of alkalinity → strength loss.
- **Aggregates** expand; *siliceous* (quartz-based) aggregates undergo a *volumetric jump* at **573 °C** (α→β quartz transition), causing explosive spalling.
- *Calcareous* (limestone-based) aggregates are more stable because **CaCO₃ absorbs heat** by calcination.

**Two reference aggregate types in Eurocode:**
1. **Siliceous (S):** flint, quartzite, granite.
2. **Calcareous (C):** limestone, chalk.

### 2.1.2 Steel (EN 1993-1-2)
Carbon steel undergoes *micro-structural transformation*:
- Below 100 °C → negligible change.
- 100–400 °C → *blue brittleness* range (yield may temporarily rise, ductility drops).
- 400–600 °C → rapid strength decay as *ferrite grains* lose coherence.
- Above 700 °C → essentially zero structural capacity.

**Three steel categories considered:**
1. **Hot-rolled structural steel (S235, S275, S355, S460).**
2. **Cold-formed steel sections.**
3. **Stainless steel (1.4301, 1.4401).**

### 2.1.3 Aluminium (EN 1999-1-2)
Wrought aluminium alloys (e.g., **5083, 6063, 6082**) lose strength rapidly due to:
- **Low melting range (510–660 °C)** — far below steel.
- **No yielding plateau** at high temperature — immediate collapse after peak.
- **High thermal expansion** → induces large thermal stresses in restrained members.

### 2.1.4 Masonry (EN 1996-1-2)
Masonry = *units* (clay, calcium silicate, concrete, autoclaved aerated) + *mortar*. Failure mode is **NOT** the units but the **mortar joints**:
- Cement mortar: dehydration ~400–600 °C.
- Lime mortar: loss of bond ~600 °C.
- Clay bricks: stable to >1000 °C (sintered product).

### 2.1.5 Wood (EN 1995-1-2)
Wood exhibits a unique behaviour: **charring**. The pyrolysis front advances at a near-constant rate, leaving behind a *char layer* with negligible strength but excellent insulation (λ ≈ 0.1 W/m·K).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation:** θ = temperature (°C); subscript *k* = characteristic (5 % fractile); subscript *y* = yield; subscript *E* = modulus of elasticity.

### A. Reduction Factors for Strength and Modulus

| Material | Property | Reduction Factor Formula / Reference Table |
|----------|----------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Concrete (siliceous, S)** | $k_c(\theta)$ (compressive) | EN 1992-1-2, Table 3.1: linear drop from 1.00 at 20 °C → 0.00 at 1200 °C; key plateau values: 100 °C = 1.00, 200 °C = 0.95, 400 °C = 0.75, 600 °C = 0.45, 800 °C = 0.15. |
| **Concrete (calcareous, C)** | $k_c(\theta)$ | EN 1992-1-2, Table 3.2: 100 °C = 1.00, 200 °C = 0.97, 400 °C = 0.85, 600 °C = 0.60, 800 °C = 0.30. |
| **Steel (hot-rolled)** | $k_{y,\theta}$ (yield), $k_{E,\theta}$ (E-modulus) | EN 1993-1-2, Table 3.1: 400 °C → $k_y = 1.00$, $k_E = 0.70$; 500 °C → $k_y = 0.78$, $k_E = 0.60$; 600 °C → $k_y = 0.47$, $k_E = 0.31$. |
| **Stainless steel** | $k_{y,\theta}$, $k_{E,\theta}$ | EN 1993-1-2, Annex E: retains ~85 % strength at 600 °C (vs. 47 % for carbon steel). |
| **Aluminium alloys (≤ 5000 series)** | $k_{y,\theta}$ | EN 1999-1-2, Table 3.2a: 150 °C → 0.70; 250 °C → 0.40; 350 °C → 0.22. |
| **Masonry (clay units)** | $k(\theta)$ strength | EN 1996-1-2, Table 3.3: linear decay from 1.00 (20 °C) to 0.00 (1000 °C); 600 °C ≈ 0.40. |
| **Wood (softwood, parallel to grain)** | $k_{\text{mod,fi}}$ | EN 1995-1-2, Table 3.1: 0 °C = 1.00, 100 °C = 0.65, 200 °C = 0.40, 300 °C = 0.20. Char zone assumed zero strength. |

### B. Thermal Property Equations

| Property | Equation / Range | Validity |
|----------|------------------|----------|
| Steel thermal conductivity $\lambda_a$ | $\lambda_a = 54 - 0.0333\,\theta$ W/(m·K) (for 20 °C ≤ θ ≤ 800 °C); $\lambda_a = 27.3$ W/(m·K) above 800 °C | EN 1993-1-2 |
| Steel specific heat $c_a$ | $c_a = 425 + 0.773\theta - 1.69\times10^{-3}\theta^2 + 2.22\times10^{-6}\theta^3$ J/(kg·K) (600 °C ≤ θ ≤ 735 °C); peak at 735 °C with $\Delta c$ of 6700 J/(kg·K) *added* | EN 1993-1-2 |
| Concrete (siliceous) $\lambda_c$ | $\lambda_c = 2 - 0.24\,\theta/120 + 0.012\,(\theta/120)^2$ W/(m·K) for 20 °C ≤ θ ≤ 1200 °C; lower bound 1.0 W/(m·K) | EN 1992-1-2 |
| Concrete $c_p$ | $c_p = 900$ J/(kg·K) for 100 °C ≤ θ ≤ 200 °C (constant plateau due to water); peaks with $\Delta c_p = 1470$ J/(kg·K) at 115 °C peak, then drops to 1100 J/(kg·K) above 400 °C | EN 1992-1-2 |
| Thermal elongation $\Delta L/L$ for steel | $\Delta L/L = 1.2\times10^{-5}\,\theta + 0.4\times10^{-8}\,\theta^2 + 0.8\times10^{-11}\,\theta^3$ for 20 °C ≤ θ ≤ 750 °C; **linear** 14×10⁻⁶ above 750 °C | EN 1993-1-2 |
| Thermal elongation for aluminium | $\Delta L/L = -4.5\times10^{-4} + \theta\,(24\times10^{-6})$ simplified | EN 1999-1-2 |
| Charring depth $d_{\text{char},n}$ (one-dimensional, softwood) | $d_{\text{char},n} = \beta_n \, t$ where $\beta_n = 0.8$ mm/min (normal) and $\beta_0 = 0.65$ mm/min (residual cross-section method). | EN 1995-1-2 |

### C. Master Strength Equation (All Materials)

The **fire-design strength** is the ambient characteristic strength multiplied by the reduction factor and, for concrete, the partial safety factor in fire ($\gamma_{M,fi}$):

$$
f_{k,\theta} = k(\theta)\, f_k \quad\text{or equivalently}\quad \sigma_{Rd,fi} = \frac{k(\theta)\, f_k}{\gamma_{M,fi}}
$$

where $\gamma_{M,fi} = 1.0$ for the fire design situation (EN 1990).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural fire engineering of high-rise buildings (Burj Khalifa, Taipei 101):** numerical simulation of column/beam heating requires thermal-property tables above.
- **Tunnel fire design** (Eurotunnel, Crossrail, Mumbai Metro): hydrocarbon fire curves push temperatures to **1300 °C** in 5 minutes — only *calcareous concrete* and *stainless steel-clad* structures survive.
- **Refinery and offshore structures:** aluminium cannot be used above 200 °C — a critical constraint.
- **Heritage masonry restoration** (e.g., Notre-Dame de Paris, 2019 fire): mortar selection dictates survivability; lime-mortar joints had to be re-pointed after thermal shock.
- **Timber-frame multi-storey buildings (CLT, glulam):** charring calculations are central to compartmentation design per EN 1995-1-2.
