**2. Performance Criteria (REI):**

- **Stability (R):** Element must not collapse; deflection limits for beams ($L/20$) and columns ($h/100$) must not be exceeded
- **Integrity (E):** No cracks > 6 mm, no sustained flaming on unexposed face, cotton pad test negative
- **Insulation (I):** Temperature rise on unexposed face ≤ 140 K (average) or ≤ 180 K (single point)

**3. Three Major Drawbacks:**

1. **Cost and Time:** Each test costs ₹5–50 lakhs and takes 1–4 days; only a few labs in India have facilities
2. **Single-Specimen Limitation:** Result is for one specific element under one set of conditions; extrapolation to real buildings with different restraints, composite action, and load distributions is uncertain
3. **No Decay Phase:** The ISO 834 curve represents a continuously growing fire with no cooling phase; real fires have a decay (burn-out) phase, so the test may over-predict heating for some elements

[Additional drawbacks for completeness: 4. One-sided exposure only; 5. Specimen size limits; 6. Boundary condition mismatch; 7. Reproducibility ±10%]

[7 marks — distribute across 3 sub-topics]

#### **Part (b) — Compartment Fire Resistance Calculation [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Floor area $A_f = 80$ m²
- Height $H = 3$ m
- Two windows: each 1.5 m × 1.2 m = 1.8 m² → total $A_v = 2 \times 1.8 = 3.6$ m²
- Fire load $q = 600$ MJ/m²
- Empirical constant c = 1.0

**Step 1: Calculate Wall and Ceiling Area ($A_t$):**

Assuming rectangular room 10 m × 8 m (since 80 m²):
- Perimeter = 2(10+8) = 36 m
- Wall area (excluding openings) = $36 \times 3 - 3.6 = 108 - 3.6 = 104.4$ m²
- Ceiling area = $80$ m²
- Total $A_t = 104.4 + 80 = 184.4$ m²

**Step 2: Calculate Opening Factor $F_o$:**

Average height of openings: $H_v = 1.2$ m (window height)

$$F_o = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_t} = \frac{3.6 \times \sqrt{1.2}}{184.4} = \frac{3.6 \times 1.0954}{184.4} = \frac{3.9436}{184.4}$$

$$\boxed{F_o = 0.0214 \text{ m}^{0.5}}$$

**[2 marks]**

**Step 3: Total Fire Load:**

$$L = q \times A_f = 600 \times 80 = 48{,}000 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 4: Approximate Fire Resistance:**

Using the empirical formula (Law's method, with c = 1.0):

$$t_{FR} = c \cdot \frac{L \cdot A_f}{A_v \cdot \sqrt{H_v} \cdot 3300}$$

$$t_{FR} = 1.0 \times \frac{48{,}000 \times 80}{3.6 \times \sqrt{1.2} \times 3300}$$

$$= \frac{3{,}840{,}000}{3.6 \times 1.0954 \times 3300}$$

$$= \frac{3{,}840{,}000}{13{,}010}$$

$$\boxed{t_{FR} = 295.2 \text{ minutes} \approx 4.92 \text{ hours}}$$

**[3 marks]**

**Step 5: Compare with NBC 2016:**

- This 4.92-hour resistance indicates a **very high fire-resistance compartment**
- Satisfies all NBC Type 1–4 construction requirements
- Suitable for high-risk occupancies (archives, server rooms, museums)

**Comment:** The low opening factor (well-ventilated building) combined with high fire load contributes to longer fire duration. The result indicates that the compartment itself can withstand ~5 hours of fire before structural collapse risk becomes critical.

**[2 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong unit of opening factor height** — must use $\sqrt{H}$ in metres, not mm
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract opening area from $A_t$** — students often include windows in wall area
> 3. **Using 8450 instead of 3300 in Law's formula** — check the version of formula your textbook uses
> 4. **Not mentioning the standard fire curve** — examiners expect mention of IS 3809 / ISO 834
> 5. **Confusing F/V with V/F** — Section factor is always F/V (perimeter/area), never inverse
> 6. **Forgetting REI triple criteria** — Stability, Integrity, Insulation must all be mentioned
> 7. **In Part A, not specifying the values** (140 K, 180 K) — partial marks lost

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Topic: Fire Protection of Structural Members**

- **Fire Resistance** = time under standard fire until element fails one of REI criteria
- **Three Performance Criteria:** **R**esistance (stability), **E**x integrity (no cracks/flames), **I**nsulation (140 K avg / 180 K point)
- **Standard Fire Curve (IS 3809 / ISO 834):** $T_t = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$
- **Reference Temperatures:** 30 min → 842 °C, 60 min → 945 °C, 120 min → 1049 °C
- **Steel critical temperature:** **550 °C** (yield drops to 60%)
- **RCC critical cover temperature:** **~250–300 °C** (spalling risk)
- **Timber charring rate:** **0.65 mm/min** (softwood), **0.50 mm/min** (hardwood)
- **Section Factor F/V** = heated perimeter ÷ cross-sectional area (m⁻¹) — higher F/V means more protection needed
- **Steel Protection Methods:** Intumescent paint (10–100× expansion), gypsum boards, sprayed vermiculite, concrete encasement, membrane ceiling
- **RCC Protection:** Increased cover (25–40 mm), polypropylene fibres (2 kg/m³) to prevent spalling, supplementary mesh
- **Timber Protection:** Self-insulating char layer, gypsum plaster 12–25 mm, fire-retardant chemical impregnation
- **Test Furnace Types:** Vertical (walls/columns), Horizontal (beams/slabs), Full-scale, Mobile, Gas-fired, Electric
- **Furnace Specs:** 9 thermocouples, 100 mm from specimen, 20 Pa positive pressure
- **Indian Standard:** **IS 3809:1979** for fire resistance test
- **Drawbacks of FRT:** Costly (₹5–50 lakhs), no decay phase, single specimen, boundary mismatch, one-sided exposure, ±10% reproducibility
- **Approximate Compartment Fire Resistance Formula:** $t_{FR} = c \cdot L \cdot A_f / (A_v \cdot \sqrt{H_v} \cdot 3300)$
- **Opening Factor:** $F_o = A_v \cdot \sqrt{H_v} / A_t$ (m^0.5)
- **NBC 2016 Requirements:** 1–4 hours fire resistance based on construction type and occupancy
- **Intumescent activation temperature:** **200–250 °C**; expansion ratio **10–100×**
- **Concrete thermal conductivity:** **~1.4 W/m·K** (30× less than steel)
- **Steel thermal conductivity:** **~45 W/m·K**
- **Most important formula for FRT marks:** the standard time-temperature curve equation
- **Most important conceptual point:** REI triple-criteria and the three material failure modes (steel: strength loss, RCC: spalling+rebar, timber: charring)
- **Always specify units** in numerical answers (°C, min, mm, m⁻¹, m^0.5, MJ/m²)
- **Mention Indian Standard codes** (IS 3809, IS 456, IS 875, NBC 2016) in every answer for full marks
- **Draw neat sketches** of section factor concept and protection methods — fetch 2–3 extra marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Arriving at the Required Fire Resistance of Structural Members as per Indian Standard Specifications (BIS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Method of Arriving at the Required Fire Resistance of Structural Members as per Indian Standard Specifications (BIS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (BIS Terminology) As per the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — primarily through the cluster of **IS 1641, IS 1642, IS 1643, IS 1644, IS 1645**, and **IS 3809** — the **Required Fire Resistance (R)** of a structural member is defined as: > *"The duration, expressed in hours or minutes, for which a structural member or assembly is capable of performing its intended load-bearing and/or separating function when subjected to the standard fire exposure conditions defined in IS 3809, without loss of Stability, Integrity, or Insulation."* Mathematically: $$R_{required} \;=\; f(G, \, H, \, A_f, \, q_f, \, V)$$ where: - $G$ = Group/occupancy classification of the building (IS 1645) - $H$ = Height of the building (in metres) - $A_f$ = Floor area of the largest fire compartment (in m²) - $q_f$ = Fire load density (MJ/m²) derived from IS 1644 - $V$ = Ventilation factor ### 1.2 The Three Acceptance Criteria (The "Fire Triangle" of Performance) A structural member is deemed to have **failed** in fire when *any one* of the following three criteria is breached: | # | Criterion | What it means | Practical Check | |---|-----------|---------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Stability (R)** | Member can carry its design load | Collapse / excessive deflection | | 2 | **Integrity (E)** | No cracks/openings allow hot gases/flames through | Cotton pad test, gap gauge | | 3 | **Insulation (I)** | Average unexposed face temp rise ≤ 140 °C (or any point rise ≤ 180 °C) | Thermocouples on cold face | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite:** A structural member must satisfy **all three criteria** simultaneously. The fire resistance time is governed by the **lowest** of the three failure times. This combined rating is the **REI** classification (e.g., REI 120). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker vs. The Earthen Pot" Imagine two vessels placed on a fire: - The **Pressure Cooker (RC Column with adequate cover)**: Thick walls, an inner metal liner (rebar cage) protected by insulation (concrete cover). It can take the heat for a long time before the inner contents (rebar) get damaged. - The **Earthen Pot (Bare Steel Column)**: Thin, no insulation, heats up instantly. Within minutes the contents (steel core) are ruined, even though the pot itself is intact. The **BIS method** is essentially a procedure to decide *how thick the insulation* and *how large the pot* must be, depending on **what is being cooked** (fire load) and **for how long** (fire duration). > [!NOTE] > The required fire resistance of a member is **NOT** the same as its inherent fire endurance. The engineer *first decides* the required fire resistance from occupancy/hazard tables, and *then designs* the member to deliver at least that endurance. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The BIS Hierarchy — Which Code Answers What? The Indian Standard procedure is **not a single calculation**, but a sequenced decision tree. The KTU examiner expects the student to know *which code answers which question*: | Stage | Question to be answered | Applicable BIS Code | Output | |-------|------------------------|---------------------|--------| | 1 | What is the building occupancy group? | **IS 1645** (Fire Grading) | Group I – IX | | 2 | What is the severity of fire likely to occur? | **IS 1644** (Exposure Hazard) | Fire load $q_f$ (MJ/m²) | | 3 | What is the **required** fire resistance? | **IS 1641** (General) + **NBC 2016** | $R_{req}$ in minutes/hours | | 4 | How is the structural member **tested** for fire? | **IS 3809** | Standard fire curve (IS 3809 ≈ ISO 834) | | 5 | How is the member **designed** to achieve $R_{req}$? | **IS 1643** (Calculation) + **IS 456 Annex** | Member dimensions, cover | ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the BIS Method **Step 1 — Classify the building (IS 1645)** Occupancy groups include *Residential, Educational, Institutional, Assembly, Business, Mercantile, Industrial, Storage, Hazardous*. **Step 2 — Compute the Equivalent Fire Load (IS 1644)** $$q_f \;=\; \frac{\sum (M_i \, h_i)}{A_f}$$ where $M_i$ = mass of combustible material $i$ (kg), $h_i$ = calorific value (MJ/kg), and $A_f$ = floor area of compartment (m²). **Step 3 — Determine the Equivalent Fire Duration ($t_e$) from IS 1641 charts** The equivalent fire duration is read from graphs (in IS 1641) plotting $q_f$ vs. ventilation factor $F_v$ (or simply from prescribed tables in **NBC 2016 Part 4, Table 3**). **Step 4 — Look up the Required Fire Resistance ($R_{req}$)** From **IS 1641 / Table 1 of NBC 2016 Part 4**, the required fire resistance depends on: - Occupancy group - Height of building - Floor area of largest compartment Typical values (extract for KTU revision): - Residential, height < 15 m → **1 hour** - Residential, height 15–24 m → **2 hours** - Educational, any height → **2 hours** - Institutional, Group B, any height → **2 hours** - High-rise business (> 24 m) → **3–4 hours** **Step 5 — Design the Member using IS 1643 Empirical Equations** (see §3). **Step 6 — Verify by Test or by Calculation against IS 3809 standard exposure.** ### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table.** Questions worth 7–14 marks are routinely drawn from these expressions. Use $\vert$ in formulas to avoid breaking table syntax. | Member Type | Empirical Formula (IS 1643 / IS 456) | Variable Meaning | Units | |-------------|---------------------------------------|------------------|-------| | RCC Column (square, axial) | $R \;=\; 0.85 \,\bigl(b - 2c\bigr) \;\big/ \; 60$ | $R$ = fire resistance; $b$ = column side; $c$ = cover | hrs ; mm ; mm | | RCC Column (rectangular) | $R \;=\; 0.85 \,\bigl(a_{\min} - 2c\bigr) \;\big/ \;60$ | $a_{\min}$ = smaller side | hrs ; mm ; mm | | RCC Beam | $R \;=\; k_1 \, b_{w} \;+\; k_2 \, c$ | $b_w$ = web width; $c$ = cover; $k_1, k_2$ from IS 1643 charts | hrs | | RCC Slab (solid) | $R \;=\; 1.1 \, t_s$ (approx, for $t_s$ in mm) | $t_s$ = slab thickness | hrs | | Steel Column (protected) | $R \;=\; \lambda_1 \, h_p \;+\; \lambda_2$ | $h_p$ = protection thickness; $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ per IS 1643 | hrs | | Steel Column (unprotected) | $R \;=\; 0.6 \, A \;/\; (160 \, H)$ | $A$ = cross-section area; $H$ = perimeter exposed | hrs | | Timber Beam | $R \;=\; (k_t \, b_t) \;/\; 60$ | $b_t$ = breadth; $k_t$ = timber constant (≈ 1.0) | hrs | ### 2.4 Real-World Utility This BIS framework is invoked for: - **Fire NOC approvals** from state Fire & Rescue Departments across India. - **Insurance underwriting** (tariff depends on declared fire resistance). - **Peer review** of high-rise and industrial structural designs. - **Litigation** post-fire incidents (forensic fire engineering). > [!NOTE] > The **IS 3809 standard fire curve** is essentially identical to the **ISO 834 curve**: > $$T(t) \;=\; T_0 \;+\; 345 \, \log_{10}\!\bigl(8t + 1\bigr)$$ > with $T_0 = 20$ °C, giving 945 °C at 60 min, 1086 °C at 240 min. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Fire Duration from Fire Load (IS 1644 Logic) The idea is to convert an *arbitrary* real fire into an *equivalent* time under the **standard fire** that would consume the same energy per unit area. Consider a compartment of floor area $A_f$ and ventilation opening of area $A_v$ × height $H_v$. The ventilation factor is: $$F_v \;=\; \frac{A_v \, \sqrt{H_v}}{A_f}$$ The equivalent fire duration (in hours), per IS 1641, is given by the empirical expression: $$t_e \;=\; \frac{q_f \, \cdot \, w}{3300 \, \cdot \, F_v^{0.5}}$$ where $w$ is a weighting factor for the type of occupancy (1.0 for most occupancies, 1.5 for hazardous storage). **Numerical demonstration:** A ground-floor office has: - Total combustible mass = 12 000 kg of wood + paper - Average calorific value $h$ = 17 MJ/kg - Floor area $A_f$ = 200 m² - Window opening: 6 m wide × 2 m tall (single opening) Step 1 — Fire load density: $$q_f \;=\; \frac{12\,000 \times 17}{200} \;=\; \frac{204\,000}{200} \;=\; 1020 \;\text{MJ/m}^2$$ Step 2 — Ventilation factor: $$A_v \sqrt{H_v} \;=\; 6 \times 2 \times \sqrt{2} \;=\; 12 \sqrt{2} \;\text{m}^{5/2}$$ $$F_v \;=\; \frac{12\sqrt{2}}{200} \;=\; 0.0849 \;\text{m}^{1/2}$$ Step 3 — Equivalent duration (office, $w=1.0$): $$t_e \;=\; \frac{1020 \times 1.0}{3300 \times \sqrt{0.0849}} \;=\; \frac{1020}{3300 \times 0.2913} \;=\; \frac{1020}{961.3} \;\approx\; 1.06 \;\text{hours}$$ Result: ~ **1 hour** equivalent fire duration. Required fire resistance $R_{req}$ ≥ 1 h from IS 1641. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Cover for an RCC Column (IS 1643) Starting from the formula for an axially loaded square RCC column: $$R \;=\; \frac{0.85 \,(b - 2c)}{60}$$ Solve for the required cover $c$ given $R$ and column size $b$: $$60R \;=\; 0.85(b - 2c)$$ $$b - 2c \;=\; \frac{60R}{0.85} \;=\; 70.588\,R$$ $$2c \;=\; b - 70.588\,R$$ $$\boxed{\;c \;=\; \frac{b - 70.588\,R}{2}\;}$$ **Numerical example:** Design an interior RCC column of size **400 mm × 400 mm** to provide a fire resistance of **2 hours**. $$c \;=\; \frac{400 - 70.588 \times 2}{2} \;=\; \frac{400 - 141.18}{2} \;=\; \frac{258.82}{2} \;\approx\; 129.4 \;\text{mm}$$ Since IS 456 allows a maximum cover of 75 mm for durability alone, the designer must either: - **Increase the column size**, or - **Provide supplementary fire protection** (vermiculite plaster, intumescent wrap, gypsum board encasement). > [!IMPORTANT] > The empirical formula in IS 1643 is valid for **normal-weight concrete** with siliceous aggregates. For carbonate aggregates, multiply the right-hand side by 1.3. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Thickness of a Solid RCC Slab (IS 456 Annex) For a simply-supported solid one-way slab exposed to fire from below, the empirical equation is: $$R \;=\; 1.05 \,t_s \;-\; 8 \quad \text{(for } t_s \text{ in mm, } R \text{ in minutes)}$$ Solve for $t_s$: $$t_s \;=\; \frac{R + 8}{1.05}$$ For a required $R = 90$ minutes: $$t_s \;=\; \frac{90 + 8}{1.05} \;=\; \frac{98}{1.05} \;\approx\; 93.3 \;\text{mm}$$ Adopt **100 mm** (next standard thickness). This aligns with **IS 456 Annex G** tabulated value of 95 mm for 90 minutes. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Choose the Right Method for a Given Member | If member is… | Then consult… | Key parameter controlling $R$ | |---------------|---------------|-------------------------------| | RCC column, axial | IS 1643, eqn. given above | $b$ and $c$ | | RCC column, eccentric | IS 1643, with eccentricity factor $\eta$ | $b$, $c$, $e$ | | RCC beam | IS 1643 charts | $b_w$ and cover $c$ | | RCC slab | IS 456 Annex G, IS 1643 | Thickness $t_s$ | | Steel column, unprotected | IS 1643 § 8 | $H_p/A$ ratio | | Steel column, protected | IS 1643 § 8 + manufacturer data | $h_p$ of plaster/board | | Timber beam | IS 1643 § 9 | $b_t$ | | Load-bearing wall | IS 1643 § 4 | Thickness $t_w$ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 BIS Fire-Resistance Determination Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Building Defined] --> B[Step 1: Classify Occupancy
IS 1645] B --> C[Step 2: Compute Fire Load
qf and Ventilation Factor Fv
IS 1644] C --> D[Step 3: Find Equivalent
Fire Duration te
IS 1641 Charts] D --> E[Step 4: Look up Required
Fire Resistance Rreq
IS 1641 Table] E --> F[Step 5: Identify Material
of Structural Member] F --> G1[RCC Member] F --> G2[Steel Member] F --> G3[Timber Member] G1 --> H1[Apply IS 1643
RCC Empirical Formula] G2 --> H2[Apply IS 1643
Steel Formula or Test] G3 --> H3[Apply IS 1643
Timber Formula] H1 --> I[Check: R_actual greater or equal Rreq] H2 --> I H3 --> I I --> J{R_actual Rreq} J -- Yes --> K[Design Acceptable
Document Compliance] J -- No --> L[Revise: Increase Cover
or Section Size or Add Protection] L --> I K --> M[End: File with Fire NOC] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic of the Three BIS Fire Failure Criteria on an RCC Member ```mermaid graph LR subgraph FIRE_EXPOSURE["HOT SIDE - FURNACE - IS 3809"] H1[Gas Burner 1100 deg C] end subgraph MEMBER["RCC MEMBER WITH COVER c"] M1[Concrete Cover Layer] M2[Reinforcement Rebar] M3[Inner Concrete Core] end subgraph COLD_SIDE["AMBIENT SIDE - INSTRUMENTED"] C1[Thermocouples for Insulation Check] C2[Deflection Gauges for Stability Check] C3[Gap and Cotton Pad for Integrity Check] end H1 --> M1 M1 --> M2 M2 --> M3 M3 --> C1 M3 --> C2 M3 --> C3 ``` ### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Annotation of a Fire-Exposed RC Column ```mermaid graph TD A[RCC Column 400x400 mm] --> B[Outer concrete cover: c mm] A --> C[Longitudinal rebar: 20 mm dia] A --> D[Ties / lateral steel] A --> E[Inner concrete core] B -.Protects.-> C C -.Loses 50 percent strength at 550 deg C.-> E ``` > [!NOTE] > The **time to reach 550 °C** at the rebar is what defines the **fire resistance** of a flexural/axial RCC member, per the limiting-temperature criterion embedded in IS 1643. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *List the three BIS criteria that define the failure of a structural member under standard fire exposure, citing the relevant IS code.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** As per **IS 3809:1979 (Method of test for fire resistance test of structures)**, a structural member is deemed to have failed when any one of the following is breached: 1. **Stability (R)** – loss of load-bearing capacity. 2. **Integrity (E)** – passage of flames/hot gases through cracks. 3. **Insulation (I)** – temperature rise on the unexposed face > 140 °C average or > 180 °C at any point. The lowest time at which any one criterion is breached is the **fire resistance** of the member. *(1 mark each)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *State the role of IS 1641 and IS 1643 in the BIS fire-resistance framework.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **IS 1641 (Code of practice for fire safety – General):** Used to determine the **required** fire resistance of a member, based on occupancy type, building height, and floor area. *(1½ marks)* - **IS 1643 (Code of practice – Calculation of fire resistance):** Provides **empirical equations and charts** to **design** the member (cover, dimensions) so as to **achieve** the required fire resistance. *(1½ marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Choice Question (14 Marks) > **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]* **(a)** Explain, with a stepwise procedure, the **BIS method of arriving at the required fire resistance of structural members**. Mention all relevant Indian Standards at each stage. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A reinforced-concrete column of size **450 mm × 450 mm** is required to provide a fire resistance of **3 hours**. Calculate the **minimum concrete cover** to the main reinforcement as per IS 1643. State the practical limitation, if any. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Step 1 – Occupancy Classification (IS 1645):** The building is classified into one of nine groups (Residential, Educational, Institutional, etc.) which sets the base hazard level. *(1 mark)* 2. **Step 2 – Fire Load Determination (IS 1644):** Compute fire load density $q_f = \sum M_i h_i / A_f$ in MJ/m²; also compute ventilation factor $F_v = A_v \sqrt{H_v} / A_f$. *(1 mark)* 3. **Step 3 – Equivalent Fire Duration (IS 1641):** From $q_f$ and $F_v$, read off the equivalent fire duration $t_e$ (in minutes) using IS 1641 charts/equations. *(1 mark)* 4. **Step 4 – Required Fire Resistance (IS 1641 / NBC 2016):** Consult Table 1 of IS 1641 (or NBC 2016 Part 4) to read the required fire resistance $R_{req}$ for the given occupancy, height, and compartment area. *(1 mark)* 5. **Step 5 – Material-Based Design (IS 1643):** Identify the material (RCC / Steel / Timber) and apply the relevant empirical formula to size the member. *(1 mark)* 6. **Step 6 – Verify Against Test Standard (IS 3809):** Confirm the design by either calculation in line with IS 3809 standard fire exposure, or by an actual fire-resistance test. *(1 mark)* 7. **Step 7 – Iterate / Re-design if $R_{actual} < R_{req}$.** *(1 mark)* **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** Using the IS 1643 empirical formula for an axially loaded square RCC column: $$R \;=\; \frac{0.85 \,(b - 2c)}{60}$$ Substituting $R = 3$ h and $b = 450$ mm: $$3 \;=\; \frac{0.85\,(450 - 2c)}{60}$$ $$180 \;=\; 0.85\,(450 - 2c)$$ $$450 - 2c \;=\; \frac{180}{0.85} \;=\; 211.76$$ $$2c \;=\; 450 - 211.76 \;=\; 238.24$$ $$\boxed{\,c \;\approx\; 119.1 \;\text{mm}\,}$$ **Practical Limitation (2 marks):** IS 456 permits a maximum cover of **75 mm** for durability under normal exposure conditions. Hence, providing 119 mm of cover is **not feasible** purely by concrete cover. The designer must either: - Increase column size to **~600 mm × 600 mm**, or - Provide supplementary fire protection such as vermiculite-cement plaster, gypsum board encasement, or intumescent coating. For $b = 600$ mm, $R = 3$ h: $$c \;=\; \frac{600 - 211.76}{2} \;=\; 194.1 \;\text{mm (still too high)}$$ Therefore, the most economical solution is to **encase the column** with 25–40 mm of vermiculite plaster, which typically adds ~2 hours of fire resistance per 25 mm thickness. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]* **(a)** Describe the **standard fire exposure curve** as per IS 3809. Derive the temperature at **120 minutes**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A ground-floor **bookstore** in a commercial building has a fire load of **1800 MJ/m²**, floor area **150 m²**, and a single window **4 m × 2 m** (height). Calculate the **equivalent fire duration** and hence the **required fire resistance** as per IS 1641, assuming an office-type weighting factor of 1.0. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Standard Fire Curve (IS 3809 ≈ ISO 834):** *(3 marks)* $$T(t) \;=\; T_0 \;+\; 345 \, \log_{10}\!\bigl(8t + 1\bigr)$$ with $T_0 = 20$ °C (ambient). This curve is: - A function of the logarithm of time. - Independent of fuel type and ventilation. - Used for comparative rating of structural assemblies. **Derivation at $t = 120$ min:** *(3 marks)* $$8t + 1 \;=\; 8(120) + 1 \;=\; 961$$ $$\log_{10}(961) \;=\; 2.9827$$ $$T(120) \;=\; 20 + 345 \times 2.9827 \;=\; 20 + 1029.0 \;\approx\; \boxed{1049 \;°\text{C}}$$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** At 120 min the standard furnace gas temperature is ~1049 °C, which is the test condition for **2-hour** fire-rated members. **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $q_f = 1800$ MJ/m², $A_f = 150$ m², window $4 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m}$, $w = 1.0$. **Step 1 — Ventilation factor:** *(2 marks)* $$A_v \sqrt{H_v} \;=\; 4 \times \sqrt{2} \;=\; 5.657 \;\text{m}^{5/2}$$ $$F_v \;=\; \frac{5.657}{150} \;=\; 0.0377 \;\text{m}^{1/2}$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent fire duration:** *(3 marks)* $$t_e \;=\; \frac{q_f \cdot w}{3300 \cdot \sqrt{F_v}} \;=\; \frac{1800 \times 1.0}{3300 \times \sqrt{0.0377}} \;=\; \frac{1800}{3300 \times 0.1942} \;=\; \frac{1800}{640.9}$$ $$\boxed{\,t_e \;\approx\; 2.81 \;\text{hours} \;\approx\; 169 \;\text{minutes}\,}$$ **Step 3 — Required fire resistance:** *(2 marks)* From IS 1641 / NBC 2016, for a **mercantile (bookstore) occupancy** with $t_e \approx 2.8$ h, the next standard fire-resistance rating is **3 hours** (REI 180). Therefore $R_{req} = 3$ h. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - Do **NOT** confuse $A_v$ (window area in m²) with $A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ (ventilation factor numerator in m^(5/2)). > - Do **NOT** forget the square-root on $F_v$ inside the denominator of $t_e$. > - Always **round up** the equivalent duration to the next standard rating (1, 2, 3, 4 h) — never round down. > - The weighting factor $w$ defaults to 1.0 unless the occupancy is **hazardous storage** (1.5) or **open-deck car park** (0.5). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Three Criteria = Stability + Integrity + Insulation** (any one failure = member failure). The **REI** label bundles them. - **IS 1645** classifies the building; **IS 1644** gives fire load; **IS 1641** gives equivalent duration and required resistance; **IS 1643** gives design equations; **IS 3809** is the test standard. - The **standard fire curve** $T = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ is the BIS/ISO benchmark — remember **T(60) ≈ 945 °C**, **T(120) ≈ 1049 °C**, **T(240) ≈ 1153 °C**. - The **ventilation factor** $F_v = A_v \sqrt{H_v} / A_f$ controls fire severity. *More ventilation = more severe but shorter fire.* - The **equivalent fire duration** $t_e = q_f w / (3300 \sqrt{F_v})$ is read in **hours**; always **round up** to next standard rating. - For **RCC columns**, the empirical formula is $R = 0.85(b - 2c)/60$. For $R = 2$ h with $b = 400$ mm, cover ≈ 129 mm — **not feasible** under IS 456 (max 75 mm), hence the need for **section enlargement or fire protection**. - For **RCC slabs**, $R \approx 1.05\,t_s - 8$ (in minutes), so 90 min → $t_s \approx 94$ mm. - For **steel**, *unprotected* endurance is very low (typically 15–30 min); protection is **mandatory** for required ratings ≥ 1 h. - For **timber**, large sections (≥ 100 mm) offer good inherent endurance, but combustibility is the trade-off. - The **design check** is $R_{actual} \geq R_{req}$ — never the reverse. - BIS provides **simplified tables** in **IS 456 Annex G** for routine design of slabs, beams, and columns. ---

topics.title: Determination of Combustibility of Materials by Fire Tube Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Determination of Combustibility of Materials by Fire Tube Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Fire Tube Method** is a standardized laboratory fire-test procedure used to determine the **combustibility** of building and structural materials by exposing a small specimen to a controlled flame inside a tubular combustion chamber (fire tube furnace) and quantitatively measuring the **temperature rise**, **flame propagation characteristics**, and **mass loss** of the specimen. The method provides a relative index that classifies a material as either *non-combustible* or *combustible* based on its thermo-physical and chemical response to a standardized fire exposure.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT835 – Structural Fire Safety)**, this method is taught under Module 3 as a fundamental reaction-to-fire property test used to classify materials before they are deployed in the fire-resistive design of buildings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Fire Tube Method is the **primary experimental route** for assessing *reaction-to-fire* properties (as opposed to *fire resistance*). It is one of the classical reaction-to-fire tests aligned with **IS 3808 / BS 476 Part 6 / ISO 5658** family of standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you take a small piece of cake (your material specimen) and hold it near a candle flame inside a glass tube. Now, instead of a candle, you use a precisely controlled gas burner that delivers a fixed amount of heat for a fixed time. The "Fire Tube Method" is essentially this — but engineered to scientific precision.

- If the cake **charring smolders but does not burst into flame**, and the temperature inside the tube does not rise sharply → the material is *non-combustible* (like granite, gypsum, or concrete).
- If the cake **catches fire, flames spread, and the tube temperature shoots up** → the material is *combustible* (like wood, plastic, or foam insulation).

The tube acts as a controlled "stage" where we observe the material's behavior under fire in a repeatable, measurable way. Think of it as a **miniature theater of combustion** where the "actor" (material) is judged by a strict, impartial "audience" (thermocouples and scales).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metrics used in classification:**
> - **ΔT** = Temperature rise (°C) inside the tube
> - **Δm** = Mass loss (%) of the specimen
> - **t_f** = Duration of sustained flaming (seconds)

### 1.3 Scope and Applications in Structural Fire Safety

The Fire Tube Method is used by **fire safety engineers, code consultants, material scientists, and regulatory bodies** for:

1. **Material classification** under National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) and IS standards.
2. **Quality control** in manufacturing of insulation boards, wall panels, ceiling tiles, and structural composites.
3. **Specification writing** for fire-safe construction.
4. **Forensic fire engineering** — post-fire investigation to identify whether a material was the cause of fire spread.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles

The Fire Tube Method rests on three intertwined physical phenomena:

**a) Heat Transfer Mechanisms**
- **Conduction** through the solid specimen (Fourier's Law).
- **Convection** from the hot gases of the burner to the specimen surface (Newton's Law of Cooling).
- **Radiation** from the burner flame and hot tube walls (Stefan–Boltzmann Law).

**b) Pyrolysis and Combustion Chemistry**
- When heated, organic materials undergo **thermal decomposition (pyrolysis)**, releasing combustible volatile gases.
- These gases mix with oxygen and ignite if the **ignition temperature** is reached, sustaining a flame.
- Inorganic materials (e.g., calcium silicate, gypsum) do **not** release combustible gases; they may only release water vapor (e.g., CaSO₄·2H₂O → CaSO₄ + 2H₂O) and remain essentially non-reactive.

**c) Heat Release Mechanism**
- Combustible specimens release **chemical energy** through exothermic oxidation reactions, raising the temperature inside the tube.
- The temperature rise (ΔT) over a fixed time interval is the key index of combustibility.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Test

1. **Specimen Preparation** — A representative sample (typically cylindrical or rectangular, ~50–100 g) is conditioned at constant humidity and weighed.
2. **Mounting in the Tube** — The specimen is placed in the specimen holder inside a refractory tube.
3. **Burner Ignition** — A gas burner (typically LPG or natural gas) is ignited, delivering a controlled flame of **~800–1000 °C**.
4. **Exposure Period** — Flame is applied for a **standardized duration** (commonly **2 minutes** in short-duration tests, or up to **10 minutes** in extended fire propagation tests).
5. **Temperature Recording** — A thermocouple (Type K or Type R) placed at the top of the tube records gas temperature at fixed intervals (every 30 s or 1 min).
6. **Mass Loss Measurement** — After the test, the specimen is reweighed to determine the percentage mass loss.
7. **Visual Observation** — Duration of flaming, smoke emission, and any falling debris are noted.
8. **Classification** — Based on ΔT and Δm, the material is classified as combustible or non-combustible.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|--------------------|----------------------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Temperature Rise | $\Delta T = T_{\text{peak}} - T_{\text{ambient}}$ | °C or K | $T_{\text{ambient}} \approx 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 2 | Percentage Mass Loss | $\Delta m\,(\%) = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_i}\times 100$ | % | $m_i$ = initial mass, $m_f$ = final mass |
| 3 | Average Heating Rate | $\bar{h} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta t}$ | °C/s | For a defined time window |
| 4 | Conductive Heat Flux (Fourier) | $q_k = -k\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | W/m² | $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K) |
| 5 | Convective Heat Flux (Newton) | $q_c = h\,(T_s - T_\infty)$ | W/m² | $h$ = convective coefficient (W/m²·K) |
| 6 | Radiative Heat Flux (Stefan–Boltzmann) | $q_r = \varepsilon\,\sigma\,(T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | W/m² | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ |
| 7 | Combustibility Index (CI) | $\text{CI} = \dfrac{\Delta T \cdot t_f}{m_i}$ | °C·s/g | Empirical composite index |
| 8 | Non-combustible Limit (typical) | $\Delta T \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ **AND** $\Delta m \le 50\ \%$ | — | Per IS 3808 / BS 476 Pt 4 conventions |
| 9 | Combustible Limit | $\Delta T > 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ **OR** $\Delta m > 50\ \%$ | — | Sustained flaming usually observed |
| 10 | Emissivity Range for building materials | $0.85 \le \varepsilon \le 0.95$ | dimensionless | Used in $q_r$ calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** A material is **non-combustible** if **BOTH** $\Delta T \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ **AND** $\Delta m \le 50\%$ are satisfied. Failure of **either** condition = **combustible**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Code Compliance:** Architects and structural engineers must verify combustibility before specifying a material in fire-rated assemblies.
- **Insurance Underwriting:** Insurers use combustibility classification to calculate fire risk premiums.
- **Forensic Fire Analysis:** Investigators reproduce the fire-tube test on debris to determine the original material's combustibility.
- **Product Development:** Manufacturers of cladding, insulation, and composite panels use the test to validate fire-safe formulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Combustibility Index (CI)

**Starting Principle:** A material's combustibility is reflected in two observable outcomes:
1. The **temperature rise (ΔT)** inside the tube (driven by heat release from combustion).
2. The **mass loss (Δm)** (driven by pyrolysis/evaporation).
3. The **flaming duration (t_f)** indicates sustained combustion.

A composite index that captures all three in a single normalized metric is:

$$
\text{CI} = \frac{\Delta T \cdot t_f}{m_i}
$$

where:
- $\Delta T$ = peak temperature rise above ambient (°C)
- $t_f$ = total flaming duration (s)
- $m_i$ = initial specimen mass (g)

**Step 1: Energy Released by Combustion**

The chemical heat release $Q_{\text{chem}}$ from the specimen is approximated as:

$$
Q_{\text{chem}} = m_{\text{burned}} \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

where $\Delta H_c$ is the **heat of combustion** (J/g) and $m_{\text{burned}} = m_i - m_f$.

**Step 2: Heat Convected into the Tube Gas**

Assuming the released heat raises the gas temperature in the tube of effective heat capacity $C_{\text{gas}}$ (J/K):

$$
C_{\text{gas}}\,\Delta T = Q_{\text{chem}} - Q_{\text{losses}}
$$

Neglecting losses for short-duration tests:

$$
C_{\text{gas}}\,\Delta T \approx m_{\text{burned}} \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

**Step 3: Solve for Mass Burned**

$$
m_{\text{burned}} = \frac{C_{\text{gas}}\,\Delta T}{\Delta H_c}
$$

**Step 4: Express as a Fraction of Initial Mass**

$$
\frac{m_{\text{burned}}}{m_i} = \frac{C_{\text{gas}}\,\Delta T}{m_i \cdot \Delta H_c}
$$

**Step 5: Introduce Flaming Duration**

The total flaming duration $t_f$ is proportional to the rate of heat release. Combining all three variables:

$$
\text{CI} = \frac{\Delta T \cdot t_f}{m_i}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- **High CI** → significant heat release, sustained flaming, large relative mass loss → **combustible**.
- **Low CI** → minimal temperature rise, no sustained flaming → **non-combustible**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A sample of particle board is tested in the Fire Tube Method. The following data are recorded:

- Initial mass: $m_i = 80\ \text{g}$
- Final mass after test: $m_f = 28\ \text{g}$
- Peak tube temperature: $T_{\text{peak}} = 715\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Ambient temperature: $T_{\text{amb}} = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Duration of sustained flaming: $t_f = 45\ \text{s}$

**Determine:** (a) Temperature rise, (b) Percentage mass loss, (c) Combustibility classification, (d) Combustibility Index.

#### Step 1: Calculate Temperature Rise

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{peak}} - T_{\text{amb}}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 715 - 25
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T = 690\ ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

#### Step 2: Calculate Percentage Mass Loss

$$
\Delta m\,(\%) = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i}\times 100
$$

$$
\Delta m = \frac{80 - 28}{80}\times 100
$$

$$
\Delta m = \frac{52}{80}\times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta m = 65\%}
$$

#### Step 3: Classification

Comparison with limits:

$$
\Delta T = 690\ ^\circ\text{C} \gg 50\ ^\circ\text{C}\quad(\text{Fails non-combustible criterion})
$$

$$
\Delta m = 65\% > 50\%\quad(\text{Fails non-combustible criterion})
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Material is classified as COMBUSTIBLE.}}
$$

#### Step 4: Calculate Combustibility Index

$$
\text{CI} = \frac{\Delta T \cdot t_f}{m_i}
$$

$$
\text{CI} = \frac{690 \times 45}{80}
$$

$$
\text{CI} = \frac{31{,}050}{80}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CI} = 388.1\ ^\circ\text{C·s/g}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A CI of $\sim 388$ is well above the typical non-combustible range (CI $< 30$ for materials like gypsum or concrete). The high value confirms the **combustible** nature of particle board — a result consistent with real-world fire behavior of engineered wood products.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix for Common Building Materials

| Material | $m_i$ (g) | $m_f$ (g) | $\Delta T$ (°C) | $\Delta m$ (%) | $t_f$ (s) | CI | Class |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|----------------|-----------|------|---------|
| Granite | 100 | 99.5 | 18 | 0.5 | 0 | 0 | **Non-combustible** |
| Gypsum board | 80 | 76 | 32 | 5.0 | 2 | 0.8 | **Non-combustible** |
| Concrete | 100 | 98 | 25 | 2.0 | 0 | 0 | **Non-combustible** |
| Mineral wool | 50 | 49 | 40 | 2.0 | 5 | 4.0 | **Non-combustible** |
| Plywood | 60 | 22 | 620 | 63.3 | 40 | 413 | **Combustible** |
| Particle board | 80 | 28 | 690 | 65.0 | 45 | 388 | **Combustible** |
| PVC insulation | 70 | 38 | 540 | 45.7 | 35 | 270 | **Combustible** |
| Rigid PU foam | 40 | 8 | 780 | 80.0 | 30 | 585 | **Highly combustible** |

> [!TIP]
> This table is a **high-yield KTU revision block** — examiners often frame numerical sub-parts on this exact pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Apparatus Schematic — Fire Tube Test Rig

The diagram below maps the functional architecture of a typical Fire Tube Method apparatus. Each block represents a physical subsystem, and the arrows show the flow of heat, gas, and data.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[GAS CYLINDER LPG NG] --> B[PRESSURE REGULATOR]
    B --> C[ROTAMETER FLOW METER]
    C --> D[GAS BURNER TUBE INLET]
    D --> E[FIRE TUBE FURNACE REFRACTORY]
    F[SAMPLE HOLDER SPECIMEN MOUNT] --> E
    E --> G[THERMOCOUPLE TYPE K POSITION]
    G --> H[DATA LOGGER TEMPERATURE RECORDER]
    E --> I[CHIMNEY DRAFT CONTROL]
    I --> J[EXHAUST VENTILATION]
    K[STOPWATCH FLAME TIMER] --> F
    L[ELECTRONIC BALANCE PRE POST] --> F
    M[OBSERVER FLAME SMOKE NOTES] --> E
    H --> N[COMPUTATION BLOCK deltaT deltam CI]
    L --> N
    K --> N
    N --> O[CLASSIFICATION COMBUSTIBLE OR NOT]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[STEP 1 SAMPLE PREPARATION] --> P2[STEP 2 CONDITIONING 24 HOURS]
    P2 --> P3[STEP 3 INITIAL MASS MEASUREMENT]
    P3 --> P4[STEP 4 SPECIMEN MOUNTING IN TUBE]
    P4 --> P5[STEP 5 BURNER IGNITION t EQUALS 0]
    P5 --> P6[STEP 6 FLAME EXPOSURE 2 TO 10 MIN]
    P6 --> P7[STEP 7 CONTINUOUS TEMP LOGGING]
    P7 --> P8[STEP 8 FLAME TIMER RECORDING]
    P8 --> P9[STEP 9 BURNER SHUT OFF]
    P9 --> P10[STEP 10 COOLING PERIOD]
    P10 --> P11[STEP 11 FINAL MASS MEASUREMENT]
    P11 --> P12[STEP 12 COMPUTE DELTAT DELMAM CI]
    P12 --> P13[STEP 13 CLASSIFICATION AS PER IS 3808]
```

### 4.3 Annotated Component List (Linked to Diagram)

1. **Gas Supply Unit** (A → B → C → D): Provides stable, metered fuel to the burner.
2. **Fire Tube Furnace** (E): The refractory tube where combustion occurs; typically 600–900 mm long, 100–150 mm inner diameter.
3. **Specimen Holder** (F): Holds the test sample at a fixed position relative to the flame.
4. **Thermocouple + Data Logger** (G → H): Type K (Chromel-Alumel) thermocouples are standard; data is logged every 10 s.
5. **Chimney + Draft Control** (I → J): Maintains a stable upward draft, ensuring uniform fire exposure.
6. **Timing Module** (K): Records start/end of flaming.
7. **Mass Measurement Module** (L): Pre-test and post-test weighing on a precision balance (±0.01 g).
8. **Computation Block** (N): Calculates ΔT, Δm, CI; compares against thresholds to classify.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** When asked to draw the apparatus in exams, label **at least 6 components** for full marks. Common omission = the **thermocouple location** (often a 1-mark loss in part (a) of 14-mark questions).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define the Fire Tube Method. What does it determine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Fire Tube Method is a **standardized laboratory test procedure** for determining the **combustibility** of building materials. A specimen of known mass is exposed to a **controlled gas flame** inside a refractory tube furnace under standardized conditions. The method determines the **temperature rise (ΔT)**, the **percentage mass loss (Δm)**, and the **duration of sustained flaming (t_f)**. Based on these measurements, the material is classified as **combustible** or **non-combustible** per the prescribed limits (typically $\Delta T \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $\Delta m \le 50\%$ for non-combustible classification).

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. List the major components of a Fire Tube Test apparatus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The major components of a Fire Tube Test apparatus are:
1. **Gas burner** with supply line, regulator, and flow meter.
2. **Refractory fire tube (furnace)** where the specimen is exposed.
3. **Specimen holder** for mounting the test sample.
4. **Thermocouple and data logger** for continuous temperature recording.
5. **Chimney with draft control** for stable fire exposure.
6. **Electronic balance** for pre- and post-test mass measurement.
7. **Timing device** for recording flaming duration.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **apparatus and procedure** for determining the combustibility of a building material using the **Fire Tube Method**. Enumerate the observations recorded. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A specimen of **coconut pith board** (mass = 75 g) was tested in the Fire Tube Method. The peak tube temperature reached was $640\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (ambient = $28\ ^\circ\text{C}$), the final mass was 25 g, and the flaming lasted 38 seconds. Determine the **temperature rise, percentage mass loss, combustibility index**, and **classify** the material. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Apparatus and Procedure (7 marks)**

**Apparatus (Sketch to be drawn — see Section 4.1):**

The Fire Tube apparatus consists of:
- A **refractory tube** (≈ 800 mm long, 100 mm inner diameter) held vertically.
- A **gas burner** at the bottom delivering a controlled flame.
- A **specimen holder** at the center of the tube.
- A **Type K thermocouple** at the top of the tube connected to a data logger.
- A **chimney** with a draft regulator above.
- A **precision balance** and **stopwatch**.

**Procedure:**

1. Cut the specimen to standard size (typically 50 × 50 × 25 mm or as per standard) and **condition** it at $27 \pm 2\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $65 \pm 5\%$ RH for 24 hours.
2. **Weigh** the specimen to the nearest 0.01 g → record $m_i$.
3. Mount the specimen in the **specimen holder** inside the tube.
4. Ignite the gas burner and adjust the flame to standard conditions (flame height ≈ 100–120 mm).
5. **Start the timer** and **begin temperature logging**.
6. Expose the specimen for the **standard duration** (e.g., 2 minutes for short test, 10 minutes for propagation test).
7. **Record observations** at 30-second intervals: gas temperature, flame persistence, smoke, falling debris.
8. **Stop the burner**, allow cooling, and **reweigh** the specimen → record $m_f$.

**Observations Recorded:**
- Initial and final mass ($m_i$, $m_f$).
- Peak tube temperature ($T_{\text{peak}}$) and ambient ($T_{\text{amb}}$).
- Time-temperature curve (graph).
- Duration of flaming ($t_f$).
- Visual observations: smoke color, dripping, sustained vs. transient flaming.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $m_i = 75\ \text{g}$, $m_f = 25\ \text{g}$
- $T_{\text{peak}} = 640\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{\text{amb}} = 28\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $t_f = 38\ \text{s}$

**Step 1: Temperature Rise**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{peak}} - T_{\text{amb}} = 640 - 28 = 612\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 2: Percentage Mass Loss**

$$
\Delta m = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i}\times 100 = \frac{75 - 25}{75}\times 100 = 66.67\%
$$

**Step 3: Combustibility Index**

$$
\text{CI} = \frac{\Delta T \cdot t_f}{m_i} = \frac{612 \times 38}{75} = \frac{23{,}256}{75} = 310.1\ ^\circ\text{C·s/g}
$$

**Step 4: Classification**

- $\Delta T = 612\ ^\circ\text{C} > 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ ✗
- $\Delta m = 66.67\% > 50\%$ ✗

**Both criteria fail → The material is classified as COMBUSTIBLE.**

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **principle** of the Fire Tube Method. Discuss the **heat transfer mechanisms** that govern the temperature rise inside the fire tube. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A specimen of **calcium silicate board** (mass = 100 g) was tested. The peak temperature rise was $42\ ^\circ\text{C}$, the final mass was 97 g, and no sustained flaming was observed. Calculate the **combustibility index** (take $t_f = 0\ \text{s}$) and **classify** the material. Comment on its suitability for **fire-rated wall assemblies**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle and Heat Transfer (7 marks)**

**Principle:**
The Fire Tube Method is based on the principle that when a material is exposed to a **standardized fire source**, its **combustibility can be quantified** by measuring the resulting **temperature rise (ΔT)**, **mass loss (Δm)**, and **flaming duration (t_f)**. Combustible materials release chemical energy through oxidation, raising the tube temperature and losing mass; non-combustible materials remain thermally and chemically inert.

**Heat Transfer Mechanisms:**

1. **Conduction (Fourier's Law):** $q_k = -k\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}$
   Heat conducts from the flame through the specimen and tube walls.

2. **Convection (Newton's Law):** $q_c = h\,(T_s - T_\infty)$
   Hot combustion gases transfer heat to the specimen surface.

3. **Radiation (Stefan–Boltzmann):** $q_r = \varepsilon\,\sigma\,(T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$
   The flame and tube walls radiate thermal energy to the specimen.

The **net heat flux** to the specimen is:

$$
q_{\text{net}} = q_c + q_r - q_{\text{re-radiation}}
$$

For non-combustibles, absorbed heat is dissipated as sensible heat with negligible chemical energy release → small ΔT.
For combustibles, absorbed heat drives **pyrolysis**, releasing combustible gases that **ignite and release chemical energy** → large ΔT and Δm.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $m_i = 100\ \text{g}$, $m_f = 97\ \text{g}$
- $\Delta T = 42\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $t_f = 0\ \text{s}$ (no sustained flaming)

**Step 1: Percentage Mass Loss**

$$
\Delta m = \frac{100 - 97}{100}\times 100 = 3\%
$$

**Step 2: Combustibility Index**

$$
\text{CI} = \frac{\Delta T \cdot t_f}{m_i} = \frac{42 \times 0}{100} = 0\ ^\circ\text{C·s/g}
$$

**Step 3: Classification**

- $\Delta T = 42\ ^\circ\text{C} \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ ✓
- $\Delta m = 3\% \le 50\%$ ✓

**Both criteria satisfied → The material is classified as NON-COMBUSTIBLE.**

**Suitability for Fire-Rated Wall Assemblies:**
Calcium silicate board, being **non-combustible** with negligible CI, is **highly suitable** for fire-rated wall assemblies. It does not contribute to fire load, does not propagate flame, and maintains structural integrity under fire exposure. It is commonly used as **fire-stopping material, partition panels, and cladding in fire-rated construction** per IS 3808 / NBC 2016 guidelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract ambient temperature** in ΔT calculation — costs **1 mark** consistently.
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta m$ (% mass loss) with final mass** — always express as a percentage.
> 3. **Wrong units in CI** — CI is in °C·s/g; verify dimensional consistency.
> 4. **Skipping the comparison step** in classification — examiners expect the **explicit comparison** with $\Delta T = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $\Delta m = 50\%$.
> 5. **Missing the apparatus diagram** in part (a) — always include a **labelled schematic** for full marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning thermocouple type and draft control** in apparatus description — these are examiner-favorite 1-mark picks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Fire Tube Method** determines **combustibility** by exposing a specimen to a **controlled gas flame** in a refractory tube.
- [x] Three primary measurements: **ΔT (°C), Δm (%), t_f (s)**.
- [x] **Non-combustible limit:** $\Delta T \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ **AND** $\Delta m \le 50\%$.
- [x] **Combustibility Index:** $\text{CI} = \dfrac{\Delta T \cdot t_f}{m_i}$ with units **°C·s/g**.
- [x] **Heat transfer** inside the tube occurs via **conduction, convection, and radiation**.
- [x] **Apparatus components:** gas burner, refractory tube, specimen holder, thermocouple (Type K), data logger, chimney with draft control, balance, timer.
- [x] **Standard test duration:** 2 minutes (short test) to 10 minutes (fire propagation test).
- [x] **Emissivity (ε)** of building materials typically ranges from **0.85 to 0.95**.
- [x] **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.
- [x] The method is governed by standards like **IS 3808, BS 476 Part 6, and ISO 5658**.
- [x] **Non-combustibles** (granite, concrete, gypsum, calcium silicate) show negligible CI; **combustibles** (wood, plastic, foam) show CI in the hundreds.
- [x] **Always** present the apparatus diagram with **at least 6 labelled components** for full marks.
- [x] **Always** show the **explicit comparison** with classification thresholds in numerical problems.
- [x] **Always** quote **units** in the final answer for every quantity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brief Description on Non-Combustibility Test as per BIS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Brief Description on Non-Combustibility Test as per BIS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Combustibility Test (per BIS / IS 3808):** A standardized laboratory procedure prescribed by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** under **IS 3808 (latest revision)** to determine whether a building/construction material is **combustible** or **non-combustible** when subjected to a controlled thermal exposure of **750 °C** in an electric tubular furnace for a specified duration (typically up to **20 minutes**).

The test classifies materials based on three concurrent observations:
1. **Sustained flaming** of the specimen (yes/no).
2. **Mean temperature rise** of the furnace (ΔT) above the baseline 750 °C set-point.
3. **Mass loss** of the specimen expressed as a percentage of its original mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Threshold Values (as per IS 3808):** A material is declared **non-combustible** if the **mean furnace temperature rise does not exceed 50 °C** AND the **mass loss does not exceed 50 %**, AND there is **no sustained flaming** lasting more than a few seconds.

The standard is harmonized in spirit with **ISO 1182** and is referenced by the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4)** for fire-resistance classification of structural and lining materials.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Oven Roast Test":** Imagine placing a small brick of butter versus a small brick of clay into a hot tandoor at 750 °C for 20 minutes.
>
> * The **butter** catches fire, melts, loses half its mass, and the oven temperature shoots up because combustion releases heat.
> * The **clay** simply glows, loses almost no mass, and the oven temperature barely changes.
>
> That, in essence, is what the Non-Combustibility Test does — it **measures whether a material gives back more than it absorbs** when heated aggressively. If the material neither flames nor dumps significant heat/mass into the furnace, it is graded **non-combustible**.

This makes the test a **go/no-go gateway** for materials intended for use in fire-rated walls, ceilings, structural steel encasements, ducts, and insulation in high-rise and assembly occupancies.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Furnace set-point temperature | **750** | °C |
| Specimen geometry (cylindrical) | Ø **45 × 50 height** | mm |
| Test duration | up to **20** | min |
| Mean furnace ΔT limit | **≤ 50** | °C |
| Mass loss limit | **≤ 50** | % |
| Thermocouple type | **Type K (Chromel-Alumel)** | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle

The test rests on a simple **thermodynamic control-volume balance**:

When a specimen is introduced into a furnace stabilized at **750 °C**, the furnace acts as a near-adiabatic reservoir. Any combustion of the specimen releases chemical enthalpy (heat of combustion), which is sensed as a **temperature rise** in the furnace walls and air. Simultaneously, the specimen loses mass as volatiles/pyrolysis products depart. By bounding these two observables (ΔT and % mass loss), the BIS standard classifies the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core "Why" of the Test:** Combustible materials release additional energy via exothermic oxidation of their carbon/hydrogen content. Non-combustible materials only undergo endothermic physical changes (drying, minor dehydration, phase change). Therefore, **ΔT and mass loss together form a reliable surrogate for combustibility.**

## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Procedure (KTU Board-Favoured Format)

**Step 1 — Specimen Preparation:**
- Cut **5 cylindrical specimens** (Ø 45 mm × 50 mm) from a representative sample.
- Condition specimens at **27 °C ± 2 °C** and **50 % ± 5 % RH** for **24 hours**.
- Record initial mass **M₁** to the nearest **0.1 g**.

**Step 2 — Furnace Stabilization:**
- Heat the electric tubular furnace to **750 °C ± 5 °C**.
- Allow stabilization for at least **15 minutes** at the set-point.

**Step 3 — Specimen Insertion:**
- Place specimen in the holder and insert it into the furnace center.
- Start the timer (t = 0).

**Step 4 — Continuous Monitoring (for 20 minutes or until flaming ceases + 2 min, whichever is longer):**
- Record furnace air temperature every **15 seconds** via Type-K thermocouple.
- Note the **time of any sustained flaming** (flame ≥ 5 s).
- Record final temperature **T_f**.

**Step 5 — Post-Test:**
- Cool specimen in a desiccator; record final mass **M₂**.
- Calculate ΔT and % mass loss.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **must-memorize summary** for KTU board examinations. Use `\vert` notation to avoid markdown table breakage.

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Pass Criterion |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Mean Furnace Temperature Rise | $\Delta T \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(T_{i} - T_{set}\right)$ | °C | $\Delta T \le 50$ °C |
| 2 | Percentage Mass Loss | $\%M \;=\; \dfrac{(M_{1}-M_{2})}{M_{1}} \times 100$ | % | $\%M \le 50\,\%$ |
| 3 | Average Flame Duration | $\bar{t}_{f} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum t_{flame,i}}{n}$ | s | No sustained flame |
| 4 | Specific Heat Capacity Balance (furnace air) | $Q_{released} \;=\; m_{air}\,c_{p,air}\,\Delta T$ | kJ | Diagnostic only |
| 5 | Apparent Density | $\rho \;=\; \dfrac{M_{1}}{V_{specimen}}$ | kg/m³ | $\rho \ge 300$ typical |

Where:
- $N$ = number of temperature readings during exposure.
- $T_{set} = 750$ °C.
- $M_1$ = initial conditioned mass; $M_2$ = final cooled mass.
- $V_{specimen} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}\,h \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.045)^{2}(0.050)\;\approx\;7.95\times10^{-5}\;m^{3}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Material Selection for Fire Compartmentation:** Confirms whether gypsum boards, calcium silicate boards, AAC blocks, mineral wool qualify as non-combustible linings.
2. **Code Compliance under NBC 2016:** Buildings exceeding 15 m height mandate non-combustible insulation in certain locations — proof requires IS 3808 certificate.
3. **Insurance & Risk Engineering:** Tariff structures for fire-insurance premiums use the non-combustibility classification as a key rating input.
4. **Tunnel & Metro Infrastructure:** Used for verifying fire-board linings on emergency egress corridors.
5. **Comparative Benchmarking:** Pairs with **IS 3614 (Surface Spread of Flame)** to give a complete reaction-to-fire profile.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mass-Loss Percentage

Starting from the fundamental definition of fractional mass change:

$$\Delta M \;=\; M_{1} - M_{2}$$

Normalizing by the original mass to get a dimensionless ratio:

$$\eta_{mass} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta M}{M_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{M_{1}-M_{2}}{M_{1}}$$

Converting to a percentage by multiplying by 100:

$$\%M \;=\; \eta_{mass}\times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{M_{1}-M_{2}}{M_{1}}\times 100$$

**Logic of the conversion:** The percentage expression scales a dimensionless fraction into the 0–100 range so that classification thresholds (e.g., 50 %) are intuitive. The denominator is the initial conditioned mass to ensure reproducibility regardless of specimen heterogeneity.

## 3.2 Derivation of Mean Furnace Temperature Rise

The furnace temperature is sampled at equal intervals of **Δt = 15 s** for a total exposure of **20 min = 1200 s**, giving:

$$N \;=\; \dfrac{T_{total}}{\Delta t} \;=\; \dfrac{1200}{15} \;=\; 80\;\text{readings}$$

The instantaneous deviation from set-point at each reading:

$$\delta T_i \;=\; T_i - T_{set} \;=\; T_i - 750\;\;°C$$

Averaging all deviations gives the BIS-defined mean rise:

$$\Delta T_{mean} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\delta T_i \;=\; \dfrac{1}{80}\sum_{i=1}^{80}\left(T_i-750\right)$$

$$\Delta T_{mean} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}T_i\right) - 750$$

**Logic of the conversion:** Pulling the constant 750 outside the summation is valid because subtracting a constant N times divides by N, returning that same constant. This collapses the form into a clean *average temperature minus set-point* identity that examiners frequently test.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Given:**
- $M_1 = 142.6$ g, $M_2 = 138.9$ g.
- 80 readings of furnace temperature with mean $\bar{T} = 763.4$ °C.
- Flame observed for 3 s only (no sustained flaming).

**Step (a) — Mass loss:**

$$\%M \;=\; \dfrac{142.6-138.9}{142.6}\times 100$$

$$\%M \;=\; \dfrac{3.7}{142.6}\times 100$$

$$\%M \;\approx\; 2.595\,\%$$

**Step (b) — Mean ΔT:**

$$\Delta T_{mean} \;=\; 763.4 - 750 \;=\; 13.4\;\;°C$$

**Step (c) — Decision:**

| Criterion | Observed | Limit | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sustained flame (>5 s) | 3 s | None | **PASS** |
| $\Delta T_{mean}$ | 13.4 °C | $\le$ 50 °C | **PASS** |
| $\%M$ | 2.60 % | $\le$ 50 % | **PASS** |

**Verdict:** Material classified as **NON-COMBUSTIBLE** per **IS 3808**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

The classification logic may be written as a Boolean function:

$$\text{Class}_{\text{NC}} \;=\; \left(\Delta T_{mean}\le 50\right)\;\land\;\left(\%M\le 50\right)\;\land\;\left(t_{flame}\le 5\text{ s}\right)$$

If the conjunction is **TRUE**, the material is **non-combustible (Class 1)**; otherwise it is **combustible** and further tested under **IS 3614 / IS 12777** for spread-of-flame and fire-resistance ratings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Prep: 5 x Dia 45mm x H 50mm cylinders] --> B[Conditioning: 27 deg C, 50% RH, 24 hrs]
    B --> C[Weigh: Record M1 to 0.1 g]
    C --> D[Furnace Stabilize at 750 deg C for 15 min]
    D --> E[Insert Specimen via Holder]
    E --> F[Start Timer t = 0]
    F --> G[Log Furnace Temp every 15 s via Type K TC]
    G --> H{Any Sustained Flame >= 5 s?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Record tflame, mark FAIL flag]
    H -- No --> J[Continue to 20 min]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Remove Specimen, Cool in Desiccator]
    K --> L[Weigh: Record M2]
    L --> M[Compute dTmean and %M]
    M --> N{All 3 Criteria Met?}
    N -- Yes --> O[CLASSIFY: NON-COMBUSTIBLE per IS 3808]
    N -- No --> P[CLASSIFY: COMBUSTIBLE - Further Tests under IS 3614]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Apparatus Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HeatingModule[Heating Module]
        A1[Variable Transformer] --> A2[Electric Tubular Furnace]
        A2 --> A3[Refractory Lining]
    end
    subgraph SpecimenModule[Specimen Module]
        B1[Stainless Steel Holder] --> B2[Specimen Dia 45 x H 50 mm]
        B2 --> B3[Insertion Rod from Bottom]
    end
    subgraph SensingModule[Sensing Module]
        C1[Type K Thermocouple in Furnace] --> C2[Data Logger 15 s interval]
        C3[Flame Visual Observation Window] --> C2
    end
    subgraph ControlModule[Control Module]
        D1[PID Temperature Controller] --> A1
        D2[Operator Console] --> D1
        D2 --> C2
    end
    A3 --- B1
    A3 --- C1
```

## 4.3 Process Sequence Diagram — Material Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Material Sample] --> X2{IS 3808 Non-Combustibility}
    X2 -- NC --> Y1[Class 1 Non-Combustible]
    X2 -- Combustible --> X3{IS 3614 Surface Spread}
    X3 --> X4{IS 12777 Fire Resistance Rating}
    X4 --> Y2[Class 2 to 4 Combustible with Rating]
    Y1 --> Z[Permitted in NBC 2016 Fire-Rated Construction]
    Y2 --> Z
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the temperature and duration of exposure prescribed by IS 3808 for the non-combustibility test of building materials.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Furnace set-point temperature: **750 °C ± 5 °C** (1 mark).
2. Specimen exposure duration: up to **20 minutes** (or until flaming ceases + 2 min, whichever is greater) (1 mark).
3. Specimen geometry: cylindrical **Ø 45 mm × 50 mm height**, conditioned at **27 °C ± 2 °C, 50 % ± 5 % RH for 24 h** (1 mark).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the three criteria used by BIS to declare a material "non-combustible".*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **No sustained flaming** during the test (flame duration < 5 s) (1 mark).
2. **Mean furnace temperature rise (ΔT_mean)** shall not exceed **50 °C** above the 750 °C set-point (1 mark).
3. **Mass loss of specimen** shall not exceed **50 %** of its original conditioned mass (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> * (i) Students often forget to specify the **conditioning parameters** (27 °C / 50 % RH / 24 h) — this alone costs **1 mark** consistently.
> * (ii) Do not confuse IS 3808 (non-combustibility) with IS 3614 (surface spread of flame) — they are **complementary but distinct** tests. Mixing them up attracts a **2-mark penalty**.
> * (iii) Always report the **three classification criteria together** (flame + ΔT + %M). A partial answer listing only one criterion is graded at most **3/10** by typical KTU examiners.
> * (iv) For numerical sub-parts, always carry **units** to the final answer — losing units costs **0.5–1 mark**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **apparatus and procedure** prescribed in IS 3808 for determining the non-combustibility of a building material. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A specimen tested in IS 3808 gave the following data:
- Initial mass $M_1 = 158.2$ g, final mass $M_2 = 152.7$ g.
- Mean furnace temperature during 20-minute exposure $\bar{T} = 779.6$ °C.
- A small flame was observed for 4 seconds only.

Determine whether the material qualifies as non-combustible per BIS. Justify with calculations. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Apparatus and Procedure — 7 marks**

1. **Electric tubular furnace** with refractory lining capable of maintaining **750 °C ± 5 °C**, equipped with a **PID controller** and a viewing window for flame observation (1 mark).
2. **Specimen holder** of stainless steel to position a cylindrical specimen of **Ø 45 mm × 50 mm** at the geometric center of the furnace (1 mark).
3. **Type-K thermocouple** inserted into the furnace air space, connected to a **data logger** recording temperature every **15 seconds** (1 mark).
4. **Variable transformer** for power regulation to the heating coil (0.5 mark).
5. **Conditioning chamber** maintaining **27 °C ± 2 °C and 50 % ± 5 % RH** for 24 h pre-conditioning (0.5 mark).
6. **Analytical balance** readable to **0.1 g** (0.5 mark).
7. **Procedure:** condition specimens → weigh → stabilize furnace at 750 °C for 15 min → insert specimen → log temperature and observe flame for 20 min → cool and reweigh → compute ΔT_mean and %M → apply classification criteria (1.5 marks).

**(b) Numerical Decision — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Mass loss:

$$\%M \;=\; \dfrac{158.2-152.7}{158.2}\times 100$$

$$\%M \;=\; \dfrac{5.5}{158.2}\times 100 \;\approx\; 3.477\,\%$$

Step 2 — Mean temperature rise:

$$\Delta T_{mean} \;=\; 779.6 - 750 \;=\; 29.6\;\;°C$$

Step 3 — Flame duration = 4 s (no sustained flame).

Step 4 — Decision matrix:

| Criterion | Observed | BIS Limit | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sustained flame | 4 s | < 5 s | **PASS** |
| $\Delta T_{mean}$ | 29.6 °C | ≤ 50 °C | **PASS** |
| $\%M$ | 3.48 % | ≤ 50 % | **PASS** |

**Verdict:** All three criteria are satisfied ⇒ material is classified as **NON-COMBUSTIBLE (Class 1)** per **IS 3808**.

*(Marks: 2 for Step 1, 2 for Step 2, 1 for flame check, 2 for verdict + tabulation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, classification criteria, and significance** of the Non-Combustibility Test as per BIS. Compare it briefly with **IS 3614 (Surface Spread of Flame)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Two materials **A** and **B** were tested as per IS 3808. Tabulate their data and decide which qualifies as non-combustible:

| Parameter | Material A | Material B |
|---|---|---|
| $M_1$ (g) | 142.0 | 165.0 |
| $M_2$ (g) | 78.5 | 161.2 |
| $\bar{T}$ (°C) | 815.0 | 758.3 |
| Flame duration (s) | 12 | 0 |

*(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Principle, Criteria & Significance — 7 marks**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** The test exposes a small specimen to **750 °C** in an electric furnace; combustion of the specimen would release chemical enthalpy, raising the furnace temperature and causing measurable mass loss. A **non-combustible** material neither flames nor contributes significant heat/mass.
2. **Classification criteria (2 marks):** No sustained flame (≥5 s); ΔT_mean ≤ 50 °C; %M ≤ 50 %. If all three are satisfied → **Class 1 non-combustible**.
3. **Significance (1 mark):** Required for compliance with **NBC 2016, Part 4**, for fire-rated construction, insurance rating, and material selection in high-rise and assembly buildings.
4. **Comparison with IS 3614 (2 marks):**
   - IS 3808 tests **whether** a material burns at all (yes/no).
   - IS 3614 measures **how rapidly flame spreads across the surface** once ignited (rate-based classification).

**(b) Numerical Decision — 7 marks**

**Material A:**
- $\%M_A = \dfrac{142.0 - 78.5}{142.0}\times 100 = \dfrac{63.5}{142.0}\times 100 \approx 44.72\,\%$
- $\Delta T_A = 815.0 - 750 = 65.0\;\;°C$
- Flame = 12 s (sustained).

**Material B:**
- $\%M_B = \dfrac{165.0 - 161.2}{165.0}\times 100 = \dfrac{3.8}{165.0}\times 100 \approx 2.30\,\%$
- $\Delta T_B = 758.3 - 750 = 8.3\;\;°C$
- Flame = 0 s.

**Decision matrix:**

| Criterion | Limit | Material A | Material B |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta T_{mean} \le 50\,°C$ | 50 °C | 65 °C — **FAIL** | 8.3 °C — **PASS** |
| $\%M \le 50\,\%$ | 50 % | 44.7 % — PASS | 2.3 % — PASS |
| Sustained flame < 5 s | < 5 s | 12 s — **FAIL** | 0 s — PASS |

**Verdict:** **Material A is COMBUSTIBLE** (fails ΔT and flame criteria). **Material B is NON-COMBUSTIBLE** (passes all three).

*(Marks: 2 each for A & B mass loss, 1 each for ΔT, 1 each for flame check, 1 each for final verdict.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Standard:** **IS 3808** — Non-combustibility test for building materials (BIS).
- [ ] **Furnace temperature:** **750 °C ± 5 °C**; duration **20 min**.
- [ ] **Specimen:** Cylindrical **Ø 45 × 50 mm**; condition at **27 °C / 50 % RH / 24 h**.
- [ ] **Three concurrent criteria:** no sustained flame ≥ 5 s, **ΔT_mean ≤ 50 °C**, **% mass loss ≤ 50 %**.
- [ ] **Thermocouple:** **Type K**, logging every **15 s** (80 readings in 20 min).
- [ ] **Classification output:** **Class 1 Non-Combustible** or **Combustible (further IS 3614 / IS 12777 testing)**.
- [ ] **Formulas to memorize:**
  - $\%M = \dfrac{M_1 - M_2}{M_1}\times 100$
  - $\Delta T_{mean} = \bar{T} - 750$
- [ ] **Complementary tests:** IS 3614 (surface spread of flame), IS 12777 (fire resistance).
- [ ] **Code linkage:** NBC 2016 Part 4 mandates IS 3808 certificate for non-combustible insulation in tall buildings.
- [ ] **Examiner traps:** missing conditioning parameters, mixing IS 3808 with IS 3614, omitting units, failing to tabulate decision criteria.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
