topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Behaviour and Performance of Fabricated Fire Proof Boards - Calcium Silicate, Gypsum, Vermiculite, and Perlite Boards; Behaviour of Non-Structural Materials on Fire - Plastics, Glass, Textile Fibers, Geopolymers and Other Household Materials; Classification of Materials Based on Surface Flame Spread and Their Suitability; Reparability of Fire Damaged Structures - Assessment of Fire Severity, Assessment of Damage to Concrete, Steel, Masonry and Timber Structures, Assessment of Feasibility of Repair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fabricated Fire-Proof Boards — Definition
**Fabricated fire-proof boards** are engineered, factory-manufactured composite panels composed of inorganic binders, fillers, and reinforcing fibers, designed to provide passive fire protection (PFP) by resisting flame penetration, limiting heat conduction, and maintaining structural integrity when exposed to standard cellulosic or hydrocarbon fire curves (ISO 834 / ASTM E119 / UL 263).

The four principal board families under the KTU 2024 syllabus are:
1. **Calcium Silicate Boards (CSB)** — binder of tobermorite / xonotlite crystals
2. **Gypsum Boards (GB)** — binder of calcium sulfate hemihydrate / anhydrous
3. **Vermiculite Boards** — exfoliated vermiculite + silicate binders
4. **Perlite Boards** — expanded perlite + binders (cement, silicate, gypsum)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** All four board types function by the *endothermic dehydration principle* — chemically bound water is released as steam, absorbing heat energy (~2,260 kJ/kg latent heat of vaporization) and keeping the unexposed face temperature below the critical threshold of **140 °C** for the stipulated fire-resistance rating (FRR).

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a fire-proof board as a **sponge full of locked-in water sitting between a fire and your structural steel**. When the fire arrives, the heat unlocks the water, turning it into steam that has to "spend" energy to escape. By the time the steam finishes leaking out, the fire has either died down or run out of fuel on that side. The board sacrifices its own chemical structure (it calcines, dehydrates, or sinters) to buy time for the structure behind it.

### 1.2 Non-Structural Materials on Fire — Definition
**Non-structural materials** are those that do not carry gravity loads but influence fire growth, smoke production, toxic gas emission, and compartmentation integrity. These include:
- **Plastics** (thermoplastics + thermosets) — fuels with high HRR
- **Glass** — annealed, toughened, laminated, wired
- **Textile Fibers** — natural (cotton, wool) vs. synthetic (polyester, nylon, acrylic)
- **Geopolymers** — alkali-activated aluminosilicate binders
- **Other Household Materials** — wood, paper, rubber, foams, laminates, paints

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** Non-structural materials are *fire loads* — they determine the *severity* and *duration* of a fire, whereas structural materials (concrete, steel, masonry, timber) determine whether the building *survives* the fire.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a campfire. The non-structural materials are the **wood, paper, and kindling** that decide *how big* the fire gets. The structural members (the steel I-beam, concrete column) are the **fire-pit ring** — they decide whether the fire *stays contained*. Fabricated boards are the **fire-retardant blanket** thrown over the pit if it spills.

### 1.3 Classification Based on Surface Flame Spread
**Surface flame spread** is the rate (mm/min or m/min) at which a flame front propagates across the surface of a material under standardized test conditions (ASTM E84, IS 8754, BS 476 Part 7).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **Flame Spread Index (FSI)** classifies materials as:
> - **Class A (or Class 1)** — FSI = **0 to 25** — Non-combustible / very low flame spread
> - **Class B (or Class 2)** — FSI = **26 to 75** — Low to moderate flame spread
> - **Class C (or Class 3)** — FSI = **76 to 200** — Medium flame spread
> - **Class D / E** — FSI = **> 200** — High / very high flame spread (unsuitable for most buildings)

**Suitability Rule of Thumb:** Class A and B are required for **escape routes, staircases, lobbies, and high-occupancy spaces** as per NBC 2016 (India) and IS 8754.

### 1.4 Reparability of Fire-Damaged Structures — Definition
**Reparability** is the engineering decision process that determines whether a fire-affected building can be restored to its pre-fire serviceability and safety levels, or must be partially / fully demolished and reconstructed.

The KTU 2024 syllabus breaks this into three sub-tasks:
- **Assessment of Fire Severity** — How hot and how long? (Standard time-temperature curves vs. actual burn evidence)
- **Assessment of Damage** — Per material: concrete spalling, steel yielding/distortion, masonry cracking, timber charring
- **Assessment of Feasibility of Repair** — Economic + structural + safety verdict

**Conceptual Analogy:** A post-fire assessment is like a **doctor's triage** after an accident. First you measure "how bad was the burn?" (severity), then you check each "limb" (column, beam, slab) for damage, and finally you decide whether to *heal, splint, or amputate* (repair, strengthen, or demolish).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants to Remember:**
> - Concrete: loses ~50% strength at **300 °C**, ~90% at **600 °C**
> - Steel: yields at ~**550 °C**, retains <50% strength at **600 °C**
> - Timber charring rate: **0.6–0.8 mm/min** for softwood, **0.5–0.7 mm/min** for hardwood
> - Masonry mortar: complete loss of bond by **600 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Action — Fabricated Fire-Proof Boards

All four board families share a common three-stage thermal response:

**Stage 1: Heat Absorption (25 – 200 °C)**
- Free and adsorbed moisture evaporates
- Material remains dimensionally stable
- Specific heat of the board governs heat flux absorption

**Stage 2: Chemical Transformation (200 – 600 °C)**
- Dehydration / dehydroxylation of binder
- Endothermic reaction absorbs latent heat
- Forms a porous insulating char/sintered layer
- **This is the protective stage** — board still functions

**Stage 3: Shrinkage, Cracking & Loss (600 – 1000 °C+)**
- Sintering or complete decomposition
- Shrinkage cracks open thermal short-circuits
- Board no longer offers full protection

---

### 2.2 Material-Specific Behaviour

#### 2.2.1 Calcium Silicate Boards (CSB)
- **Composition:** Lime (CaO) + Silica (SiO₂) → autoclaved → tobermorite (C₅S₆H₅) crystals
- **Density:** 200–1000 kg/m³ (low-density to high-density)
- **Operating temperature:** up to **1050 °C**
- **Mechanism:** Tobermorite loses water gradually; forms stable xonotlite phase around 700 °C
- **Key advantage:** Minimal shrinkage, no toxic emissions, dimensionally stable
- **Applications:** Steel column encasement, duct covers, ceiling membranes, fire-rated partitions
- **Limitation:** More expensive than gypsum; lower mechanical strength per unit thickness

#### 2.2.2 Gypsum Boards
- **Composition:** Calcium sulfate dihydrate (CaSO₄·2H₂O) → set into board
- **Density:** 800–1100 kg/m³
- **Operating temperature:** up to **~1200 °C** short-term; sustained exposure ~**800 °C**
- **Mechanism:** Dehydration reaction:
  $$\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\uparrow$$
- About **21% of mass** is chemically bound water released as steam
- **Types:** Regular (GRG), Type X (glass-fiber reinforced for FRR), Type C (silicone-treated for moisture)
- **Limitation:** Loses all strength after dehydration (~95% loss at 800 °C) — the calcined CaSO₄ becomes crumbly

#### 2.2.3 Vermiculite Boards
- **Composition:** Exfoliated vermiculite (hydrated Mg-Al-Fe silicate) + silicate binder
- **Density:** 350–800 kg/m³
- **Operating temperature:** up to **1100 °C**
- **Mechanism:** Vermiculite exfoliation provides layered "accordion" microstructure; water between silicate sheets is released progressively
- **Key advantage:** Excellent thermal insulation; used in firestop systems and as a component of spray-applied fire-resistive materials (SFRM)
- **Limitation:** Sensitive to high humidity (re-adsorbs moisture)

#### 2.2.4 Perlite Boards
- **Composition:** Expanded perlite (volcanic glass, 70–75% SiO₂) + gypsum/cement/silicate binder
- **Density:** 200–500 kg/m³ (very lightweight)
- **Operating temperature:** up to **~1000 °C** (binder dependent)
- **Mechanism:** Cellular glass structure traps air; binder (e.g., gypsum) provides dehydration cooling
- **Key advantage:** Ultra-lightweight, good for ceiling applications where dead load is critical
- **Limitation:** Lower mechanical strength; friable surface

---

### 2.3 Behaviour of Non-Structural Materials on Fire

#### 2.3.1 Plastics
- **Thermoplastics** (PE, PP, PVC, PS) → melt at 100–200 °C → flow and spread fire
- **Thermosets** (PU, epoxy, phenolic) → char formation; PU releases HCN, isocyanates
- **PVC:** releases **HCl gas** above 200 °C — highly corrosive to steel
- **Heat Release Rate (HRR):** Polyurethane foam ≈ **1500–2500 kW/m²** (extremely dangerous)
- **Smoke Production:** Plastics dominate — up to **80% of fire deaths** involve plastic-combustion toxicants

#### 2.3.2 Glass
- **Annealed glass:** shatters at thermal gradient of ~**30–40 °C** across pane (typical failure ~250 °C)
- **Toughened glass:** better thermal gradient resistance (~**200 °C** gradient); small granular fragments on failure
- **Laminated glass:** PVB/SGP interlayer holds shards; longer integrity
- **Wired glass:** holds fragments; still in use historically for fire-rated glazing
- **Ceramic / Borosilicate glass:** maintains integrity for **60–120 min** in special fire-rated assemblies

#### 2.3.3 Textile Fibers
- **Natural:**
  - Cotton: ignites ~**400 °C**; rapid flame spread
  - Wool: ignites ~**600 °C**; self-extinguishing; chars
- **Synthetic:**
  - Polyester: melts at ~**260 °C**, ignites ~**450 °C** — *sticky melt-drip fire spread*
  - Nylon: melts ~**220 °C**, ignites ~**530 °C**
  - Acrylic: melts ~**230 °C**, ignites ~**560 °C** — releases HCN
- **Critical hazard:** synthetic textile melt-drips act as secondary ignition sources

#### 2.3.4 Geopolymers
- **Composition:** Alkali-activated aluminosilicate (e.g., fly ash + NaOH/KOH solution)
- **Fire behaviour:** Excellent — no calcium hydroxide (which decomposes at 580 °C in OPC concrete)
- **Residual strength at 800 °C:** retains **60–80%** of original strength (vs. OPC concrete 20–40%)
- **Mechanism:** Amorphous to crystalline phase transformation at high temperature; lower thermal conductivity

#### 2.3.5 Other Household Materials
- **Wood:** Chars at ~**250 °C**, self-ignites ~**300–400 °C**; char layer provides some insulation (charring rate formula given below)
- **Paper:** Ignition ~**230 °C**; complete combustion by **500 °C**
- **Rubber:** Releases dense black smoke + SO₂
- **Paints/Coatings:** Oil-based paints accelerate surface flame spread; intumescent paints expand to protect substrate

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for the KTU 2024 Exam** — memorize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charring depth of timber (idealized) | $d_c = \beta \cdot t$ | mm; mm/min, min | Section loss in timber assessment |
| 2 | Standard fire temperature (ISO 834) | $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + T_0$ | °C; minutes | Fire severity reference |
| 3 | Standard fire temperature (ASTM E119 / same as ISO 834) | $T = 750[1 - e^{-0.49 \sqrt{t}}] + T_0 + 20$ | °C; hr (approx.) | Alternate form |
| 4 | Equivalent fire severity (time equivalence, $t_e$) | $t_e = \sum \frac{A_i}{A_{ref}}$ where $A$ = area under time-temp curve | min | Comparing real fires to standard |
| 5 | Heat release rate (HRR), simplified | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi$ | kW; kg/s, MJ/kg, – | Fire load estimation |
| 6 | Fire load density | $q = \frac{\sum (M_i \cdot H_i)}{A_{floor}}$ | MJ/m² | Compartmental design |
| 7 | Effective residual cross-section of timber | $A_{eff} = b \cdot (h - d_c)$ | mm² | Repaired timber capacity |
| 8 | Concrete strength reduction factor (Eurocode empirical) | $k_c(\theta) = f_{c,\theta} / f_{ck}$ | dimensionless | Damage assessment |
| 9 | Steel critical temperature (load-ratio method) | $\theta_{cr} = 39.19 \ln \left[ \frac{1}{0.9674 \mu_0^{3.833}} - 1 \right] + 482$ | °C; μ₀ = load ratio | Repaired steel members |
| 10 | Fire-resistance rating (F-factor for boards) | $F = k \cdot \tau^{0.5}$ or lookup from BS 476 Pt 22 | min; W/m·K, s | Board thickness selection |
| 11 | Heat conduction through board (1-D steady) | $q'' = \frac{k}{\ell}(T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | W/m²; W/m·K, m, °C | Board thermal performance |
| 12 | Flame Spread Index (FSI) ratio | $FSI = \frac{L_{sample}}{L_{cement-asb}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | Classification |
| 13 | Mass loss in gypsum board | $\Delta m = 0.21 \cdot m_{0}$ | kg; kg | Dehydration water release |
| 14 | Smoke yield (specific) | $S_y = \frac{m_{smoke}}{m_{fuel,consumed}}$ | kg/kg | Toxicity assessment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** $T_0$ in standard fire equations = initial ambient temperature = **20 °C** (ISO) or **10 °C** (some older codes). Always specify in solutions.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Real Practice

- **Boards** protect structural steel and concrete in tunnels, high-rises, offshore platforms, and petrochemical facilities — extending FRR to 1–4 hours.
- **Plastic fire behaviour** drives material selection in aircraft, trains, and ships (low-FSI / low-smoke / low-toxicity, i.e., LSZH cables, fire-retardant PU foams).
- **Surface flame spread classification** governs interior finish regulations (NBC 2016 Part 4 — Fire and Life Safety).
- **Reparability assessment** is a multi-million-rupee decision per building — wrong assessment = either wasteful demolition or unsafe re-occupancy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Standard Fire Time–Temperature Curve (ISO 834)

The ISO 834 standard fire curve represents the **gas-phase temperature** in a fully developed compartment fire under standard test conditions. We derive the standard equation step-by-step from first principles of heat transfer correlation.

**Step 1 — Empirical Observation**
Test data shows that furnace temperature rises rapidly in the first 30 min, then the rate of rise slows logarithmically.

**Step 2 — Curve Fit Assumption**
Assume a logarithmic form:
$$T(t) = A \log_{10}(B t + 1) + T_0$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants to be determined.

**Step 3 — Boundary Conditions**
At $t = 0$: $T = T_0$ (ambient). This is automatically satisfied by the +1 inside the logarithm.

At $t = 30 \text{ min}$: $T = 842 \text{ °C}$ (from test data with $T_0 = 20$ °C).
At $t = 60 \text{ min}$: $T = 945 \text{ °C}$.
At $t = 120 \text{ min}$: $T = 1049 \text{ °C}$.

**Step 4 — Solve for A and B**
$$T - T_0 = A \log_{10}(B t + 1)$$

At $t = 30$, $T - T_0 = 822$:
$$822 = A \log_{10}(30 B + 1) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

At $t = 60$, $T - T_0 = 925$:
$$925 = A \log_{10}(60 B + 1) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5 — Division Eliminates A**
$$\frac{925}{822} = \frac{\log_{10}(60 B + 1)}{\log_{10}(30 B + 1)}$$

$$1.1253 = \frac{\log_{10}(60 B + 1)}{\log_{10}(30 B + 1)}$$

Try $B = 8$:
- $60B + 1 = 481$; $\log_{10}(481) = 2.682$
- $30B + 1 = 241$; $\log_{10}(241) = 2.382$
- Ratio = $2.682 / 2.382 = 1.126$ ✓

So $B = 8$ and $A = 822 / 2.382 = 345.0$.

**Step 6 — Final Standard Equation**
$$\boxed{T(t) = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + T_0 \quad [\text{°C},\ t \text{ in minutes}]}$$

**Step 7 — Verification at 120 min**
$$T = 345 \log_{10}(961) + 20 = 345 (2.983) + 20 = 1029 + 20 = 1049 \text{ °C} \ \checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Fire Severity (Time-Equivalence Method, $t_e$)

The **time-equivalent concept** (also called the "equal-area concept") converts a real (non-standard) fire into an equivalent duration of the standard fire that produces the same thermal damage.

**Step 1 — Define Damage Integral**
The damage done to a member is assumed proportional to the area under the time–temperature curve above a reference temperature $T_{ref}$ (often 300 °C for concrete, 500 °C for steel).

$$D = \int_{0}^{t_{real}} \max[0, T(\tau) - T_{ref}]\, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Equal-Area Condition**
Set the area under the real fire curve above $T_{ref}$ equal to the area under the standard curve:
$$\int_{0}^{t_e} [T_{std}(\tau) - T_{ref}]\, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t_{real}} \max[0, T_{real}(\tau) - T_{ref}]\, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t_e$ Numerically**
Substitute ISO 834 form and integrate:
$$\int_{0}^{t_e} [345 \log_{10}(8\tau + 1) - 300]\, d\tau = A_{real}$$

Let $u = 8\tau + 1$, $du = 8\, d\tau$:
$$I = \int \left[345 \log_{10} u - 300 \right] \frac{du}{8}$$
$$I = \frac{345}{8} \int \log_{10} u \, du - \frac{300}{8} \int du$$
$$I = \frac{345}{8} \left[\frac{u}{\ln 10}(\ln u - 1)\right] - 37.5 u + C$$

Numerically evaluating between limits gives $t_e$, which is then used as the **effective standard fire duration** for structural assessment.

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Matrix — Board Selection for FRR

Given a structural steel column requiring **120 minutes of FRR**, select the most appropriate board:

| Criterion | Calcium Silicate | Gypsum (Type X) | Vermiculite | Perlite |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Required thickness (typ., 120 min) | 25–35 mm | 2 × 15.9 mm layers | 30–40 mm | 35–50 mm |
| Density (kg/m³) | 450–900 | 800–950 | 500–700 | 250–400 |
| Cost (₹/m², indicative) | High | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Mechanical strength | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Max service °C | 1050 | 800 (then degrades) | 1100 | 1000 |
| Moisture resistance | Excellent | Poor (Type C improved) | Moderate | Good |
| Suitability for column encasement | Excellent | Good (multi-layer) | Good (spray) | Limited |

**Decision rule for KTU exam answers:**
- High-rise / industrial / humid: **Calcium Silicate**
- Cost-driven / dry interiors: **Gypsum (Type X)**
- Spray-applied / irregular surfaces: **Vermiculite (SFRM)**
- Lightweight ceilings only: **Perlite**

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Timber Char Loss

**Problem:** A timber beam (Pine, softwood) of original cross-section 150 mm × 250 mm was exposed to a fire for 45 minutes. Estimate the effective residual cross-section and percentage strength loss. Use charring rate $\beta = 0.65 \text{ mm/min}$ for softwood.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate char depth on each exposed face.
$$d_c = \beta \cdot t = 0.65 \times 45 = 29.25 \text{ mm} \approx 30 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2: Effective cross-section.
$$b_{eff} = 150 - 2(30) = 90 \text{ mm}$$
$$h_{eff} = 250 - 2(30) = 190 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3: Effective area.
$$A_{eff} = 90 \times 190 = 17{,}100 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 4: Original area.
$$A_{orig} = 150 \times 250 = 37{,}500 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 5: Area ratio (proxy for bending capacity in rectangular timber).
$$\frac{A_{eff}}{A_{orig}} = \frac{17{,}100}{37{,}500} = 0.456 = 45.6\%$$

**Result:** The beam retains ~45.6% of original cross-section, hence ~45.6% of original bending capacity (timber strength is governed by the un-charred core, which behaves nearly like the original wood).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The char layer itself is typically neglected as a structural contributor because it is friable and at low density. Additionally, code practice (Eurocode 5) defines an "effective charring depth" of $d_{ef} = d_c + k_0 d_0$ where $d_0 = 7$ mm and $k_0$ depends on fire duration (1.0 for $t \geq 20$ min).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thermal Response Stage Diagram — Fire-Proof Board Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Exposure Begins T approx 25 C] --> B[Stage 1: Moisture Loss 25 to 200 C]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Chemical Dehydration 200 to 600 C]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Sintering and Shrinkage 600 to 1000 C plus]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Loss of Integrity beyond 1000 C]
    B --> F[Board stays stable no phase change]
    C --> G[Endothermic reaction absorbs heat water released as steam]
    D --> H[Cracks and gaps form thermal short circuits]
    E --> I[Board no longer protects substrate]
    F --> J[Substrate protected]
    G --> J
    H --> K[Substrate temperature rises]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Fire Resistance Rating Achieved]
    K --> M[Fire Resistance Failed]
```

### 4.2 Fabricated Board Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BOARDS[Fire Proof Boards]
        CSB[Calcium Silicate Board]
        GYP[Gypsum Board Type X]
        VRM[Vermiculite Board]
        PRL[Perlite Board]
    end
    CSB -->|Binder Tobermorite| CSBM[High Density 450 to 900 kg per m3]
    GYP -->|Binder CaSO4 2H2O| GYPM[Density 800 to 1100 kg per m3]
    VRM -->|Binder Silicate plus Vermiculite| VRMM[Density 350 to 800 kg per m3]
    PRL -->|Binder Gypsum plus Perlite| PRLM[Low Density 200 to 500 kg per m3]
    CSBM --> CSBAPP[Steel Column Encasement Duct Cover]
    GYPM --> GYPAPP[Partition Wall Ceiling Membrane]
    VRMM --> VRMAPP[Spray Applied Fireproofing Firestop]
    PRLM --> PRLAPP[Lightweight Ceiling Tile]
```

### 4.3 Non-Structural Material Fire Behaviour Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NSM[Non Structural Materials in Fire] --> PLA[Plastics]
    NSM --> GLS[Glass]
    NSM --> TXT[Textile Fibers]
    NSM --> GEO[Geopolymers]
    NSM --> OTH[Other Households]
    PLA --> P1[Thermoplastics melt 100 to 200 C drip spread]
    PLA --> P2[Thermosets char PU releases HCN]
    PLA --> P3[PVC releases HCl above 200 C]
    GLS --> G1[Annealed shatters 250 C]
    GLS --> G2[Toughened granular 200 C gradient]
    GLS --> G3[Ceramic Borosilicate 60 to 120 min integrity]
    TXT --> T1[Natural cotton wool char melt]
    TXT --> T2[Synthetic polyester nylon melt drip 220 to 260 C]
    GEO --> GE1[Retains 60 to 80 percent strength at 800 C]
    OTH --> O1[Wood char 250 C self ignite 300 to 400 C]
    OTH --> O2[Paper ignite 230 C combust 500 C]
    OTH --> O3[Rubber releases SO2 black smoke]
```

### 4.4 Reparability Assessment Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Incident Reported] --> B[Visual Site Survey and Documentation]
    B --> C[Estimate Fire Severity via Time Temperature Curve]
    C --> D[Material Wise Damage Assessment]
    D --> E1[Concrete Spalling Cracks Cover Loss]
    D --> E2[Steel Yielding Distortion Buckling]
    D --> E3[Masonry Cracking Mortar Loss]
    D --> E4[Timber Char Depth Section Loss]
    E1 --> F[Compile Damage Report Material Specific]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Feasibility Analysis]
    G --> G1[Structural Adequacy Check]
    G --> G2[Economic Repair vs Rebuild]
    G --> G3[Safety and Code Compliance]
    G1 --> H{Decision}
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H -->|Repair| I[Repair and Strengthen per IS and ACI Guidelines]
    H -->|Partial| J[Selective Demolition plus Reconstruction]
    H -->|Total| K[Full Demolition and Rebuild]
```

### 4.5 Surface Flame Spread Classification (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Material Tested ASTM E84 Tunnel Test] --> B{Flame Spread Index FSI}
    B -->|0 to 25| C[Class A or 1 Non Combustible Low Spread]
    B -->|26 to 75| D[Class B or 2 Low to Moderate]
    B -->|76 to 200| E[Class C or 3 Medium Spread]
    B -->|above 200| F[Class D or E High Spread Unsuitable]
    C --> G[Suitable Escape Routes Hospitals Schools]
    D --> H[Suitable General Offices Commercial]
    E --> I[Limited Use Restricted Areas]
    F --> J[Not Permitted in Buildings]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Explain the mechanism by which gypsum boards provide fire resistance to structural members.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Gypsum boards (CaSO₄·2H₂O) provide fire resistance via the **endothermic dehydration reaction**. When exposed to fire, the chemically bound water (~21% by mass) is released as steam at temperatures between 100 °C and 200 °C. This phase change absorbs ~2,260 kJ/kg of latent heat per kg of water, slowing temperature rise on the unexposed face. The calcined CaSO₄ residue then acts as a porous insulating char. Thus, gypsum boards delay heat transmission to the protected structural member, contributing to the required fire resistance rating. (1 mark each for: bound water content, endothermic mechanism with energy value, role of calcined residue).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
*Define Flame Spread Index (FSI). State the classification of materials as per ASTM E84.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Flame Spread Index (FSI)** is a dimensionless number representing the rate at which flame propagates along the surface of a material under the ASTM E84 tunnel test, expressed as a percentage of the calibrated cement-asbestos board reference (FSI = 100).
Classification: (1 mark)
- **Class A (Class 1):** FSI 0–25 — non-combustible / very low spread
- **Class B (Class 2):** FSI 26–75 — low to moderate spread
- **Class C (Class 3):** FSI 76–200 — medium spread
- **Class D/E:** FSI > 200 — high / very high (unsuitable for most buildings). (1 mark each for two classes, plus the definition)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**(a)** Compare the behaviour and performance of **Calcium Silicate, Gypsum, Vermiculite, and Perlite boards** under fire conditions. Discuss their composition, density, temperature limits, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **behaviour of plastics and glass** when exposed to fire. How do thermoplastic and thermoset plastics differ in their fire response? Explain with reference to typical temperatures and hazardous emissions. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison of Fabricated Fire-Proof Boards (7 marks)**

| Property | Calcium Silicate | Gypsum (Type X) | Vermiculite | Perlite |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Lime + silica → tobermorite crystals | CaSO₄·2H₂O (set calcium sulfate) | Exfoliated vermiculite + silicate binder | Expanded perlite + gypsum/cement binder |
| **Density** | 450–900 kg/m³ | 800–1100 kg/m³ | 350–800 kg/m³ | 200–500 kg/m³ |
| **Mechanism** | Dehydration of tobermorite to xonotlite; minimal shrinkage | Endothermic release of 21% bound water | Layered silicate dehydroxylation; cellular insulating char | Cellular glass structure + binder dehydration |
| **Max service T** | 1050 °C | ~800 °C (then degrades to powder) | 1100 °C | 1000 °C |
| **FRR (typical 25 mm)** | 90–120 min | 60–90 min (single layer); 120 min (double) | 60–120 min (board or spray) | 45–90 min |
| **Applications** | Steel column/beam encasement; offshore; petrochemical; high-humidity zones | Drywall partitions, ceiling membranes, residential/commercial | Firestop collars, spray-applied SFRM on steel | Lightweight ceiling panels, sandwich panels |
| **Cost (relative)** | High | Low | Medium | Medium |

(1 mark for tabulated comparison; 1 mark for mechanism; 1 mark for density & temperature; 1 mark for applications)

**Best choice for a 120-minute FRR steel column in humid coastal environment:** Calcium Silicate (low shrinkage, moisture resistance, sustained high-T performance). (1 mark for justified selection)

---

**(b) Plastics and Glass Behaviour in Fire (7 marks)**

**Plastics — Thermoplastic vs Thermoset:**

**Thermoplastics (PE, PP, PVC, PS):**
- Soften/melt at 100–200 °C, then flow as a flammable liquid → *melt-drip fire spread*
- PVC releases **HCl gas** above 200 °C (corrosive to steel, toxic)
- Polystyrene (PS) yields dense black smoke + styrene monomer
- High **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** — polyurethane foam reaches 1500–2500 kW/m²

**Thermosets (PU, epoxy, phenolic, melamine):**
- Do not melt; undergo **pyrolysis and charring** at 300–500 °C
- Polyurethane releases **HCN, isocyanates** (highly toxic)
- Phenolic resins char with low smoke — preferred for FR applications
- Epoxy loses 50% strength at ~120 °C (T_g transition)

| Aspect | Thermoplastic | Thermoset |
|---|---|---|
| Phase change | Melts (100–250 °C) | Chars (no melt) |
| HRR | Very high | Moderate (with char) |
| Smoke | High | Variable |
| Toxicity | HCl from PVC | HCN from PU |

(2 marks for plastic behaviour)

**Glass Behaviour in Fire:**
- **Annealed glass:** Shatters at thermal gradient of ~30–40 °C; typically fails at ~250 °C; large dangerous shards.
- **Toughened (tempered) glass:** Withstands ~200 °C gradient; shatters into small harmless granules — still loses compartmentation integrity.
- **Laminated glass:** PVB/SGP interlayer holds fragments; maintains integrity ~30–60 min.
- **Wired glass:** Steel mesh holds pieces; lower optical quality; ~60 min integrity.
- **Ceramic / Borosilicate fire-rated glazing:** 60–120 min integrity + insulation; used in fire-rated doors and windows.

(2 marks for glass types & temperatures)

**Comparative suitability:**
- Escape corridors: fire-rated ceramic glass
- Internal partitions: laminated or toughened with sprinklers
- Wired glass: legacy buildings, low-hazard areas
(1 mark for suitability; 1 mark for hazardous emissions summary)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of materials based on surface flame spread** as per ASTM E84 / IS 8754. State their suitability for various building occupancies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **reparability of fire-damaged structures**. Describe the procedure for assessment of fire severity and the criteria for assessing damage to concrete, steel, masonry, and timber members. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification Based on Surface Flame Spread (7 marks)**

The **ASTM E84** (also aligned with **IS 8754** in India) **Steiner Tunnel Test** measures how far and how fast flame spreads along the underside of a horizontally mounted test specimen (600 × 9000 mm) under a controlled gas flame for 10 minutes. The result is the dimensionless **Flame Spread Index (FSI)**, normalized to:
- Red oak flooring = 100 (reference for organic materials)
- Cement-asbestos board = 0 (reference for non-combustible)

| Class | FSI Range | Description | Typical Examples | Suitable Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (or 1) | 0–25 | Non-combustible / very low | Gypsum, calcium silicate, mineral wool, fibre-cement | Escape routes, hospitals, schools, high-rise lobbies |
| B (or 2) | 26–75 | Low to moderate | Fire-retardant treated wood, FR-PVC | General offices, commercial |
| C (or 3) | 76–200 | Medium | Standard wood, melamine laminates | Restricted / service areas only |
| D / E | > 200 | High / very high | Untreated polyurethane foam, untreated plywood, most thermoplastics | Not permitted in occupied buildings |

(2 marks for description; 1 mark for FSI scale reference; 2 marks for class definitions)

**Code requirements (NBC 2016 Part 4):**
- Class A mandatory in staircases, lift lobbies, exit passageways
- Class A or B permitted in offices, hotels
- Class C only allowed in concealed service voids
(1 mark for code linkage; 1 mark for examples)

**Suitability Decision Rule:**
$$\text{Suitability} = f(\text{FSI},\ \text{Smoke Developed Index (SDI)},\ \text{Occupancy Type},\ \text{Egress Function})$$

---

**(b) Reparability of Fire-Damaged Structures (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Assessment of Fire Severity (1.5 marks):**
- Visual evidence: depth of char, color of concrete, distortion of steel
- Fire load inventory: $q = \frac{\sum M_i H_i}{A_{floor}}$ [MJ/m²]
- Equivalent fire severity: $t_e$ via equal-area method (Section 3.2 above)
- Compare to ISO 834 standard curve to estimate maximum temperature reached

**Step 2 — Damage Assessment per Material (4 marks):**

| Material | Damage Indicators | Assessment Method | Repair Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concrete** | Spalling depth, color change, cracking pattern, cover loss | Hammer sounding, UPV test, core sampling, petrography | Spalling < 25 mm cover, no through cracks → repair; deeper → strengthen |
| **Steel** | Distortion, bowing, buckling, yield-line patterns, protective coating damage | Visual + dimensional survey; hardness test; coating thickness | Distortion < L/500 or small → straighten; > critical → replace |
| **Masonry** | Mortar crumbling, crack width, brick spalling | Tap test, mortar joint inspection | Cracks < 5 mm → repoint; > 5 mm or stepped → rebuild |
| **Timber** | Char depth, residual section, member deflection | Measure char with knife, residual caliper measurement | Residual capacity > 70% of design → retain; < 70% → sister/laminate |

(1 mark for severity; 1 mark for damage indicators table; 1 mark for assessment methods; 1 mark for thresholds)

**Step 3 — Feasibility of Repair (1.5 marks):**
Three-criteria decision:
- **Structural adequacy:** Does residual capacity meet design loads at required safety factor?
- **Economic viability:** Is repair cost < 50–60% of replacement cost? (rule-of-thumb, project-specific)
- **Code compliance:** Does the repaired structure meet current code requirements (fire, seismic, accessibility)?

Verdict options:
- **Repair** — minor cosmetic + minor structural
- **Strengthen** — major structural but localized
- **Partial demolition + reconstruct**
- **Total demolition** — if overall integrity compromised or fire was a hydrocarbon pool reaching > 1100 °C for extended duration

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **confuse the dehydration temperature with the failure temperature** of gypsum boards — remember dehydration starts at ~100 °C but the board retains some insulating function up to ~800 °C.
> 2. For **timber charring**, do NOT use density of char in load calculations — it is friable and structurally useless; the un-charred core carries all loads.
> 3. For **equivalent fire severity**, always specify the **reference temperature** ($T_{ref}$) used; without it the integration limit is ambiguous and full marks will not be awarded.
> 4. Many students write "calcium silicate board is best" without justifying against **cost, density, and the specific FRR required** — always tie board selection to FRR and exposure conditions.
> 5. For **Flame Spread Index**, mention both ASTM E84 (international) and IS 8754 (Indian) to demonstrate awareness of Indian Standards (NBC 2016).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Prep):**

- [x] **Fire-proof boards work by ENDOTHERMIC DEHYDRATION** — memorise the three-stage thermal response (moisture loss → dehydration → shrinkage).
- [x] **Gypsum** releases ~21% bound water; loses strength above ~800 °C.
- [x] **Calcium Silicate** uses tobermorite; minimal shrinkage; best for humid/high-T service.
- [x] **Vermiculite** is a layered silicate — used in SFRM sprays and firestops.
- [x] **Perlite** is ultra-light; used in ceilings; limited structural role.
- [x] **Plastics = major fire load**; thermoplastics melt-drip, thermosets char; PVC→HCl, PU→HCN.
- [x] **Annealed glass** fails at ~250 °C; **ceramic glass** survives 60–120 min.
- [x] **Textile fibers:** Natural = char/ignite; Synthetic = melt-drip (220–260 °C) — secondary ignition source.
- [x] **Geopolymers** retain 60–80% strength at 800 °C (vs OPC 20–40%).
- [x] **Flame Spread Index (FSI):** Class A (0–25), B (26–75), C (76–200), D/E (>200).
- [x] **ISO 834 equation:** $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + T_0$ — $T_0 = 20$ °C standard.
- [x] **Charring rate (softwood):** 0.6–0.8 mm/min; effective charring depth uses $d_{ef} = d_c + 7$ mm.
- [x] **Concrete strength:** ~50% at 300 °C, ~90% loss at 600 °C.
- [x] **Steel critical T:** 550–600 °C under full design load.
- [x] **Repair feasibility** = structural adequacy + economic + code compliance.
- [x] Always **cite both ASTM/IS codes** and **NBC 2016** for KTU answer completeness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Board Properties — "C G V P":**
> **C**alcium silicate — **C**hemically stable to 1050 °C, **C**orrosion/moisture resistant
> **G**ypsum — **G**reat for cost, gives **G**ood 1-hr FRR in single layer Type X
> **V**ermiculite — **V**ersatile, used in **V**ermiculite spray & **V**oids (firestops)
> **P**erlite — **P**articularly **P**orous, **P**referred for **P**lasterboards and ceilings

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repair Techniques - Repair Methods to Reinforced Concrete Columns, Beams and Slabs, Repair to Steel Structural Members, Repair to Masonry Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Repair Techniques - Repair Methods to Reinforced Concrete Columns, Beams and Slabs, Repair to Steel Structural Members, Repair to Masonry Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Repair Techniques** in the context of structural fire safety refer to a systematic set of engineered interventions employed to restore the **load-carrying capacity, durability, and serviceability** of structural members that have been damaged due to fire exposure, thermal shock, spalling, corrosion, or mechanical impact. Repair methods are distinct from *strengthening* (which enhances capacity beyond the original design) — they aim to reinstate the **as-built performance** of the element using codes of practice such as **IS 456:2000**, **IS 13935:1993 (Repair and Seismic Strengthening of Buildings – Guidelines)**, **ACI 562**, and **fib Bulletin 14**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** A repair strategy is the **planned engineered response** that addresses both the **cause** (e.g., chloride-induced corrosion, fire spalling) and the **symptom** (cracks, loss of section, deflection) of structural distress, ensuring the member regains its original design intent under service and ultimate limit states.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a road pothole: you wouldn't just fill the hole with loose gravel — you'd **cut out the damaged area**, clean the edges, apply a **bonding primer**, fill with **engineered material** (asphalt mix), and compact it. Similarly, structural repair is "surgery" for buildings:

- **Diagnosis** = X-ray / scan (Non-Destructive Testing)
- **Removal of damaged material** = Cutting out the pothole
- **Surface preparation** = Cleaning edges
- **Bonding agent** = Primer
- **New material** = Asphalt mix
- **Curing & finishing** = Compaction and road-marking

If you skip a step (e.g., apply new concrete on a dusty, cracked surface), the patch *will* fail — exactly like an unprimed pothole repair.

## 1.3 Why Repairs Are Critical After Fire

| Fire-Induced Damage | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|
| Concrete spalling (cover loss ≥ 25 mm) | Exposed rebar → accelerated corrosion |
| Reduction in rebar yield strength (up to 30% at 600 °C) | Loss of flexural/capacity |
| Micro-cracking of concrete matrix | Permeability ↑, durability ↓ |
| Buckling of steel members | Loss of stability and load path |
| Loss of masonry bond | Out-of-plane failure risk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** Concrete loses **~50% of its compressive strength** at **600 °C**; steel loses **~50% of yield strength** at **~550 °C**. The repair strategy is chosen based on **residual strength assessment** (typically done via NDT — Rebound Hammer, UPV, Core Test).

## 1.4 General Repair Workflow (Applies to All Members)

1. **Damage assessment** (visual + NDT)
2. **Removal of unsound material** (chipping, hydro-demolition)
3. **Substrate preparation** (cleaning, roughening)
4. **Reinforcement treatment** (de-rusting, replacement)
5. **Application of repair material** (mortar, concrete, epoxy, FRP)
6. **Curing and quality control**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Repair Methods to Reinforced Concrete (RC) Columns

RC columns are the most critical vertical load-carrying members. Fire damage in columns typically manifests as **concrete spalling, longitudinal bar exposure/buckling, and tie/confinement failure**.

### 2.1.1 Concrete Jacketing (Most Common for Fire-Damaged Columns)

A new layer of concrete (typically **60–100 mm** thick) is added around the existing column after removing damaged cover. This increases cross-section and provides additional confinement.

**Steps:**
- Prop and relieve the load from the column.
- Chip off damaged/spalled concrete until sound substrate is exposed.
- Clean corroded rebar (wire brush / sandblasting); add new longitudinal bars if section loss > 15%.
- Weld/add new lateral ties (typically 8 mm dia. @ 100–150 mm c/c).
- Apply **bonding coat** (e.g., epoxy or cement-slatex slurry).
- Pour **micro-concrete or polymer-modified mortar** using formwork.
- Cure for **at least 7 days** (preferably 14).

### 2.1.2 Steel Jacketing

Used where column cross-section increase is restricted. Steel plates (typically **6–10 mm** thick) are welded around the column and the gap is filled with **non-shrink grout**.

**Design equation for axial capacity enhancement (IS 15988:2013 based):**

$$P_{u,\text{enhanced}} = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st} + 0.67 \cdot f_{ys} \cdot A_{sc}$$

where:
- $A_c$ = concrete area (new + existing)
- $A_{st}$ = longitudinal rebar area
- $A_{sc}$ = steel jacket area
- $f_{ys}$ = yield strength of steel jacket (typically **250–350 MPa**)

### 2.1.3 FRP (Fibre-Reinforced Polymer) Wrapping

Carbon/Glass FRP sheets are bonded with epoxy resin. Provides **confinement** and restores ductility. Most effective for **circular columns** (confinement pressure uniform).

**Confinement pressure from FRP:**

$$f_l = \frac{2 \cdot t_{frp} \cdot f_{frp}}{D}$$

where:
- $t_{frp}$ = thickness of FRP (typically **1–2 mm**)
- $f_{frp}$ = tensile strength of FRP (CFRP ≈ **3000–4000 MPa**)
- $D$ = column diameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For square/rectangular columns, effective confinement is only **~50%** of circular columns because of stress concentrations at corners. Always round corners (radius ≥ **20 mm**) before wrapping.

## 2.2 Repair Methods to Reinforced Concrete Beams

Beams suffer **flexural and shear cracks** under fire due to loss of bottom reinforcement strength and shear capacity in stirrups.

### 2.2.1 Epoxy Injection (For Cracks < 2 mm Width)

Low-viscosity epoxy (viscosity **100–500 cP**) is injected under pressure (**0.2–0.5 MPa**) to restore monolithic action.

### 2.2.2 Section Enlargement (Bottom Soffit Jacketing)

- Prop the beam.
- Remove damaged concrete from the soffit.
- Add new longitudinal bars (welded/lapped to existing).
- Apply polymer-modified mortar (typically **30–50 mm** thick).

**Increase in moment capacity (approximate):**

$$M_{u,\text{new}} = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st,\text{total}} \cdot d_{\text{eff}}$$

where $d_{\text{eff}}$ increases due to section enlargement.

### 2.2.3 External CFRP / GFRP Plates/Sheets

Bonded to the **tension face** for flexural strengthening. Adhesive: **epoxy** (24 h cure).

**Strengthening ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{A_{frp} \cdot f_{frp}}{A_s \cdot f_y}$$

Recommended $\eta \leq 1.0$ to avoid sudden brittle failure.

### 2.2.4 Near-Surface Mounted (NSM) Reinforcement

CFRP/GFRP bars are inserted into grooves cut into the cover concrete, then bonded with epoxy paste. Provides better fire performance than externally bonded FRP.

## 2.3 Repair Methods to Reinforced Concrete Slabs

### 2.3.1 Patch Repair (Localized Spalling)

Used for small areas (typically < **0.5 m²**). Damaged concrete removed, rebar treated, void filled with **polymer-modified mortar**.

### 2.3.2 Overlay / Screeding

For widespread surface damage (e.g., entire slab soffit):
- Apply **bonded concrete overlay** (50–75 mm) with bonding agent.
- For thin overlays, use **self-compacting concrete (SCC)** or **latex-modified concrete**.

### 2.3.3 Replacement

When slab is severely damaged (deflection > L/150 or reinforcement yield strain reached), full-depth replacement is required.

**Deflection check (IS 456):**

$$\delta_{\text{max,allowed}} = \frac{L}{250} \quad \text{(for simply supported)}$$

$$\delta_{\text{max,allowed}} = \frac{L}{350} \quad \text{(for cantilever)}$$

### 2.3.4 Carbon Fibre Reinforcement for Slabs

CFRP strips bonded in a **grid pattern** to the tension face. Effective for one-way and two-way slabs.

## 2.4 Repair to Steel Structural Members

Steel members fail under fire primarily due to **buckling, yielding, and connection failure**. Repair methods include:

| Damage Type | Repair Method |
|---|---|
| Local buckling | Heat-straightening or section replacement |
| Member yielding | Plate addition / splicing |
| Connection failure | Bolt/weld replacement |
| Section loss (corrosion) | Welded plate augmentation |
| Global deformation | Jack-straightening |

### 2.4.1 Heat Straightening

A controlled process where oxy-fuel torches heat the deformed region to **600–700 °C** (below steel's phase change at **723 °C**), then the member is restrained and allowed to cool — this redistributes internal stresses and restores geometry.

### 2.4.2 Plate Splicing (Cover Plates)

For tension members with reduced capacity, additional plates are welded (or bolted) parallel to the existing section.

**Capacity check:**

$$P_{u,\text{new}} = f_y \cdot A_{\text{existing}} + f_y \cdot A_{\text{plate}}$$

### 2.4.3 Gusset Plate Repair

Damaged gusset plates in truss connections are removed by **air-arc gouging** and replaced with new plates of matching thickness.

### 2.4.4 Welded vs Bolted Repairs

| Criterion | Welded | Bolted |
|---|---|---|
| Load transfer | Full strength | Slip-critical required |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Inspection | NDT mandatory (UT/RT) | Visual + torque test |
| Fire damage to weld | Critical | Less critical |

## 2.5 Repair to Masonry Structures

Masonry fails in fire through **loss of mortar bond, cracking, and unit spalling** (especially for clay bricks above 600 °C).

### 2.5.1 Repointing

Removal of deteriorated mortar to a depth of **2–2.5 times the joint width** (typically **15–25 mm**) and replacement with new mortar of matching composition. Critical for **lime mortars** (compatibility with historic substrate).

### 2.5.2 Grouting

Low-pressure injection of **cementitious or polymer grout** into voids and cracks. Used for multi-leaf walls where inner wythes have separated.

**Grout pressure:**

$$P_{\text{grout}} \leq 0.05 \text{ to } 0.1 \text{ MPa (for historic masonry)}$$

### 2.5.3 Stitching / Tie Installation

- **Helical ties** (stainless steel, **6–10 mm** dia.) are installed across cracks at intervals of **300–450 mm**.
- Bed joint reinforcement (e.g., **murfor** type) embedded in repointing mortar.

### 2.5.4 Wall Jacketing (Ferrocement / FRP)

- **Ferrocement:** Wire mesh (chicken mesh + skeletal steel) with **25–40 mm** cement mortar overlay.
- **FRP:** CFRP/GFRP strips bonded on both faces for out-of-plane strengthening.

### 2.5.5 Replacement (Toothing-In)

For severely damaged localized areas, new bricks are toothed into existing wall in a **staggered pattern** with fresh mortar.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_l = \dfrac{2 t_{frp} f_{frp}}{D}$ | FRP confinement pressure (circular column) | MPa |
| 2 | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{st}$ | Axial capacity of jacketed RC column | kN |
| 3 | $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} d$ | Flexural capacity of repaired beam | kN·m |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{frp} f_{frp}}{A_s f_y}$ | Strengthening ratio (beam) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{u,\text{new}} = f_y (A_{\text{old}} + A_{\text{plate}})$ | Steel member capacity after plate addition | kN |
| 6 | $\delta_{\text{allow}} = L/250$ | Max deflection (simply supported slab) | mm |
| 7 | $T_{\text{repair}} = T_{\text{surr}} + 7 \text{ days}$ | Curing time for cementitious repair | days |
| 8 | $f_{ck,\text{loss}} \approx 50\% \text{ at } 600°C$ | Residual concrete strength (fire-damaged) | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations are used by structural auditors, fire damage assessors (typically **5–10% of building cost** lost to fire annually in India), and post-disaster (fire, earthquake) rapid-assessment teams under the NDMA framework.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Confinement Pressure from FRP Wrap on a Circular RC Column

**Problem Setup:** A circular RC column of diameter $D = 400$ mm is wrapped with 2 layers of CFRP, each of thickness $t_{frp} = 1.2$ mm. The CFRP tensile strength is $f_{frp} = 3500$ MPa. Find the lateral confinement pressure $f_l$.

**Step 1: Total FRP thickness**

$$t_{total} = n \cdot t_{frp}$$

$$t_{total} = 2 \times 1.2 \text{ mm} = 2.4 \text{ mm} = 0.0024 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Apply confinement equation**

For a circular column, equilibrium of FRP wrap (considering the wrap as a thin cylinder under internal pressure):

$$f_l \cdot D = 2 \cdot t_{total} \cdot f_{frp}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $f_l$**

$$f_l = \frac{2 \cdot t_{total} \cdot f_{frp}}{D}$$

$$f_l = \frac{2 \times 2.4 \times 3500}{400}$$

**Step 4: Numerator computation**

$$2 \times 2.4 \times 3500 = 16{,}800 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 5: Final value**

$$f_l = \frac{16{,}800}{400} = 42 \text{ MPa}$$

This is a **very high confinement pressure** — the column is heavily confined, and the compressive strength of the concrete core can increase by **40–80%** depending on the constitutive model (e.g., Mander's model).

## 3.2 Derivation: Enhanced Axial Capacity of RC Column After Concrete Jacketing

**Given:**
- Original column: $400 \times 400$ mm, $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa, $4 \text{ bars of } 20$ mm dia. ($A_{st} = 1256$ mm²), $f_y = 415$ MPa.
- Jacketing adds: **100 mm** on all four sides + new $4 \text{ bars of } 16$ mm dia. + **8 mm ties @ 100 mm c/c**.

**Step 1: New gross cross-section**

$$A_g = (400 + 2 \times 100)^2 = 600^2 = 360{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Effective concrete area (subtract rebar)**

New rebar: $4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^2$

Old rebar (existing): $1256$ mm²

Total rebar: $A_{st,\text{total}} = 1256 + 804.25 = 2060.25 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 3: Effective concrete area**

$$A_c = A_g - A_{st,\text{total}} = 360{,}000 - 2060.25 = 357{,}939.75 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Apply capacity equation (IS 456 short column)**

$$P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st}$$

Using $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa (new M25 concrete) and $f_y = 415$ MPa:

$$P_u = 0.4 \times 25 \times 357{,}939.75 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 2060.25$$

$$P_u = 3{,}579{,}397.5 + 573{,}438.2$$

$$P_u \approx 4153 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Compare with original column capacity**

Original: $0.4 \times 20 \times (160000 - 1256) + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1256$

$$P_{u,\text{old}} = 1{,}270{,}176 + 349{,}488.8 \approx 1620 \text{ kN}$$

**Capacity enhancement ratio:**

$$\frac{P_{u,\text{new}}}{P_{u,\text{old}}} = \frac{4153}{1620} \approx 2.56$$

The jacketed column carries **2.56 times** the original load — typical for major repair projects.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Damage Severity | Recommended Repair | Cost Index (₹/m²) | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minor cracks (< 0.3 mm) | Surface sealing / epoxy injection | ₹200–500 | 1–2 days |
| Moderate cracks (0.3–2 mm) | Epoxy injection + patch repair | ₹500–1500 | 3–7 days |
| Spalling (cover loss 25–50 mm) | Concrete jacketing | ₹1500–3000 | 7–14 days |
| Severe (cover loss > 50 mm, bar exposed) | Concrete jacketing + rebar addition | ₹3000–6000 | 14–28 days |
| Buckled / failed RC column | Steel jacketing or full replacement | ₹6000–12000 | 21–45 days |
| Severely damaged slab | Full-depth replacement | ₹4000–8000 | 21–35 days |
| Steel member buckled | Heat-straightening / plate addition | ₹3000–7000 | 7–21 days |
| Masonry cracks (hairline) | Repointing + stitching | ₹400–1000 | 3–7 days |
| Masonry out-of-plane failure | Ferrocement/FRP jacketing | ₹1500–3500 | 14–28 days |

> [!NOTE]
> Cost indices are **approximate (2024–25 market rates)** and may vary by location, accessibility, and project size. The decision matrix is widely used in post-fire damage assessment reports.

## 3.4 Quality Control Checks (Step-by-Step)

1. **Before repair:** NDT (Rebound Hammer, UPV) — strength verification.
2. **After substrate prep:** Pull-off test of bonding agent (≥ **1.5 MPa**).
3. **After FRP wrap:** Tap test (delamination check), pull-off test.
4. **After concrete jacketing:** Cube test of repair material (28-day strength ≥ original $f_{ck}$).
5. **Final load test** (if required by engineer): Typically **1.25 × design load** held for **24 hours**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Concrete Jacketing" and state two situations where it is preferred for fire-damaged RC columns.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
Concrete jacketing is a repair technique in which a new layer of concrete (typically **60–100 mm** thick), along with additional longitudinal reinforcement and ties, is added around an existing column. The new concrete (usually M25 or higher) is bonded to the old substrate using a bonding agent such as epoxy or cement-slatex slurry.

**Preferred situations:**
1. When **cover loss exceeds 25 mm** and the longitudinal bars are exposed with section loss between **5–15%**.
2. When there is a need to **enhance axial load capacity** by 50% or more, and there is no space constraint preventing column cross-section increase.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks situations)**

### Question 2: What is the role of helical ties in stitching of cracked masonry walls? Mention typical spacing.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
Helical ties (typically **6–10 mm** diameter stainless steel) are installed across cracks in masonry walls to **transfer tensile stresses** across the crack plane and **restore monolithic action**. They resist the relative displacement of wall segments and prevent crack propagation. Typical spacing is **300–450 mm** along the crack, with the ties embedded in fresh mortar or grouted into drilled holes.

**(3 marks: 2 marks role + 1 mark spacing)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: 14 Marks

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the step-by-step procedure for the repair of a fire-damaged RC column by concrete jacketing. Also, list the quality control checks to be carried out. [7 marks]**
**(b) A circular RC column of diameter 400 mm is wrapped with 3 layers of CFRP, each of thickness 1.2 mm. If the tensile strength of CFRP is 3500 MPa, calculate the lateral confinement pressure. If the original column had a compressive strength of 25 MPa, estimate the enhanced confined concrete strength using Mander's model (assume k1 = 2.0 for circular column). [7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Propping and load relief** — The column is supported by adjustable props on all four sides to relieve the load to **0%–30%** of the design load during repair. (*1 mark*)

**Step 2: Removal of damaged concrete** — Spalled and unsound concrete is removed using chipping hammers or **hydro-demolition** until sound substrate is exposed. Minimum chipping depth is the full cover plus an additional **10 mm** behind the bar. (*1 mark*)

**Step 3: Reinforcement treatment** — Exposed bars are cleaned by **wire brushing** (St 2.5 surface preparation per **ISO 8501-1**). If section loss exceeds 15%, additional bars are welded/lapped. New ties (**8 mm @ 100–150 mm c/c**) are added. (*1 mark*)

**Step 4: Surface preparation** — Substrate is roughened (amplitude **3–5 mm**), cleaned with compressed air, and pre-wetted (SSD condition). (*0.5 marks*)

**Step 5: Bonding agent application** — A **bonding coat** (e.g., SBR latex-modified cement slurry or epoxy) is applied on the damp substrate. (*0.5 marks*)

**Step 6: Formwork and pouring** — Micro-concrete (M25 or higher) with **maximum aggregate size 10 mm** and **superplasticizer** is poured or injected. Vibration is done by needle vibrators. (*1 mark*)

**Step 7: Curing** — Curing is done for **at least 7 days** (preferably 14 days) using wet hessian or curing compound. (*0.5 marks*)

**Quality Control Checks:**
- Pull-off test of bonding agent (**≥ 1.5 MPa** at 7 days). (*0.5 marks*)
- Cube test of repair concrete (**28-day strength ≥ original $f_{ck}$**). (*0.5 marks*)
- NDT (Rebound Hammer, UPV) at **7, 14, 28 days** for uniformity. (*0.5 marks*)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $D = 400$ mm
- $n = 3$ layers, $t_{frp} = 1.2$ mm each
- $f_{frp} = 3500$ MPa

**Step 1: Total FRP thickness**

$$t_{total} = 3 \times 1.2 = 3.6 \text{ mm} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Confinement pressure**

$$f_l = \frac{2 \cdot t_{total} \cdot f_{frp}}{D}$$

$$f_l = \frac{2 \times 3.6 \times 3500}{400} = \frac{25{,}200}{400} = 63 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Confined concrete strength (Mander's model)**

For circular column with $k_1 = 2.0$:

$$f_{cc}' = f_{co}' \cdot \left( 2.254 \sqrt{1 + \frac{7.94 f_l}{f_{co}'}} - 2 \cdot \frac{f_l}{f_{co}'} - 1.254 \right)$$

Substituting $f_{co}' = 25$ MPa and $f_l = 63$ MPa:

$$\frac{f_l}{f_{co}'} = \frac{63}{25} = 2.52$$

$$f_{cc}' = 25 \times \left( 2.254 \sqrt{1 + 7.94 \times 2.52} - 2 \times 2.52 - 1.254 \right)$$

$$= 25 \times \left( 2.254 \sqrt{21.0} - 5.04 - 1.254 \right)$$

$$= 25 \times \left( 2.254 \times 4.583 - 6.294 \right)$$

$$= 25 \times (10.33 - 6.294) = 25 \times 4.036$$

$$f_{cc}' \approx 100.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**The confined concrete strength is enhanced by approximately 4× the original.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to multiply by the number of layers ($n$) and use $t_{frp} = 1.2$ mm instead of $t_{total} = 3.6$ mm — this gives $f_l = 21$ MPa, an error of **~67%**. Always check that you use **total** laminate thickness.

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative)

**(a) Discuss the various methods of repair to masonry structures damaged by fire. [7 marks]**
**(b) An RC beam of size 230 mm × 450 mm (effective) is repaired by bonding a CFRP strip of width 100 mm and thickness 1.4 mm on its tension face. Calculate the strengthening ratio $\eta$. Take $f_{frp} = 3000$ MPa. Original tension steel is 3 bars of 16 mm dia. (Fe415). Comment on whether the strengthening is safe as per codal recommendation. [7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The various methods of repair to masonry structures damaged by fire are:

**1. Repointing:** Deteriorated mortar is raked out to a depth of **2–2.5 times the joint width** (typically **15–25 mm**) and replaced with fresh mortar matching the original composition. Suitable for hairline cracks and surface erosion. (*1.5 marks*)

**2. Grouting:** Low-pressure injection (**0.05–0.1 MPa** for historic masonry) of cementitious or polymer grout into voids and delaminated regions of multi-leaf walls. Restores monolithic action. (*1.5 marks*)

**3. Stitching / Tie Installation:** Stainless steel helical ties (**6–10 mm** dia.) are installed across cracks at **300–450 mm** spacing. Resists relative displacement and prevents crack propagation. (*1 mark*)

**4. Ferrocement Jacketing:** Wire mesh (chicken mesh + skeletal steel) with **25–40 mm** cement mortar overlay applied on one or both faces. Provides tensile strength and out-of-plane capacity. (*1.5 marks*)

**5. FRP Strengthening:** CFRP/GFRP strips bonded to wall faces for out-of-plane and in-plane strengthening. (*1 mark*)

**6. Replacement (Toothing-In):** Severely damaged bricks/blocks are removed and replaced with new units in a **staggered pattern** toothing into existing wall. (*0.5 marks*)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Beam: $b = 230$ mm, $d = 450$ mm
- Original steel: $3 \text{ bars of } 16$ mm dia., $f_y = 415$ MPa
- CFRP strip: $b_f = 100$ mm, $t_f = 1.4$ mm, $f_{frp} = 3000$ MPa

**Step 1: Calculate areas**

$$A_s = 3 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 3 \times 201.06 = 603.19 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{frp} = b_f \times t_f = 100 \times 1.4 = 140 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Strengthening ratio**

$$\eta = \frac{A_{frp} \cdot f_{frp}}{A_s \cdot f_y}$$

$$\eta = \frac{140 \times 3000}{603.19 \times 415} = \frac{420{,}000}{250{,}323.85}$$

$$\eta = 1.68$$

**Comment:** As per **ACI 440.2R** and **fib Bulletin 14** recommendations, the strengthening ratio should be **$\eta \leq 1.0$** to prevent sudden brittle failure due to FRP rupture or concrete crushing. Since $\eta = 1.68 > 1.0$, the strengthening is **unsafe** as designed.

**Recommendation:** Reduce CFRP strip thickness to:
$$t_{f,\text{max}} = \frac{1.0 \times A_s \cdot f_y}{b_f \cdot f_{frp}} = \frac{1.0 \times 603.19 \times 415}{100 \times 3000} = 0.83 \text{ mm}$$

Or use a **2-layer CFRP** of 0.4 mm each (with intermediate epoxy layer) to maintain **$\eta = 1.0$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert mm² to consistent units and end up with a wrong value. Also, the **codal limit $\eta \leq 1.0$** is a key concept — losing 1 mark if not mentioned.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Damage assessment** is the foundation — NDT (Rebound, UPV, Core) before any repair.
- ✅ **Concrete jacketing** is the workhorse for fire-damaged RC columns; **steel jacketing** is for space-restricted cases.
- ✅ **FRP wrapping** is most efficient for **circular** columns — always round corners (radius ≥ 20 mm) for rectangular sections.
- ✅ **Confinement pressure** $f_l = \dfrac{2 t_{frp} f_{frp}}{D}$ — use **total** laminate thickness (multiply by number of layers).
- ✅ **Strengthening ratio** $\eta \leq 1.0$ is the code limit (ACI 440.2R / fib 14) to ensure ductile failure.
- ✅ **Epoxy injection** for cracks < 2 mm; **grouting** for masonry voids; **stitching** for through-cracks.
- ✅ **Repointing depth** = 2–2.5 × joint width, **15–25 mm** typical.
- ✅ **Curing** of cementitious repair: **minimum 7 days** (preferred 14 days).
- ✅ **Bond strength** of repair interface must be **≥ 1.5 MPa** (verified by pull-off test).
- ✅ **Slab deflection limit** = L/250 (simply supported) per IS 456 — full replacement if exceeded.
- ✅ **Steel repair** uses heat-straightening (600–700 °C) or plate splicing; never exceed 723 °C (phase change).
- ✅ **Mander's model** for confined concrete strength: $f_{cc}' = f_{co}'(2.254\sqrt{1+7.94 f_l/f_{co}'} - 2 f_l/f_{co}' - 1.254)$.
- ✅ **Codal references** for KTU exam: IS 456:2000, IS 13935:1993, IS 15988:2013, ACI 440.2R, fib Bulletin 14.
- ✅ **Fire residual strength rule of thumb:** Concrete loses **~50%** strength at 600 °C; steel at 550 °C.
- ✅ **Cost and time** of repair scale non-linearly with damage severity — early intervention saves cost.
- ✅ **Quality control** is non-negotiable: pull-off tests, cube tests, NDT, and load tests (1.25× design load, 24 h).
- ✅ **Mortar compatibility** is critical for masonry — use lime mortar for historic, cement mortar for modern.
- ✅ **Decision matrix** is a high-yield item for KTU theory questions — memorize the cost index and time ranges.
- ✅ **Always** address the **cause** of damage (e.g., chloride source, moisture ingress) — not just the symptom.
- ✅ **Fire-damaged members** should be **propped** before any repair work to ensure structural safety during execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *Assess → Diagnose → Clean → Bond → Strengthen → Cure → Test*. Skipping any step costs marks AND lives in real-world practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Area - Calculation of Building Fire Area, Subdivision of Fire Areas in Industrial, Residential and Public Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire Area - Calculation of Building Fire Area, Subdivision of Fire Areas in Industrial, Residential and Public Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fire Area** is formally defined (as per **NBC 2016 – Part 4: Fire and Life Safety**, Clause 2.21 and Clause 3.1.2) as the **maximum permissible floor area of a building, or part thereof, enclosed by fire-resistant walls, floors and ceilings (fire compartments)** within which a fire is assumed to originate and spread freely, before the intervention of active fire protection systems (sprinklers, fire brigade, etc.) can contain it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OESFT835 Module 4):**
> The *Fire Area* of a building is the total floor area (or sum of connected floor areas through unprotected vertical openings) considered as a single fire compartment for the purpose of designing fire resistance, means of egress, sprinkler zoning and active suppression system layout.

Mathematically, the fire area is the **product of the maximum horizontal projected plan area enclosed by fire separation elements** and the **number of communicating floors via unprotected vertical openings**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are blowing air into a **balloon inside a cardboard box**. The balloon is the fire — it expands until it hits a barrier. The *fire area* is essentially the **floor space of that box** before the cardboard walls stop the fire from spreading.

- If the **walls are weak (low fire resistance)** → balloon (fire) escapes easily → you must **divide the box into many smaller boxes** (subdivide fire area) so that fire remains confined.
- If the **walls are strong (high fire resistance)** → one big box (large fire area) is acceptable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** A shopping mall (Public Building) is often subdivided into **fire compartments of 1000–2000 m²** using 2-hour fire-rated walls. Without this subdivision, a single fire in a shop could engulf the entire mall within minutes.

### 1.3 Why Fire Area Matters

| Aspect | Relevance |
|--------|-----------|
| **Structural Design** | Determines fire resistance rating of structural members |
| **Active Systems** | Limits sprinkler zoning & hydrant spacing |
| **Means of Egress** | Calculates occupant load and travel distance |
| **Compartmentation** | Drives layout of fire-rated walls & floors |
| **Insurance** | Basis for risk-based premium calculation |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Reference Fire Load (q_r) for residential occupancies = 1500 MJ/m²**
- **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** of separating walls: **2 hours (Type 1), 1 hour (Type 2), etc.**
- **Fire Load Density (q)** is expressed in **MJ/m² of floor area**
- **Occupant Load Factor** ranges from **2 m²/person (offices)** to **10 m²/person (industrial)**
- **Specific Heat of Combustion of Wood ≈ 17 MJ/kg**
- **Specific Heat of Combustion of Paper ≈ 16 MJ/kg**
- **Specific Heat of Combustion of PVC ≈ 46 MJ/kg**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Components of a Fire Area

A *Fire Area* is determined by three interlocking criteria:

1. **Horizontal Boundary:** Fire-resistant walls extending from floor to ceiling slab.
2. **Vertical Boundary:** Fire-resistant floor/ceiling slabs.
3. **Openings:** All protected openings (fire doors, dampers, sealed penetrations).

### 2.2 The Fire Area Equation (Generic)

For a building of plan area A enclosed by walls of fire resistance ≥ the standard rating, the fire area is simply:

$$
A_f = A_{\text{plan}} \times N_{\text{unprotected floors}}
$$

If **all vertical openings (staircases, ducts, services) are fire-separated**, then each floor acts as an independent fire compartment:

$$
A_f = A_{\text{plan}} \quad \text{(per floor)}
$$

If **vertical openings are unprotected** (no fire doors, open staircases), then all floors are counted together:

$$
A_f = A_{\text{plan}} \times N_{\text{stories}}
$$

### 2.3 Subdivision Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

Step 1: **Identify the Occupancy Group** of the building (A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, J as per NBC 2016).

Step 2: **Identify the Construction Type** (Type 1, Type 2, Type 3, Type 4 — based on fire resistance of structural elements).

Step 3: **Read the Maximum Permissible Fire Area** from Table 3 of NBC 2016 / Part 4.

Step 4: **Compare Actual Floor Area with Maximum Permissible Fire Area.**
- If **A_actual ≤ A_max** → No subdivision required.
- If **A_actual > A_max** → **Subdivide** using fire-rated walls into compartments of area ≤ A_max.

Step 5: **Verify vertical separations** — even if horizontal compartments are within limits, vertical fire spread can multiply fire area.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fire Area (single floor)** | $A_f = A_{\text{plan}}$ | m² | All floors isolated |
| 2 | **Fire Area (multi-storey, unprotected)** | $A_f = A_{\text{plan}} \times N$ | m² | Open staircase, ducts unsealed |
| 3 | **Fire Area (subdivided)** | $A_f = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | m² | $n$ compartments with rated walls |
| 4 | **Fire Load (Q)** | $Q = \sum_i M_i H_i$ | MJ | Total heat energy released |
| 5 | **Fire Load Density (q)** | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A_f}$ | MJ/m² | Energy per unit floor area |
| 6 | **Equivalent Fire Area (A_eq)** | $A_{eq} = \dfrac{q}{q_r}$ | m² | Comparative severity index |
| 7 | **Equivalent Fire Duration** | $t_e = \dfrac{q}{q_c}$ | minutes | Time-fire severity correlation |
| 8 | **Occupant Load (P)** | $P = \dfrac{A_f}{F_{\text{occ}}}$ | persons | $F_{\text{occ}}$ = occupant load factor (m²/person) |
| 9 | **Required FRR (Type 1 construction)** | $t_{\text{FRR}} \geq 2 \text{ hr}$ | hours | Industrial / public |
| 10 | **Reference Fire Load (q_r)** | $1500$ (residential) – $8000$ (storage) | MJ/m² | NBC Annex A |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Architectural Planning:** Dictates location of fire walls, fire doors and compartment lines.
- **Structural Engineering:** Determines the fire resistance rating (FRR) of beams, columns and slabs.
- **MEP Engineering:** Defines sprinkler zoning, detection zone limits and HVAC damper locations.
- **Fire Service Operations:** The Indian Fire Service uses fire area to estimate fire load deployment (number of pumps, hose lines, water quantity).

> [!NOTE]
> The **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee) of India** also uses fire area to classify industrial risks into *Light*, *Moderate* and *High* hazard categories for insurance rating. A building with A_f > 1000 m² and high fire load density is rated *High Hazard*, requiring automatic sprinklers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fire Area with Vertical Communication

**Problem Setup:**
A multi-storey office building has plan dimensions **30 m × 20 m = 600 m² per floor** with **5 floors**. The staircase is enclosed by **2-hour fire-rated walls** with **fire doors**, but the **service ducts (electrical, AC) are not sealed** at floor levels.

**Determine the fire area.**

**Step 1:** Identify horizontal extent per floor.

$$
A_{\text{plan}} = 30 \times 20 = 600 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Identify vertical communication.

- Staircase: **protected** (2-hr fire door + 2-hr enclosure) → **does NOT** contribute to vertical fire spread.
- Service ducts: **unsealed** → **DOES** contribute to vertical fire spread.

**Step 3:** Count the number of floors connected via unprotected openings.

$$
N = 5 \ \text{floors (all connected through service ducts)}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the multi-storey fire area formula.

$$
A_f = A_{\text{plan}} \times N = 600 \times 5 = 3000 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with maximum permissible fire area for **Business (Group E) occupancy** in **Type 1 construction = 2500 m²**.

$$
A_f^{\text{actual}} = 3000 \ \text{m}^2 > A_f^{\text{max}} = 2500 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** **Non-compliant.** The building **must be subdivided** by providing a 2-hour fire barrier (or sealing the service ducts) every 4 floors (so that each fire compartment = 4 floors = 2400 m²).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fire Load Density & Equivalent Fire Area

**Problem Setup:**
A warehouse (Group H – Storage) of plan area **1000 m²** contains the following combustible inventory:

| Material | Mass M_i (kg) | Heat of Combustion H_i (MJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Cardboard boxes | 5000 | 16 |
| Wooden pallets | 3000 | 17 |
| Plastic packaging | 1000 | 46 |

**Find:** (i) Total fire load Q, (ii) Fire load density q, (iii) Equivalent fire area.

**Step 1 — Total Fire Load (Q):**

$$
Q = \sum_i M_i \cdot H_i
$$

$$
Q_{\text{cardboard}} = 5000 \times 16 = 80{,}000 \ \text{MJ}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{wood}} = 3000 \times 17 = 51{,}000 \ \text{MJ}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{plastic}} = 1000 \times 46 = 46{,}000 \ \text{MJ}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 80{,}000 + 51{,}000 + 46{,}000 = 177{,}000 \ \text{MJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Fire Load Density (q):**

$$
q = \frac{Q}{A_f} = \frac{177{,}000}{1000} = 177 \ \text{MJ/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Fire Area (A_eq):**

For storage occupancies, reference fire load $q_r = 8000 \ \text{MJ/m}^2$ (high hazard).

$$
A_{eq} = \frac{q}{q_r} = \frac{177}{8000} = 0.022 \ (\text{normalized ratio})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *equivalent fire area* is a dimensionless ratio used to compare the severity of fire in different occupancies on a common scale. A higher q implies greater fire severity, requiring a higher FRR of structural elements.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Subdivision of a Large Industrial Shed

**Problem Setup:**
A single-storey industrial shed has plan dimensions **80 m × 50 m = 4000 m²**. The construction type is **Type 2 (steel with 1-hr fire resistance)**. The occupancy is **Moderate Hazard Industrial (Group G-2)**.

**Find:** Number of fire compartments required.

**Step 1:** Identify maximum permissible fire area from NBC 2016 Part 4 Table 3.

For **Moderate Hazard Industrial, Type 2 construction:**

$$
A_f^{\text{max}} = 1500 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate required number of subdivisions.

$$
n_{\text{sub}} = \left\lceil \frac{A_{\text{actual}}}{A_f^{\text{max}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{4000}{1500} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.667 \right\rceil = 3
$$

**Step 3:** Determine size of each compartment.

$$
A_{\text{compartment}} = \frac{4000}{3} \approx 1333.33 \ \text{m}^2 \leq 1500 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Layout arrangement (one possible solution):

- Compartment 1: 50 m × 26.7 m = 1335 m²
- Compartment 2: 50 m × 26.7 m = 1335 m²
- Compartment 3: 50 m × 26.6 m = 1330 m²

Each compartment is separated by **1-hour fire-rated walls (Type 2)**.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Matrix — Fire Area Standards by Occupancy

| Occupancy Group | NBC Classification | Typical Max Fire Area (Type 1 Construction) | Subdivision Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Residential (Group A)** | Lodging, Apartment, Hotel | 500 – 2000 m² | Floor-wise separation mandatory |
| **Educational (Group B)** | Schools, Colleges | 1500 m² | At 1500 m² mark |
| **Institutional (Group C)** | Hospitals, Jails | 1000 m² | Each ward isolated |
| **Assembly (Group D)** | Theatres, Stadiums | 500 – 1500 m² | Around spectator zones |
| **Business (Group E)** | Offices, Banks | 2500 m² | Multi-storey vertical hazard |
| **Mercantile (Group F)** | Shops, Malls | 500 – 1500 m² | High occupant density |
| **Industrial (Group G)** | Factories | 750 – 3000 m² | Hazard category-dependent |
| **Storage (Group H)** | Warehouses | 1000 – 3000 m² | Fire load density > 8000 MJ/m² |
| **Hazardous (Group J)** | Petroleum, Chemicals | 150 – 500 m² | Most stringent requirements |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Fire Area Calculation — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Building Plan & Specification] --> A1[Identify Occupancy Group as per NBC 2016]
    A1 --> A2[Identify Construction Type 1 / 2 / 3 / 4]
    A2 --> A3[Read A_f max from NBC Table 3]
    A3 --> A4[Calculate A actual = A plan x N unprotected floors]
    A4 --> A5{Is A actual less than or equal to A f max}
    A5 -->|Yes| A6[Compliant No Subdivision Required]
    A5 -->|No| A7[Calculate Number of Compartments n sub]
    A7 --> A8[Determine Subdivision Layout]
    A8 --> A9[Apply Fire Rated Walls FRR based on Construction Type]
    A9 --> A10[Verify Vertical Separation Stairs Ducts]
    A10 --> A11[Compliant with Subdivisions]
    A11 --> A12[END Approved Design]
```

### 4.2 Industrial Building — Subdivision Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Industrial Shed 80m x 50m = 4000 m2] --> B2{Hazard Category}
    B2 -->|Light| B3[Compartment Size 2500 m2]
    B2 -->|Moderate| B4[Compartment Size 1500 m2]
    B2 -->|High| B5[Compartment Size 750 m2]
    B3 --> B6[Subdivide into 2 Blocks]
    B4 --> B7[Subdivide into 3 Blocks]
    B5 --> B8[Subdivide into 6 Blocks]
    B6 --> B9[1 hr Fire Rated Walls]
    B7 --> B10[2 hr Fire Rated Walls]
    B8 --> B11[3 hr Fire Rated Walls]
    B9 --> B12[Final Layout Approved]
    B10 --> B12
    B11 --> B12
```

### 4.3 Residential Building — Floor-wise Compartmentation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Floor 1 Apartment 600 m2] --> C2[Floor 2 Apartment 600 m2]
    C2 --> C3[Floor 3 Apartment 600 m2]
    C3 --> C4[Floor 4 Apartment 600 m2]
    C4 --> C5[Floor 5 Apartment 600 m2]
    C5 --> C6{Penthouse Mech 200 m2}
    C1 --- C7[Fire Rated Staircase Enclosure 2 hr]
    C2 --- C7
    C3 --- C7
    C4 --- C7
    C5 --- C7
    C7 --> C8[Protected Lobby at Each Level]
    C8 --> C9[Each Apartment Separated by 1 hr Wall]
    C9 --> C10[Independent Fire Compartment per Apartment]
```

### 4.4 Public Building — Mall Compartmentation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[Shopping Mall Total 10000 m2] --> D2[Anchor Store 3000 m2]
    D1 --> D3[Retail Zone 5000 m2]
    D1 --> D4[Food Court 1500 m2]
    D1 --> D5[Cinema 500 m2]
    D3 --> D6[Subdivide into 5 Retail Pods of 1000 m2 each]
    D4 --> D7[Separated from Retail by 2 hr Wall]
    D5 --> D8[Independent Exit Staircase]
    D6 --> D9[Fire Curtains and Smoke Barriers]
    D7 --> D9
    D8 --> D9
    D9 --> D10[Approved Compartmentation Plan]
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture — Fire Load to FRR Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Inventory of Combustibles] --> E2[Compute Q = Sum M_i H_i]
    E2 --> E3[Compute q = Q over A_f]
    E3 --> E4{q less than 1500 MJ per m2}
    E3 --> E5{q between 1500 and 8000 MJ per m2}
    E3 --> E6{q greater than 8000 MJ per m2}
    E4 --> E7[Light Hazard FRR 1 hr]
    E5 --> E8[Moderate Hazard FRR 2 hr]
    E6 --> E9[High Hazard FRR 4 hr]
    E7 --> E10[Select Construction Type]
    E8 --> E10
    E9 --> E10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., A1, B2) and avoid reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase or sentence-case text — no markdown formatting inside node labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP
