### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define "Fire Area" as per NBC 2016. List any two factors on which the fire area of a building depends.**

**Model Answer:**
Fire area is the maximum permissible floor area of a building (or part thereof) enclosed by fire-resistant walls, floors and ceilings, within which a fire is assumed to freely spread before firefighting intervention.

Two factors on which fire area depends:
1. **Occupancy classification** (Residential, Industrial, Public, etc.) – each group has different maximum permissible limits per NBC Table 3.
2. **Construction Type** (Type 1 to Type 4) – based on fire resistance of structural members.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between "fire area" and "fire compartment".**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fire Area | Fire Compartment |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| Definition | Total floor area assumed to be involved in a single fire | A physically enclosed volume bounded by fire-resistant elements |
| Purpose | Sizing of fire protection systems | Limiting fire/smoke spread |
| Boundary | May include vertical communication | Strictly sealed on all sides |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 6-storey office building has a plan area of **40 m × 25 m = 1000 m² per floor**. The central staircase and lift lobby are enclosed by **2-hour fire-rated walls and doors**, but the **AC duct shafts and electrical risers are unsealed**. Determine:
- (i) Fire area of the building,
- (ii) Whether the building complies with NBC 2016 for **Business Group E, Type 1 construction**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a sketch the concept of **compartmentation** in a multi-storey residential building. List the **NBC-prescribed maximum fire areas** for Apartments, Hotels and Hospitals.

---

##### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1:** Identify horizontal extent per floor.

$$
A_{\text{plan}} = 40 \times 25 = 1000 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Identify vertical communication status.

- Staircase & lift lobby: **protected (2-hr)** → does **not** contribute to vertical fire spread.
- AC ducts & electrical risers: **unsealed** → **contribute** to vertical fire spread.

**Step 3:** Apply the multi-storey fire area formula (unprotected openings present).

$$
A_f = A_{\text{plan}} \times N = 1000 \times 6 = 6000 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Reference value for Business Group E (Type 1 construction) per NBC 2016.

$$
A_f^{\text{max}} = 2500 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Comparison.

$$
A_f^{\text{actual}} = 6000 \ \text{m}^2 \gg A_f^{\text{max}} = 2500 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** The building is **NON-COMPLIANT**. Remediation:
- Seal AC duct shafts and electrical risers at **every floor penetration** using **2-hr fire-rated dampers and intumescent sealants**.
- After sealing, each floor becomes an independent fire compartment:

$$
A_f^{\text{corrected}} = 1000 \ \text{m}^2 < 2500 \ \text{m}^2 \quad (\text{COMPLIANT})
$$

---

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Compartmentation in a Residential Building (Sketch-style description):**

A multi-storey residential building is divided into **vertical and horizontal compartments**:

- **Horizontal Compartments:** Each apartment unit is separated from the next by a **1-hour fire-rated wall** extending floor-to-ceiling. Service penetrations (pipes, cables) are sealed with fire-rated sealants.
- **Vertical Compartments:** Each floor slab is a **2-hour fire-rated horizontal barrier**. Staircases and lift shafts are enclosed by **2-hr fire-rated walls** with **self-closing fire doors** at every landing.
- **Service Shafts:** Electrical risers, plumbing stacks and AC ducts are enclosed in fire-rated shafts with **fire dampers** at every floor crossing.

**NBC-Prescribed Maximum Fire Areas (Approximate, NBC 2016 Part 4, Type 1 Construction):**

| Occupancy | Maximum Fire Area |
|---|---|
| Apartments (Group A-2) | 2000 m² |
| Hotels (Group A-3) | 1000 m² |
| Hospitals (Group C-1) | 1000 m² |

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A pharmaceutical manufacturing unit (Moderate Hazard Industrial, Type 2 construction) has a plan area of **90 m × 40 m = 3600 m²** on a single floor. Calculate:
- (i) Number of fire compartments required,
- (ii) Maximum permissible area of each compartment,
- (iii) Required fire resistance rating of separating walls.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **subdivision criteria of fire areas in public buildings** (assembly and mercantile occupancies). How does the occupant load influence fire area sizing?

---

##### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1:** Identify Maximum Permissible Fire Area for Moderate Hazard Industrial, Type 2 construction (NBC 2016 Part 4 Table 3).

$$
A_f^{\text{max}} = 1500 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate number of compartments.

$$
n_{\text{sub}} = \left\lceil \frac{3600}{1500} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.4 \right\rceil = 3
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate area per compartment.

$$
A_{\text{compartment}} = \frac{3600}{3} = 1200 \ \text{m}^2 \leq 1500 \ \text{m}^2 \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 4:** Required FRR for separating walls (Type 2 construction, industrial).

$$
t_{\text{FRR}} = 2 \ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 5:** Layout (one possible solution):

| Compartment | Dimensions | Area |
|---|---|---|
| C-1 | 30 m × 40 m | 1200 m² |
| C-2 | 30 m × 40 m | 1200 m² |
| C-3 | 30 m × 40 m | 1200 m² |

Separated by 2-hr fire-rated walls with **self-closing fire doors**.

---

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Subdivision of Fire Areas in Public Buildings:**

Public buildings (Groups B, C, D, F) require **denser subdivision** than industrial buildings because of:
1. **High occupant density** — theaters, malls and schools pack hundreds of people into small footprints.
2. **Panic-driven egress** — fire spread must be limited to allow orderly evacuation.
3. **Mixed occupancies** — malls combine retail, food court, cinema and parking.

**NBC 2016 Maximum Fire Areas for Public Buildings (Type 1 Construction):**

| Occupancy | Max Fire Area |
|---|---|
| Schools (Group B) | 1500 m² |
| Hospitals (Group C) | 1000 m² |
| Theatres/Auditoriums (Group D) | 500 m² |
| Restaurants (Group D-2) | 1000 m² |
| Shops/Malls (Group F) | 1500 m² |

**Influence of Occupant Load:**

The occupant load $P = A_f / F_{\text{occ}}$ governs:
- **Egress capacity** — total stair width = $P \times 0.5$ m (per person unit width).
- **Travel distance** — limited to 30 m for unsprinklered, 45 m for sprinklered buildings.
- **Compartmentation density** — higher P → smaller fire compartments to ensure all occupants can exit within **2.5 minutes** (the assumed fire evacuation time per NBC).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to **multiply by the number of floors** when vertical openings are unprotected. This leads to **underestimation of fire area** and a **non-compliant** design being marked as compliant.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **fire area (m²)** with **fire load (MJ)**. They are **dimensionally different** and **must not be interchanged**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not specifying the **fire resistance rating in hours** of the separating walls. The examiner deducts marks if the FRR is omitted.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Treating all occupancies with the same $A_f^{max} = 1500 \ \text{m}^2$. This is **incorrect** — NBC 2016 Table 3 has **different limits per occupancy and construction type**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- Fire Area = Plan Area × Number of unprotected communicating floors.
- Maximum permissible fire area varies with **Occupancy Group + Construction Type**.
- NBC 2016 Part 4 Table 3 is the **primary reference** — memorize the headline values for Groups A, D, E, G.
- Fire Load Density $q = Q / A_f$ is the **basis for sizing structural FRR**.
- Service shafts and unprotected ducts **multiply fire area vertically**.
- Apartments ≤ 2000 m², Hotels ≤ 1000 m², Hospitals ≤ 1000 m² (Type 1).
- Industrial: Light Hazard = 3000 m², Moderate = 1500 m², High = 750 m² (Type 2).
- FRR scales with fire load density: < 1500 MJ/m² → 1 hr; 1500–8000 → 2 hr; > 8000 → 4 hr.
- Subdivision always uses **fire-rated walls + fire doors + sealed penetrations**.
- Occupant load $P$ controls egress width and travel distance, not fire area directly — but indirectly influences subdivision density.
- The **three pillars of fire area design** are: **(i) occupancy, (ii) construction, (iii) vertical communication.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Trick:** *"Big building + weak walls = many compartments. Small building + strong walls = few compartments."* Apply the formula $n = \lceil A / A_{max} \rceil$ as the universal divider.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Separation Between Building - Principle of Calculation of Safe Distance; Design Principles of Fire Resistant Walls and Ceilings
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Separation Between Buildings — Formal Definition

**Fire Separation** is the deliberate provision of physical distance and/or compartmentalising structural barriers between adjacent buildings (or fire compartments within a building) intended to prevent the spread of fire, heat, smoke, and toxic gases from a fire-involved structure to neighbouring properties, thereby limiting property loss and safeguarding life.

According to the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety)**, fire separation is achieved by a combination of:
- **Spatial separation (safe distance)** between buildings, and
- **Structural fire resistance** provided by walls, floors, and ceilings of prescribed fire resistance ratings (FRR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Fire separation is a **mandatory** design requirement for all Group A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, and J occupancies as classified under NBC 2016.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a chain of rooms in a hospital. If a fire starts in the operation theatre, you don't want the flames to leap into the ICU next door. The **wall** between them is the **fire-resistant barrier**, and the **empty corridor outside** is the **safe distance (spatial separation)**. Together, they buy precious time for evacuation — much like a *lava-break* (a gap) and a *firewall* (a dam) in a volcanic landscape.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ |
| Acceptable radiant heat flux (combustibles) | $q_{crit}$ | **12.5** | $\text{kW/m}^2$ |
| Acceptable radiant heat flux (non-combustibles) | $q_{crit}$ | **25.0** | $\text{kW/m}^2$ |
| Standard fire test temperature | $T_t$ | $345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ | °C |
| Emissivity of flame (sooty) | $\varepsilon_f$ | **0.7 to 0.9** | dimensionless |
| Emissivity of fire window | $\varepsilon_w$ | **0.8** | dimensionless |
| Fire resistance rating (NBC) | FRR | 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 (hours) | h |

> [!NOTE]
> The **standard fire curve** above is the ISO 834 / IS 3809 hydrocarbon-equivalent time-temperature profile, used uniformly in India for fire-resistance testing.

## 1.4 Why Fire Separation Matters — The "Why"

1. **Life Safety** — Protected means of egress remain tenable.
2. **Property Protection** — Limits total fire loss, insurance costs.
3. **Firefighter Access** — Safe operational zones for rescue teams.
4. **Business Continuity** — Prevents cascading economic failure.
5. **Code Compliance** — Statutory requirement under NBC 2016 and local municipal bylaws.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Calculation of Safe Distance

The safe distance between two buildings is governed by **radiant heat transfer** from the fire-exposed façade (or opening) of the burning building to the receiving façade of the adjacent building. Three failure criteria exist:

- **Criterion A (Ignition of Combustibles):** Radiant flux on adjacent façade $\leq 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$
- **Criterion B (Structural Damage to Non-combustible):** Radiant flux $\leq 25 \text{ kW/m}^2$
- **Criterion C (Window Breakage):** Incident flux $\leq 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ on glass

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Identify the fire size** — typically the **fully-developed fire** producing maximum emissive power.
2. **Compute the emissive power** of the flame/vent using Stefan–Boltzmann radiation.
3. **Determine the configuration factor (view factor)** between emitting and receiving surfaces.
4. **Apply the inverse-square attenuation** of radiant flux with separation distance.
5. **Equate received flux to allowable critical flux** $\rightarrow$ Solve for **safe distance $D$**.

## 2.2 Heat Flux Received at a Target Façade

The radiant heat flux received at the centre of a receiving opening/window is given by:

$$q_r = \varepsilon_f \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^4 \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau_{atm}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_f$ = effective emissivity of the fire/flame column
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- $T_f$ = absolute flame temperature (K)
- $F_{12}$ = configuration (view) factor from emitter to receiver
- $\tau_{atm}$ = atmospheric transmissivity (often taken as 1.0 for engineering simplicity, or reduced for smoke absorption)

## 2.3 View Factor $F_{12}$ (Parallel Rectangular Surfaces)

For two parallel, vertically-aligned rectangular openings of width $W_1$ and $W_2$, separated horizontally by distance $D$ and of equal height $H$:

$$F_{12} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\frac{X}{\sqrt{1+X^2}}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{Y}{\sqrt{1+X^2}}\right) + \frac{Y}{\sqrt{1+Y^2}}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X}{\sqrt{1+Y^2}}\right)\right]$$

where $X = \dfrac{W_1}{D}$ and $Y = \dfrac{H}{D}$.

## 2.4 Simplified Safe Distance Formula (NBC / IS 1644 Approach)

For a typical room fire with window opening of area $A_w$ emitting through a fully-engulfed flame, the safe distance simplifies to:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{F \cdot q_{total}}{q_{crit}}}$$

where:
- $F$ = fractional area of flame coverage
- $q_{total}$ = total heat release rate (kW) from the opening
- $q_{crit}$ = critical heat flux (kW/m²)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_t = 345\log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ | Standard fire time-temperature (ISO 834 / IS 3809) | °C, t in min |
| 2 | $q_r = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 F_{12}$ | Radiant heat flux at target | $\text{kW/m}^2$ |
| 3 | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{F \cdot Q}{q_{crit}}}$ | Simplified safe distance | m |
| 4 | $F_{12}$ (parallel rect.) | Configuration factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $FRR_{wall} = 0.05 \cdot R_{design}$ (rule-of-thumb) | Empirical FRR-Relationship | h |
| 6 | $t_{collapse} = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{A \cdot F_y}$ | Load ratio method for columns | min |
| 7 | $T_{insulation} \leq 140°C$ average / $180°C$ max single point | Insulation criterion (IS 3809) | °C |
| 8 | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{H}{D}$ | Separation index (NBC) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $R_{req} = R_{c} + R_{s} + R_{i}$ | Total thermal resistance of wall | $\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for Exams:** In KTU board exams, examiners frequently test the relationship between **view factor geometry** and **safe distance**. Memorise the parallel-rectangular view factor form for full marks.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planning:** Determines minimum setback in high-density cities.
- **Industrial Safety:** Spacing of LPG storage, transformer yards, ammunition depots.
- **Heritage Conservation:** Required between modern structures and heritage precincts.
- **High-Rise Design:** Stack effect and façade flame spread must be quantified.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Safe Distance from First Principles of Radiation

### 3.1.1 Start with Stefan-Boltzmann Law

The total emissive power of a blackbody at temperature $T$ is:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

For a real (grey) body radiator with emissivity $\varepsilon$:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

### 3.1.2 Define the Radiative Heat Transfer Between Two Surfaces

For two surfaces, 1 (emitter, fire) and 2 (receiver, adjacent building), the heat flux incident on surface 2 is:

$$q_{2 \leftarrow 1} = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau_{atm}$$

### 3.1.3 Define the Geometric View Factor (Configuration Factor)

By definition, $F_{12}$ is the fraction of radiation leaving surface 1 that strikes surface 2. For two parallel rectangles of width $W_1, W_2$, separation $D$, and height $H$:

$$F_{12} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[ \frac{X}{\sqrt{1+X^2}}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{Y}{\sqrt{1+X^2}}\right) + \frac{Y}{\sqrt{1+Y^2}}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X}{\sqrt{1+Y^2}}\right) \right]$$

with $X = W_1 / D$ and $Y = H / D$.

### 3.1.4 Apply the Safety Criterion

The condition for no ignition / no structural damage is:

$$q_{2 \leftarrow 1} \leq q_{crit}$$

Substituting:

$$\varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau_{atm} \leq q_{crit}$$

### 3.1.5 Solve for $D$ in the Far-Field Limit

For practical engineering, when $D \gg W_1, H$ (the "point source" approximation), $F_{12}$ reduces to:

$$F_{12} \approx \frac{A_1}{2\pi D^2}$$

where $A_1 = W_1 \cdot H$ is the area of the emitting opening. Substituting:

$$\varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 \cdot \frac{A_1}{2\pi D^2} \cdot \tau_{atm} \leq q_{crit}$$

Rearranging for $D$:

$$D \geq \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 \cdot A_1 \cdot \tau_{atm}}{2\pi \cdot q_{crit}}}$$

This is the **fundamental safe-distance equation**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Calculation of Safe Distance

**Problem Statement:** Two adjacent buildings each have rectangular windows of width **2.0 m** and height **1.5 m** at the same level. In a fully-developed room fire, the flame temperature is **$T_f = 1000 \text{ K}$** with effective emissivity **$\varepsilon_f = 0.8$**. The atmospheric transmissivity is **$\tau_{atm} = 1.0$**. Determine the minimum safe distance $D$ so that the radiant heat flux on the receiving window does not exceed the **combustible-ignition criterion of $12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$**.

### Step 1 — Compute Emissive Power

$$E = \varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^4 = 0.8 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1000)^4$$

$$E = 0.8 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{12} = 0.8 \times 56700 = 45360 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$E = 45.36 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Far-Field Approximation

For point-source approximation:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot A_1}{2\pi \cdot q_{crit}}}$$

where $A_1 = 2.0 \times 1.5 = 3.0 \text{ m}^2$.

### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{45360 \times 3.0}{2\pi \times 12500}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{136080}{78539.8}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{1.7325} = 1.316 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Apply a Safety Factor of 1.5 (KTU Convention)

$$D_{design} = 1.5 \times 1.316 = 1.974 \text{ m}$$

$$D_{design} \approx 2.0 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A minimum safe horizontal separation of approximately **2.0 m** is required between the two buildings' window façades.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Safe Distance Criteria

| Criterion | $q_{crit}$ ($\text{kW/m}^2$) | Application | Result for above example |
|-----------|------------------------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| A — Ignition of combustibles | 12.5 | Wood, paper, plastics | $D \approx 2.0 \text{ m}$ |
| B — Structural damage | 25.0 | Non-combustible façade | $D \approx 1.4 \text{ m}$ |
| C — Window glass breakage | 12.5 | Standard annealed glass | $D \approx 2.0 \text{ m}$ |
| D — Occupant tenability (skin pain) | 4.0 | Firefighter exposure | $D \approx 3.5 \text{ m}$ |

## 3.4 Derivation of Insulation Criterion for Fire-Resistant Walls

For a wall of thickness $L$, thermal conductivity $k$, and exposed to standard fire $T_t$, the temperature at the unexposed face after time $t$ is governed by 1-D transient heat conduction:

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

where $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ is thermal diffusivity.

Using lumped-capacitance if Biot number $Bi = hL/k \ll 0.1$:

$$T(t) = T_0 + (T_\infty - T_0)\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

with time-constant $\tau = \rho c_p L / h$.

**Insulation criterion (IS 3809):** Average unexposed-face temperature rise $\leq 140 \text{ °C}$ and maximum single-point rise $\leq 180 \text{ °C}$.

Solving the implicit finite-difference form of the heat equation yields the **fire resistance rating** as a function of $L$, $k$, $\rho$, $c_p$, and the standard fire curve.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fire Separation Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Building A Fire Source] -->|Radiant heat flux| nodeB[Adjacent Building B Receiver]
    nodeB -->|If q greater than qcrit| nodeC[Ignition / Structural Damage]
    nodeB -->|If q less than qcrit| nodeD[Safe State]
    nodeE[Spatial Separation D] -->|Reduces q with 1 by D squared| nodeB
    nodeF[Fire Resistant Wall] -->|Provides 1 to 6 hour FRR| nodeB
    nodeG[NBC 2016 Part 4] -->|Governs design| nodeA
    nodeG -->|Governs design| nodeB
    nodeH[Configuration Factor F12] -->|Geometry dependent| nodeB
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Safe Distance Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Fire Load and Fuel Package] --> stepA[Determine Q dot HRR]
    input2[Opening Geometry W and H] --> stepB[Compute Emitting Area A1]
    input3[Flame Temperature Tf] --> stepC[Compute Emissive Power E]
    stepA --> stepC
    stepB --> stepD[Compute View Factor F12]
    stepC --> stepE[Formulate q received]
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF{Compare with qcrit}
    stepF -->|q greater| stepG[Increase D or Add Barrier]
    stepF -->|q less| stepH[Acceptable Safe Distance]
    stepG --> stepI[Iterate and Converge]
    stepI --> stepD
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Fire-Resistant Wall Cross-Section (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FireExposedFace[FIRE EXPOSED FACE]
        layer1A[Plaster 12 mm]
    end
    subgraph CoreStructure[CORE STRUCTURE]
        layer2A[Reinforced Concrete 150 mm to 200 mm]
        layer3A[Insulation Layer Mineral Wool 50 mm]
    end
    subgraph UnexposedFace[UNEXPOSED FACE]
        layer4A[Plaster 12 mm]
    end
    layer1A --> layer2A
    layer2A --> layer3A
    layer3A --> layer4A
    note1[FRR 2 to 4 hours per IS 3809] --- layer2A
    note2[Insulation 140 deg C average] --- layer3A
    note3[Stability 250 deg C steel OR 600 deg C concrete] --- layer2A
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Fire-Resistant Ceiling Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopSide[TOP SIDE ROOF OR FLOOR ABOVE]
        tA[Concrete Deck 100 mm]
    end
    subgraph SuspendedCeiling[SUSPENDED CEILING SYSTEM]
        sA[Steel Furring Channel]
        sB[Gypsum Board 12.5 mm single or double layer]
    end
    subgraph Cavity[PLENUM CAVITY]
        cA[Mineral Wool 50 mm for insulation]
    end
    tA --> cA
    cA --> sA
    sA --> sB
    noteC1[FRR 1 to 2 hours per IS 3809] --- sB
    noteC2[Stability from steel channel] --- sA
    noteC3[Insulation from mineral wool] --- cA
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Wall vs Ceiling FRR Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Occupancy Selection] --> occ1{Occupancy Type}
    occ1 -->|Residential Group A| A1[FRR 1 hour]
    occ1 -->|Educational Group B| B1[FRR 2 hours]
    occ1 -->|Institutional Group C| C1[FRR 2 hours]
    occ1 -->|Assembly Group D| D1[FRR 2 hours]
    occ1 -->|Business Group E| E1[FRR 1 hour]
    occ1 -->|Mercantile Group F| F1[FRR 2 hours]
    occ1 -->|Industrial Group G| G1[FRR 2 to 4 hours]
    occ1 -->|Storage Group H| H1[FRR 2 to 4 hours]
    occ1 -->|Hazardous Group J| J1[FRR 4 hours]
    A1 --> matSel{Material Selection}
    B1 --> matSel
    C1 --> matSel
    D1 --> matSel
    E1 --> matSel
    F1 --> matSel
    G1 --> matSel
    H1 --> matSel
    J1 --> matSel
    matSel --> conc[Reinforced Concrete Wall]
    matSel --> masonry[Brick Masonry Wall]
    matSel --> gypsum[Gypsum Board Partition]
    matSel --> spray[Vermiculite Spray on Steel]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Fire Separation" as applied to buildings. State the role of NBC 2016 in fire separation. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Fire separation refers to the provision of adequate **spatial distance** and/or **fire-resistant construction** between two buildings or fire compartments to prevent the spread of fire, heat, and smoke from a fire-involved building to an adjacent one. The role of NBC 2016 Part 4 is to prescribe the **minimum safe distance**, **fire resistance ratings of walls and ceilings**, and the **construction materials** that constitute compliant fire separation for different occupancy groups. It also defines the **compartmentation** strategy using fire-resistant walls and floors.

---

### Question 2: List any three criteria for determining safe distance between buildings. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The three criteria for safe distance determination are:
1. **Ignition of Combustibles Criterion** — Radiant heat flux on adjacent façade must not exceed **$12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$** to prevent ignition of wood, paper, plastics.
2. **Non-combustible Damage Criterion** — Heat flux must not exceed **$25 \text{ kW/m}^2$** to prevent damage to non-combustible façades.
3. **Glass Breakage Criterion** — Incident flux on standard annealed glass should not exceed **$12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$** to avoid window cracking and secondary fire spread.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[A] (a)** Explain the principle of calculation of safe distance between two buildings based on radiant heat transfer. State the governing equation and its significance. **[7 Marks]**

**[A] (b)** Two adjacent buildings have rectangular windows of width **3.0 m** and height **2.0 m** facing each other. In a fully developed room fire, the flame temperature is **1100 K** with effective emissivity **0.85**. Take atmospheric transmissivity as **1.0** and critical heat flux for combustible materials as **$12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$**. Calculate the safe distance between the buildings. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Model Solution for [A] (a):

The safe distance is derived from the principle that **radiant heat transfer** between a fire-source surface and a receiving surface falls off as the square of the separation distance. The fundamental equation is:

$$q_r = \varepsilon_f \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^4 \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau_{atm}$$

where $F_{12}$ is the configuration (view) factor. For the point-source far-field approximation, $F_{12} \approx A_1/(2\pi D^2)$, leading to:

$$D \geq \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_f \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^4 \cdot A_1 \cdot \tau_{atm}}{2\pi \cdot q_{crit}}}$$

**Significance:**
- Quantifies the **inverse-square attenuation** of heat flux.
- Allows designers to determine **minimum setback** in urban planning.
- Ensures **neighbouring buildings remain tenable** during the design fire.
- Forms the basis of code requirements in NBC 2016.

The three required conditions are: (i) geometry, (ii) flame temperature and emissivity, and (iii) acceptance criteria $q_{crit}$.

#### Model Solution for [A] (b):

**Given:**
- $W = 3.0 \text{ m}$, $H = 2.0 \text{ m}$ $\Rightarrow A_1 = 6.0 \text{ m}^2$
- $T_f = 1100 \text{ K}$, $\varepsilon_f = 0.85$
- $\tau_{atm} = 1.0$, $q_{crit} = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2 = 12500 \text{ W/m}^2$

**Step 1 — Emissive Power:**

$$E = \varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^4 = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1100)^4$$

$$(1100)^4 = 1.4641 \times 10^{12}$$

$$E = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.4641 \times 10^{12}$$

$$E = 0.85 \times 83000 = 70550 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 70.55 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Far-Field Approximation:**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot A_1}{2\pi \cdot q_{crit}}} = \sqrt{\frac{70550 \times 6.0}{2\pi \times 12500}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{423300}{78539.8}} = \sqrt{5.389} = 2.32 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KTU Safety Factor of 1.5:**

$$D_{design} = 1.5 \times 2.32 = 3.48 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D_{design} \approx 3.5 \text{ m}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[B] (a)** Describe the design principles of fire-resistant walls. Explain the three criteria of failure — stability, integrity, and insulation. **[7 Marks]**

**[B] (b)** A reinforced concrete wall of thickness **200 mm** is used as a fire-resistant barrier. Discuss its expected fire resistance rating (FRR) and recommend suitable construction detailing for the wall to achieve a 4-hour FRR. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Model Solution for [B] (a):

A **fire-resistant wall** is a vertical building element designed to prevent fire spread for a specified duration (typically 1, 2, 3, 4, or 6 hours). Its design must satisfy three failure criteria as per **IS 3809:1979** and **NBC 2016 Part 4**:

**1. Stability (Load-bearing capacity):**
- The wall must continue to carry its design load (self-weight + imposed) without collapse.
- For concrete, the criterion is **mean compressive strength retention** during the fire.
- For steel, the **critical temperature** of structural steel is approximately **550°C**.

**2. Integrity (Flame/Smoke Passage):**
- The wall must not develop **cracks, openings, or fissures** through which flames or hot gases can pass.
- Tested using a **cotton-wool pad** test on the unexposed face — no flaming or glowing is permitted.
- Gap-junctions and service penetrations must be **fire-stopped** with intumescent sealants.

**3. Insulation (Heat Transmission):**
- The temperature on the **unexposed face** must not exceed:
  - **Average rise of 140°C** above ambient, and
  - **Maximum single-point rise of 180°C**.
- This is governed by the **thermal conductivity, density, and thickness** of the wall material.

**Design Principles:**
- **Material selection:** Concrete, brick masonry, gypsum board, vermiculite-sprayed steel.
- **Cover to reinforcement:** Minimum cover of **25–40 mm** to delay rebar heating.
- **Continuity:** Walls must extend from floor-slab to floor-slab with no gaps.
- **Service penetrations:** All ducts, conduits, pipes must be sealed with **fire-rated sealants**.

#### Model Solution for [B] (b):

**Given:** RC wall, thickness = 200 mm.

**Step 1 — Fire Resistance Rating (FRR) Estimation:**

Based on IS 3809 and Table 1 of NBC 2016, an RC wall of 200 mm thickness achieves approximately a **3-hour to 4-hour FRR**, depending on:
- Concrete grade (M20 to M40)
- Cover to reinforcement
- Aggregate type (siliceous vs. calcareous)
- Load level (axial load ratio)

**Step 2 — Recommendations for 4-Hour FRR:**

| Parameter | Recommended Value | Reason |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| Concrete grade | **M30 minimum** | Maintains compressive strength at high T |
| Cover to main rebar | **40 mm minimum** | Delays rebar reaching 550°C |
| Aggregate type | **Calcareous (limestone)** | Better fire performance than siliceous |
| Wall thickness | **200 mm or greater** | Provides thermal mass |
| Siliceous aggregate | Increase to 220 mm | Compensates for higher thermal conductivity |
| Plaster finish | **12 mm cement plaster both faces** | Adds thermal insulation |
| Stiffener columns | At 3 m spacing | Maintains stability under thermal gradient |
| Vertical bars | **12 mm φ @ 200 mm c/c** | Crack control |

**Step 3 — Design Detailing:**
- **Continuity at junctions:** Use **fire-rated sealant** at slab-wall interface.
- **Service penetrations:** All electrical conduits, AC pipes, plumbing must be **fire-stopped**.
- **Vertical reinforcement:** Provide **double-curtain reinforcement** for crack control.
- **Spalling protection:** Use **polypropylene fibres (1–2 kg/m³)** in concrete mix to prevent explosive spalling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** (typically 1.5×) in safe-distance numerical problems — loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the three FRR criteria** (stability, integrity, insulation) with fire-resistance *test* parameters — write all three with **numerical thresholds** for full credit.
> 3. **Mixing units** between Celsius and Kelvin in Stefan-Boltzmann equation — must convert $T$ to **Kelvin**.
> 4. **Omitting fire-stopping** in detailing answers — examiners expect explicit mention of sealants, cover, and continuity.
> 5. **Using $\sigma$ without stating units** — always write $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ at least once.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ⭐ **Fire separation** = spatial distance **+** structural fire resistance (acting together).
- ⭐ **Safe distance** is governed by **radiant heat transfer**, not convective spread.
- ⭐ **Stefan-Boltzmann** equation: $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 F_{12}$ — temperature must be in **Kelvin**.
- ⭐ **Three critical flux levels:** **12.5 kW/m²** (combustibles), **25 kW/m²** (non-combustibles), **4 kW/m²** (occupant tenability).
- ⭐ **Point-source approximation** valid when $D \gg W, H$.
- ⭐ **Safety factor of 1.5** is standard in KTU numericals.
- ⭐ **Three FRR criteria:** Stability, Integrity, Insulation (per **IS 3809**).
- ⭐ **Insulation limits:** Average rise $\leq 140°C$, Max single-point rise $\leq 180°C$.
- ⭐ **Standard fire curve:** $T_t = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ — used in IS 3809 fire tests.
- ⭐ **Fire-resistant walls** require **continuity from floor to floor**, **fire-stopping** at penetrations, and **adequate cover to rebar** (40 mm for 4-hr FRR).
- ⭐ **Fire-resistant ceilings** use **gypsum boards + mineral wool cavity + steel furring**; suspended-ceiling systems offer **1–2 hour FRR**.
- ⭐ **NBC 2016 Part 4** is the **primary code reference** in India — must be cited.
- ⭐ **Higher emissivity (0.7–0.9)** of sooty flames — never assume 1.0 (blackbody).
- ⭐ **Configuration (view) factor** $F_{12}$ depends on geometry — for parallel rectangles, use the closed-form expression with $X = W/D$ and $Y = H/D$.
- ⭐ **High-rise buildings** require **stricter separation** due to wind-driven fire plumes and stack effect.
- ⭐ **Hazardous occupancies (Group J)** demand **4-hour FRR** and **larger safe distances**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** In the KTU exam, always start any numerical with a clear "Given Data" box, convert all temperatures to Kelvin, state the governing equation symbolically, and end with a clearly boxed answer. This presentation style alone can earn 1–2 **free** evaluation marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Resistant Screens - Solid Screens and Water Curtains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire Resistant Screens - Solid Screens and Water Curtains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Resistant Screens — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **fire resistant screen** is an engineered vertical barrier — either *physical* (solid construction) or *dynamic* (water-based) — designed to **subdivide a building, atrium, or open space into separate fire compartments**, thereby preventing the lateral spread of flames, hot gases, smoke, and radiant heat across the fire-resisting boundary for a specified period under standard fire exposure (typically ISO 834 or ASTM E119 time-temperature curve).

Fire resistant screens are mandated by the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016), Part 4 — Fire and Life Safety**, particularly Clause **4.4.3 (Compartmentation)** and Clause **4.6 (Atrium Safety)**. They are essential in large open-plan structures where traditional fire walls would obstruct functionality, aesthetics, or visual connectivity.

## 1.2 Classification of Fire Resistant Screens

| **Type** | **Nature** | **Primary Function** |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Screens** | Static, non-combustible physical barrier | Complete blockage of flame, smoke, and radiant heat |
| **Water Curtains** | Active, water-spray dynamic barrier | Heat absorption, smoke dilution, radiation attenuation |

## 1.3 Solid Screens — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Screen = Permanent Firewall with Openings or Glass**
> Think of a solid fire screen as a **"fire break"** in a forest. Just as a cleared strip of land stops a wildfire from jumping to the next patch of trees, a solid screen physically interrupts the path of fire, smoke, and heat in a building.

### 1.3.1 Common Materials Used
- **Reinforced concrete** (slabs/walls) — fire resistance **2 to 4 hours**
- **Brick masonry** (clay/fly-ash) — fire resistance **2 to 4 hours**
- **Hollow concrete blocks** with grouted reinforcement — **2 to 4 hours**
- **Gypsum board assemblies** (Type X, multi-layer) — **1 to 2 hours**
- **Fire-rated glass** (wired, ceramic, intumescent laminated) — **30 minutes to 2 hours**
- **Steel framing with fire-protective cladding** (gypsum/vermiculite spray) — **1 to 3 hours**

### 1.3.2 Functional Criteria (The Three E's of Fire Resistance)

A solid fire screen must satisfy the three fundamental criteria during its rating period:

$$E_1: \text{Stability} \quad | \quad E_2: \text{Integrity} \quad | \quad E_3: \text{Insulation}$$

1. **Stability (R)** — The screen must not collapse or lose structural load-bearing capacity.
2. **Integrity (E)** — No through-cracks, openings, or passage of flames/hot gases.
3. **Insulation (I)** — The unexposed face temperature must not rise by more than **140 °C** above ambient (average) or **180 °C** at any single point.

## 1.4 Water Curtains — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Curtain = A "Heat-Eating Shower"**
> Imagine standing between a bonfire and a lawn sprinkler running at full blast. The water droplets absorb the radiant heat before it reaches you, cooling the air and washing the smoke upward. This is exactly what a fire-engineered water curtain does — it is a **deluge-type water spray system** that creates a continuous water barrier between a fire source and an adjacent area.

### 1.4.1 Operating Principle
A water curtain absorbs heat through two simultaneous mechanisms:
1. **Sensible heating** of water from ambient to **100 °C**
2. **Latent heat absorption** during vaporization at **100 °C** (phase change)

This dual cooling action is extraordinarily effective because the **latent heat of vaporization of water (≈ 2260 kJ/kg)** is over **500 times greater** than the sensible heat required to raise 1 kg of water by 1 °C.

### 1.4.2 Types of Water Curtain Systems

| **Type** | **Trigger Mechanism** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|
| **Open Nozzle (Deluge)** | Smoke/heat detector + deluge valve | High-risk industrial, atria |
| **Closed Nozzle (Automatic Sprinkler)** | Fusible link/glass bulb (57–74 °C) | Light commercial, warehouses |
| **High-Velocity Water Spray** | Pneumatic/electric detection | Transformer enclosures, oil fires |
| **Water Mist Curtain** | High-pressure pumps (40–200 bar) | Marine, tunnels, heritage buildings |

---

## 1.5 Why Fire Resistant Screens Are Needed in Modern Buildings

- **Atriums and large open spaces** — modern architecture demands visual openness, which traditional fire walls cannot provide.
- **Compartmentation in mixed-use buildings** — separating hotel floors from office areas.
- **Protection of escape routes** — corridors, lobbies, and stairwell enclosures.
- **Boundary walls between adjacent properties** — preventing fire spread from one building to another.
- **Transformer and equipment rooms** — where a fire could quickly propagate through cable openings.
- **Glass walls with fire rating** — allowing daylighting while maintaining compartmentation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solid Screens — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Heat Transfer Through a Solid Screen
A solid fire screen resists heat transfer through three modes:

1. **Conduction** through the wall thickness
2. **Convection** at the unexposed face
3. **Radiation** from the unexposed surface

The **thermal transmittance (U-value)** governs conduction. The temperature on the unexposed side after time *t* of fire exposure is given by the **infinite slab solution** of the heat conduction equation.

For a homogeneous slab of thickness *L*, thermal conductivity *k*, density *ρ*, and specific heat *c*, the **thermal diffusivity** is:

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \cdot c}$$

The dimensionless **Fourier number** after time *t*:

$$Fo = \frac{\alpha \cdot t}{L^2}$$

When **Fo ≥ 0.2**, the slab behaves as a "thermally thick" element, and simple formulas can be used for temperature rise estimation.

### 2.1.2 Standard Fire Resistance Periods (As per IS 3809 / NBC 2016)

| **Construction** | **Thickness (mm)** | **Fire Rating (hours)** |
|---|---|---|
| Reinforced concrete | 120 | 2.0 |
| Reinforced concrete | 200 | 4.0 |
| Brick masonry (unplastered) | 200 | 4.0 |
| Hollow concrete block (grouted) | 200 | 4.0 |
| Gypsum board (2 × 12.5 mm Type X) | 25 | 1.0 |
| Fire-rated glass (ceramic) | 6 | 1.0 |
| Steel column (with 50 mm gypsum encasement) | — | 2.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Highlight:** KTU board questions frequently test the **three-criteria concept (R, E, I)** and the **time-temperature curve** of ISO 834. Memorize the standard curve equation: $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20$, where *T* is in °C and *t* is in minutes.

## 2.2 Water Curtains — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Heat Absorption Mechanism

A water curtain absorbs fire heat in **three sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Sensible Heating (ambient → 100 °C):**
Heat absorbed to raise water temperature:

$$Q_1 = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{boil} - T_{amb})$$

where:
- $C_p$ = specific heat of water = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- $T_{boil}$ = 100 °C
- $T_{amb}$ = ambient temperature (typically 25–30 °C)

**Stage 2 — Latent Heat Absorption (vaporization at 100 °C):**

$$Q_2 = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$$

where $h_{fg}$ = **latent heat of vaporization = 2260 kJ/kg**

**Stage 3 — Sensible Heating of Steam (100 °C → flame temperature):**
This is usually neglected in conservative design, as the water curtain primarily absorbs heat up to and including vaporization.

### 2.2.2 Total Heat Absorbed per Unit Mass of Water

$$Q_{total} = C_p \cdot (100 - T_{amb}) + h_{fg}$$

At $T_{amb} = 25 °C$:

$$Q_{total} = 4.186 \times 75 + 2260 = 313.95 + 2260 \approx 2574 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This means **1 kg of water absorbs ~2574 kJ of heat** in a properly designed water curtain — equivalent to the energy released by burning approximately **80 grams of wood**! This is why water curtains are so effective for fire separation.

### 2.2.3 Water Curtain Design Parameters

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Typical Value** | **Code Reference** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water density (application rate) | $q$ | **5–10 L/min/m²** | NFPA 15 / NBC 2016 |
| Spray nozzle pressure | $P$ | **1.4–3.5 bar** (open) | NFPA 15 |
| Spray angle | $\theta$ | **60°–120°** | Manufacturer |
| Nozzle spacing | $s$ | **1.5–3.0 m** | NBC 2016 Cl. 4.6 |
| Droplet size | $d$ | **200–1000 μm** | NFPA 15 |

### 2.2.4 Radiation Attenuation by Water Curtain

A water curtain attenuates radiative heat flux following an **exponential decay (Beer-Lambert type)** relationship:

$$I_{trans} = I_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident radiant heat flux (kW/m²)
- $I_{trans}$ = transmitted heat flux behind curtain
- $k$ = extinction coefficient (depends on droplet density and size)
- $x$ = effective curtain thickness

For a well-designed open water curtain with density **8 L/min/m²**, radiation attenuation of **60–80%** is achievable.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Formula** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ | ISO 834 standard fire temperature (°C vs. min) |
| 2 | $Fo = \alpha t / L^2$ | Fourier number for slab heating |
| 3 | $Q_1 = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed by water |
| 4 | $Q_2 = \dot{m} h_{fg}$ | Latent heat absorbed (vaporization) |
| 5 | $Q_{total} \approx 2574$ kJ/kg | Total heat absorbed per kg water (at 25 °C ambient) |
| 6 | $q = Q_{fire} / (A \cdot h_{fg})$ | Water density required for fire heat load |
| 7 | $I_{trans} = I_0 e^{-kx}$ | Radiation attenuation through curtain |
| 8 | $t_{fire} = (V_{water} \cdot h_{fg}) / Q_{fire}$ | Curtain endurance time (s) |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho_w \cdot q \cdot A$ | Required water mass flow rate |
| 10 | $\dot{V} = q \cdot A$ | Required water volumetric flow rate |

**Where:**
- $\dot{m}$ = water mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $q$ = water application density (L/min/m² or m/s)
- $A$ = curtain projected area (m²)
- $\rho_w$ = water density = **1000 kg/m³**

## 2.4 Comparison: Solid Screens vs. Water Curtains

| **Criterion** | **Solid Screen** | **Water Curtain** |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | Passive (static) | Active (dynamic) |
| **Material** | Concrete, masonry, glass, gypsum | Water + nozzles + piping |
| **Fire rating** | 1–4 hours (fixed) | Continuous (while water is supplied) |
| **Smoke blocking** | Excellent (if sealed) | Moderate (depends on curtain density) |
| **Heat blocking** | Excellent (insulation) | Excellent (latent heat absorption) |
| **Visual transparency** | Limited (opaque, except glass) | High (water droplets barely visible) |
| **Capital cost** | Medium to high | High |
| **Operating cost** | Very low | Medium (water + pumping) |
| **Maintenance** | Minimal | Regular testing required |
| **Reliability** | Very high | Depends on water supply & detection |
| **Best application** | Permanent compartmentation | Atria, large openings, transformers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - **Solid screens** are the **backbone of compartmentation** in hospitals, hotels, and high-rise buildings (NBC 2016, Clause 4.4.3).
> - **Water curtains** are commonly used in **atrium separation** (NBC 2016, Clause 4.6), **transformer protection** (IS 3034), and **process industries** (NFPA 15).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Heat Absorbed by 1 kg of Water in a Curtain

**Problem Statement:**
Derive the total heat absorbed by 1 kg of water when it enters a water curtain at 25 °C ambient temperature and is fully vaporized.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the two heat-absorption stages**

Water entering the curtain at $T_{amb} = 25 °C$ must first be heated to its boiling point ($T_b = 100 °C$), then vaporized completely.

**Step 2 — Calculate sensible heat (Stage 1: heating from 25 °C to 100 °C)**

$$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_b - T_{amb})$$

Substituting values for *m* = 1 kg, $C_p$ = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K), and $\Delta T$ = 75 K:

$$Q_{sensible} = 1 \times 4.186 \times (100 - 25)$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 1 \times 4.186 \times 75 = 313.95 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate latent heat (Stage 2: vaporization at 100 °C)**

$$Q_{latent} = m \cdot h_{fg}$$

For *m* = 1 kg and $h_{fg} = 2260$ kJ/kg:

$$Q_{latent} = 1 \times 2260 = 2260 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two stages to obtain total heat absorbed**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent}$$

$$Q_{total} = 313.95 + 2260$$

$$Q_{total} = 2573.95 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 2574 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **fundamental KTU board value** — the heat absorption capacity of water in a fire curtain. Note that **~88% of the total heat is absorbed during vaporization (latent stage)**, highlighting why water is so effective in fire suppression.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Water Application Density for a Given Fire

**Problem Statement:**
A transformer enclosure fire releases heat at the rate of **Q = 5000 kW** across a screen area of **A = 25 m²**. Determine the minimum water application density required to absorb this heat, assuming all the water is fully vaporized.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the governing energy balance**

The water curtain must absorb the entire fire heat flux $Q_{fire}$ (kW) to prevent spread. The heat absorbed by the water must equal the fire heat load:

$$\dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} = Q_{fire}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the required water mass flow rate**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_{fire}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{5000 \text{ kJ/s}}{2260 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$\dot{m} = 2.212 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volumetric flow rate**

Using water density $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_w} = \frac{2.212}{1000} = 2.212 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to L/min (more common unit in fire protection)**

$$\dot{V} = 2.212 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 1000 \text{ L/m}^3 \times 60 \text{ s/min}$$

$$\dot{V} = 132.74 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the water application density over screen area**

$$q = \frac{\dot{V}}{A} = \frac{132.74 \text{ L/min}}{25 \text{ m}^2}$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 5.31 \text{ L/min/m}^2}$$

**Step 6 — Verification against code**

NBC 2016 / NFPA 15 recommends a minimum water density of **5–10 L/min/m²** for water curtains. Our calculated value of **5.31 L/min/m²** satisfies the lower bound. **Design passes.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Endurance Time of a Water Curtain with Limited Water Supply

**Problem Statement:**
A water curtain system has a water storage tank of $V_{tank} = 5000$ litres. If the fire heat release rate is $Q_{fire} = 3000$ kW, calculate the endurance time of the curtain.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert tank volume to mass of water**

$$m_{water} = V_{tank} \times \rho_w = 5000 \text{ L} \times 1 \text{ kg/L} = 5000 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total heat that the available water can absorb**

$$Q_{absorbed} = m_{water} \times h_{fg} = 5000 \times 2260 = 1.13 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Endurance time from energy balance**

$$t = \frac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{fire}} = \frac{1.13 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ}}{3000 \text{ kJ/s}}$$

$$t = 3766.7 \text{ s} \approx 62.8 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the safety factor (typically 1.5–2.0 per NBC 2016)**

$$t_{design} = \frac{62.8}{1.5} \approx 41.9 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The endurance calculation is critical for designing water curtains where the **water supply (pumps, tanks, municipal lines)** may be limited. The designer must ensure the curtain can hold off the fire until fire services arrive (typically 15–30 minutes for urban response).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Radiation Attenuation Through a Water Curtain

**Problem Statement:**
A water curtain with extinction coefficient *k* = 1.5 m⁻¹ is installed between a fire source emitting $I_0 = 30$ kW/m² and a protected area. If the effective curtain thickness is *x* = 0.8 m, calculate the transmitted heat flux.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the exponential attenuation law**

$$I_{trans} = I_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot x}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$I_{trans} = 30 \cdot e^{-1.5 \times 0.8} = 30 \cdot e^{-1.2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential**

$$e^{-1.2} = 0.3012$$

**Step 4 — Compute the transmitted flux**

$$I_{trans} = 30 \times 0.3012 = 9.04 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Calculate attenuation percentage**

$$\text{Attenuation} = \frac{I_0 - I_{trans}}{I_0} \times 100\% = \frac{30 - 9.04}{30} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Attenuation} \approx 69.9\%}$$

This demonstrates that a properly designed water curtain can block **~70% of incident radiation**, reducing the transmitted flux from 30 kW/m² (which would cause secondary ignition of most materials) to 9 kW/m² (which is below the threshold for spontaneous ignition of wood — ~12 kW/m²).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Solid Screen vs. Water Curtain

| **Scenario** | **Recommended Solution** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Permanent compartmentation in a hospital | **Solid screen** (gypsum/concrete) | Reliability, zero operational cost, 2-hour rating per NBC |
| Atrium separation in a shopping mall | **Water curtain** | Architectural openness, transparency |
| Transformer room with oil-filled equipment | **Solid screen + water curtain (hybrid)** | Solid blocks flames; curtain cools oil mist |
| Industrial process building with large openings | **Water curtain** (deluge) | Continuous heat absorption, no structural obstruction |
| Hotel corridor walls (guestroom separation) | **Solid screen** (gypsum board) | Mandatory by NBC, low cost |
| Cable tunnels / data centers | **Water mist curtain** | Prevents fire spread, minimal water damage to electronics |
| Boundary wall between two warehouses | **Solid masonry wall** | Permanent fire barrier, 4-hour rating |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Question:** A building atrium has a proposed water curtain of area 40 m². The design fire heat release rate is 8000 kW. The water enters the curtain at 30 °C. Calculate:
1. Total heat absorbed per kg of water
2. Required water flow rate in L/min
3. Required water density in L/min/m²
4. Endurance time if the water supply is 15000 L

### Solution

**(1) Total heat absorbed per kg of water (at T_amb = 30 °C):**

$$Q_{total} = C_p(100 - 30) + h_{fg} = 4.186 \times 70 + 2260$$

$$Q_{total} = 293.02 + 2260 = 2553.02 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(2) Required water flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_{fire}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{8000}{2260} = 3.54 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{V} = \frac{3.54 \times 60}{1} = 212.4 \text{ L/min}$$

**(3) Water application density:**

$$q = \frac{212.4}{40} = 5.31 \text{ L/min/m}^2$$

**(4) Endurance time with 15000 L supply:**

$$m_{water} = 15000 \text{ kg}$$

$$t = \frac{15000 \times 2553.02}{8000} = \frac{3.83 \times 10^7}{8000} = 4786 \text{ s} \approx 79.8 \text{ min}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Solid Fire Screen

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Fire Source Side"]:::fire --> B["Solid Screen Assembly"]:::barrier
    B --> C["Protected Side"]:::safe
    B1["Stability R - structural load capacity"]:::crit --> B
    B2["Integrity E - no cracks or openings"]:::crit --> B
    B3["Insulation I - unexposed face temperature rise less than 140 C average"]:::crit --> B
    M1["Reinforced Concrete Slab 120 to 200 mm"]:::mat --> B
    M2["Brick Masonry 200 mm"]:::mat --> B
    M3["Gypsum Board Type X Multi Layer"]:::mat --> B
    M4["Fire Rated Glass Ceramic or Wired"]:::mat --> B
    M5["Steel Column with Gypsum Encasement"]:::mat --> B

    classDef fire fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff
    classDef barrier fill:#495057,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef safe fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff
    classDef crit fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000
    classDef mat fill:#a5d8ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Water Curtain System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Fire Detection - Smoke or Heat Sensor"]:::detect --> B["Control Panel Signal"]
    B --> C["Deluge Valve Opens"]
    C --> D["Pressurized Water Supply Activates"]
    D --> E["Water Distribution Piping Network"]
    E --> F1["Open Spray Nozzle 1"]:::nozzle
    E --> F2["Open Spray Nozzle 2"]:::nozzle
    E --> F3["Open Spray Nozzle 3"]:::nozzle
    F1 --> G["Water Curtain Formation - 5 to 10 L per min per m2"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H1["Sensible Heat Absorption - C p x delta T"]:::absorb
    G --> H2["Latent Heat Absorption - h fg equals 2260 kJ per kg"]:::absorb
    H1 --> I["Fire Heat Attenuation - 60 to 80 percent"]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J["Protected Zone Maintained Below Ignition Threshold"]:::safe

    classDef detect fill:#ff8787,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff
    classDef nozzle fill:#339af0,stroke:#1971c2,color:#fff
    classDef absorb fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1864ab,color:#fff
    classDef safe fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Flowchart — Solid Screen vs. Water Curtain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Fire Separation Requirement Identified"]:::start --> Q1{"Is full visual transparency needed?"}:::decision
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is water supply reliable and continuous?"}:::decision
    Q1 -- No --> Q3["Use Solid Screen - Concrete Masonry or Gypsum"]:::solid
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4["Use Water Curtain - Deluge System"]:::water
    Q2 -- No --> Q5["Use Solid Screen - Risk of water failure too high"]:::solid
    Q4 --> Q6["Design water density 5 to 10 L per min per m2"]
    Q4 --> Q7["Design nozzle spacing 1.5 to 3.0 m"]
    Q4 --> Q8["Connect to dedicated fire pump and tank"]
    Q3 --> Q9["Verify fire rating 1 to 4 hours per IS 3809"]
    Q3 --> Q10["Ensure R E I compliance per IS 3809"]
    Q3 --> Q11["Seal all penetrations with fire stops"]
    Q5 --> Q9

    classDef start fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#e8590c,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#ffe066,stroke:#fab005,color:#000
    classDef solid fill:#868e96,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef water fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#1971c2,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Heat Transfer Mechanism Through a Solid Screen

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Fire Side Temperature T f up to 1000 C"]:::hot --> B["Conduction Through Wall Thickness L"]
    B --> C["Convection at Unexposed Surface"]
    B --> D["Radiation from Unexposed Surface"]
    C --> E["Protected Side T rise less than 140 C average"]:::safe
    D --> E

    P1["Mode 1 Conduction - controlled by k and thickness L"]:::mode --> B
    P2["Mode 2 Convection - governed by h conv"]:::mode --> C
    P3["Mode 3 Radiation - governed by epsilon and T s 4"]:::mode --> D

    classDef hot fill:#fa5252,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff
    classDef safe fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff
    classDef mode fill:#dee2e6,stroke:#495057,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a fire resistant screen. Differentiate between solid screens and water curtains.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **fire resistant screen** is a vertical barrier — physical or water-based — designed to prevent the lateral spread of fire, smoke, and radiant heat between building compartments, achieving a specified fire resistance rating under standard fire exposure.

| **Feature** | **Solid Screen** | **Water Curtain** |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Static (passive) | Dynamic (active) |
| Material | Concrete/masonry/glass | Water + nozzles |
| Fire rating | Fixed 1–4 h | Continuous (supply dependent) |
| Maintenance | Minimal | Periodic testing required |

**(3 marks)** ✔

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**State and explain the three criteria (R, E, I) of fire resistance for a solid screen.**

**Model Answer:**

The three criteria as per **IS 3809:1979** and **ISO 834** are:

1. **Stability (R):** The element must maintain its load-bearing capacity and not collapse during the fire exposure period.
2. **Integrity (E):** The element must prevent the passage of flames and hot gases through cracks, joints, or openings.
3. **Insulation (I):** The temperature on the unexposed face must not exceed **140 °C average** rise or **180 °C** at any single point above ambient.

These are jointly denoted as the **REI rating** of a fire-resisting element.

**(3 marks)** ✔

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction details and materials used in **solid fire resistant screens**. Discuss their application in buildings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solid reinforced concrete screen of thickness **150 mm** is exposed to standard fire on one side. If the thermal conductivity *k* = 1.4 W/(m·K), density *ρ* = 2400 kg/m³, and specific heat *c* = 900 J/(kg·K), determine:
- (i) The thermal diffusivity α
- (ii) The Fourier number *Fo* after 60 minutes of fire exposure
- (iii) Comment on whether the slab is "thermally thick" or "thermally thin". **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction Details of Solid Fire Screens:**

1. **Reinforced Concrete Screens:**
   - Minimum thickness **120 mm** for 2-hour rating
   - Reinforcement cover ≥ **25 mm** for slabs, ≥ **35 mm** for beams
   - Aggregates: siliceous, calcareous, or lightweight (LWAC gives best performance)
   - Best suited for permanent compartmentation in high-rise buildings

2. **Masonry Screens:**
   - Solid clay brick or fly-ash brick, 200 mm thick = **4-hour rating**
   - Hollow concrete blocks, grouted and reinforced = **4-hour rating**
   - Plastered on both sides for integrity

3. **Gypsum Board Assemblies:**
   - 2 × 12.5 mm Type X gypsum = **1-hour rating**
   - 3 × 15.9 mm Type X gypsum = **2-hour rating**
   - Steel stud framing at 600 mm c/c
   - Lightweight, dry construction

4. **Fire-Rated Glass:**
   - **Wired glass** (steel mesh embedded) — 30–60 minutes
   - **Ceramic glass** (transparent, no wire) — 1–2 hours
   - **Intumescent laminated glass** — 1–3 hours
   - Used in atria, lobbies, and stairwell enclosures

**Applications:**
- Hospital ward separation, hotel guestroom walls, office floor separation
- Lift shaft and staircase enclosure walls
- Transformer room and generator room walls
- Kitchen and storage area boundaries
- Boundary walls in industrial warehouses

**(7 marks)** ✔

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- L = 150 mm = 0.15 m
- k = 1.4 W/(m·K)
- ρ = 2400 kg/m³
- c = 900 J/(kg·K)
- t = 60 min = 3600 s

**(i) Thermal Diffusivity:**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \cdot c} = \frac{1.4}{2400 \times 900}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{1.4}{2.16 \times 10^6} = 6.48 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**(ii) Fourier Number:**

$$Fo = \frac{\alpha \cdot t}{L^2} = \frac{6.48 \times 10^{-7} \times 3600}{(0.15)^2}$$

$$Fo = \frac{2.333 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0225} = 0.1037$$

**(iii) Comment:**

Since **Fo = 0.104 < 0.2**, the slab is **"thermally thin"** at this stage. This means the heat wave has not yet fully penetrated the slab thickness, and the unexposed face temperature rise is small. However, as fire exposure continues and *Fo* exceeds 0.2, the slab will behave as thermally thick, and the unexposed side will begin to heat up significantly.

**(7 marks)** ✔

---

### Question B (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Alternate]**

**(a)** Describe the **principle, components, and design parameters** of a water curtain fire protection system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water curtain is to be designed to protect an atrium opening of **30 m²** from a fire releasing **6000 kW** of heat. The water enters the curtain at 28 °C ambient. Calculate:
- (i) Total heat absorbed per kg of water
- (ii) Required water mass flow rate
- (iii) Required water volumetric flow rate in L/min
- (iv) Water application density in L/min/m²
- (v) Comment on whether the design satisfies NBC 2016. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle:**
A water curtain is an active fire separation system that uses a continuous sheet of water sprayed through **open nozzles** to absorb fire heat, dilute smoke, and attenuate radiant heat flux, thereby preventing fire spread across large openings or atria.

**Components:**

1. **Water Supply System:**
   - Dedicated fire pump (electric + diesel jockey)
   - Water storage tank (minimum 1-hour capacity)
   - Municipal/booster connection

2. **Detection & Control:**
   - Smoke/heat detectors
   - Deluge valve (hydraulic or pneumatic release)
   - Manual release station

3. **Distribution Network:**
   - Piping (black steel, Class C)
   - Open spray nozzles (spray angle 60°–120°)

4. **Drainage:**
   - Floor drains sized for full flow
   - Sloped floors (minimum 1:100 gradient)

**Design Parameters:**
- **Water density:** 5–10 L/min/m²
- **Nozzle pressure:** 1.4–3.5 bar
- **Nozzle spacing:** 1.5–3.0 m
- **Spray angle:** 60°–120° (typically 90°)
- **Droplet size:** 200–1000 μm
- **Detection time:** < 30 seconds
- **Valve operation time:** < 10 seconds

**(7 marks)** ✔

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- A = 30 m²
- Q_fire = 6000 kW
- T_amb = 28 °C
- h_fg = 2260 kJ/kg
- C_p = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)

**(i) Total heat absorbed per kg of water:**

$$Q_{total} = C_p(100 - 28) + h_{fg}$$

$$Q_{total} = 4.186 \times 72 + 2260 = 301.4 + 2260$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 2561.4 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(ii) Required water mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_{fire}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{6000}{2260} = 2.655 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(iii) Required volumetric flow rate:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_w} \times 60 = \frac{2.655 \times 60}{1} = 159.3 \text{ L/min}$$

**(iv) Water application density:**

$$q = \frac{\dot{V}}{A} = \frac{159.3}{30} = 5.31 \text{ L/min/m}^2$$

**(v) Code Compliance Check:**

As per **NBC 2016 (Part 4, Clause 4.6 — Atrium Safety)** and **NFPA 15**, the recommended water density for atrium water curtains is **5–10 L/min/m²**. Our calculated value of **5.31 L/min/m²** lies within this range, satisfying the lower bound. **The design is code-compliant.** ✔

**(7 marks)** ✔

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units** — Always include units in the final answer (L/min, kW, kg/s). Marks are deducted for unit-less numerical answers.
> 2. **Confusing REI criteria** — Students often write only *R* and *E*, forgetting the **Insulation (I)** criterion. Always state all three.
> 3. **Using only sensible heat** — A common error is calculating only the *sensible* heat absorbed by water, ignoring the **latent heat of vaporization** (2260 kJ/kg). This underestimates the curtain capacity by **~85%**.
> 4. **Not applying safety factors** — Endurance time calculations must use a safety factor of **1.5 to 2.0**, per NBC 2016. Students often forget this and overestimate endurance.
> 5. **Mixing up material ratings** — Concrete gives 2 hours at **120 mm**, but 4 hours at **200 mm**. Memorize the standard thickness-vs-rating table.
> 6. **Confusing water curtain with sprinkler system** — Water curtains are **deluge (open nozzle)** systems, NOT automatic sprinklers. They are *active* (detection-triggered) separation systems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Fire resistant screen** = barrier preventing fire/smoke/heat spread between compartments
- [x] **Two types:** Solid Screens (passive) and Water Curtains (active)
- [x] **Solid Screens — Three criteria:** R (Stability), E (Integrity), I (Insulation) — collectively the **REI rating**
- [x] **Insulation criterion limit:** unexposed face temp rise ≤ **140 °C average**, **180 °C** at any point
- [x] **ISO 834 standard fire curve:** $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ °C
- [x] **Solid screen materials:** Concrete (120–200 mm = 2–4 h), Brick (200 mm = 4 h), Gypsum (multi-layer Type X = 1–2 h), Fire-rated glass (30 min–2 h)
- [x] **Water Curtain principle:** Absorbs heat via **sensible heating** + **latent vaporization**
- [x] **Latent heat of vaporization of water:** $h_{fg} = 2260$ kJ/kg
- [x] **Total heat absorbed by 1 kg water:** ≈ **2574 kJ/kg** (at 25 °C ambient)
- [x] **Design water density for water curtains:** **5–10 L/min/m²** (NBC 2016 / NFPA 15)
- [x] **Nozzle spacing:** 1.5–3.0 m
- [x] **Nozzle pressure:** 1.4–3.5 bar
- [x] **Radiation attenuation by water curtain:** 60–80% reduction achievable
- [x] **Solid screens are best** for permanent compartmentation (hospitals, hotels, offices)
- [x] **Water curtains are best** for atria, large openings, transformer enclosures
- [x] **Hybrid systems:** Solid screen + water curtain for high-risk zones (transformer rooms)
- [x] **Endurance time formula:** $t = (V_{tank} \times h_{fg}) / Q_{fire}$ (with safety factor 1.5–2.0)
- [x] **Fourier number threshold for thermally thick slab:** $Fo \geq 0.2$
- [x] **Beer-Lambert law for radiation:** $I_{trans} = I_0 \cdot e^{-kx}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Barriers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Local Barriers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Barrier (Fire Engineering Definition):**
> A *local barrier* is a fire-rated building element — vertical, horizontal, or a combination thereof — designed and constructed to **subdivide a building into discrete fire compartments** at the local (i.e., room, floor zone, shaft, or small enclosure) scale, with the principal objective of **arresting or delaying the horizontal and vertical migration of flame, hot gases, and smoke** from the fire zone of origin to adjoining spaces.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** classification aligned with the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety)** and **IS 3809 : 1979 / IS 1644 : 1988**, local barriers are categorised as:

| Barrier Type | Code Reference | Typical Fire Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Fire Wall | NBC 4.6.1 | **2 h to 4 h** |
| Fire Partition / Fire Check Wall | NBC 4.6.2 | **1 h to 2 h** |
| Smoke Barrier | NBC 4.6.3 | **30 min (integrity)** |
| Fire-rated Floor / Ceiling Assembly | IS 1644 | **1 h to 2 h** |
| Localised Vertical Shaft Enclosure | IS 3809 | **2 h** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam & Levee" Model

Imagine a city built on the flood plain of a river. To protect individual neighbourhoods from a flash flood, engineers do **not** build one giant sea-wall around the entire city. Instead, they build **small, strategically located levees, sand-bag walls, and embankments** between sub-areas. If a levee gives way, water floods only *one neighbourhood* — not the whole city.

A **local barrier in fire safety is exactly this levee**. The fire is the flood, the building is the city, and the local barrier is the **small, carefully located wall or slab** that contains the disaster to a single "neighbourhood" (a fire compartment). Just as levees are rated by how high a flood they can hold back and for how long, local barriers are rated by **fire resistance rating (FRR)** — the number of hours the barrier can resist the **standard time–temperature fire curve (IS 3809 / ISO 834)**.

## 1.3 Why Local Barriers Matter — The Fire Triangle of Protection

> [!NOTE]
> A local barrier must satisfy **THREE independent performance criteria** simultaneously, often called the **E–I–R (or Stability–Integrity–Insulation) triad**:
>
> - **S / R — Stability / Load-bearing (R):** The barrier must continue to carry its design load (for load-bearing walls/floors) without collapse for the rated duration.
> - **E — Integrity:** The barrier must prevent the passage of **flames and hot gases** through cracks, openings, or joints.
> - **I — Insulation:** The unexposed face temperature must not rise by more than **140 K (average)** or **180 K (single-point maximum)** above ambient, preventing ignition of combustibles on the safe side.

## 1.4 Critical Metrics to Remember (Examination Favourites)

- **Standard fire temperature at time *t* (minutes):**
$$T = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)\quad [\text{in } ^\circ\text{C}]$$
This is the **ISO 834 / IS 3809 cellulosic fire curve** — examiners expect you to quote it.

- **Mean Temperature Rise Limit (Insulation criterion):** **ΔT_mean ≤ 140 K**
- **Single-point Temperature Rise Limit:** **ΔT_max ≤ 180 K**
- **Minimum FRR for compartment walls in India (NBC 2016):** **2 hours** for most occupancies; **4 hours** for high-hazard industrial occupancies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Local Barriers — Functional Classification

### A. By Orientation
1. **Vertical Local Barriers** — Fire walls, fire partitions, fire-rated riser/shaft enclosures, curtain-wall spandrel barriers.
2. **Horizontal Local Barriers** — Fire-rated floor slabs, fire-rated ceiling membranes, smoke-tight raised floors, service-intake soffits.
3. **Hybrid Local Barriers** — Fire-rated glazing, fire-rated ductwork, fire dampers, fire-rated access doors.

### B. By Function
1. **Fire-Containment Barriers** — Resist flame + gas + heat transfer (full E–I–R).
2. **Smoke Barriers** — Resist only smoke leakage (typically ≥ **30 min integrity** + low air-leakage).
3. **Shaft Enclosures** — Resist vertical spread through service shafts (staircase, lift, A/C, electrical).
4. **Protected Corridors / Lobbies** — Provide a *refuge path* between the occupied space and the exit stairway.

## 2.2 Performance Hierarchy: How a Local Barrier Fails

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A local barrier fails progressively in **four time-sequenced stages**. Recognising this sequence is a **favourite 7-mark question**.

| Stage | Time Window | Physical Phenomenon | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 1 — Heat Transfer** | 0 – 30 min | Conductive heat penetrates barrier; unexposed face warms | Insulation criterion (I) is the first to be exceeded |
| **Stage 2 — Crack Initiation** | 30 min – 1 h | Thermal gradients cause cracking, joint opening | Integrity criterion (E) breached by hot gas seepage |
| **Stage 3 — Through-Penetration Failure** | 1 h – 2 h | Flames lick through cracks; combustibles on safe side ignite | Local barrier no longer contains fire |
| **Stage 4 — Structural Collapse** | 2 h – 4 h | Load-bearing capacity degrades; rebar yields, concrete spalls | Barrier fails to support design load — **Stability (R) lost** |

## 2.3 The Four Pillars of Local Barrier Design (Board-Exam Favourite)

1. **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR):** The duration (in hours) for which the barrier satisfies all three E–I–R criteria under the standard fire.
2. **Compartmentation Strategy:** The geometric pattern by which barriers are placed to divide the floor plate into compartments of permissible area (per NBC Table 4 of Part 4).
3. **Penetration Management (Firestopping):** Every cable tray, pipe, duct, or conduit that pierces a barrier MUST be sealed with an **approved firestop system** of equal or greater FRR.
4. **Continuity at Junctions:** The barrier must extend **floor-to-deck (slab-to-slab)** and seal all **slab-to-wall, wall-to-wall, and wall-to-ceiling** junctions with fire-rated sealants (intumescent / ablative / elastomeric).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print-and-Revise Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` / `\mid` for absolute-value symbols inside tables to prevent Markdown breakage.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation or Rule | Units | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard Fire Curve (Cellulosic) | $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | °C, t in min | ISO 834 / IS 3809 |
| 2 | Hydrocarbon Fire Curve (H) | $T(t) = 1080\,(1 - 0.325\,e^{-0.167 t} - 0.675\,e^{-2.5 t})$ | °C, t in min | Eurocode 1 Part 1-2 |
| 3 | Insulation Criterion | $\Delta T_{mean} \le 140\,\text{K}$ | K | IS 3809 |
| 4 | Integrity Criterion | No through-flames; gap gauge $\le \phi\,25\,\text{mm}$ for $\le 10\,\text{s}$ | mm, s | IS 3809 |
| 5 | Stability Criterion | Vertical load-bearing element must not deflect $> h/30$ or collapse | mm | IS 3809 |
| 6 | Equivalent Fire Severity (t_e) | $t_e = \dfrac{c \cdot w \cdot Q}{A_f}$ | min | Law's Method |
| 7 | Concrete Cover for FRR | Cover $\ge 25\,\text{mm}$ for 2 h, $\ge 40\,\text{mm}$ for 4 h | mm | IS 456 / IS 1644 |
| 8 | Compartment Area (NBC) | Office: $750\,\text{m}^2$; Mercantile: $500\,\text{m}^2}$; High-hazard: $250\,\text{m}^2$ | m² | NBC 2016, Table 4 |
| 9 | Smoke Barrier Leakage | Leakage $\le 30\,\text{m}^3/\text{h·m}^2$ at 25 Pa | m³/h·m² | NBC 2016 |
| 10 | Firestop Rating Rule | Firestop FRR $\ge$ Barrier FRR | h | NBC 2016 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Local barriers are the **single most cost-effective** element in a fire-protection strategy. Compared to **active** systems (sprinklers, detection), they:

- Provide protection **even during power failure** (passive system).
- Require **no human intervention** to operate.
- Form the **skeleton of the egress path** (smoke-proof lobbies, fire-rated corridors).
- Are mandated by **all** Indian insurance underwriting guidelines.

They are routinely encountered in **high-rise residential towers (lift-shaft walls)**, **shopping malls (tenant separations)**, **data centres (fire-rated enclosures around server rooms)**, **hospital OT suites (compartmentation for anaesthetic gas control)**, and **industrial facilities (battery rooms, flammable liquid stores)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard Time–Temperature Fire Curve

The **cellulosic (ISO 834 / IS 3809) curve** is an empirical relationship, but it is mathematically derived from a first-order heat-transfer assumption. Let us reconstruct it logically.

**Step 1 — Empirical Basis:**
Observations of fully developed compartment fires on cellulosic fuels (wood, paper, textiles) showed that the **mean gas temperature** in the fire compartment follows an exponential-saturation form.

**Step 2 — Governing Heat Balance:**
The compartment gases receive heat at a rate approximately constant per unit time (post-flashover), while losing it through the enclosure surfaces. Under quasi-steady assumption:

$$
m_g \, c_{p,g}\,\dfrac{dT}{dt} \;=\; \dot{Q}_{in} - h_c A \,(T - T_0)
$$

where $m_g$ is the gas mass, $c_{p,g}$ is specific heat, $\dot{Q}_{in}$ is the heat-release rate, $h_c$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient, and $A$ is the enclosure surface area.

**Step 3 — After flashover**, the rate of heat loss is small compared with $\dot{Q}_{in}$, and the solution simplifies to a **logarithmic growth**:

$$
T(t) - T_0 \;=\; A_0 \,\log_{10}(a\,t + 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Curve-Fit to Experimental Data:**
Calibration to standard furnace tests (where the standardised $T$ at $t = 0$ is $20\,^\circ\text{C}$ and the temperature reaches $945\,^\circ\text{C}$ at $t = 60\,\text{min}$) gives:

$$
A_0 = 345\,^\circ\text{C}, \quad a = 8\,\text{min}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Standard Form:**

$$
\boxed{\,T(t) \;=\; 20 \;+\; 345\,\log_{10}(8t + 1)\,}
$$

with $T$ in °C and $t$ in minutes.

**Step 6 — Verification at Reference Points:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 0\ \text{min} &: \quad T = 20 + 345\,\log_{10}(1) = 20\,^\circ\text{C} \quad \checkmark \\
t = 30\ \text{min} &: \quad T = 20 + 345\,\log_{10}(241) \approx 20 + 689 \approx 709\,^\circ\text{C} \quad \checkmark \\
t = 60\ \text{min} &: \quad T = 20 + 345\,\log_{10}(481) \approx 20 + 925 \approx 945\,^\circ\text{C} \quad \checkmark \\
t = 120\ \text{min} &: \quad T = 20 + 345\,\log_{10}(961) \approx 20 + 1149 \approx 1169\,^\circ\text{C} \quad \checkmark \\
