t = 240\ \text{min} &: \quad T = 20 + 345\,\log_{10}(1921) \approx 20 + 1373 \approx 1393\,^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Fire Severity by Law's Method

**Law's Method** converts an actual fire's severity into an equivalent ISO 834 duration, allowing designers to size local barriers using the standard test data.

**Step 1 — Fire Load Definition:**
The fire load per unit floor area is:

$$
q = \dfrac{\sum M_i \, H_i}{A_f}
$$

where $M_i$ = mass of combustible $i$ (kg), $H_i$ = calorific value (MJ/kg), $A_f$ = floor area (m²).

**Step 2 — Equivalent Standard Fire Duration:**

$$
t_e \;=\; \dfrac{c \, w \, Q}{A_f}
$$

where:
- $t_e$ = equivalent time (min),
- $w$ = ventilation factor (depends on opening size),
- $Q$ = total fire load (MJ),
- $A_f$ = floor area (m²),
- $c$ = conversion constant depending on structural material.

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**

> Given: A small office compartment of floor area $A_f = 40\,\text{m}^2$ contains $1500\,\text{kg}$ of cellulosic furnishings with $H = 17\,\text{MJ/kg}$. The compartment has one window $2\,\text{m} \times 1.5\,\text{m}$. Take $c = 0.09\,\text{min·m}^2/\text{MJ}$ and $w = 6$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 1500 \times 17 = 25\,500\ \text{MJ} \\[4pt]
t_e &= \dfrac{0.09 \times 6 \times 25\,500}{40} \\[4pt]
t_e &= \dfrac{13\,770}{40} \\[4pt]
t_e &= 344.25\ \text{min} \;\approx\; 5.74\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A standard cellulosic fire of approximately **5.7 hours** equivalent severity would develop in this compartment. Hence, all local barriers must be designed for a **minimum FRR of 2 h** (per NBC) and ideally **4 h** to provide a safety margin.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Local Barrier FRR by Occupancy

| Occupancy Group (NBC) | Fire Load (MJ/m²) | Equivalent Severity | Minimum Wall FRR | Minimum Floor FRR | Smoke Barrier Required? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Residential | 500 – 800 | 1 – 2 h | **1 h** | **1 h** | No |
| Office | 800 – 1200 | 2 – 3 h | **2 h** | **2 h** | Yes (corridor) |
| Mercantile (Mall) | 1000 – 1500 | 2 – 4 h | **2 h** | **2 h** | Yes (atrium) |
| Hospital (Ward) | 300 – 600 | 1 – 1.5 h | **2 h** | **2 h** | Yes (mandatory) |
| Industrial – High Hazard | > 1500 | > 4 h | **4 h** | **4 h** | Yes |

## 3.4 Insulation Thickness for a Local Barrier (Concrete Wall)

Using the simplified **silica–aggregate concrete formula** for plane walls exposed to ISO 834 fire on one side, the minimum thickness $L$ (mm) for fire resistance $t$ (hours):

$$
L_{\min} \;=\; a_0 + a_1\,t + a_2\,t^2
$$

with coefficients (for siliceous aggregate, $\rho \ge 2300\,\text{kg/m}^3$):

$$
a_0 = 80,\quad a_1 = 60,\quad a_2 = 10
$$

For a **2-hour FRR**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\min} &= 80 + 60(2) + 10(2)^2 \\
L_{\min} &= 80 + 120 + 40 \\
L_{\min} &= 240\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Tip:** Quote the **240 mm siliceous-aggregate concrete wall** as the textbook 2-hour local barrier in India. Add a **15 mm cement–lime plaster** on the exposed face to enhance insulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Hierarchy of Local Barrier Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Local Barrier System] --> B[Vertical Barriers]
    A --> C[Horizontal Barriers]
    A --> D[Penetration Seals]

    B --> B1[Fire Wall - 2h to 4h]
    B --> B2[Fire Partition - 1h to 2h]
    B --> B3[Shaft Enclosure - 2h]
    B --> B4[Protected Lobby - 1h]

    C --> C1[Fire Rated Floor Slab - 1h to 2h]
    C --> C2[Fire Rated Ceiling Membrane - 1h]
    C --> C3[Smoke Barrier - 30 min]

    D --> D1[Intumescent Firestop - Pipes]
    D --> D2[Ablative Firestop - Cables]
    D --> D3[Elastomeric Firestop - Joints]
    D --> D4[Fire Damper - Ducts]

    B1 --> E[E-I-R Triad Satisfied]
    B2 --> E
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> F[FRR equals Parent Barrier]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Failure-Stage Sequence of a Local Barrier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 - 0 to 30 min] --> S2[Stage 2 - 30 min to 1 h]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 - 1 h to 2 h]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 - 2 h to 4 h]

    S1 --> L1[Insulation Criterion Breached]
    S2 --> L2[Integrity Criterion Breached]
    S3 --> L3[Through Penetration]
    S4 --> L4[Structural Collapse]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Local Barrier Placement in a Building

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FireCompartment1
        R1[Occupied Room] --> FW1[Fire Wall - 2 h]
        R1 --> CS1[Corridor Smoke Barrier]
    end

    subgraph FireCompartment2
        R2[Adjacent Room] --> FW2[Fire Partition - 1 h]
        R2 --> CS2[Corridor Smoke Barrier]
    end

    FW1 --- FW2
    CS1 --- CS2
    R1 --> FS[Floor Slab - 2 h]
    R2 --> FS
    FS --> SE[Shaft Enclosure - 2 h]

    FS --> PL[Protected Lobby - 1 h]
    PL --> ST[Exit Staircase]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams illustrate the **placement logic** of local barriers:
>
> 1. **Fire walls** separate *adjacent fire compartments*.
> 2. **Floor slabs** act as **horizontal local barriers** preventing vertical fire migration.
> 3. **Smoke barriers** segment **corridors** to maintain tenable conditions along egress paths.
> 4. **Shaft enclosures** carry the local barrier vertically for lifts, stairs, and services.
> 5. **Protected lobbies** form the **buffer zone** between an occupied space and the exit staircase.
>
> Every transition point (fire wall ↔ floor slab, fire wall ↔ fire partition) requires a **continuity detail** with fire-rated sealants — the most common point of failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Q: Define a "local barrier" in the context of structural fire safety. List the three performance criteria it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

> A local barrier is a fire-rated building element designed to subdivide a building into discrete fire compartments at the room or floor-zone scale, arresting the spread of flame, hot gases, and smoke. It must satisfy three criteria:
> 1. **Stability / Load-bearing (R)** — must carry design loads without collapse.
> 2. **Integrity (E)** — must prevent passage of flames and hot gases.
> 3. **Insulation (I)** — unexposed-face temperature rise ≤ 140 K (mean) and ≤ 180 K (single-point).

*(3 marks — full marks requires naming all three criteria with their thresholds.)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between a fire wall and a smoke barrier. State typical ratings.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Fire Wall | Smoke Barrier |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Resists flame + gas + heat transfer | Resists smoke leakage only |
| Typical FRR | **2 h to 4 h** | **30 min integrity** |
| Criteria | E + I + R | E only |
| Application | Compartment walls, party walls | Corridor subdivision, atrium perimeter |

*(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice — KTU ESE Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> Both **Question A** and **Question B** below carry 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **E–I–R performance criteria** of a local barrier. State the quantitative limits for each criterion as per IS 3809 / ISO 834.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small office compartment of floor area $50\,\text{m}^2$ contains $1800\,\text{kg}$ of cellulosic furniture with calorific value $17\,\text{MJ/kg}$. The compartment has a window of size $2\,\text{m} \times 1.5\,\text{m}$. Using **Law's method**, compute the equivalent standard fire severity and recommend the **minimum FRR** for the local barriers surrounding the compartment. (Take $c = 0.09$ and $w = 6$.)
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) E–I–R Criteria — Explanation:**

> **Sketch description (verbal, for the answer sheet):**
> Draw a horizontal cross-section of a wall with the **exposed face** on the left (showing flames and the standard fire curve), the **unexposed face** on the right (showing a thermocouple array), and three annotations: Stability arrow downward, Integrity arrows across the wall thickness, and Insulation arrows indicating temperature measurement.

**Stability (R):** The barrier, if load-bearing, must continue to carry its design dead load + superimposed load for the rated duration. Limit: vertical deflection $\le L/30$ of the clear height and no collapse. **Time of failure = Stability rating.**

**Integrity (E):** No through-passage of flames or hot gases. Test criterion: a $\phi\,25\,\text{mm}$ cotton-wool pad held against the unexposed face must not ignite, and any through-gap must not exceed $\phi\,25\,\text{mm}$ for more than $10\,\text{s}$. **Time of failure = Integrity rating.**

**Insulation (I):** The mean temperature of the unexposed face must not rise by more than $\mathbf{140\,K}$ above ambient, and no single-point reading must exceed a rise of $\mathbf{180\,K}$. **Time of failure = Insulation rating.**

The **fire resistance rating (FRR)** is the **lowest** of the three times. For a properly designed local barrier, the FRR is governed by the criterion with the **shortest failure time** — typically **Insulation** for thin walls, **Integrity** for walls with service penetrations, and **Stability** for slender load-bearing walls.

**(b) Law's Method Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad Q &= M \times H = 1800 \times 17 = 30\,600\ \text{MJ} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad t_e &= \dfrac{c \cdot w \cdot Q}{A_f} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad t_e &= \dfrac{0.09 \times 6 \times 30\,600}{50} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad t_e &= \dfrac{16\,524}{50} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5:} \quad t_e &= 330.48\ \text{min} \;\approx\; 5.51\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** Equivalent severity ≈ **5.5 hours**. As per NBC 2016 and IS 3809, the minimum FRR for office-occupancy compartment walls is **2 hours**. However, considering the high equivalent severity, the local barriers should be designed for a **4-hour FRR** as a design margin, with all penetrations sealed by **4-hour-rated firestop systems**.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Classify **local barriers** based on orientation and function. Give two examples of construction materials for each class, along with typical fire resistance ratings.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **240 mm siliceous-aggregate concrete wall** with **15 mm cement–lime plaster** on the exposed face is proposed as a local barrier in an office building. Using the simplified thickness formula, verify its adequacy for a **2-hour FRR**. What is the FRR if the plaster is omitted?
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Classification of Local Barriers:**

**By Orientation:**
- **Vertical:** (i) Fire wall — 200 mm RCC or 240 mm brickwork with plaster, **2 h to 4 h**; (ii) Fire partition — 100 mm gypsum board on steel studs, **1 h to 2 h**.
- **Horizontal:** (i) Fire-rated floor slab — 150 mm RCC, **2 h**; (ii) Fire-rated suspended ceiling — 25 mm gypsum, **1 h**.
- **Hybrid:** (i) Fire-rated glass — wired glass / ceramic glass, **1 h to 2 h**; (ii) Fire damper — intumescent or fusible-link, **1 h to 2 h**.

**By Function:**
- **Fire-Containment:** resists flame + gas + heat transfer (full E–I–R).
- **Smoke Barriers:** resist smoke leakage only (low air-leakage + 30 min integrity).
- **Shaft Enclosures:** resist vertical spread through service shafts.
- **Protected Corridors:** provide refuge path between occupied space and exit.

**(b) Verification of 240 mm Concrete Wall:**

Using $L_{\min} = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2$ with $a_0 = 80$, $a_1 = 60$, $a_2 = 10$ for $t = 2$ h:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\min} &= 80 + 60(2) + 10(2)^2 \\
L_{\min} &= 80 + 120 + 40 \\
L_{\min} &= 240\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The actual wall thickness (240 mm) **exactly equals** the required minimum thickness. **Verification: ADEQUATE for 2-hour FRR** (with the 15 mm plaster providing additional insulation margin).

**Without plaster — solving for $t$ when $L = 240$ mm:**
Since the formula already includes the base thickness, the plaster acts as an additional **insulation-enhancing layer** without contributing to load-bearing thickness. Without plaster, the same wall typically achieves approximately **1.5 h to 2 h**, depending on aggregate type. For siliceous aggregate without the plaster protection, the FRR drops by approximately **20 – 30 %**, giving an effective FRR of about **1.4 h to 1.6 h** — **NOT adequate** for the 2-hour requirement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Integrity" with "Insulation":** Integrity = no flame/gas passage. Insulation = temperature-rise limit. Don't interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting the 140 K / 180 K numbers:** Always quote the **quantitative limits** for insulation criterion.
> 3. **Omitting the *units* in Law's method calculation:** $t_e$ is in **minutes**, not hours. Convert at the end.
> 4. **Missing the "ceiling effect" of ISO 834 curve:** Many students forget that the standard fire curve is **logarithmic** — temperature rises fast early, then plateaus. Don't use a linear approximation.
> 5. **Treating the fire wall as a smoke barrier (or vice versa):** Fire wall = E + I + R. Smoke barrier = E only. Examiners deduct full marks for this confusion.
> 6. **Ignoring penetrations:** A fire wall is only as strong as its **weakest penetration seal**. Always mention **firestopping** in any compartmentation answer.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Local Barriers:**
>
> - **Definition:** Local barrier = passive, fire-rated element that compartmentalises a building at the **room/floor-zone scale**.
> - **Three criteria:** **R** (Stability, load), **E** (Integrity, no flame/gas), **I** (Insulation, ΔT ≤ 140 K mean / 180 K max).
> - **FRR is the minimum of the three failure times** — typically I fails first for thin walls.
> - **ISO 834 / IS 3809 standard fire:** $T = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$, with $T$ in °C and $t$ in minutes.
> - **Hydrocarbon fire:** $T = 1080\,(1 - 0.325\,e^{-0.167 t} - 0.675\,e^{-2.5 t})$ — used for petrochemical occupancies.
> - **Vertical local barriers:** Fire walls (2 h to 4 h), fire partitions (1 h to 2 h), shaft enclosures (2 h).
> - **Horizontal local barriers:** Floor slabs (1 h to 2 h), fire-rated ceilings (1 h), smoke barriers (30 min integrity).
> - **NBC 2016 minimum FRR:** 1 h (residential), 2 h (office/mercantile/hospital), 4 h (high-hazard industrial).
> - **Law's equivalent severity:** $t_e = c w Q / A_f$ — convert to required FRR by adding a **safety factor** (typically 1.5).
> - **Firestop rule:** Every penetration seal must have **FRR ≥ parent barrier FRR**.
> - **Continuity rule:** Barrier must extend **slab-to-slab** (floor-to-deck) with **fire-rated sealants** at all junctions.
> - **Concrete wall thickness formula:** $L = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2$; for siliceous aggregate at $t = 2$ h, $L = 240$ mm.
> - **Common failure modes (in order):** Heat transfer → cracking → through-penetration → collapse.
> - **Real-world mantra:** *A fire wall is only as good as its weakest penetration.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Stopped Areas - In Roof, in Partitioned Fire Areas, and in Connecting Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire Stopped Areas - In Roof, in Partitioned Fire Areas, and in Connecting Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fire Stopped Area** (also called a **Fire Compartment** or **Fire Cell**) is a section of a building that is physically isolated from adjacent sections by approved fire-resisting construction, so that fire (and the products of combustion) cannot pass freely from one section to another for a specified minimum duration — typically **1, 2, 3, or 4 hours**, depending on the building's occupancy, height, and floor area.

The three core performance criteria that every fire-stopping element must satisfy (as per IS 1641 / IS 1642 / NBC 2016 Part 4 framework adopted in KTU curriculum) are:

1. **Stability (R)** — the element must not collapse or lose load-bearing capacity during the fire exposure period.
2. **Integrity (E)** — no through-cracks, fissures, or openings may develop that would allow passage of flames or hot gases.
3. **Insulation (I)** — the average temperature rise on the unexposed face must not exceed **140 °C**, with no single point exceeding **180 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Fire Resistance Rating Notation:** A wall with rating `REI 120` means it retains **R**esistance, **E**xposed-face **I**ntegrity, and **I**nsulation for **120 minutes** under standard fire exposure (IS 3809 / ISO 834 time-temperature curve).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a large ship (the building) crossing an ocean. Inside the ship, there are **watertight bulkheads** (fire walls) dividing the hull into separate sealed rooms (fire compartments). If the ship strikes an iceberg and water floods one room (a fire breaks out in one compartment), the watertight doors and sealed bulkheads prevent water (fire) from rushing into the other rooms. The ship stays afloat (the building remains safe) long enough for the crew (occupants) to evacuate to the lifeboats (exits).

**The three things that must NOT happen in a fire compartment are:**
- The wall must not **fall down** (Stability ✗)
- The wall must not develop **cracks** that let flames/smoke through (Integrity ✗)
- The wall must not become so **hot** on the safe side that it ignites adjacent materials (Insulation ✗)

## 1.3 Why Fire Stopping is Critical

| Hazard Without Fire Stopping | Effect With Proper Fire Stopping |
|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Fire spreads freely across the entire floor plate | Fire is confined to a manageable, designed area |
| Vertical fire spread up through shafts & openings | Compartmentation limits vertical chimney effect |
| Occupants in remote areas are exposed to toxic smoke | Safe areas of refuge maintained for evacuation |
| Structural collapse becomes a global failure | Localized damage; progressive collapse prevented |
| Firefighter access is overwhelmed | Defendable space exists for fire service operations |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 OBE (Outcome-Based Education) framework emphasizes that **fire stopping is the single most cost-effective fire safety measure** — it limits damage, saves lives, and reduces insurance loss by orders of magnitude compared to active systems like sprinklers alone.

## 1.4 Three Locations of Fire Stopping (Module 4 Scope)

The Module 4 syllabus identifies **three distinct structural locations** where fire-stopping design must be applied:

1. **In Roof** — horizontal termination at the top of the building.
2. **In Partitioned Fire Areas** — internal vertical and horizontal subdivision of floor plates.
3. **In Connecting Structures** — junctions between structural elements and between buildings.

Each location has unique constructional challenges, which are detailed in subsequent sections.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fire Stopping in the Roof

The roof is often the most **overlooked** fire stop location, but it is critical because:
- Roof spaces can act as **horizontal chimneys** spreading fire across an entire floor plate.
- Trusses, purlins, and timber decking can be highly combustible.
- Fire can bypass compartment walls by traveling **over** them through the roof void.

### 2.1.1 Constructional Principles

- **Fire walls must continue through the roof** by a minimum height — typically **0.5 m to 1.0 m above the highest point of roof intersection** on each side (parapet extension). This prevents fire from jumping over the wall via the roof.
- **Parapet walls** of non-combustible construction (RCC or masonry) must be provided where fire walls meet the roof.
- **Roof slabs** must have a fire resistance rating matching the highest-rated compartment below them.
- **Roof penetrations** (for AC ducts, conduits, plumbing) must be sealed with intumescent fire-stopping collars or fire-rated sealant (commonly **FRL ≥ 2 hours** for commercial buildings).
- **Combustible insulation** (e.g., thermocol, polyurethane foam) must be protected by non-combustible covering or replaced with mineral wool / glass wool of appropriate fire rating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** A common exam pitfall is assuming a fire wall is automatically fire-stopped at the roof. **Without proper parapet extension or continuation, fire travels over the wall** — rendering the entire compartmentation strategy ineffective.

## 2.2 Fire Stopping in Partitioned Fire Areas (Vertical & Horizontal Compartmentation)

A **partitioned fire area** is a section of a building enclosed by fire-resisting walls and floors so that fire originating within cannot spread beyond the enclosure for the specified period.

### 2.2.1 Types of Internal Fire-Resisting Elements

| Element | Function | Typical FRL (hrs) |
|---------|----------|-------------------|
| **Fire Wall** | Separates buildings or large fire areas; usually starts from foundation | 4 |
| **Fire Partition / Compartment Wall** | Subdivides floors into manageable areas | 2 to 3 |
| **Fire-Resisting Floor** | Provides horizontal compartmentation between stories | 2 |
| **Smoke Barrier** | Restricts smoke spread (often 1 hr integrity) | 1 |
| **Fire Door Assembly** | Closes openings in fire walls; self-closing | 1 to 2 |
| **Fire Damper** | Closes HVAC ducts at fire wall penetrations | 1 to 2 |

### 2.2.2 Maximum Compartment Area (Design Basis)

The maximum permitted floor area of an unsprinklered fire compartment is governed by occupancy type, fire load, and structural fire resistance. The **Fire Area formula** is:

$$
A_{\text{compartment,max}} = k \times FRL \times f_{\text{occ}} \times f_{\text{sprinkler}}
$$

Where:
- $A_{\text{compartment,max}}$ = maximum permissible fire compartment area (m²)
- $k$ = base factor (m²/hr), typically **750 to 1500 m²/hr** depending on construction type
- $FRL$ = fire resistance level in hours
- $f_{\text{occ}}$ = occupancy factor (residential 1.0, office 0.8, storage 0.5, mercantile 0.7)
- $f_{\text{sprinkler}}$ = 1.0 for unsprinklered, **2.0** for fully sprinklered (100% area increase allowed by NBC)

### 2.2.3 Typical Permissible Fire Compartment Areas (NBC 2016 Reference Frame)

| Occupancy Group | Floor Area (m²) Unsprinklered | Floor Area (m²) Sprinklered |
|------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Residential** | 750 | 1500 |
| **Educational** | 1000 | 2000 |
| **Institutional (Hospital)** | 500 | 1000 |
| **Assembly (Theatre, Hall)** | 1000 | 2000 |
| **Mercantile (Shopping)** | 750 | 1500 |
| **Business (Office)** | 1500 | 3000 |
| **Industrial (Low Risk)** | 2000 | 4000 |
| **Storage (High Hazard)** | 500 | 1000 |

## 2.3 Fire Stopping in Connecting Structures

Connecting structures are the **interfaces** between different building elements, between adjacent buildings, or between separate structures joined by bridges/corridors. These junctions are inherently weak points because:
- They involve **discontinuities** in the fire-resisting envelope.
- **Differential movement** (thermal expansion, settlement) can open gaps over time.
- **Penetrations** for services (pipes, cables, ducts) are routinely made and often improperly sealed.

### 2.3.1 Critical Connecting Structure Locations

1. **Junction of Wall and Floor Slab**
   - A continuous fire-resistant joint (firestop) is required at every slab-wall intersection.
   - Material: mineral wool backing + intumescent sealant.

2. **Junction of Wall and Roof Slab**
   - Same principle; requires non-combustible filling (e.g., cement mortar + rock wool) over the wall-roof interface.

3. **Penetration Openings (Pipes, Conduits, Cables, Ducts)**
   - Each penetration must be sealed with tested **firestop systems** classified per IS 12458 / EN 1366.
   - Plastic pipes require **intumescent collars** that expand on heating to close the softened pipe.
   - Cables are sealed with **fire-rated pillows, putty, or mortar**.

4. **Expansion Joints**
   - Must accommodate building movement without compromising fire integrity.
   - Use **fire-rated expansion joint covers** with mineral wool core and elastomeric facing.

5. **Door and Window Openings in Fire Walls**
   - Must be protected by **fire-rated door assemblies** (tested to IS 3614 Part 2 / BS 476 Part 22).
   - Fire doors must be **self-closing** and **self-latching**.

6. **Curtain Wall / Glass Façade Intersections**
   - **Spatially separated spandrel panels** at each floor level provide the vertical fire stop.
   - Each spandrel zone must achieve the same FRL as the floor slab.

7. **Junctions Between Two Buildings (Party Walls)**
   - Must extend through the full height and roof as a true **fire-separating element** with no openings (except under strict conditions).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Principle | Symbol | Value / Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|--------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Standard fire time-temperature curve | $T = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | T in °C, t in minutes | Used in furnace testing (IS 3809 / ISO 834) |
| 2 | Critical steel temperature | $T_{\text{crit}}$ | **550 °C** (plain bars), **600 °C** (prestressed) | Loss of load-bearing capacity |
| 3 | Insulation criterion (avg.) | $\Delta T_{\text{avg}}$ | $\leq \mathbf{140\ ^\circ C}$ | Fire wall temperature rise on cold face |
| 4 | Insulation criterion (point) | $\Delta T_{\text{max}}$ | $\leq \mathbf{180\ ^\circ C}$ | Single-point temperature on cold face |
| 5 | Maximum compartment area | $A_{\max} = k \cdot FRL \cdot f_{\text{occ}} \cdot f_{\text{sp}}$ | m² | Sizing fire compartments |
| 6 | Sprinkler increase factor | $f_{\text{sp}}$ | **2.0** for full sprinkler system | Doubles allowable compartment area |
| 7 | Parapet extension height (fire wall to roof) | $h_p$ | $\geq \mathbf{0.5\ m}$ to **1.0 m** | Prevents lateral fire spread over wall |
| 8 | Minimum FRL (fire walls) | $FRL_{\text{wall}}$ | **2 to 4 hours** | Based on building height & occupancy |
| 9 | Minimum FRL (compartment floor) | $FRL_{\text{floor}}$ | **1 to 2 hours** | Standard for residential/office |
| 10 | Fire door rating | $FRL_{\text{door}}$ | $\geq \mathbf{1/2}$ of wall rating | For openings in fire walls |
| 11 | Travel distance to exit | $d_{\text{travel}}$ | $\leq \mathbf{30\ m}$ (unsprinklered), **45 m** (sprinklered) | For occupant egress |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas form the **design spine** of any KTU Structural Fire Safety project involving compartmentalization. The Fire Engineer's role is to size compartments (Formula 5), specify the FRL of each element (Formulas 8–10), and verify the roof terminations (Formula 7) so that fire cannot escape the originating cell.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Fire Time-Temperature Curve (ISO 834 / IS 3809)

The standard fire curve provides the **thermal input** that a structural element is expected to withstand. It is the basis of all furnace-based fire resistance testing.

### Step 1 — Empirical Observation

During real compartment fires, the temperature rises rapidly in the first 30 minutes (due to flashover) and then gradually approaches an asymptotic plateau. Multiple fire tests on fully developed compartment fires showed a logarithmic relationship between temperature and time.

### Step 2 — Curve Fitting

Researchers fitted the experimental data to a logarithmic equation:

$$
T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

Where:
- $T(t)$ = furnace temperature at time $t$, in °C
- $T_0$ = ambient temperature at start (typically **20 °C**)
- $t$ = time in **minutes** since ignition
- The constant **345** is a curve-fitting coefficient (units: °C)
- The constant **8** ensures the curve passes through the 20 °C initial point

### Step 3 — Verification at Key Time Points

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 0 \text{ min}: \quad T &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 0 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot 0 \\
&= 20\ ^\circ C \quad \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 30 \text{ min}: \quad T &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 30 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(241) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot 2.382 \\
&\approx 842\ ^\circ C \quad \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 60 \text{ min}: \quad T &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 60 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(481) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot 2.682 \\
&\approx 945\ ^\circ C \quad \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 120 \text{ min}: \quad T &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 120 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(961) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot 2.983 \\
&\approx 1049\ ^\circ C \quad \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation

After **2 hours** of standard fire exposure, the structural element is subjected to approximately **1050 °C** on the exposed face. This is why structural steel without protection (which loses strength at ~550 °C) will fail in about 15–20 minutes, while a properly protected element with intumescent paint or gypsum board can survive 2+ hours.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Sizing a Fire Compartment

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> An office building has an unsprinklered floor plate of 2400 m². If the fire resistance level of compartment walls is 2 hours and the occupancy factor is 0.8, determine the number of fire compartments required. Use $k = 1000 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula

$$
A_{\text{compartment,max}} = k \cdot FRL \cdot f_{\text{occ}} \cdot f_{\text{sprinkler}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Known Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{compartment,max}} &= 1000 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 1.0 \\
&= 1600 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Number of Compartments

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{compartments}} &= \frac{A_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{compartment,max}}} \\
&= \frac{2400}{1600} \\
&= 1.5 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since we cannot have a fractional compartment, **round up** to the next whole integer:

$$
n_{\text{compartments}} = 2
$$

### Step 4 — If the Building Is Sprinklered

With $f_{\text{sprinkler}} = 2.0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{compartment,max,sprinklered}} &= 1000 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 2.0 \\
&= 3200 \text{ m}^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the total area (2400 m²) is less than 3200 m², **only 1 compartment** is required — a major design and cost benefit.

### Step 5 — Decision Matrix

| Scenario | $f_{\text{sprinkler}}$ | $A_{\max}$ | Compartments Needed |
|----------|------------------------|------------|----------------------|
| Unsprinklered | 1.0 | 1600 m² | **2** |
| Sprinklered | 2.0 | 3200 m² | **1** |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fire Stopping by Location

| Location | Primary Risk | Key Design Element | Typical FRL | Critical Detail |
|----------|--------------|---------------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Roof (slab)** | Horizontal fire spread | Fire-rated roof slab | **2 hr** | Continuous slab, no openings |
| **Roof (wall termination)** | Lateral spread over wall | Parapet extension ≥ 0.5 m | **Same as wall** | Non-combustible material |
| **Roof (penetrations)** | Vertical spread via ducts | Fire dampers + collars | **1–2 hr** | Tested assemblies |
| **Partitioned areas (walls)** | Horizontal spread on floor | Fire compartment walls | **2 hr** | Self-closing fire doors |
| **Partitioned areas (floors)** | Vertical spread between floors | Fire-rated floor slab | **1–2 hr** | Edge protection at slab edge |
| **Partitioned areas (smoke)** | Smoke spread | Smoke barriers | **1 hr integrity** | Tight-fitting non-combustible |
| **Connections (wall-floor junction)** | Bypass via joint gap | Firestop sealant | **Same as wall/floor** | Tested per IS 12458 |
| **Connections (expansion joints)** | Gap formation over time | Fire-rated joint cover | **2 hr** | Mineral wool + elastomer |
| **Connections (party walls)** | Fire spread to next building | Continuous fire wall | **4 hr** | No openings permitted |
| **Connections (curtain walls)** | Floor-to-floor spread | Spandrel panel | **Same as floor** | Spatially separated zones |

## 3.4 Verification of FRL Through Three-Criteria Test (Conceptual)

For a fire wall to receive a **2-hour rating (REI 120)**, it must satisfy the following under standard furnace exposure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stability:} \quad & \text{No collapse, no loss of load-bearing capacity for } 120 \text{ min} \\
\text{Integrity:} \quad & \text{No through-cracks, no flame passage on unexposed face for } 120 \text{ min} \\
\text{Insulation:} \quad & \Delta T_{\text{avg}} \leq 140\ ^\circ C \text{ and } \Delta T_{\text{max}} \leq 180\ ^\circ C \text{ for } 120 \text{ min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why all three?** A wall that is stable but has a crack has failed in **integrity**. A wall that is integral but conducts too much heat has failed in **insulation**. A wall that is integral and insulating but is unable to support loads has failed in **stability**. **All three criteria must be satisfied** for the FRL designation to be valid.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a "Fire Stopped Area" and list the three criteria that determine its fire resistance rating.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Fire Stopped Area** is a portion of a building that is completely enclosed by fire-resisting walls, floors, and roof so that a fire originating within cannot spread beyond the enclosure for a specified minimum period.

The three criteria that determine the fire resistance rating (FRL) are:

1. **Stability (R)** — the element retains its load-bearing capacity under fire exposure without collapse.
2. **Integrity (E)** — the element does not develop cracks or openings that allow passage of flames or hot gases to the unexposed face.
3. **Insulation (I)** — the average temperature rise on the unexposed face does not exceed **140 °C** (with no single point exceeding **180 °C**).

The combined rating is denoted **REI** followed by the time in minutes (e.g., REI 120 = 2-hour fire resistance).

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why must a fire wall be extended above the roof level as a parapet? What is the typical minimum height of such extension?**

**Model Answer:**

A fire wall must be extended above the roof level (as a parapet) to **prevent fire from spreading laterally over the top of the wall** through the roof void or across the roof surface. Without a parapet extension, the fire could simply travel around the wall and bypass the entire compartmentation strategy, effectively rendering the fire wall useless.

The typical minimum height of such parapet extension is **0.5 m to 1.0 m above the highest point of the roof intersection** on each side of the wall, depending on the type of roof construction and the FRL required.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the design principles for **fire stopping in roof structures**. Discuss the role of parapet walls, fire-rated roof slabs, and sealing of roof penetrations with suitable sketches.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An unsprinklered commercial building has a total floor plate of 4800 m² on a single story. The proposed fire compartment walls have an FRL of 3 hours, the occupancy factor is 0.7, and the base factor $k = 1200 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}$. Calculate the maximum permissible compartment area and the number of fire compartments required. If the building is fully sprinklered, how many compartments would be needed?

---

### Model Solution — Question B-A

**(a) Fire Stopping in Roof Structures — Design Principles [7 Marks]:**

The roof is one of the most critical but often neglected areas of fire-stopping design. The primary design principles are:

**1. Parapet Wall Extension (Most Critical) [2 Marks]:**
- Fire compartment walls and fire walls must **continue through the entire roof construction**.
- The wall must extend above the roof surface as a parapet by a minimum of **0.5 m to 1.0 m**.
- The parapet must be constructed of **non-combustible, fire-resistant materials** (RCC or reinforced brickwork).
- This prevents lateral fire spread over the wall via the roof space.

**2. Fire-Rated Roof Slab [2 Marks]:**
- The roof slab itself must possess an FRL matching the highest compartment below it (typically **2 hours** for commercial/office buildings).
- A reinforced concrete slab of minimum **100 mm thickness** with adequate cover generally achieves REI 120.
- Timber roofs must be protected by **gypsum board, mineral wool, or vermiculite-sprayed coating** to achieve the required FRL.

**3. Sealing of Roof Penetrations [2 Marks]:**
- All penetrations (HVAC ducts, plumbing pipes, electrical conduits) must be sealed with **fire-stopping materials** tested as per IS 12458 / EN 1366.
- HVAC ducts require **fire dampers** that close automatically at 68 °C (fusible link) or upon smoke detection.
- Plastic pipes need **intumescent collars** that expand on heating to close the softened pipe cross-section.
- Cable trays must be sealed using **fire-rated pillows, putty, or mortar**.

**4. Protection of Combustible Roof Structure [1 Mark]:**
- If timber trusses are used, they must be enclosed by non-combustible materials (fire-rated suspended ceiling) or coated with fire-retardant paint.
- Combustible insulation (thermocol, polyurethane foam) must be replaced or protected by non-combustible covering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch Mention:** A sectional elevation showing the fire wall with parapet extension above the roof slab, with HVAC duct penetration sealed by fire damper, should be drawn on the answer script for full credit.

---

**(b) Compartment Area Calculation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Formula:**
$$
A_{\max} = k \cdot FRL \cdot f_{\text{occ}} \cdot f_{\text{sprinkler}}
$$

**Step 2 — Unsprinklered Case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\max,\text{unsprinklered}} &= 1200 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 1.0 \\
&= 2520 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Compartments (Unsprinklered):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{unsprinklered}} &= \frac{A_{\text{total}}}{A_{\max,\text{unsprinklered}}} \\
&= \frac{4800}{2520} \\
&= 1.905 \\
\therefore n &= 2 \text{ compartments (round up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sprinklered Case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\max,\text{sprinklered}} &= 1200 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 2.0 \\
&= 5040 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of Compartments (Sprinklered):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{sprinklered}} &= \frac{A_{\text{total}}}{A_{\max,\text{sprinklered}}} \\
&= \frac{4800}{5040} \\
&= 0.952 \\
\therefore n &= 1 \text{ compartment (round up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**
| Case | $A_{\max}$ (m²) | Compartments Needed |
|------|------------------|----------------------|
| Unsprinklered | 2520 | **2** |
| Sprinklered | 5040 | **1** |

Installing a full sprinkler system reduces the required compartments from **2 to 1**, saving on fire wall construction cost.

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **principles of fire stopping in connecting structures**, with emphasis on junctions of wall-floor, wall-roof, service penetrations, expansion joints, and curtain wall spandrels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What are the **three criteria of fire resistance testing** under the standard time-temperature curve? Derive the standard fire curve temperature at $t = 45$ minutes and $t = 90$ minutes, and explain why all three criteria must be satisfied for an element to receive its rated FRL.

---

### Model Solution — Question B-B

**(a) Fire Stopping in Connecting Structures [7 Marks]:**

Connecting structures are the weakest points of any fire-stopping strategy. The following principles apply:

**1. Wall-Floor Slab Junction [1.5 Marks]:**
- A continuous fire-resistant joint is required at every slab-wall interface.
- Filling: mineral wool backfill + intumescent sealant on both faces.
- Tested per IS 12458 / EN 1366.

**2. Wall-Roof Slab Junction [1 Mark]:**
- Non-combustible filling (cement mortar + rock wool) over the wall-roof interface.
- Must be continuous over full wall length.

**3. Service Penetrations [2 Marks]:**
- **Pipes:** Intumescent collars expand on heating to close softened plastic pipe cross-section.
- **Cables:** Fire-rated pillows, putty, or mortar seals.
- **Ducts:** Fire dampers in HVAC ducts that close at 68 °C or upon smoke detection.
- Each penetration must use a tested firestop assembly, NOT field improvisation.

**4. Expansion Joints [1 Mark]:**
- Fire-rated expansion joint covers with mineral wool core + elastomeric facing.
- Must accommodate building movement without compromising integrity.

**5. Curtain Wall Spandrel Panels [1 Mark]:**
- Spatially separated spandrel zones at each floor level provide vertical fire stop.
- Each spandrel must achieve the same FRL as the floor slab.

**6. Door and Window Openings [0.5 Mark]:**
- Must be protected by **fire-rated door assemblies** (IS 3614 Part 2), self-closing and self-latching.

---

**(b) Three Criteria of Fire Resistance Testing [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Statement of Three Criteria [2 Marks]:**

A structural element is tested under the standard fire exposure curve:

$$
T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

For the element to receive a fire resistance rating, all three criteria must be met:

1. **Stability (R)** — No collapse; load-bearing capacity retained for the rated period.
2. **Integrity (E)** — No through-cracks or openings; no flame passage on the unexposed face.
3. **Insulation (I)** — Average temperature rise on unexposed face ≤ **140 °C**; max single-point rise ≤ **180 °C**.

The rating is denoted **REI** followed by time in minutes (e.g., REI 120 = 2 hours).

**Step 2 — Standard Fire Curve at t = 45 minutes [2 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(45) &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 45 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(361) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot 2.5575 \\
&= 20 + 882.34 \\
&\approx 902.3\ ^\circ C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Fire Curve at t = 90 minutes [2 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(90) &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 90 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(721) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot 2.8579 \\
&= 20 + 985.98 \\
&\approx 1006\ ^\circ C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Why All Three Criteria Must Be Satisfied [1 Mark]:**

A wall that is stable but has a crack has failed in **integrity** — flames and hot gases will pass through.
A wall that is integral but conducts too much heat has failed in **insulation** — combustible material on the cold face may ignite.
A wall that is integral and insulating but cannot support loads has failed in **stability** — it may collapse onto occupants or firefighters.

**Only when all three criteria are satisfied simultaneously** for the full rated period can the element be assigned its REI designation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the round-up rule** in compartment count: students often write $n = 1.5$ compartments and stop. Always round UP to the next whole integer.
> 2. **Omitting the sprinkler factor**: students frequently use $f_{\text{sprinkler}} = 1.0$ even when the problem says "fully sprinklered." The sprinkler factor is **2.0** (doubles the permissible area).
> 3. **Confusing Integrity with Insulation**: students sometimes interchange the 140 °C / 180 °C limits. Memorize: **140 °C average, 180 °C maximum single point**.
> 4. **Ignoring parapet height** in roof fire stopping questions: the answer is incomplete without specifying the **0.5 m to 1.0 m extension**.
> 5. **Skipping unit check** in numerical answers: always state final units (m² for areas, °C for temperatures, hours for FRL).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Fire Stopped Area** is a compartment enclosed by fire-resisting construction that limits fire spread for a specified period.
- ✅ The **three criteria** for any fire-resisting element are **Stability (R)**, **Integrity (E)**, and **Insulation (I)** — all three must be satisfied.
- ✅ The standard fire curve is **$T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$** °C, with $t$ in minutes (IS 3809 / ISO 834).
- ✅ **Insulation limits:** average temperature rise ≤ **140 °C**, single-point ≤ **180 °C** on unexposed face.
- ✅ **Fire walls** must extend through the roof with a parapet of **minimum 0.5 m to 1.0 m** to prevent lateral fire spread.
- ✅ **Roof penetrations** (HVAC, plumbing, electrical) must be sealed with tested firestop systems (fire dampers, intumescent collars, fire-rated pillows).
- ✅ **Maximum compartment area:** $A_{\max} = k \cdot FRL \cdot f_{\text{occ}} \cdot f_{\text{sprinkler}}$.
- ✅ **Sprinkler benefit:** $f_{\text{sprinkler}} = 2.0$ doubles the permissible area — a major cost-saving incentive.
- ✅ **Always round up** the number of compartments to the next whole integer.
- ✅ **Typical FRLs:** Fire wall = 4 hr, Compartment wall = 2 hr, Floor slab = 1–2 hr, Fire door = ≥ ½ of wall rating.
- ✅ **Connecting structure fire stops** must be provided at: wall-floor, wall-roof, service penetrations, expansion joints, curtain wall spandrels, and party walls.
- ✅ **Curtain wall spandrels** provide the vertical fire stop between floors and must match the floor slab FRL.
- ✅ **Expansion joints** need specialized fire-rated covers (mineral wool + elastomer) that accommodate movement.
- ✅ **Combustible roof materials** (timber trusses, thermocol insulation) must be protected or replaced.
- ✅ **Fire doors** must be self-closing AND self-latching — one without the other is non-compliant.
- ✅ **NBC 2016 / IS 12458 / IS 3614 / IS 3809** are the key reference standards for fire-stopping design in India.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Doors - Low Combustible, Non-Combustible and Spark-Proof Doors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire Doors - Low Combustible, Non-Combustible and Spark-Proof Doors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fire Door?

A **fire door** is a specialized door assembly (door leaf, frame, hardware, seals, and vision panel) that, together with its surrounding structure, is designed and tested to resist the passage of fire and smoke for a specified period under standardized fire-exposure conditions. The period for which the door resists fire is called the **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** and is expressed in minutes — typically **30, 60, 90, or 120 minutes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from syllabus intent):**
> A fire door is a *complete assembly* — not just the leaf — comprising the frame, the door leaf, glazing (if any), intumescent/smoke seals, hinges, latches, and closing devices. It must satisfy three independent performance criteria simultaneously during a fire test:
> 1. **Stability (R)** — the door must not collapse or warp.
> 2. **Integrity (E)** — no flames, hot gases, or continuous flaming on the unexposed face.
> 3. **Insulation (I)** — temperature rise on the unexposed face ≤ **140 °C average / 180 °C at any single point**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Model

Think of a building on fire as a **pressure cooker** that is rapidly heating up. A fire door acts like the **heavy, spring-loaded safety valve** on that cooker:
- It stays open during normal life (ease of egress).
- The moment heat is detected, seals expand and the door shuts **automatically**, sealing the cooker.
- It then refuses to let heat, flame, or smoke leak to the unburnt side until its timer (FRR) runs out — buying precious minutes for evacuation and rescue.

## 1.3 Why Three Sub-Categories?

Different industrial and occupancy environments have different **ignition triggers**:
- An **office corridor** mainly needs to stop fire spread (integrity).
- A **chemical storage room** may contain flammable vapours, where even a tiny spark from door hardware could ignite the room.
- A **boiler room or paint shop** deals with low-energy ignition sources — sparks, not flames.

Hence the same FRR can be achieved through **different material strategies**:
| Sub-Category | Primary Combustion Risk | Key Material Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Combustible Door** | Surface flame spread | Treated timber with limited contribution to fire |
| **Non-Combustible Door** | Structural collapse under heat | Steel/GI construction; zero fuel contribution |
| **Spark-Proof Door** | Frictional/impact sparks at hardware | Non-ferrous metal (brass, aluminium, bronze) at all friction points |

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Context — IS 3614 (Part 2): 1992** classifies fire-resistant doors into four grades based on FRR:
> - Grade A → **2 hours**, Grade B → **1 hour**, Grade C → **½ hour**, Grade D → **¼ hour**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Performance Criteria — Stability, Integrity, Insulation

Every fire door test is governed by the **time-temperature curve** of **ISO 834** (also adopted by IS 3614), which simulates a fully-developed compartment fire. The standard furnace temperature is given by:

$$
T \;=\; T_0 \;+\; 345 \, \log_{10}\!\left(8t+1\right)
$$

where:
- $T$ = furnace temperature in **°C** at time $t$
- $T_0$ = ambient temperature (usually **20 °C**)
- $t$ = time elapsed from ignition in **minutes**
- $\log_{10}$ = base-10 logarithm

The door is rated by the **shortest time** at which *any* of the failure criteria is breached. A door achieving 60 minutes for all three is rated **"EI 60"** (Europe) or **"Grade B (1 hour)"** (India).

### Logic Behind Each Sub-Type

#### A. Low Combustible Doors
- Built from **hardwood timber** (teak, sal, deodar) **impregnated** with fire-retardant chemicals — typically *monoammonium phosphate (MAP)*, *diammonium phosphate (DAP)*, or *boric acid–borax* mixtures.
- The chemicals **dehydrate** and release non-flammable gases when heated, diluting combustible vapours.
- Contribution to fire is *limited* (not zero) — hence the term **"low combustible"** rather than "non-combustible".
- FRR achievable: typically **30 to 60 minutes**.

#### B. Non-Combustible Doors
- Constructed primarily from **steel** (galvanized iron sheets, mild-steel angles, hollow sections).
- Per **IS 3614**, a material is "non-combustible" if it does not ignite, burn, or release flammable vapours when tested to **IS 3808**.
- Steel does **not** burn, but it **loses strength rapidly** above **550 °C** (about 50% of yield strength lost).
- FRR achievable: **60 to 120 minutes**, often with internal insulation (rock wool / ceramic wool).
- Critical design feature: **thermal bridging control** — preventing the unexposed face from exceeding 140 °C average.

#### C. Spark-Proof Doors
- Used in **hazardous areas** classified under **IS 5572** (Zone 0, 1, 2 for gases; Zone 20, 21, 22 for dusts).
- Every component that can produce friction sparks — hinges, latches, handles, kick plates, threshold — is fabricated from **non-ferrous alloys** (aluminium, brass, bronze, copper).
- Often built on a **non-combustible** (steel) skeleton but with non-ferrous surfacing where friction occurs.
- Tested to ensure that a **5 mm steel striker** dropped from **300 mm** does not ignite a **methane–air mixture (5–15% by volume)**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` pipe inside the table cells. Math-only symbols used.

| Parameter | Governing Equation / Value | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Fire Temperature | $T = T_0 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | °C, min | Furnace exposure |
| Heat Transfer (Conduction through leaf) | $q = \dfrac{k \, A \, \Delta T}{d}$ | W | Insulation design |
| Critical Steel Temperature | $T_{cr} \approx 550$ | °C | Non-combustible door integrity |
| Char Depth (Timber, charring rate $\beta$) | $d_c = \beta \, t$ with $\beta = 0.6$–$0.8$ | mm/min | Low combustible door |
| Char Rate Formula (Eurocode 5) | $\beta_0 = 0.65$ (softwood), $0.7$ (hardwood) | mm/min | Char rate constant |
| Effective Thickness Remaining | $t_{eff} = t_{original} - \beta \, t_{fire}$ | mm | Residual load-bearing |
| Smoke Leakage Rate | $\dot{V}_{leak} = K \, A \, \Delta P^{0.5}$ | m³/s | Cold smoke seal design |
| Intumescent Activation | Expansion ratio 5:1 to 30:1 (typically) | — | Sealing design |
| Spark Energy Threshold | $\geq 20$ mJ ignites methane-air | mJ | Spark-proof hazard |

> ⚠️ Note: Pipes inside table cells avoided by using `\vert` notiation only in math mode.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building Code Compliance (NBC 2016, Part 4)** mandates fire doors of specified FRR at:
  - Staircase enclosures
  - Fire-escape corridors
  - Electrical rooms, transformer rooms
  - Boiler rooms, generator rooms
  - Kitchen and pantry exits
- **Insurance Premiums**: Higher FRR + non-combustible construction reduce property insurance premiums by **15–30%** for industrial occupancies.
- **Oil & Gas Industry**: Spark-proof doors are mandatory at all openings between classified hazardous (Zone 1/2) and non-hazardous areas per **OISD-150**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Standard Fire Time–Temperature Relationship

The ISO 834 curve is empirically derived to mimic a real post-flashover compartment fire. We can **re-derive** the shape step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Empirical Observation
Real compartment fires in the **post-flashover** phase follow a quasi-logarithmic heating profile:

$$
T(t) \;\propto\; \log_{10}(t+1)
$$

This is because the fire's energy release stabilizes (fuel-controlled regime) and the building's thermal mass absorbs heat logarithmically.

### Step 2 — Establish Boundary Conditions
At $t = 0$ (ignition), $T = T_0$ (ambient).
At $t = 30$ min, real fire data shows $T \approx 842$ °C.
At $t = 60$ min, real fire data shows $T \approx 945$ °C.
At $t = 120$ min, real fire data shows $T \approx 1049$ °C.

### Step 3 — Calibrate the Equation
The general form is:
$$
T = T_0 + K \, \log_{10}(8t+1)
$$

The constant **8** inside the log is chosen so that at $t = 0$:
$$
\log_{10}(8 \cdot 0 + 1) = \log_{10}(1) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T = T_0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

### Step 4 — Determine K
Using $T_0 = 20$ °C and $T(30) = 842$ °C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
842 &= 20 + K \, \log_{10}(8 \cdot 30 + 1) \\
842 - 20 &= K \, \log_{10}(241) \\
822 &= K \cdot 2.382 \\
K &= \dfrac{822}{2.382} \\
K &\approx 345.07 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{K = 345 \;°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Standard Form
$$
T(t) \;=\; T_0 \;+\; 345 \, \log_{10}(8t+1)
$$

This is the universal ISO 834 / IS 3614 standard fire curve used in **every Indian fire-door furnace test**.

### Step 6 — Verify at t = 60 min
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(60) &= 20 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8 \cdot 60 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \, \log_{10}(481) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot 2.682 \\
&= 20 + 925.4 \\
&\approx 945.4 \;°C \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Excellent match with empirical data.

## 3.2 Derivation: Timber Door Charring Depth (Low Combustible)

### Step 1 — Charring Mechanics
When fire-exposed timber heats up:
- Below **100 °C** — moisture evaporates.
- Between **200–300 °C** — volatile gases released.
- Above **300 °C** — pyrolysis + charring begins.

A **charring layer** forms at $\approx$ 0.6–0.8 mm/min and acts as an insulator protecting inner wood.

### Step 2 — Eurocode 5 / IS 1635 Formula

$$
d_c \;=\; \beta_0 \, t
$$

where $\beta_0 = 0.65$ mm/min for softwood, $0.7$ mm/min for hardwood (densities $\rho > 450$ kg/m³).

### Step 3 — Effective Load-Bearing Thickness

$$
t_{eff} \;=\; t_{orig} \;-\; d_c \;=\; t_{orig} \;-\; \beta_0 \, t
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example
A low combustible hardwood door of original thickness **45 mm** exposed for **30 minutes**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_c &= 0.7 \times 30 = 21 \;\text{mm} \\
t_{eff} &= 45 - 21 = 24 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The remaining 24 mm still carries load → structural integrity (Stability criterion) maintained.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Low Combustible (Timber) | Non-Combustible (Steel) | Spark-Proof (Hazardous) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base Material | Treated hardwood | Mild/Galvanized steel | Steel skeleton + non-ferrous facing |
| Material Density | 600–800 kg/m³ | 7850 kg/m³ | Variable |
| Critical Failure Temperature | ~ 300 °C (charring onset) | ~ 550 °C (yield loss) | Hardware ignition > 20 mJ |
| Typical FRR | 30–60 min | 60–120 min | 30–120 min |
| Cost Index (relative) | 1.0× (baseline) | 1.4–1.8× | 2.5–4.0× |
| Typical Use | Office, hotels, residential | Staircases, plant rooms | Petrochemical, pharma, paint shops |
| Test Standard | IS 3614 / BS 476 Pt 22 | IS 3614 / EN 1634 | IS 5572 + IS 3614 |
| Smoke Seal | Optional intumescent | Mandatory intumescent + cold smoke | Mandatory; anti-static seals |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fire Door Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Door Frame Anchor] --> B[Galvanised Steel Frame 1.6 mm]
    B --> C[Intumescent Seal Strip Groove]
    B --> D[Cold Smoke Seal Groove]
    B --> E[Hinge Reinforcement Plate]
    E --> F[Door Leaf Core]
    F --> G[Mineral Wool Rock Wool 48 kg per m cube]
    F --> H[GI Sheet Skin 0.8 mm]
    F --> I[Non Ferrous Kick Plate]
    J[Top Channel Closer Arm] --> K[Hydraulic Door Closer]
    L[Panic Bar Latch] --> F
    M[Vision Panel Ceramic Glass 6 mm Wired] --> F
    N[Sill Threshold Anti Spark] --> B
    O[Coordinated Hold Open Electromagnetic Release] --> K
    P[Fire Alarm Signal Source] --> O
    Q[Door Coordination Switch 230 V AC] --> O
    R[Smoke Detector Photoelectric] --> P
    S[Heat Detector Fixed Temp 57 C] --> P

    classDef hw fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000

    class B,C,D,E,H,I,N,M hw
    class R,S,P sensor

```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Fire Door Sub-Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Room Hazard Class] --> B{Does room contain flammable vapours dust gases}
    B -- Yes --> C[Classify Zone 0 1 2 OR 20 21 22]
    C --> D[Select Spark Proof Door]
    B -- No --> E{Is the FRR requirement more than 60 minutes}
    E -- Yes --> F[Select Non Combustible Steel Door]
    E -- No --> G{Is the building aesthetic sensitive hotel office heritage}
    G -- Yes --> H[Select Low Combustible Treated Timber Door]
    G -- No --> F
    D --> I[Verify IS 5572 compliance]

    H --> J
    J --> K[Check intumescent seal activation temp 180 C]

```

## 4.3 Schematic of Fire Door Cross-Section (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unexposed Face Temperature Monitored] --> B[GI Skin 0.8 mm]
    B --> C[Air Gap 4 mm]
    C --> D[Mineral Wool Insulation 50 mm]
    D --> E[GI Skin 0.8 mm]
    E --> F[Intumescent Strip Activates at 180 C]
    F --> G[Exposed Face Subjected to ISO 834 Fire]
```
