
> [!NOTE]
> The **intumescent strip** is the heart of any fire door. Under heat, it expands 5–30× its original volume, sealing the gap between leaf and frame and blocking hot gas infiltration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]
**Define a fire door and state the three performance criteria it must satisfy as per IS 3614.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **fire door** is a complete assembly — door leaf, frame, hardware, glazing, and seals — designed and tested to resist the passage of fire and smoke for a specified duration (Fire Resistance Rating, FRR).

The three performance criteria per **IS 3614 (Part 2) : 1992** are:

1. **Stability (R)** — Door must not collapse, warp, or fail structurally during the test. *(1 mark)*
2. **Integrity (E)** — No passage of flames or hot gases through cracks, gaps, or the unexposed face; no sustained flaming on the unexposed side. *(1 mark)*
3. **Insulation (I)** — Average temperature rise on the unexposed face ≤ **140 °C** above ambient, and ≤ **180 °C** at any single point. *(1 mark)*

The FRR is the shortest time (in minutes or hours) at which any one criterion is breached.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
**Distinguish between Low Combustible and Non-Combustible fire doors with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Low Combustible Door | Non-Combustible Door |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Door in which the *combustible content is limited* by chemical impregnation. | Door made of materials that *do not ignite or burn* under fire conditions. |
| **Material** | Hardwood (teak, sal) impregnated with fire retardants such as monoammonium phosphate. | Galvanised iron / mild steel sheets with mineral-wool insulation. |
| **Fire Behaviour** | Chars slowly; contributes limited fuel. | Does not burn; loses strength above **550 °C**. |
| **Typical FRR** | 30–60 minutes | 60–120 minutes |
| **Example Use** | Hotel guest rooms, heritage buildings | Staircase enclosures, transformer rooms |

*(3 marks: 1 each for definition, material, and FRR/example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### OPTION A

#### Q.A (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]
**Explain in detail the construction, materials, and application areas of Non-Combustible fire doors. Discuss how the critical steel temperature influences their design.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition and Material Choice (2 marks):**
A non-combustible fire door is fabricated primarily from **mild steel or galvanised iron (GI)** with non-combustible core infill. As per IS 3614, the materials used must satisfy the non-combustibility test of IS 3808 — i.e., they must not ignite, burn, or release flammable vapours when exposed to 750 °C in a tubular furnace.

**2. Construction Details (2 marks):**
- **Door leaf:** Two GI sheets of 0.8–1.2 mm thickness bonded to a 48–64 kg/m³ rock-wool or ceramic-wool core.
- **Frame:** 1.6 mm GI sheet pressed into a channel section, anchored to the wall with masonry bolts at ≤ 600 mm centres.
- **Hardware:** Steel ball-bearing hinges, mortice latch, fire-rated panic bar; **180 °C** intumescent strips on the perimeter.
- **Glazing (if any):** 6 mm wired ceramic glass with steel beading.

**3. Application Areas (1.5 marks):**
- Staircase enclosures (NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 4.4.3).
- Electrical / transformer rooms.
- Plant rooms, boiler rooms, generator enclosures.
- Lift-machine rooms.
- Fire-rated corridors in high-rise (≥ 15 m) buildings.

**4. Critical Steel Temperature (1.5 marks):**
Structural steel begins to lose yield strength significantly above **400 °C** and retains only ~50% of its room-temperature yield at **550 °C**. Design of non-combustible doors therefore uses:

- **Insulation strategy** — rock-wool core to keep unexposed-face average temperature ≤ 140 °C.
- **Heat-sink sizing** — sheet thickness chosen so that temperature at unexposed face remains below 140 °C for the desired FRR.
- **Deflection check** — frame stiffness designed for the thermal expansion differential between the exposed and unexposed sides.

Thus, critical steel temperature governs both the **insulation thickness** and the **sheet gauge** selection.

---

#### Q.A (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]
**Describe the testing and certification procedure of fire doors as per IS 3614 (Part 2). Include the test apparatus, loading, observations, and FRR assignment.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Test Apparatus (1.5 marks):**
- **Furnace:** Gas-fired, capable of achieving the standard ISO 834 time–temperature curve.
- **Specimen wall:** Door installed in a representative masonry / concrete wall of thickness ≥ 200 mm.
- **Loading frame:** Hydraulic jacks apply design load (typically 1.0 kN/m²).
- **Thermocouples:** ≥ 5 on the exposed face, ≥ 5 on the unexposed face, plus 1 in the furnace.

**2. Standard Fire Exposure (1 mark):**

$$
T(t) \;=\; T_0 \;+\; 345 \, \log_{10}(8t+1)
$$

**3. Observations Recorded (1.5 marks):**
- Temperatures at all thermocouples every 60 seconds.
- **Integrity breaches:** Sustained flaming on unexposed face (> 10 s), cotton-wool pad ignition from hot gases through cracks, gap gauge of 6 mm / 25 mm.
- **Insulation breach:** Unexposed face average > 140 °C rise, or any point > 180 °C rise.
- **Stability breach:** Door collapse, separation from frame, or inability to bear load.

**4. Duration & FRR Assignment (1 mark):**
The test continues until *any* one criterion fails. The time elapsed (in whole hours or half-hours) is the **FRR**. Doors are graded per IS 3614 Part 2:
- Grade A — 2 h
- Grade B — 1 h
- Grade C — ½ h
- Grade D — ¼ h

**5. Certificate and Marking (1 mark):**
Each door carries a permanent **metal label / embossed marking** with:
- Manufacturer's name and licence number (BIS).
- FRR / Grade.
- Test reference and date.
- Direction of fire exposure (if not symmetric).

**6. Re-Test Conditions (1 mark):**
Doors with hardware changes, glazing changes, or leaf thickness changes require **re-testing** under the same protocol.

---

### OPTION B (Alternative Choice)

#### Q.B (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the construction, working principle, and design considerations of Spark-Proof fire doors used in hazardous (flammable vapour / dust) areas.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. When and Why (1.5 marks):**
Spark-proof doors are mandated wherever the **hazardous area classification** under IS 5572 identifies a risk of ignition from mechanical sparks. Examples:
- LPG bottling plants (Zone 1).
- Petrol pump pump-rooms (Zone 2).
- Pharmaceutical powder handling (Zone 21/22).
- Paint spray booths, solvent storage rooms.

**2. Working Principle (1.5 marks):**
Eliminate the **ignition source**. The door is constructed so that **any** mechanical operation (hinge rotation, latch engagement, handle grip, door swinging against a metal stop) cannot generate a spark of energy ≥ **20 mJ** — the minimum ignition energy of a stoichiometric methane–air mixture.

**3. Material and Hardware Choices (2 marks):**
- **Frame & leaf:** Non-combustible GI / steel skeleton.
- **All friction surfaces:** Non-ferrous alloys — **brass, aluminium bronze, copper-beryllium**, austenitic stainless steel.
- **Hinges:** Brass or aluminium-bronze pins in brass bushes.
- **Kick plates & thresholds:** Brass or aluminium; flush-mounted.
- **Anti-static intumescent seals** to dissipate static charge.

**4. Construction Details (1 mark):**
- Double-leaf with **coordinator** to ensure correct closing sequence.
- **Self-closing** via fusible-link closer (activation at 68 °C).
- **Panic hardware** in aluminium-bronze for escape.

**5. Design Considerations (1 mark):**
- Compliance with IS 5572 (hazardous area classification) and OISD-150 (oil industry).
- Spark test: striker of 5 mm steel dropped 300 mm on the door hardware in methane-air mixture must not ignite.
- Periodic inspection (every 6 months) for hardware wear.

---

#### Q.B (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Discuss the role of intumescent seals and smoke seals in fire doors. Compare their activation mechanisms and locations.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition and Role (1.5 marks):**
- **Intumescent seal:** A passive material that expands when heated, sealing gaps to block fire and hot gases.
- **Smoke seal:** A cold-smoke seal that operates at ambient temperatures to prevent leakage of toxic combustion gases (especially in the first 5–10 minutes before the fire intensifies).

**2. Activation Mechanism — Intumescent (2 marks):**
- Material: **Graphite-based** or **sodium silicate** compound bonded in a polymer carrier.
- Activation temperature: **150–200 °C**.
- Expansion ratio: typically **5:1 to 30:1**.
- Reaction: Heat → carrier softens → graphite flakes expand → seal swells to fill the gap between leaf and frame.
- Performance: Maintains integrity for the FRR period.

**3. Activation Mechanism — Smoke Seal (1.5 marks):**
- Material: **Silicone rubber**, **neoprene**, or **brush strip** with fine nylon filaments.
- Activation: **Mechanical compression** at ambient; no heat required.
- Function: Limits cold-smoke leakage to < **3 m³/h per metre of gap** at 25 Pa pressure (per IS 3614 Pt 4).
- Critical in staircases — smoke kills before fire does.

**4. Locations (1 mark):**
- **Intumescent:** Recessed in grooves on the perimeter of the leaf (top and two vertical edges), or behind the latch in the frame.
- **Smoke seal:** Along the meeting stile of double-leaf doors and the perimeter, in addition to (and often combined with) the intumescent strip.

**5. Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Intumescent Seal | Smoke Seal |
|---|---|---|
| Activation | Heat (≈ 180 °C) | Ambient |
| Material | Graphite / sodium silicate | Silicone / nylon brush |
| Expansion | Yes (5–30×) | No |
| Function | Hot-gas seal | Cold-smoke seal |
| Location | Perimeter, behind latch | Perimeter + meeting stile |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 evaluations:**
> 1. **Conflating "Low Combustible" with "Non-Combustible"** — they are NOT synonyms. Low combustible is *limited-fuel*; non-combustible is *zero-fuel*.
> 2. **Forgetting the three criteria (R, E, I)** — answers that omit Insulation lose at least 2 marks in Part A.
> 3. **Quoting FRR without units** — always say "**120 minutes**" or "**2 hours / Grade A**", not just "120".
> 4. **Ignoring intumescent seals** — Part B answers on fire doors that omit intumescent strips lose up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing IS 3614 with IS 3808** — IS 3614 is the *test method*; IS 3808 is the *non-combustibility test* for materials.
> 6. **Using SI units inconsistently** — temperatures must be in **°C**, time in **minutes or hours**, never mixed.
> 7. **Omitting the standard** — always cite IS 3614 (Part 2), IS 5572, or ISO 834 alongside the statement.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **fire door** is a **system**, not just a leaf — frame, hardware, seals, and glazing together decide FRR.
- **Three performance criteria**: Stability (R), Integrity (E), Insulation (I) — all checked against the **ISO 834** standard fire curve.
- **IS 3614 (Part 2)** grades doors as A (2 h), B (1 h), C (½ h), D (¼ h).
- **Low Combustible Doors** = chemically treated hardwood; char rate ≈ 0.7 mm/min; FRR 30–60 min.
- **Non-Combustible Doors** = GI/steel + mineral-wool core; critical steel temperature ≈ 550 °C; FRR 60–120 min.
- **Spark-Proof Doors** = all friction hardware in **brass / aluminium-bronze / copper**; used in **IS 5572 Zone 1/2 or 21/22** areas.
- **Intumescent seals** activate at **≈ 180 °C** and expand 5–30× to seal gaps.
- **Smoke seals** work at ambient temperature to block cold toxic smoke.
- **Hazardous-area** doors must pass a **spark-ignition test** with a 5 mm steel striker on a methane-air mixture.
- Always **cite the relevant IS code** in answers.
- Hardware (hinges, latch, closer) must be **fire-rated** and **listed** with the door assembly — substitution voids certification.
- Door must be **self-closing** — either by **door closer** or **fusible-link hold-open**.
- A fire door's **integrity** can be compromised by **unauthorised modifications** — adding a viewer, vent, or trimming the leaf voids the FRR.
- **Annual inspection** of seals, hardware, and closing force is mandatory under NBC 2016.
- The ISO 834 temperature formula **$T = T_0 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8t+1)$** is high-yield — expect either a 3-mark direct question or a 7-mark derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Suspension of Fire Doors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Method of Suspension of Fire Doors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method of Suspension of Fire Doors:** The engineered system of hinges, pivots, sliding tracks, or self-closing hardware used to hang, support, and operate a fire-rated door assembly, while maintaining its **fire resistance rating** (typically **30, 60, 90, 120, or 240 minutes**) and ensuring the door remains operable under fire and smoke conditions.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the *suspension method* is the **mechanical hanging mechanism** that transfers the door's dead load to the structural frame, while simultaneously allowing the door leaf to swing, slide, or close automatically to compartmentalize a fire. The hardware used **must be fire-rated** — that is, manufactured from materials with a **melting point ≥ 800 °C** (as per **IS 3614 (Part 1)** and **NFPA 80** guidelines).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a fire door like a shield hanging on a warrior's back.** A shield is useless if the strap (the hinge) melts the moment the fire begins. The "suspension" is the **strap, pivot, or track** that holds the shield in place — and just like a warrior's armor, every part of it (hinges, pivots, pivots pins, bearings) must be made of fireproof material so the shield stays ready to drop into place when needed.

In real buildings, when the **fusible link** (usually a 68 °C–74 °C alloy) melts during a fire, the door **must self-close** without fail. If the hinge melts, the door collapses into the frame and fails its **integrity (E)** and **insulation (I)** criteria under **BS 476 / IS 3614** testing.

---

## 1.3 Key Components Involved in Suspension

| Component | Primary Function | Fire-Resistance Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Hinge (Butt / Continuous / Spring)** | Pivotal support for swinging doors | Stainless Steel / Brass with intumescent pads |
| **Pivot Set (Offset / Center-Hung)** | Floor-and-head pivoting for heavy doors | Steel pivot pin with sealed bearings |
| **Self-Closing Device** | Automatic closure during fire | Spring steel with fusible link |
| **Coordinator / Selector** | Sequence closing of paired doors | Steel brackets |
| **Intumescent Hinge Pads** | Seal hinge gaps under heat | Graphite-based, expands at ~200 °C |
| **Track & Trolley** | Suspension for sliding fire doors | Galvanized steel, fire-rated |

---

## 1.4 Fire Door Rating Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield Data)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fire door's rating is **NOT** about the door leaf alone — **the entire assembly** (leaf + frame + suspension + seals) must achieve the rating. If the suspension fails at 30 minutes, the door fails at 30 minutes, **regardless of the leaf's rating**.

$$\boxed{\text{Fire Rating}_{\text{Assembly}} = \min(\text{Rating}_{\text{Leaf}}, \text{Rating}_{\text{Frame}}, \text{Rating}_{\text{Hardware}})}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Suspension Methods

Fire door suspension is broadly classified into **four categories** based on the **mode of operation**:

### 🔹 2.1.1 Hinged Suspension (Most Common)
- **Single-Action Hinges:** Door swings one way only; common in stairwell and corridor doors.
- **Double-Action (Swing) Hinges:** Allow 180° swing both ways; used in **kitchen and kitchen-pass doors** in hotels.
- **Continuous (Piano) Hinges:** Run full height of the door; used for **heavy acoustic or high-traffic fire doors**.

### 🔹 2.1.2 Pivot Suspension
- **Offset Pivot:** Pivot point is offset from door edge; allows wide swing and clean aesthetics.
- **Center-Hung Pivot:** Pivot axis is at the **center of door thickness**; reduces hinge-side gap and is preferred for **180° swing capability**.
- **Intermediate Pivot:** Used with top/bottom pivots to carry very heavy doors (>100 kg).

### 🔹 2.1.3 Sliding Suspension
- Used where swing is impractical (e.g., large industrial openings, lift lobbies).
- **Top-Hung Sliding System:** Trolleys run on overhead track; door is **suspended from above**.
- **Bottom-Rolling Sliding System:** Trolleys run on floor track; door is supported from below.
- **Wall-mounted Sliding:** Door slides into a wall pocket.

### 🔹 2.1.4 Special / Powered Suspension
- **Magnetic Hold-Open Devices:** Hold door open normally; release on **smoke detector signal** (fail-safe).
- **Pneumatic / Hydraulic Closers:** Controlled closure to prevent slamming.
- **Motorized Sliding Fire Doors:** Used in airports, malls, and industrial warehouses.

---

## 2.2 Why the Suspension Method Matters — The "Why & How"

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** A fire door is **only as strong as its weakest link**. The suspension is the mechanical backbone. If hinges fail, the door drops, gaps open, and **fire + smoke spread** to adjoining compartments — defeating the entire compartmentalization strategy of **NBC 2016 (Part 4, Fire & Life Safety)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "How":** Suspension hardware is selected based on **door weight, frequency of use, fire rating, swing direction, and code requirements**. Each suspension type transfers loads differently and behaves differently under thermal stress.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard Specification | Unit / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum Hinge Melting Point** | $\geq 800$ °C | IS 3614 / NFPA 80 |
| 2 | **Number of Hinges (Standard Door)** | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{H}{0.9} \right\rceil$ for door height $H$ in m | IS 3614 |
| 3 | **Hinge Size (Height of Knuckle)** | $h_{k} = 0.04 \times H_{\text{door}}$ (typical) | mm |
| 4 | **Door-to-Frame Clearance (Sides/Top)** | $\leq 3$ mm (typical 2–3 mm) | mm |
| 5 | **Door-to-Floor Clearance** | $\leq 6$ mm (smoke seals) ; $\leq 10$ mm (no seal) | mm |
| 6 | **Self-Closer Closing Force** | $F_{c} \geq 30$ N (min) at start, $F_{c} \geq 15$ N at 30° from latch | N (Newtons) |
| 7 | **Fusible Link Activation Temp** | $T_{f} = 68$ °C (ordinary) ; $T_{f} = 74$ °C (high-temp zones) | °C |
| 8 | **Intumescent Seal Expansion Temp** | $T_{\text{exp}} \approx 180$–200 °C | °C |
| 9 | **Fire Rating Equation** | $\text{Rating}_{\text{Assembly}} = \min(\text{Leaf, Frame, Hardware})$ | minutes |
| 10 | **Door Weight per m² (Timber)** | $w \approx 35$–$45$ kg/m² | kg/m² |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Suspension | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Hospital corridor doors** | Continuous (piano) hinge | Heavy daily use, hygiene, durability |
| **Hotel stairwell doors** | Spring-loaded butt hinge + self-closer | Auto-closure on fusible link |
| **Industrial warehouse** | Top-hung sliding track | Wide openings, no swing space |
| **Lift lobby** | Offset pivot + magnetic hold-open | Aesthetics + fail-safe release |
| **Server room** | Center-hung pivot + smoke release | 120-min rating + clean look |
| **Commercial kitchen pass** | Double-action spring hinge | 180° swing both ways |

---

## 2.5 Comparison of Suspension Methods (Engineering Trade-offs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever asked to "compare" suspension methods, always discuss on these **five parameters**: (1) Load capacity, (2) Fire rating capability, (3) Swing angle, (4) Maintenance needs, (5) Cost.

| Parameter | Butt Hinge | Pivot | Sliding Track | Powered |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max Load | Medium (~80 kg) | High (>150 kg) | Very High | Very High |
| Fire Rating Achievable | 30/60/120 min | 60/120/240 min | 60/120/240 min | 120/240 min |
| Swing | 90°–180° | Up to 180°+ | Linear | Linear |
| Maintenance | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Fail-Safe Closure | Via closer | Via closer | Via counterweight | Via power + battery backup |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Derivation

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Selecting the Number of Hinges for a Fire Door

### **Problem Context**
A fire-rated timber door has the following specifications:
- **Door height:** $H = 2100$ mm
- **Door width:** $W = 900$ mm
- **Door leaf thickness:** $t = 45$ mm
- **Material:** Teak (density $\rho \approx 650$ kg/m³)
- **Fire rating required:** 60 minutes

> We must determine the **number of hinges** and the **self-closer force** required as per KTU / IS 3614 guidelines.

---

### **Step 1 — Compute Door Mass**

The door's surface area is:

$$A = H \times W = 2.1 \, \text{m} \times 0.9 \, \text{m} = 1.89 \, \text{m}^2$$

The volume of the door leaf is:

$$V = A \times t = 1.89 \times 0.045 = 0.08505 \, \text{m}^3$$

The mass of the door is:

$$m = \rho \times V = 650 \times 0.08505 = 55.28 \, \text{kg}$$

---

### **Step 2 — Compute Door Weight (Dead Load on Hinges)**

$$W_{\text{door}} = m \times g = 55.28 \times 9.81 = 542.30 \, \text{N}$$

$$W_{\text{door}} \approx 542.3 \, \text{N} \;(\approx 54.23 \, \text{kgf})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Each hinge carries $W_{\text{door}}/N$ load. If $N$ is too low, the hinge **sags**, the door **binds**, and the **fire seal gaps open** — failing the integrity (E) criterion.

---

### **Step 3 — Compute Number of Hinges (N)**

Using the KTU formula:

$$N = \left\lceil \dfrac{H}{0.9} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{2.1}{0.9} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.33 \right\rceil = 3$$

$$\boxed{N = 3 \, \text{hinges}}$$

**Distribution along door height:**
- Top hinge: at $\dfrac{H}{N+1} = \dfrac{2.1}{4} = 0.525$ m from top
- Middle hinge: at center of door
- Bottom hinge: at $\dfrac{H}{N+1} = 0.525$ m from bottom

---

### **Step 4 — Hinge Sizing (Knuckle Height)**

For a 60-min rated fire door, the hinge knuckle height should be:

$$h_{k} = 0.04 \times H_{\text{door}} = 0.04 \times 2100 = 84 \, \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{h_{k} \approx 84 \, \text{mm} \quad (\text{use 100 mm standard hinge})}$$

---

### **Step 5 — Material Specification of Hinge**

Per **IS 3614 (Part 1)**, hinges for fire doors must be:

- **Material:** Stainless steel, brass, or mild steel with **intumescent backing pads**
- **Melting point:** $\geq 800$ °C
- **Bearing type:** Ball-bearing (for 60+ min ratings) or plain bearing with **graphite-impregnated pads**

---

### **Step 6 — Self-Closer Force Verification**

Per **EN 1154 / IS 3614**, the closer must deliver:
- $F_{c} \geq 30$ N at **0° (door fully open)**
- $F_{c} \geq 15$ N at **30° before latch**

**Torque check on the closer:**

$$\tau_{\text{closer}} = F_{c} \times r_{\text{arm}}$$

For a typical closer arm length $r_{\text{arm}} = 0.25$ m:

$$\tau_{\text{closer}} = 30 \times 0.25 = 7.5 \, \text{N·m}$$

Compare with door's moment of inertia-induced resistance — generally, this is satisfied for doors up to **100 kg**.

---

### **Step 7 — Clearance Verification (Critical for Fire Integrity)**

| Location | Required Clearance | Provided | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sides | $\leq 3$ mm | 2.5 mm | ✅ OK |
| Top | $\leq 3$ mm | 2.5 mm | ✅ OK |
| Bottom (no seal) | $\leq 10$ mm | 8 mm | ✅ OK |
| Bottom (with smoke seal) | $\leq 6$ mm | 5 mm | ✅ OK |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why < 3 mm?** During a fire, the **intumescent seal** at the perimeter expands up to **10× its original thickness**. If the gap is > 3 mm initially, even after expansion, **flame/ hot gas leakage may exceed the failure threshold** under BS 476 Part 22.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Suspension

| Scenario | Door Weight | Frequency | Recommended Suspension |
|---|---|---|---|
| Office cabin, 30 min rating | < 50 kg | Low | 2 × butt hinges + standard closer |
| Hospital, 60 min rating | 50–80 kg | High | 3 × ball-bearing butt + heavy closer |
| Industrial, 120 min rating | 80–150 kg | Medium | 3 × continuous (piano) hinge + heavy closer |
| Warehouse, 240 min rating | > 150 kg | Low | Top-hung sliding track with counterweight |
| Stairwell (lobby) | 50–100 kg | Medium | 3 × butt + electromagnetic hold-open + smoke release |
| Cinema/Airport | 80–120 kg | High | Center-hung pivot + concealed closer |

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three types of suspension methods used for fire doors and state the material requirement for fire-rated hinges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Hinged Suspension** — Butt, continuous (piano), and spring-loaded hinges.
2. **Pivot Suspension** — Offset, center-hung, and intermediate pivots.
3. **Sliding Suspension** — Top-hung track and bottom-rolling track systems.

**Material requirement for fire-rated hinges:** The hinge material (steel/brass) must have a **melting point ≥ 800 °C** as per IS 3614. For 60-min and above rated doors, **ball-bearing hinges with intumescent backing pads** are mandatory to maintain integrity and insulation.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of fusible link in the suspension system of a self-closing fire door.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **fusible link** is a temperature-activated device (typically made of a low-melting-point alloy rated at **68 °C** for normal conditions or **74 °C** for high-temperature zones) installed in the **chain or arm** of the self-closing mechanism. During a fire, when the ambient temperature at the ceiling reaches the link's activation temperature, the link **melts and breaks**, releasing the door from its hold-open state. The door then **automatically swings closed** under the action of the spring-loaded closer, ensuring **compartmentalization of the fire**. Without the fusible link, a propped-open fire door would allow **unrestricted fire and smoke spread**, defeating the entire fire safety design.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the following two questions. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

#### **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the different methods of suspending fire doors. Compare butt hinges and pivot suspension with respect to load capacity, swing angle, fire rating, and maintenance.

#### **(b) [7 marks]** A fire door is 2.4 m high, 1.0 m wide, and 50 mm thick, made of teak (density 700 kg/m³). Determine: (i) the mass of the door, (ii) the number of hinges required, and (iii) the closing torque required at the door's edge if a closer arm of 0.3 m is used. Assume minimum closing force of 30 N.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

The four primary methods of suspending fire doors are:

**1. Hinged Suspension:**
- Uses butt hinges (single or paired), continuous (piano) hinges, or spring-loaded self-closing hinges.
- The hinge pin is the **rotational axis**; door swings 90°–180°.
- **Load capacity:** Up to ~80 kg for standard butt hinges; >120 kg for ball-bearing.
- **Fire rating:** Achievable 30/60/120 min with correct intumescent pads.

**2. Pivot Suspension:**
- Uses offset, center-hung, or intermediate pivot sets mounted in **floor and head** of the frame.
- The pivot pin is **offset from the door edge** (offset) or at the **center of thickness** (center-hung).
- **Load capacity:** Up to 300 kg with intermediate pivots.
- **Fire rating:** 60/120/240 min.

**3. Sliding Suspension:**
- Door suspended from an **overhead track** with trolleys or supported by **floor-mounted rollers**.
- **Load capacity:** Practically unlimited; suitable for industrial doors.
- **Fire rating:** 60/120/240 min using counterweight or motorized closure.

**4. Powered/Magnetic Suspension:**
- **Electromagnetic hold-open** combined with smoke detection system.
- On alarm, **power cut** and door closes under spring or gravity.
- Used in hospitals, lobbies, and high-traffic areas.

**Comparison Table (Butt Hinge vs Pivot):**

| Parameter | Butt Hinge | Pivot |
|---|---|---|
| Load Capacity | Up to 80 kg | Up to 300 kg |
| Swing Angle | 90° (single) or 180° (double) | Up to 180°+ (smooth) |
| Fire Rating | 30/60/120 min | 60/120/240 min |
| Maintenance | Low (greasing) | Medium (floor pivot wear) |
| Aesthetic | Visible hinge leaves | Concealed (clean look) |
| Cost | Low | Medium-High |

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

#### **(i) Mass of the door**

$$A = H \times W = 2.4 \times 1.0 = 2.4 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$V = A \times t = 2.4 \times 0.050 = 0.12 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$m = \rho \times V = 700 \times 0.12 = 84 \, \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{m = 84 \, \text{kg}}$$

#### **(ii) Number of hinges**

$$N = \left\lceil \dfrac{H}{0.9} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{2.4}{0.9} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.67 \right\rceil = 3$$

$$\boxed{N = 3 \, \text{hinges}}$$

> **Placement:** Top hinge at 0.6 m from top, middle hinge at center, bottom hinge at 0.6 m from bottom (using $H/4$ distribution rule).

#### **(iii) Closing torque at the door edge**

Given closer arm length $r = 0.3$ m and minimum closing force $F_c = 30$ N:

$$\tau = F_c \times r = 30 \times 0.3 = 9.0 \, \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 9.0 \, \text{N·m}}$$

**Verification against door's moment arm:** The door is 84 kg, and the closing moment is applied at the **latch edge (1.0 m from hinge axis)**. The closer's torque is sufficient if:

$$\tau \geq W_{\text{door}} \times \mu \times \frac{W}{2} \times 9.81$$

For typical friction coefficient $\mu \approx 0.05$ in hinges:

$$\tau_{\text{resist}} = 84 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 0.5 = 20.6 \, \text{N·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** The student may forget to add the **gravity-induced moment** when the door is not at 0° (fully open). The closer's torque is **not constant** — it varies with door angle, peaking at 0° and falling as the door closes. The closer's spring constant must be **selected from the manufacturer's sizing chart** based on door weight and width.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

#### **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the constructional details of a top-hung sliding fire door. What are its advantages over hinged fire doors in industrial applications?

#### **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the importance of intumescent hinge pads in fire door assemblies. What happens if non-fire-rated hinges are used in a 60-minute rated fire door?

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

A **top-hung sliding fire door** consists of the following components:

1. **Door Leaf:** Steel-faced or timber-faced, fire-rated core (mineral wool / calcium silicate).
2. **Overhead Track:** Heavy-gauge galvanized steel channel bolted to the lintel/header.
3. **Trolleys / Hangers:** Steel roller-bearing trolleys running on the track.
4. **Bottom Guide Channel:** Floor-mounted channel that prevents lateral sway.
5. **Counterweight (optional):** For manually operated doors; balances the leaf weight.
6. **Self-Closing Device:** Spring-loaded or motorized, with fusible link.
7. **Perimeter Intumescent Seals:** Expand at ~200 °C to seal gaps.

**Working Principle:** The door leaf is **suspended from above** via trolleys. During a fire, the fusible link releases, the spring-loaded or counterweighted mechanism drives the door across the opening, and the intumescent seals expand to **close gaps** between leaf and frame.

**Advantages over Hinged Fire Doors in Industrial Applications:**

| Parameter | Top-Hung Sliding | Hinged |
|---|---|---|
| Opening Width | Up to 6 m+ (single) | Limited to ~1.2 m practical |
| Floor Space | No swing arc needed | Needs 1–1.5 m clear arc |
| Load Capacity | Practically unlimited | Limited by hinge rating |
| Air Pressure Surge | Resists blast/pressure | Door may be blown open |
| Wear Pattern | Trolley bearings | Hinge pin wear |
| Maintenance | Track cleaning, lubrication | Hinge replacement |

**Industrial Use Cases:** Warehouses, factories, aircraft hangars, large loading bays, transformer rooms, and petrochemical facilities.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Intumescent Hinge Pads:**

Intumescent hinge pads are **thin graphite-based or sodium-silicate-based strips** adhered to the back of hinge leaves or to the frame rebate behind the hinge. They remain **inert at room temperature** but **expand up to 10× their original thickness** when exposed to temperatures of **~180–200 °C**.

**Functions:**
1. **Seal the hinge gap** — Hinge leaves create a **natural 1–2 mm gap** when the door is closed. During a fire, this gap is a leakage path for **flames and hot gases**. The intumescent pad expands to **fill the gap**, restoring integrity.
2. **Maintain fire rating** — Without the pad, a 60-min rated door may fail at **30–40 minutes** due to hinge-gap leakage.
3. **Protect hinge integrity** — Some pads also insulate the hinge pin, delaying thermal degradation.

**Consequence of Using Non-Fire-Rated Hinges in a 60-min Rated Door:**

> [!WARNING]
> If **non-fire-rated hinges** (e.g., standard brass or unrated steel with **melting point < 600 °C**) are used in a 60-min rated fire door assembly:
> 1. The hinge **deforms and fails** at 25–35 minutes under fire exposure.
> 2. The door **collapses or sags**, opening a **major gap** between leaf and frame.
> 3. The intumescent perimeter seals alone **cannot compensate** for the structural collapse of the hinge.
> 4. **Flames and hot gases** spread to the protected compartment, defeating the compartmentalization.
> 5. The door fails the **Integrity (E)** and possibly **Insulation (I)** criteria of **BS 476 Part 22** / **IS 3614**, **invalidating the entire assembly's 60-min certification**.
> 6. The building becomes **non-compliant** with **NBC 2016 Part 4** and may lead to **legal and insurance liabilities**.

---

## 🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Fire Door Suspension Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "number of hinges" formula** — Students often write $N = H/0.6$ or $N = H/2$. **Correct: $N = \lceil H/0.9 \rceil$**.
> 2. **Forgetting the intumescent hinge pad** — In 60-min+ doors, this is **mandatory**, not optional.
> 3. **Ignoring door weight in closer selection** — Closers are **sized by door weight and width**, not just by "fire rating".
> 4. **Mixing up fire rating of leaf vs. assembly** — A 120-min leaf with 30-min hinges yields a **30-min assembly**.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify clearances** — Side gaps > 3 mm and bottom gaps > 6 mm (with seal) **invalidate** the assembly.
> 6. **Not drawing labelled diagrams** — Examiners award **partial credit** for clear schematics; a 14-mark answer **without a diagram is at risk of losing 1–2 marks**.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Method of Suspension of Fire Doors**

- 🔥 **Fire door assembly rating = min(leaf, frame, hardware)** — the suspension is **part of the assembly**.
- 🔥 **Hinge melting point must be ≥ 800 °C** per **IS 3614 / NFPA 80**.
- 🔥 **Standard hinges:** Butt, Continuous (Piano), Spring — and **material:** Stainless steel or brass.
- 🔥 **Pivots:** Offset, Center-Hung, Intermediate — used for **heavy doors** and **wide swing** requirements.
- 🔥 **Sliding doors:** Top-hung track is **most common** in industrial applications; bottom-rolling for lighter doors.
- 🔥 **Number of hinges formula:** $N = \lceil H/0.9 \rceil$ — for a 2.1 m door → **3 hinges**.
- 🔥 **Hinge knuckle height:** $h_k \approx 0.04 \times H_{\text{door}}$.
- 🔥 **Clearances:** Sides/Top ≤ 3 mm; Bottom ≤ 6 mm (with smoke seal) or ≤ 10 mm (without).
- 🔥 **Fusible link:** 68 °C (normal) or 74 °C (high-temp zones) — **releases** self-closer on activation.
- 🔥 **Intumescent hinge pads:** Expand at ~180–200 °C; **mandatory** for 60-min+ doors.
- 🔥 **Self-closer force:** ≥ 30 N at 0° opening, ≥ 15 N at 30° before latch.
- 🔥 **Magnetic hold-open** must have **battery/smoke-release backup** for fail-safe operation.
- 🔥 **NBC 2016 Part 4** mandates fire doors in **stairwell enclosures, lift lobbies, corridors, and refuge areas**.
- 🔥 **Codes referenced:** IS 3614 (Parts 1 & 2), BS 476 Part 22, NFPA 80, EN 1634.
- 🔥 **Always verify:** Door weight, swing arc, fire rating, and **sealing arrangement** before selecting suspension.
- 🔥 **In exam answers:** Draw a **labelled block diagram** of the suspension arrangement — earns partial credit.

---

*End of Module 4 Notes — Method of Suspension of Fire Doors*
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — OESFT835: Structural Fire Safety*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air-Tight Sealing of Doors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air-Tight Sealing of Doors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Air-tight sealing of doors** in the context of structural fire safety refers to the engineered use of compression gaskets, intumescent strips, smoke seals, and threshold devices at the perimeter interfaces (head, jambs, and sill) of fire-rated doorsets to prevent the uncontrolled passage of **hot gases, toxic smoke, and flame** between fire compartments during a fire event.

According to **IS 3614 (Part 2):1992** and **BS EN 1634-3:2004**, the air-tightness of a fire door assembly is a non-negotiable performance parameter because it directly controls the **integrity criterion (E)** during the standard fire exposure test (ISO 834 / BS 476 Part 22 cellulosic curve). Any leakage beyond the stipulated cotton-wool pad / gap-gauge tolerance causes immediate test failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition Snapshot**
> *Air-tight sealing of doors* is the *combination of perimeter gaskets, intumescent materials, and threshold devices* designed and tested to maintain *integrity (E)* and *smoke leakage rate (Sₐ or S₂₀₀)* criteria of a fire-resisting doorset throughout its rated fire-resistance period.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your kitchen pressure cooker. The rubber gasket (the black ring inside the lid) is the **air-tight seal of a fire door**. When the cooker heats up, the gasket swells slightly to plug every micro-leak — preventing steam (smoke and fire gases) from jetting out. Now scale this idea up to a **multi-storey building on fire**: every fire door is a *pressurised lid* sealing off a *burning compartment*. If the gasket (intumescent seal) is missing, broken, painted over, or wrongly installed, the smoke escapes into the corridor and stairwell — exactly what killed many occupants in past high-rise tragedies.

- The **door leaf** = pressure cooker body
- The **door frame** = pressure cooker lid
- The **intumescent seal** = rubber gasket that swells under heat
- The **smoke seal** = silicone O-ring that blocks cold smoke even before heating
- The **threshold drop seal** = the safety valve at the bottom

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Metrics & Tolerances

| Parameter | Typical Value / Limit |
|---|---|
| Perimeter gap (closed door) | **2 mm – 4 mm** (flush); up to **6 mm** (rebated) |
| Threshold gap | **≤ 10 mm** (with drop seal), else **≤ 3 mm** |
| Intumescent activation temperature | **150 °C – 200 °C** |
| Expansion ratio (graphite) | **5:1 to 30:1** |
| Smoke leakage — Class Sₐ | **≤ 3 m³/h per metre** at **25 Pa** (ambient temperature) |
| Smoke leakage — Class S₂₀₀ | **≤ 20 m³/h per metre** at **25 Pa** at **200 °C** |
| Test pressure differential | **25 Pa** (BS EN 1634-3) |
| Integrity (E) failure | Sustained flaming > **10 s** OR cotton pad ignition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two temperatures for seals?** Cold smoke (≤ 200 °C) is generated in the very first 60–90 seconds of a fire — *before* the intumescent strip even activates. That is why a **dual-seal strategy** (silicone smoke seal + intumescent fire seal) is mandatory for KTU-classification fire doors in escape routes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Anatomy of a Sealed Fire Doorset

A complete air-tight fire doorset is composed of **five functional layers**, each engineered to counter a specific threat:

1. **Door leaf (core)** — Solid timber, particle board, or mineral core providing thermal mass. Rated for **30 / 60 / 90 / 120 minutes** (FD30, FD60, FD90, FD120).
2. **Door frame** — Hardwood or steel; must be *grouted* into the wall and *intumescent-sealed* at the back-fill.
3. **Hinges & self-closing device** — Three hinges minimum, CE-marked, fire-tested *as an assembly*.
4. **Perimeter seals** (the actual airtight system):
    - (a) **Intumescent strip** — graphite / sodium silicate in PVC carrier, fitted in the *leaf edge* or *frame reveal*.
    - (b) **Smoke seal** — silicone, EPDM, or neoprene brush/gasket fitted to the *stop* of the frame.
    - (c) **Threshold (drop-down) seal** — automatic, spring-loaded; drops on closure.
    - (d) **Glazing seals** — ceramic-fibre tape + intumescent strip around vision panels.
5. **Lock/latch** — Cannot penetrate the intumescent zone; must be steel-reinforced.

### 2.2 Types of Seals — Engineering Logic

**A. Intumescent Seals (Reactive Seals)**
- *Mechanism*: At ~150 °C the material undergoes a chemical decomposition releasing water/CO₂ and expanding into a low-density carbonaceous char, sealing the gap mechanically.
- *Materials*:
  - *Graphite-based*: high expansion (10:1 to 30:1), low pressure — preferred.
  - *Sodium silicate (water glass)*: low expansion, high pressure — older technology.
  - *Hybrid ceramic-graphite*: for 120-minute ratings.
- *Sizing rule*: Graphite strip width ≈ **1.5 × maximum design gap**; depth ≈ 2 mm minimum.

**B. Smoke Seals (Passive Seals)**
- *Mechanism*: Mechanical compression prevents cold-smoke migration.
- *Materials*: Silicone (best for ageing), EPDM (cost-effective), neoprene (industrial).
- *Profile*: Strip (D-shape, P-shape, brush).
- *Compression*: 1–2 mm; over-compression sets the seal permanently.

**C. Threshold / Bottom Seals**
- *Automatic drop seals*: aluminium housing with silicone gasket, gravity/spring-activated.
- *Brush seals*: for uneven floors; less airtight, only for smoke (Sₐ) not integrity.
- *Ramped thresholds*: external doors, wheelchair-accessible.

**D. Glazing Seals**
- Ceramic-fibre tape + intumescent liner around fire-rated glass (e.g., Pyrobel, Pyrostop).
- Maintains **insulation (I) criterion** — limits radiative heat transfer through glass.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the *exact* set required for KTU ESE numerical/derivation questions on door sealing.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{V} = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Orifice flow through door gaps (Bernoulli-derived leakage) | m³/s |
| 2 | $\Delta P_{stack} = \rho_0 \, g \, H \, \left(\dfrac{T_i - T_o}{T_o}\right)$ | Stack pressure driving leakage in a fire shaft | Pa |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, C_p \, v^2$ | Wind-induced pressure differential across façade door | Pa |
| 4 | $t_{act} = \dfrac{T_{act} - T_\infty}{R \, \dot{q}''}$ | Time for intumescent to reach activation temp (1-D heat conduction) | s |
| 5 | $k_{exp} = \dfrac{V_{final}}{V_{initial}}$ | Volumetric expansion ratio of intumescent material | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{leak} = \rho \, C_d \, A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Mass leakage rate (smoke mass balance) | kg/s |
| 7 | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^4$ | Radiative heat through glazed door (insulation I criterion) | W |
| 8 | $R_{total} = \dfrac{L}{\lambda \, A}$ | Thermal resistance of seal composite | K/W |

> **Notation:** $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (~0.6 for sharp-edged gap); $A$ = total gap area (m²); $\Delta P$ = pressure differential (Pa); $\rho$ = gas density (kg/m³); $T_{act}$ = activation temperature (K); $\dot{q}''$ = incident heat flux (W/m²); $R$ = thermal resistance of door leaf (m²K/W); $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of glass/char; $\sigma$ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smoke control pressurisation** in lift lobbies and stair shafts depends entirely on the *Sₐ-class* smoke leakage of the doorset. A poorly sealed door defeats the pressurisation system.
- **Energy code compliance** (ECBC / ECBC+) for green buildings requires sealed fire doors to also act as *thermal breaks*.
- **Insurance valuations** (e.g., NFPA / FM Global audits) downgrade a building if intumescent seals are missing, painted over, or replaced with generic PVC.
- **Forensic fire investigation** (Arson cases in India) frequently reveals *failure of air-tight seals* as the proximate cause of smoke spread across compartments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Air Leakage Through Perimeter Gap of a Closed Door

**Problem statement.** A 1.0 m × 2.1 m fire door is closed in a stairwell pressurised to +12.5 Pa above the fire floor. The perimeter gap (head + 2 jambs) totals 8.0 m long with an average width of 3 mm. The corridor gas is at 300 K; the stairwell at 293 K. Estimate the volumetric leakage rate of smoke into the stairwell.

**Step 1 — Total gap area**

$$
A = L_{gap} \times w = 8.0 \times 0.003 = 0.024 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply orifice equation**

For a thin gap, $C_d \approx 0.60$.

$$
\dot{V} = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert gas density at 300 K**

$$
\rho = \frac{p}{R T} = \frac{101325}{287 \times 300} \approx 1.177 \;\text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values**

$$
\dot{V} = 0.60 \times 0.024 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12.5}{1.177}}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate inner radical**

$$
\sqrt{\frac{25.0}{1.177}} = \sqrt{21.24} = 4.609 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Final leakage rate**

$$
\dot{V} = 0.60 \times 0.024 \times 4.609 = 0.0664 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 239 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation**

This single door leaks ~239 m³/h of smoke. If a stairwell pressurisation system is sized for 0.5 m³/s but has 20 such doors, leakage alone = **1.33 m³/s** — *exceeding* the supply. **Engineering takeaway**: smoke seals are not optional accessories; they are integral to pressurisation design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time to Activate Intumescent Seal (1-D Transient Conduction)

**Problem statement.** A 45 mm thick timber-core door leaf is exposed to a standard furnace at $T_\infty = 945\;\text{°C}$. The intumescent seal activates at $T_{act} = 180\;\text{°C}$. Calculate the *first-arrival* activation time at the leaf edge, assuming $R_{leaf} = 0.27\;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$ and incident flux $\dot{q}'' = 35\;\text{kW/m}^2$.

**Step 1 — Driving temperature difference**

$$
\Delta T = T_{act} - T_\infty = 180 - 945 = -765\;\text{°C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply conduction-based activation time**

$$
t_{act} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{leaf}\,\dot{q}''} = \frac{-765}{0.27 \times 35{,}000}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify denominator**

$$
0.27 \times 35{,}000 = 9{,}450\;\text{K·m}^{-2}\text{W}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute**

$$
t_{act} = \frac{765}{9{,}450} \approx 0.081\;\text{min} \approx 4.9\;\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Real-world correction**

In practice, $t_{act}$ is delayed to **30–90 s** because:
- The seal is recessed (not directly on the hot face).
- The fire is *not* immediately adjacent.
- Char formation insulates underlying material.

**Engineering takeaway**: activation is fast enough that **smoke seals must be the first line of defence** in the initial 60 s — intumescent alone is too late for the tenability window.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stack Pressure Driving Smoke Leakage

**Problem statement.** A fire in a 30 m high atrium creates a hot layer at $T_i = 873\;\text{K}$; ambient $T_o = 293\;\text{K}$. Calculate the stack pressure pushing smoke through a door at the *base* of the shaft.

**Step 1 — Write the stack equation**

$$
\Delta P_{stack} = \rho_0 \, g \, H \left(\frac{T_i - T_o}{T_o}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Air density at ambient**

$$
\rho_0 = 1.204\;\text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$
\Delta P_{stack} = 1.204 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times \left(\frac{873 - 293}{293}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate temperature ratio**

$$
\frac{580}{293} = 1.980
$$

**Step 5 — Final pressure**

$$
\Delta P_{stack} = 1.204 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 1.980 = 701.5\;\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 6 — Implication**

A **701 Pa** pressure differential is enormous — it will blow past almost any *single* intumescent strip. This is why **stairwell pressurisation of +12.5 Pa to +50 Pa** is mandated by NBC 2016, and why *smoke leakage class S₂₀₀* is rated at 25 Pa *only at 200 °C* — not at full stack pressure.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection Guide

| Threat → | Cold Smoke (< 200 °C) | Hot Gas (200–600 °C) | Flame (> 600 °C) | Radiation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Silicone smoke seal** | ✔✔✔ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ |
| **Intumescent (graphite)** | ✘ | ✔✔✔ | ✔✔✔ | ✔ |
| **Mineral wool in frame** | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✔✔✔ | ✔✔ |
| **Ceramic-fibre glazing tape** | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✔✔✔ | ✔✔✔ |
| **Drop-down threshold** | ✔✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✔ | ✘ |

> [!NOTE]
> **No single seal handles every threat** — this matrix is the canonical justification for the *multi-layer perimeter sealing system* mandated by **IS 3614** and **BS 8214**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sealed Fire Doorset

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Source in Compartment A] --> B[Hot Plume rises to Ceiling]
    B --> C[Radiative and Convective Heating of Door Leaf]
    C --> D[Door Leaf Core - Timber or Mineral]
    D --> E[Intumescent Strip in Leaf Edge]
    E --> F[Frame Reveal with Smoke Seal]
    F --> G[Door Frame Grouted into Wall]
    G --> H[Threshold Drop Seal at Sill]
    H --> I[Sealed Compartment B - Protected]
    C --> J[Glazing Panel with Ceramic Tape Seal]
    J --> I
    K[Hinges and Self-Closer] --> D
    L[Stairwell Pressurisation +12.5 Pa] --> F
```

### 4.2 Failure-Cascade Diagram — How a Defective Seal Causes Compartment Failure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Smoke Generation] --> S2[Step 2: Pressure Build-up]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Gap Leakage Initiated]
    S3 --> S4{Seal Status}
    S4 -- Intact --> S5[Smoke Blocked - Compartment Holds]
    S4 -- Missing --> S6[Smoke Infiltration into Corridor]
    S4 -- Painted Over --> S7[Intumescent Cannot Expand]
    S6 --> S8[Tenability Failure in Escape Route]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Flashover in Adjacent Compartment]
    S9 --> S10[Full Building Smoke Logging]
```

### 4.3 Layered Seal Cross-Section (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frame Reveal - Hardwood] --> B[Smoke Seal - Silicone P-Profile]
    B --> C[Intumescent Strip - Graphite 10x20mm]
    C --> D[Door Leaf Edge - Timber 45mm]
    D --> E[Intumescent Strip on Opposite Edge]
    E --> F[Frame Reveal - Opposite Side]
    G[Threshold] --> H[Drop Seal Aluminium Housing]
    H --> I[Silicone Gasket to Floor]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree for Selecting a Smoke Seal Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Door Application Identified] --> B{Fire Rating Required}
    B -- FD30 --> C[Intumescent 10x2mm + Smoke Seal Optional]
    B -- FD60 --> D[Intumescent 15x2mm + Smoke Seal Mandatory]
    B -- FD90 --> E[Intumescent 20x4mm + Dual Smoke Seal]
    B -- FD120 --> F[Intumescent 25x4mm + Ceramic + Smoke Seal]
    C --> G{Smoke Rating}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- Escape Route --> H[Sa Class - Smoke Seal Required]
    G -- Non-Escape --> I[Seal Optional]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*What is an intumescent seal? State its activation mechanism with reference to graphite-based material.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An intumescent seal is a *reactive fire-sealing strip* fitted in the perimeter edge of a fire door leaf or frame, which *expands on heating* to seal the gap between the door and frame, thereby preventing passage of hot gases and flame. In graphite-based material, at temperatures between **150 °C and 200 °C**, the graphite intercalation compound oxidises and expands along the c-axis, producing a voluminous, low-density carbonaceous char with expansion ratios of **10:1 to 30:1**. This char mechanically plugs the gap, maintaining the *integrity (E)* criterion of the doorset throughout its fire-resistance rating (FD30/FD60/FD90/FD120). (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
*Differentiate between Sa and S200 smoke leakage classes as per BS EN 1634-3.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Class Sₐ | Class S₂₀₀ |
|---|---|---|
| Test temperature | Ambient (~20 °C) | **200 °C** |
| Test pressure | **25 Pa** | **25 Pa** |
| Permissible leakage | **≤ 3 m³/h per metre** | **≤ 20 m³/h per metre** |
| Intended use | Cold-smoke barrier in escape routes | Combined cold/hot smoke barrier |

Class Sₐ is verified at ambient temperature to confirm the *smoke seal alone* is functional before intumescent activation, whereas S₂₀₀ tests the *combined intumescent + smoke seal* performance at elevated temperature. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> *Students commonly lose marks in three places:* (1) failing to mention *both* integrity (E) *and* smoke leakage classes (Sₐ / S₂₀₀) when asked about "air-tightness"; (2) confusing *intumescent* with *smoke seal* — they are *not* interchangeable; (3) omitting reference to **IS 3614 / BS 476 Part 22 / BS EN 1634-3** standards — naming the code book carries **at least 1 mark** in every 14-mark answer.

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the *functional components* of a fully sealed fire-resisting doorset rated FD60. Explain the role of each component in maintaining integrity (E) and insulation (I) criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stairwell is pressurised to +12.5 Pa against a fire floor at 30 °C. The door perimeter gap totals 6 m with an average width of 3 mm. Compute the smoke leakage rate (m³/s) into the stairwell. State whether a smoke seal of class Sₐ (≤ 3 m³/h per m at 25 Pa) would satisfy NBC 2016 for this door. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION A

**(a) — Components of an FD60 Fire Doorset (7 marks)**

An FD60 doorset is a complete, factory-assembled, tested unit designed to maintain *integrity (E) and insulation (I)* for **60 minutes** under the standard cellulosic fire exposure (ISO 834 / BS 476 Part 22). The functional components are:

1. **Door leaf (1 mark):** Typically 45–54 mm thick, with a *solid timber* or *mineral-core* construction. Provides thermal mass and structural stability during fire exposure. Must be certified for FD60 as a complete assembly — not the leaf alone.

2. **Door frame (1 mark):** Hardwood (density ≥ 650 kg/m³) or cold-formed steel. *Grouted* into the wall reveal along the full perimeter. Frame-to-wall gap filled with *cementitious mortar or mineral wool* to maintain continuity of the fire compartment.

3. **Perimeter intumescent strip (2 marks):** Graphite-based strip of cross-section **15 × 2 mm** fitted in the leaf edge OR frame reveal. Activates at ~180 °C, expands to ~30 mm char width, sealing the gap and maintaining the *integrity (E)* criterion by blocking flame and hot gas passage.

4. **Smoke seal (1 mark):** Silicone P-profile or brush seal fitted to the frame *stop*. Maintains the *smoke leakage (Sₐ)* criterion by mechanically blocking cold smoke in the first 60–90 seconds — *before* the intumescent activates.

5. **Threshold drop-down seal (1 mark):** Spring-loaded aluminium extrusion at the sill. Drops on door closure to seal the **≤ 10 mm** floor gap. Critical because the *bottom of the door* is the most common leakage point.

6. **Hardware — three hinges + self-closer (1 mark):** Fire-tested as part of the assembly; ensures positive closure under any condition. Closer prevents the door being propped open and defeating all sealing.

**Insulation (I) criterion** is maintained primarily by the *thermal mass of the leaf* and the *char expansion of the intumescent*, which together limit mean temperature rise on the unexposed face to **≤ 140 °C** average / **≤ 180 °C** at any point (per BS 476 Part 22).

---

**(b) — Leakage Calculation and Sₐ Verification (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Total gap area** (1 mark)

$$
A = 6.0 \times 0.003 = 0.018 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Air density at 30 °C = 303 K** (1 mark)

$$
\rho = \frac{101325}{287 \times 303} = 1.165 \;\text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply orifice equation with $C_d = 0.60$** (2 marks)

$$
\dot{V} = 0.60 \times 0.018 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12.5}{1.165}}
$$

$$
\dot{V} = 0.0108 \times \sqrt{21.46} = 0.0108 \times 4.633
$$

**Step 4 — Final volumetric leakage** (1 mark)

$$
\dot{V} = 0.0500 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 180 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalise to per-metre leakage** (1 mark)

$$
\frac{180}{6} = 30 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h·m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with Sₐ class** (1 mark)

Class Sₐ permits **≤ 3 m³/h·m** at 25 Pa. The calculated value is **30 m³/h·m** — *ten times* the limit. A generic smoke seal would NOT satisfy NBC 2016; only a *tested, certified Sₐ-class* seal installed to the manufacturer's tolerances would comply, AND only after verification that the door has been installed within the manufacturer's gap tolerances (≤ 3 mm perimeter, ≤ 10 mm threshold with drop seal).

> **Conclusion:** Door leakage at typical installation tolerances vastly exceeds the Sₐ limit unless the door is fitted with a tested smoke seal AND installed within manufacturer tolerances. Stairwell pressurisation design must include door leakage as an explicit load on the supply fan.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the *classification, materials, and selection criteria* for intumescent seals used in fire doors. Discuss the role of expansion ratio and activation temperature in design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 25-storey office building has a fire in the 20th floor. The stack height from fire floor to roof is 30 m. The fire-floor gas temperature rises to 600 °C. Calculate the stack pressure driving smoke into a corridor door and comment on whether a standard intumescent strip can resist this pressure differential unaided. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION B

**(a) — Intumescent Seal Classification (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** An intumescent seal is a *passive fire protection device* fitted in the perimeter of a fire door that *reversibly or irreversibly expands* when exposed to heat, sealing gaps that would otherwise allow passage of flame and hot gases.

**Classification (2 marks):**

| Type | Activator | Expansion Ratio | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Graphite-based* | Intercalation oxidation ~150 °C | **10:1 – 30:1** | Modern FD60/FD90/FD120 |
| *Sodium silicate* | Dehydration ~100 °C | **3:1 – 5:1** | Older FD30 designs |
| *Hybrid ceramic-graphite* | Combined | **5:1 – 15:1** | High-rating + impact resistance |
| *Monoammonium phosphate* | Decomposition ~200 °C | **5:1 – 8:1** | Humid environments |

**Material selection criteria (2 marks):**
1. *Activation temperature* must be **< 200 °C** to seal before timber char becomes brittle.
2. *Expansion ratio* must overcome the *maximum design gap* (typically 4–6 mm) — graphitic strips are preferred for larger gaps.
3. *Pressure of expansion* must be sufficient (≥ 0.5 MPa) to resist fire-side pressure but not so high as to *bow the door leaf*.
4. *Ageing & humidity resistance*: PVC carrier must be UV-stable; some carriers degrade above 80 % RH.
5. *Test certification*: only seals *tested as part of the doorset assembly* are valid.

**Design role (2 marks):** The expansion ratio $k_{exp} = V_{final}/V_{initial}$ directly determines the *minimum strip cross-section* required:

$$
w_{strip} \geq \frac{g_{max}}{k_{exp} - 1}
$$

For a 6 mm gap with $k_{exp} = 10$, $w_{strip} \geq 0.67\;\text{mm}$. Hence a **2 mm thick strip** is universally adopted to provide a *safety factor of 3* and account for char shrinkage.

---

**(b) — Stack Pressure Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin** (1 mark)

$$
T_i = 600 + 273 = 873\;\text{K}, \qquad T_o = 30 + 273 = 303\;\text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the stack equation** (2 marks)

$$
\Delta P_{stack} = \rho_0 \, g \, H \left(\frac{T_i - T_o}{T_o}\right)
$$

with $\rho_0 = 1.165 \;\text{kg/m}^3$ at 30 °C, $H = 30\;\text{m}$, $g = 9.81\;\text{m/s}^2$.

**Step 3 — Temperature ratio** (1 mark)

$$
\frac{T_i - T_o}{T_o} = \frac{873 - 303}{303} = \frac{570}{303} = 1.881
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation** (1 mark)

$$
\Delta P_{stack} = 1.165 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 1.881 = 645\;\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 5 — Pressure comparison with intumescent capacity** (1 mark)

Graphite intumescent generates a sealing pressure of **0.4 – 1.0 MPa** (400 000 – 1 000 000 Pa) — vastly greater than 645 Pa. **In isolation, the intumescent can resist this pressure.**

**Step 6 — Critical commentary** (1 mark)

However, *in practice*, the intumescent alone is **inadequate** for the following reasons:
1. *Activation delay*: takes ~30–90 s to fully expand; smoke moves in the first 60 s.
2. *Pressure peaks* during flashover can transiently exceed **2 kPa**, overwhelming even expanded seals.
3. *Gap tolerance*: if installed outside manufacturer tolerances (gap > 6 mm), the char cannot fully seal the gap regardless of expansion ratio.
4. *No cold-smoke protection*: cold smoke (≤ 200 °C) is generated *before* activation.

**Hence, the design requires a *combination* of smoke seal (Sₐ-class) for the first 60 s + intumescent for sustained integrity, complemented by active stairwell pressurisation (typically +12.5 to +50 Pa) to counter the stack pressure.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Air-tight sealing of doors = perimeter seals + intumescent + smoke seals + threshold device** — never just one component.
- ✅ **Two-stage strategy**: cold smoke seal handles **0–60 s**, intumescent handles **60 s onward** under fire exposure.
- ✅ **Integrity (E)** failure modes: *sustained flaming > 10 s*, *gap gauge penetration*, *cotton pad ignition*.
- ✅ **Insulation (I)** criterion: mean temperature rise **≤ 140 K**, max single point **≤ 180 K** on unexposed face.
- ✅ **Standard gap tolerances**: perimeter **2–4 mm**, threshold **≤ 10 mm with drop seal** OR **≤ 3 mm without**.
- ✅ **Smoke leakage classes**: **Sₐ ≤ 3 m³/h·m @ 25 Pa (ambient)**, **S₂₀₀ ≤ 20 m³/h·m @ 25 Pa (200 °C)** — per BS EN 1634-3.
- ✅ **Intumescent activation temperature** ≈ **150 – 200 °C**; expansion ratio up to **30:1** (graphite).
- ✅ **Stack pressure** in tall buildings can reach **600–700 Pa** — intumescent seals *alone* are inadequate without smoke seals and pressurisation.
- ✅ **Test standards to cite**: **IS 3614 (Part 2):1992**, **BS 476 Part 22**, **BS EN 1634-1**, **BS EN 1634-3**, **NBC 2016 (India)**.
- ✅ **Common installation faults**: painted-over intumescent strips, wrong gap tolerance, missing threshold seal, missing closer, glazing seals omitted — all nullify certification.

> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** ***I-S-T-S*** = **I**ntumescent + **S**moke seal + **T**hreshold + **S**elf-closer — every fire door needs **all four**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification, Test and Performance Criteria of Plate, Metal Covered and Rolling Type Fire Doors as per BIS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT835 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Specification, Test and Performance Criteria of Plate, Metal Covered and Rolling Type Fire Doors as per BIS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Fire Doors** are specialized assemblies of materials and components (frames, shutters, hardware, and seals) designed to provide a specific degree of **fire resistance** when tested in accordance with **IS 3614 (Part 1): 2021** and **IS 3614 (Part 2): 1992** – the governing Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) specifications. They are mandatory passive fire protection elements used to compartmentalize buildings and prevent the spread of fire and smoke through openings in fire-rated walls and floors.

As per the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4)** and **IS 3614**, the three principal categories of fire doors covered in this module are:

1. **Plate Type Fire Doors** (also called Flush Fire Doors)
2. **Metal Covered (Hollow Metal) Fire Doors**
3. **Rolling (Roller) Type Fire Doors / Fire Shutters**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key BIS Standards Governing This Topic:**
> - **IS 3614 (Part 1): 2021** – Fire-resistant doors: Plate, Metal Covered, and Rolling Type – *Specification*
> - **IS 3614 (Part 2): 1992** – Fire-resistant doors: Plate, Metal Covered, and Rolling Type – *Performance Criteria and Test Method*
> - **IS 3808: 1989** – Fire-resistant rolling shutters – *Specification*
> - **IS 14609: 2018** – Fire resistant rolling shutters – *Specification* (revised)
> - **IS 3614 (Part 4): 1996** – Thermally insulated fire doors (supplementary reference)
> - **BS 476 (Parts 20–22)** – The international reference for fire-resistance testing (referenced indirectly by BIS)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine your house is divided into rooms by walls, and a fire starts in the kitchen.

- **Without fire doors:** The fire and smoke rush through every doorway into bedrooms, hallways, and staircases, trapping occupants within minutes.
- **With fire doors:** Each doorway is sealed with a special "smart" door that stays strong and intact for **2 hours**, **1 hour**, or **120 minutes** (depending on its rating), allowing people to evacuate safely and firefighters to contain the blaze.

**Real-World Analogy:**

Think of fire doors like the **bulkhead doors in a submarine**. A submarine is divided into several sealed compartments. If one section floods, the watertight doors (bulkheads) prevent water from filling the entire vessel. Similarly, fire doors act as **fire bulkheads** in buildings — they prevent fire from spreading from one compartment (zone) to another, buying precious evacuation time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rating of Fire Doors:** Expressed in minutes/hours, e.g., **2-hour fire door (FD 120)**, **1-hour (FD 60)**, **4-hour (FD 240)**. The number indicates how long the door assembly maintains its integrity (E), insulation (I), and stability when exposed to the standard fire curve (IS 3614 Part 2 / BS 476 Part 20).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Test Duration:** **120 minutes (2 hours)** is the most common commercial/industrial fire door rating.
- **Standard Fire Exposure Curve (Heating):** Follows **IS 3614 (Part 2)** / ISO 834 / BS 476 – the temperature rises approximately as:
  
  $$T = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$
  
  Where $T$ = furnace temperature (°C), $T_0$ = ambient temperature (°C), $t$ = time elapsed (minutes).

- **Mean Temperature Rise Limit:** **140 °C above ambient** on the unexposed face (insulation criterion).
- **Single Point Temperature Rise Limit:** **180 °C above ambient** at any location on the unexposed face.
- **Cotton Pad Test Threshold:** Gases escaping through cracks must not ignite a cotton pad held 20–30 mm away (integrity criterion).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fire Doors – Structured Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Plate Type Fire Doors (Flush Type)

**"Why" this design?**
A plate-type (flush) fire door provides a smooth, seamless appearance suitable for offices, hospitals, hotels, and residential buildings where aesthetics matter. The door leaf is a sandwich panel: a **core of fire-resistant material** (calcium silicate, vermiculite, mineral wool, perlite, or gypsum) bonded between two steel skins or hardwood facings.

**"How" it works:**

| Component | Material | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Core** | Calcium silicate / vermiculite / mineral wool | Low thermal conductivity, releases bound water under heat (endothermic cooling) |
| **Skins/Facings** | 1.0–1.5 mm GI/MS sheets OR commercial ply | Provide structural rigidity and impact resistance |
| **Stiles & Rails** | Hardwood (teak) or steel | Frame the core; provide hinge & lock support |
| **Intumescent Seals** | Graphite/borosilicate-based strips on edges | Expand 10–30× when heated (~200 °C) to seal gaps |
| **Smoke Seals** | Silicone/EPDM gaskets | Prevent cold smoke leakage |

**Specification Highlights (as per IS 3614 Part 1):**
- Door leaf thickness: **44 mm to 55 mm** (typical)
- Core density: **400–900 kg/m³** for calcium silicate boards
- Hardware: 3 heavy-duty ball-bearing hinges (minimum), CE-marked/self-closing device, fire-rated lockset.

### 2.1.2 Metal Covered (Hollow Metal) Fire Doors

**"Why" this design?**
These are the **workhorses of industrial fire protection** — factories, warehouses, power plants, transformer rooms, basements, and commercial kitchens. The door is essentially a **steel shell** filled with insulating core.

**"How" it works:**

The leaf is constructed from **two steel sheets (0.8–1.5 mm thick)** formed into a hollow panel by welding or interlocking, then **filled** with a fire-resistant core (rock wool, ceramic fibre, calcium silicate). The result is a robust, impact-resistant door capable of **FD 60 to FD 240** ratings.

**Specification Highlights:**
- Shell thickness: **0.8 mm to 1.5 mm** galvanized/CRCA steel
- Core: 50–70 mm thick mineral wool (density **48–96 kg/m³**)
- Frame: 1.2–2.0 mm steel press-formed, with anchor bolts at ≤ 600 mm c/c
- **No visible welds** on exposed surfaces; joints ground flush
- Hot-dip galvanized as per **IS 4759** or **IS 2629** (for corrosion resistance)

### 2.1.3 Rolling Type Fire Doors / Fire Shutters

**"Why" this design?**
Rolling shutters are used where **headroom is limited**, doorways are **very large** (factories, aircraft hangars, shopping mall atria, transformer yards), or a **sliding/swinging door is impractical**. The door is a curtain of interlocking galvanized steel slats that roll up into a barrel assembly above the opening.

**"How" it works:**

The shutter comprises **slats** (cold-formed galvanized steel, 18–22 gauge) interlocked together, wound around a **barrel/pipe** at the top, guided by **channel guides** on both sides, and closed by gravity (fail-safe) when activated.

**Specification Highlights (per IS 3808 / IS 14609):**

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Slats** | 75 mm wide × 1.0–1.25 mm thick GI steel, interlocking |
| **Barrel** | MS pipe of 100–150 mm diameter, 3.2 mm thick |
| **Guide Channels** | 50–75 mm wide × 1.6 mm thick GI |
| **Bottom Lock** | Push-pull type or slide bolt |
| **Drive** | Manual chain OR gear-operated electric motor (3-phase, 415 V) |
| **Fail-safe** | Gravity drop with governor-controlled descent (≤ 0.2 m/s) |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression / Value | Units | Significance |
|--------------------|---------------------|-------|--------------|
| **Standard Fire Curve (IS 3614 Pt 2 / ISO 834)** | $T = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | °C, min | Furnace temperature vs time |
| **Mean Temperature Rise (Insulation)** | $\Delta T_{mean} \leq 140$ | °C above ambient | Insulation criterion |
| **Single Point Temperature Rise** | $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180$ | °C above ambient | Local hotspot limit |
| **Thermal Conductivity (Insulation Core)** | $k = 0.04$ to $0.07$ | W/m·K | Mineral wool / calcium silicate |
| **Heat Transfer Through Slab** | $q = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{d}$ | W | One-dimensional conduction |
| **Cotton Pad Test** | No ignition in 30 s | — | Integrity (cracks/flames) |
| **Stability Criterion** | No collapse for full duration | — | Stability under self-load + fire |
| **Door Rating (FD Rating)** | $FD = \text{Integrity Time (min)}$ | minutes | Standard designation |
| **Fire Resistance Rating** | $E + I$ time | minutes | E = Integrity, I = Insulation |
| **Intumescent Expansion Ratio** | $L_{final}/L_{initial} = 10$ to $30$ | — | Sealing expansion factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Fire Door Criteria – "ESI":**
> - **E** – **E**ntegrity: No flames/hot gases escape (cotton pad test).
> - **S** – **S**tability: Door remains in place (no collapse).
> - **I** – **I**nsulation: Un-exposed face stays cool (≤ 140 °C mean rise).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Compartmentalization** in high-rise buildings (NBC 2016 mandates fire doors in stairwell enclosures, lift lobbies, service shafts).
2. **Transformer room protection** in substations (typically **FD 120**).
3. **Industrial process safety** — oil refineries, chemical plants (FD 240 for high-hazard zones).
4. **Transport infrastructure** — metro stations, airports, tunnels.
5. **Insurance compliance** — most property insurance policies require BIS-rated fire doors.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Fire Curve (IS 3614 Pt 2 / ISO 834)

The standard fire exposure curve mathematically describes how furnace temperature rises with time during a fire-resistance test. The BIS adopts the same formulation as ISO 834 and BS 476 Part 20.

**Given:**
- $T_0$ = initial ambient temperature inside furnace (°C), typically **20 °C to 30 °C**
- $t$ = elapsed time (minutes)
- $T$ = furnace temperature at time $t$ (°C)

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Standard Empirical Form (BIS/ISO/BS adopted):**

The standard equation for the time-temperature curve is:

$$
T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at standard reference times:**

| Time $t$ (min) | $\log_{10}(8t+1)$ | $T(t)$ at $T_0 = 20$ °C (°C) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.000 | 20.0 |
| 5 | 1.653 | 590.0 |
| 10 | 1.954 | 694.0 |
| 30 | 2.462 | 869.0 |
| 60 | 2.748 | 968.0 |
| 90 | 2.914 | 1025.0 |
| 120 | 3.033 | 1066.0 |
| 240 | 3.301 | 1158.0 |

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The curve is **logarithmic** — temperatures rise very rapidly in the first 10 minutes, then plateau. After **30 minutes**, the furnace exceeds **860 °C**, simulating a fully developed (post-flashover) compartment fire.

**Step 4 — Application to fire door design:**

The door must survive this heating regime. The heat flux to the un-exposed face is governed by Fourier's law:

$$
q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{d}
$$

Where:
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of door core (W/m·K)
- $A$ = door area (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference across door leaf (°C)
- $d$ = door leaf thickness (m)

**Step 5 — Insulation Verification:**

The criterion $\Delta T_{mean} \leq 140$ °C is checked on the un-exposed face using thermocouples (typically **5 thermocouples** as per IS 3614 Pt 2, Clause 6).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the values at $t$ = 30, 60, 90, 120 minutes — these are frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions!

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Performance Criteria per Door Type

| Performance Criterion | Plate Type (Flush) | Metal Covered | Rolling Type |
|----------------------|---------------------|---------------|--------------|
| **Typical Rating (min)** | 60–120 | 120–240 | 60–120 |
| **Integrity (E)** | ✓ Cotton pad test | ✓ Cotton pad test | ✓ Cotton pad test |
| **Insulation (I)** | ✓ Mean ΔT ≤ 140 °C | ✓ Mean ΔT ≤ 140 °C | ✓ Mean ΔT ≤ 140 °C (with infill) |
| **Stability (R)** | ✓ Self-load + impact | ✓ Self-load + impact | ✓ Self-load only |
| **Self-closing Device** | Required | Required | Gravity drop (fail-safe) |
| **Intumescent Seals** | Recommended | Required on edges | Required on guides |
| **Smoke Leakage** | < 3 m³/h/m (per IS 3614 Pt 4) | < 3 m³/h/m | < 3 m³/h/m |
| **Cycle Test (Opening/Closing)** | 50,000 cycles | 50,000 cycles | 5,000 cycles |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Hot-dip GI / powder coat | Hot-dip GI as per IS 4759 | Hot-dip GI as per IS 2629 |

## 3.3 Test Sequence as per IS 3614 (Part 2)

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Full-size door installed in test frame; conditions for 28 days prior to testing.
2. **Instrumentation:** Thermocouples on unexposed face (5 minimum), furnace thermocouples, pressure sensors.
3. **Heating Phase:** Subject door to standard fire curve (Section 3.1).
4. **Monitoring:** Record integrity, insulation, stability every minute.
5. **Hose Stream Test:** For some ratings, hot specimen sprayed with water (impact + cooling).
6. **Determination of Rating:** Time at which **any** of E, I, or S is breached.
